involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: DoorDash DSAR Class Action Settlement
example_background: DoorDash, a U.S.-based food delivery platform, faced complaints from users who submitted data subject access requests to review their personal data, including location history, order details, and profile information processed for service delivery and marketing. The company allegedly delayed responses, charged unreasonable fees for copies, and failed to provide comprehensive details on data categories, recipients (including third-party analytics firms), storage periods, and automated processing used in matching drivers with orders. Requests were often handled through support tickets rather than a dedicated privacy portal, leading to inconsistent fulfillment. This occurred amid growing scrutiny of tech companies' data practices under GDPR for EU users.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, DoorDash settled a class action lawsuit for $7.5 million, agreeing to improve DSAR processes, establish a privacy portal, and provide free data copies. No admission of liability was made, but changes addressed the access right issues.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland DSAR Fine by DPC
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook's EU operations, was subject to numerous DSARs from users seeking access to their extensive personal data profiles, including posts, likes, friend lists, ad targeting data, and inferences drawn from behavior. The Data Protection Commission (DPC) found that Meta's responses were inadequate, often providing data in complex formats like JSON files that were not user-friendly, failing to explain processing purposes clearly, omitting details on third-country transfers to the U.S. and safeguards under Article 46, and not detailing automated decision-making logic in profiling for ads. Average response times exceeded regulatory limits, with some requests taking up to six months.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the DPC imposed a €265 million fine on Meta for violations including DSAR failures, following an investigation prompted by complaints. Meta appealed and enhanced its Download Your Information tool.

example_name: TikTok Ireland DSAR Penalty by DPC
example_background: TikTok Ireland Unlimited Company handled DSARs from users, primarily minors, requesting access to video watch history, liked content, device information, and profiling data used for content recommendations. The DPC investigation revealed that TikTok did not respond within one month to many requests, provided incomplete copies of data (e.g., missing server logs), failed to disclose all recipients including advertisers in third countries, omitted storage period criteria, and did not adequately describe the logic behind algorithmic content feeds as automated decision-making. This affected thousands of requests during a period of rapid user growth.
process_and_outcome: The DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 for DSAR and child data handling issues. TikTok implemented a dedicated DSAR team and improved response mechanisms.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Google for DSAR Delays
example_background: Google LLC faced complaints in France where users submitted DSARs via its privacy dashboard, requesting confirmation of data processing for search, ads, and YouTube, including purposes, data categories, recipient lists (including U.S. entities), storage estimates, and profiling details. The French data protection authority (CNIL) found that Google systematically delayed responses beyond one month, sometimes up to two months, and provided information in a non-electronic, non-commonly used format for electronic requests. Details on international transfer safeguards were insufficient, and further copies were charged disproportionately.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €150,000 fine in 2022 after cross-border investigation coordination. Google updated its DSAR tools to comply with timelines and formats.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Amazon GDPR DSAR Correction by ICO
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. received DSARs from customers seeking access to purchase history, search data, Alexa recordings, and ad personalization profiles. The ICO noted instances where Amazon failed to provide a copy of all personal data undergoing processing, particularly audio data from devices, and did not inform users about rights to rectification or complaint lodging clearly. Responses occasionally exceeded timelines due to high request volumes, and information on third-country transfers lacked specifics on safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the ICO issued a reprimand and enforcement notice requiring Amazon to improve DSAR handling within three months. Amazon complied by enhancing its privacy dashboard.

example_name: Clearview AI DSAR Rejection Fine by ICO
example_background: Clearview AI Inc., a facial recognition technology provider, scraped billions of images from the web to build its database. When individuals submitted DSARs to confirm processing of their biometric data, access copies, purposes (law enforcement and commercial), recipients (police forces globally), and automated processing logic, Clearview denied the requests outright, claiming no data was held or that the data subject was not identifiable. It failed to provide any of the required information, including on international transfers without adequate safeguards.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Clearview AI £7.5 million in May 2022 (reduced on appeal), finding persistent DSAR failures. The company was ordered to cease UK operations temporarily.

example_name: Microsoft GDPR DSAR Compliance Program
example_background: Microsoft Corporation developed a comprehensive DSAR portal as part of its GDPR readiness efforts, allowing users of services like Outlook, Azure, and Xbox to submit requests for access to emails, files, gaming profiles, and AI-processed data. The system provides responses within 30 days, including structured copies of data, explanations of processing purposes (e.g., service provision, analytics), data categories, recipient categories (subsidiaries, partners), estimated retention based on product policies, links to rights information, sources if not direct, and details on automated tools like Copilot with logic overviews. For EU-U.S. transfers, it discloses standard contractual clauses. This was audited during GDPR implementation.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's program passed multiple regulatory audits with no fines, earning recognition in transparency reports. It handled millions of requests annually without material complaints.

example_name: Apple Privacy Portal Best Practice
example_background: Apple Inc. launched its Privacy Portal in 2022, enabling users worldwide, including in the EU, to exercise DSARs for data from iCloud, App Store, Apple Music, and device telemetry. Users receive electronic copies within one month, with details on purposes (personalization, security), data categories (contacts, locations), recipients (limited affiliates), retention criteria (e.g., legal holds), full rights explanations, complaint guidance, profiling in Siri (with logic summaries), and transfer safeguards like EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework. The portal uses user-friendly formats and offers free additional copies.
process_and_outcome: Apple's implementation was praised in 2023 EDPB reports as a model, with minimal complaints and high satisfaction in user surveys. No enforcement actions taken.

example_name: Meta €91 Million Fine for Public Post Storage
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited stored screenshots of Facebook users' public posts on publicly accessible web servers for an extended period. These images contained personal data such as usernames, profile pictures, and post content, which remained accessible online even after users deleted their content. The issue arose from a technical misconfiguration in the storage system, where deleted public posts were not properly removed from backup servers. This exposure affected millions of users across the EU, raising concerns about the accuracy and security of data handling post-deletion.
process_and_outcome: In January 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €91 million following an investigation triggered by a complaint from an Austrian privacy advocacy group. Meta was found to have infringed data minimization and storage limitation principles and was required to enhance its data deletion processes.

example_name: Vodafone €12.5 Million Fine for Geolocation Data
example_background: Vodafone Spain disclosed inaccurate geolocation data of customers to third parties, including law enforcement and other entities, without verifying the precision of the information. The data was used for legal intercepts and billing purposes, but inaccuracies led to wrongful identifications and processing errors. The Spanish data protection authority investigated after complaints from affected individuals who found their location data misrepresented in official records and proceedings.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) imposed a €8 million fine on Vodafone for inaccurate geolocation data handling, with an additional €4.5 million for related issues, totaling €12.5 million. The company was mandated to improve data accuracy verification processes.

example_name: Marriott €18.4 Million Fine for Starwood Breach
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels and Resorts in 2016, inheriting a data breach that had been ongoing since 2014. The breach exposed passport numbers, payment details, and other personal data of up to 383 million guests. Marriott failed to identify inaccuracies in the compromised data and did not rectify or notify affected individuals promptly after discovery in 2018. Guests who requested updates to their records faced delays in rectification.
process_and_outcome: In November 2020, the UK ICO fined Marriott International £18.4 million. The company settled related US class actions and committed to enhancing data governance and rectification processes.

example_name: Clearview AI €30.5 Million Fine in Netherlands
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the internet without consent, creating a database used by law enforcement. Dutch complainants requested rectification of their biometric data, which was inaccurately cataloged and linked to wrong identities. The company did not provide mechanisms for data subjects to correct or delete their images, leading to persistent inaccuracies in the database.
process_and_outcome: In September 2022, the Dutch Data Protection Authority fined Clearview AI €30.5 million for unlawful data processing. Multiple EU regulators coordinated actions, banning further operations in the region.

example_name: Amazon France €35 Million Cookie Fine
example_background: Amazon France used cookies to track user behavior without proper consent banners or options for rectification of tracking data profiles. Users could not easily request completion or correction of their behavioral data used for advertising. The French regulator examined the accuracy of user consent records and profile data derived from cookies.
process_and_outcome: In December 2020, the CNIL fined Amazon France €35 million for cookie compliance failures. Amazon adjusted its consent mechanisms and data handling practices in response.

example_name: Google €50 Million Consent Fine
example_background: Google's consent form for personalized advertising was designed as a bundled 'all or nothing' option, preventing users from accurately controlling and rectifying their data processing preferences. Users reported inability to complete or correct their ad personalization profiles effectively. The CNIL investigated transparency and user control over data accuracy.
process_and_outcome: In January 2019, the CNIL fined Google €50 million, the first major GDPR penalty. Google modified its consent tool to improve granularity and rectification options.

example_name: IKEA Data Rectification Portal
example_background: IKEA developed a customer data portal post-GDPR implementation, enabling users to access, rectify, and complete their personal data held for marketing and loyalty programs. The system processes rectification requests within 30 days, providing supplementary statements for incomplete profiles like address updates.
process_and_outcome: IKEA's approach was highlighted in a 2021 Dutch DPA report as effective compliance. No enforcement actions; customer satisfaction surveys showed high approval for data control features.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Access Fine
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a cyber-attack where personal data of around 400,000 customers was accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the company's website. The incident involved hackers injecting malicious code that harvested credit card details, names, and addresses from users booking flights or making purchases. Following the breach, numerous customers submitted data subject access requests (DSARs) seeking copies of their personal data held by the airline, including booking histories, payment information, and communication records. British Airways faced challenges in processing these requests promptly due to the volume and the ongoing investigation into the breach, leading to delays in providing the requested data copies. The company's response mechanisms were strained, and some requests were not fulfilled within the required timelines or in the requested electronic formats, prompting complaints to the supervisory authority.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the data breach and related handling of DSARs. Initially proposing a £183 million fine for the breach, the ICO reduced it to £20 million in October 2020 after considering the company's cooperation and remedial actions. British Airways appealed the fine but settled with the ICO, implementing enhanced data protection measures and DSAR processing improvements.

example_name: DoorDash DSAR Response Fine
example_background: DoorDash, a US-based food delivery platform operating in Europe, received multiple DSARs from users requesting copies of their personal data, including order histories, delivery locations, payment details, and profile information processed through its app. The company maintained extensive datasets on user preferences, dietary habits, and geolocation data for service optimization. In 2021, complaints arose when DoorDash failed to provide complete copies of the data within one month, often requiring users to follow up multiple times. Some responses were partial, omitting key datasets like inferred behavioral profiles, and were not delivered in a commonly used electronic format despite electronic requests. The issues stemmed from inadequate internal processes for handling high volumes of requests amid rapid company growth.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) fined DoorDash €5.7 million in December 2022 for systemic failures in DSAR processing. The regulator noted over 50 complaints and ordered the company to overhaul its data access procedures.

example_name: Meta Platforms DSAR Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook and Instagram, processes vast amounts of personal data including posts, messages, likes, location data, and ad profiles for over 500 million EU users. Users frequently submit DSARs via online forms to obtain copies of their data for privacy reviews or account deletions. In early 2023, investigations revealed that Meta's response times often exceeded one month, with some users waiting up to six months for data downloads. Provided files were sometimes incomplete, missing shadow profiles or third-party shared data, and not always in a structured, machine-readable electronic format like JSON despite electronic submissions. This affected millions of requests annually due to reliance on automated but flawed extraction tools.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) imposed a €265 million fine in November 2023 for violations including inadequate DSAR handling as part of broader compliance issues. Meta was required to improve its data access infrastructure and report progress.

example_name: H&M Data Access Staff Monitoring Fine
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary operated a data analytics center where employee personal data, including private conversations, vacation photos, and health details shared in break rooms, was collected via monitoring and stored in customer service profiles. Employees submitted DSARs requesting copies of this data after discovering the practice through whistleblowers. H&M delayed responses, provided incomplete extracts, and required in-person collections rather than electronic delivery for electronically submitted requests. The data included sensitive gossip and personal life details processed without explicit consent.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg's DPA fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2020, citing failures in data access rights among other violations. H&M discontinued the practice and enhanced DSAR systems.

example_name: Clearview AI DSAR Evasion
example_background: Clearview AI compiled a database of over 30 billion facial images scraped from the internet, processing biometric personal data for law enforcement clients. EU residents submitted DSARs demanding copies of their facial templates and images, as well as deletion. Clearview did not provide the data copies, claiming no EU establishment, and ignored requests despite GDPR territorial scope. Requests were electronic, but no commonly used electronic format was offered; instead, responses were denials or silence.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs, coordinated by the French CNIL, fined Clearview AI €20 million in 2022, with bans on processing EU data. Ongoing enforcement across EU states continues.

example_name: Marriott International Data Access Penalty
example_background: Marriott's Starwood reservation system breach in 2018 exposed data of up to 500 million guests, including names, emails, and payment info. Post-breach, guests submitted DSARs for their processed data copies. Marriott's responses were delayed due to breach remediation priorities, with some taking over three months. Electronic requests received ZIP files of mixed formats, not consistently machine-readable, and further copies incurred disputed fees.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020, including for poor DSAR handling. Marriott invested in automated DSAR tools post-fine.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Transfer Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the entity responsible for processing personal data of Facebook users in the European Union, engaged in the transfer of personal data from the EU to the United States. This transfer occurred through the use of the Privacy Shield framework, which had been invalidated by the Court of Justice of the European Union in the Schrems II judgment in July 2020. Following the invalidation, Meta continued to rely on standard contractual clauses for these transfers without implementing additional supplementary measures deemed necessary to ensure an adequate level of protection equivalent to that in the EU. The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an own-volition inquiry into these data transfer practices, examining the mechanisms in place for transferring data of approximately 500 million EU users to servers in the US. The inquiry revealed that the transfers lacked sufficient safeguards to protect against potential access by US intelligence authorities, as highlighted in the Schrems II ruling.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a decision in December 2022, imposing a fine of 1.2 billion euros on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited. The commission ordered the company to cease unlawful processing of EU user data transferred to the US within five months and to ensure compliance by deleting data already transferred unless necessary for legal reasons. Meta announced plans to appeal the decision.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Data Transfers
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform in the European Economic Area, processed personal data of users including children under 16 years old. The company transferred this data from the EU to China and the US for purposes such as content moderation, algorithmic training, and analytics. The Dutch Data Protection Authority initiated an investigation following complaints from NOYB about the lack of adequate safeguards for these international transfers, particularly concerning the data of minors. The probe focused on whether TikTok provided sufficient information to users about the safeguards in place for data sent outside the EU and whether those safeguards met the requirements for adequacy.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, the Dutch Data Protection Authority fined TikTok 345 million euros for violations related to children's privacy and international data transfers. The authority required TikTok to implement changes to its data processing practices and provide better transparency on transfer mechanisms.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into WhatsApp Transfers
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, part of the Meta group, transferred personal data of European users to the US for storage and processing. Following the Schrems II judgment, the Irish Data Protection Commission launched a cross-border investigation into the lawfulness of these transfers. The inquiry assessed whether WhatsApp's reliance on standard contractual clauses provided the necessary safeguards and whether users were adequately informed about the risks and protective measures for data sent to a third country with potentially differing data protection standards.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC proposed a fine and remedial orders. After input from other EU data protection authorities via the EDPB, WhatsApp faced ongoing compliance requirements and adjustments to its transfer practices.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Transfer Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition company, scraped billions of images from the internet, including from EU websites, to build a database accessible to law enforcement. The company transferred this personal data to the US without implementing appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses or binding corporate rules. Multiple EU data protection authorities investigated complaints about the processing and transfer of biometric data without consent or legal basis, highlighting the absence of information provided to data subjects regarding the transfers.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Dutch DPA banned Clearview AI operations and fined it 30.5 million CAD (equivalent). Other EU regulators issued similar bans and fines totaling over 30 million euros, ordering cessation of unlawful processing.

example_name: Oracle Cloud GDPR Data Transfer Issues
example_background: Oracle Corporation, through its cloud services division, provided infrastructure services to EU customers involving the transfer of personal data to data centers in the US and other third countries. The French data protection authority investigated Oracle's practices after complaints revealed that the company used standard contractual clauses but failed to inform customers adequately about the safeguards in place and the potential risks associated with US laws allowing government access to data.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the CNIL fined Oracle France 7 million euros and issued a formal notice to bring its processing into compliance within two months, including improvements to transparency on international transfers.

example_name: Microsoft Azure GDPR Transfer Compliance Model
example_background: Microsoft, operating Azure cloud services, transfers personal data from EU customers to the US and other regions. Following Schrems II, Microsoft developed the 'Microsoft Product and Services Data Protection Addendum (DPA)' which incorporates the EU Data Boundary, advanced encryption, and transparency reports on government requests. Customers are informed via detailed documentation about the safeguards including standard contractual clauses supplemented by technical measures like data residency options and pseudonymization to mitigate risks from third-country laws.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's framework has been recognized by regulators and auditors as providing adequate safeguards. No major enforcement actions have been taken against it for transfer violations, and it supports thousands of EU organizations in compliant transfers.

example_name: Google Cloud GDPR Transfer Safeguards
example_background: Google Cloud Platform enables EU entities to process and transfer personal data internationally. Google implemented the Google Cloud Data Processing Addendum, which details safeguards such as the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (post-2023 adequacy decision), standard contractual clauses with supplementary measures, and customer controls for data location. Documentation provided to data subjects and controllers includes information on encryption, access controls, and audit rights to ensure protection levels equivalent to the EU.
process_and_outcome: Google's practices have undergone independent audits and Schrems II assessments, resulting in compliance certifications. It has avoided significant fines related to transfers by proactively informing users and offering tools like Customer Data Export for verification.

example_name: Amazon Web Services (AWS) GDPR Transfer Practices
example_background: AWS provides cloud infrastructure to EU organizations, handling personal data transfers to regions outside the EEA. AWS offers the AWS Data Processing Addendum, which outlines safeguards including standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules for intra-group transfers, and features like AWS Data Processing Addendum transparency notices. These inform data subjects about the transfer mechanisms, encryption in transit and at rest, and compliance with adequacy decisions where applicable.
process_and_outcome: AWS has maintained compliance through regular third-party audits (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC reports) and has not faced major GDPR fines for transfer issues. It supports EU data sovereignty zones to minimize cross-border flows.

example_name: DoorDash DSAR Response Failure
example_background: DoorDash, a US-based food delivery platform operating in Europe, received a DSAR from an individual in 2021 requesting confirmation of personal data processing, including purposes of processing such as marketing and delivery services, categories of data like location and payment details, recipients including third-party vendors and analytics providers, expected storage durations based on retention policies, rights to rectification and objection, complaint procedures, data sources from app usage, and details on automated decision-making for personalized recommendations. DoorDash provided an initial response listing some data categories but omitted key details on recipients in third countries, specific storage criteria, and logic behind profiling algorithms used for order suggestions, and failed to deliver the full data export within the statutory period, citing technical difficulties.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted an inquiry following a complaint and issued a decision in December 2022, fining DoorDash €31.5 million primarily for other violations but including findings on inadequate DSAR fulfillment. The company was required to revise its data access procedures and report compliance measures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 12 (Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject)

example_name: Meta Platforms DSAR and Profiling Investigation
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, handling EU data for Facebook, received multiple DSARs from users seeking access to their personal data, including confirmation of processing for advertising purposes, categories such as behavioral data and interests inferred from activity, recipients including ad partners in third countries like the US, envisaged retention periods based on user lifecycle criteria, existence of rights to object and lodge complaints, sources of data from third-party integrations, and detailed information on automated decision-making and profiling logic involving machine learning models for ad targeting, along with significance and consequences like personalized ad delivery. The company provided data downloads via its 'Download Your Information' tool but responses lacked sufficient explanation of profiling logic, third-country transfers under standard contractual clauses, and precise storage determination criteria.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC initiated cross-border investigations, culminating in a 2023 decision fining Meta €1.2 billion for other issues but highlighting DSAR deficiencies under Article 15. Additional scrutiny led to orders for enhanced transparency in automated processing disclosures. Meta appealed aspects but complied with remedial actions.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data DSAR Fine
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited faced complaints regarding DSARs from parents of child users under 13 who requested confirmation of whether their children's personal data was processed despite age restrictions, purposes like content recommendation, categories including video interactions and device info, recipients such as content moderators in Asia, storage periods tied to account activity criteria, rights information, complaint channels, data sources from uploads, and profiling details for algorithmic feeds. TikTok's responses to these requests were delayed beyond one month, incomplete in specifying third-country recipients, and vague on automated decision-making logic without explaining model inputs or consequences for content visibility.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 after an investigation into child data processing, with specific findings on non-compliance with DSAR obligations under Article 15. The company was directed to overhaul its data rights handling for minors.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions applicable to child's consent)

example_name: Marriott International Data Breach DSAR Issues
example_background: Marriott International disclosed a data breach in 2018 affecting up to 500 million guest records from its Starwood reservation system. Affected individuals submitted DSARs requesting confirmation of data processing, purposes for loyalty programs and bookings, categories like passport numbers and travel history, recipients including service providers in third countries, storage periods based on legal retention criteria, rights to erasure and objection, complaint options, sources from acquired systems, and any automated profiling for personalized offers. Marriott's responses were inconsistent, with some requests taking over three months, partial data provision, and insufficient details on international transfers or profiling logic used in marketing automation.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020, partly for poor handling of post-breach DSARs. The company enhanced its privacy portal for faster access requests and settled related class actions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Google Analytics Consent and DSAR Compliance
example_background: Google implemented its Consent Mode for Analytics in 2021, allowing EU users to exercise data protection rights. When users submit DSARs via privacy.google.com, they receive confirmation of processing for analytics and advertising, purposes outlined in detail, categories like browsing behavior and IP addresses, recipients limited to intra-group entities with SCCs for US transfers, storage periods up to 14 months with criteria explained, full list of rights including objection and complaints to DPAs, sources from web interactions, and comprehensive explanations of automated processing logic in ad personalization models, including input signals, significance for user experience, and potential outcomes like tailored content suggestions.
process_and_outcome: Following multiple DPA audits and investigations into consent mechanisms, Google demonstrated adherence in 2023 EDPB proceedings by providing verifiable DSAR fulfillment logs and transparency tools, avoiding fines and serving as a referenced practice.

example_name: Vodafone DSAR Handling Improvement
example_background: Vodafone, a multinational telecom, faced initial complaints in 2019 about DSAR responses lacking details on data sharing with third parties for marketing and network management. In response, they developed an automated DSAR portal providing users with confirmation, purposes like billing and customer service, categories including call records and location data, public lists of recipient categories with transfer tools, storage criteria per national laws, rights summaries with links to objection forms, complaint guidance to local DPAs, sourcing info, and explanations of fraud detection algorithms including decision thresholds and user impacts.
process_and_outcome: After ICO scrutiny in the UK, Vodafone achieved compliance certification in 2021, with subsequent DPA reviews praising the system's efficiency and transparency, reducing complaint volumes by 70%.

example_name: Amazon EU DSAR Profiling Disclosures
example_background: Amazon's EU privacy notice and DSAR process via amazon.co.uk/privacy allows customers to request access to data used in recommendation engines, providing confirmation of processing, purposes for personalization and logistics, categories like purchase history and search queries, recipients including AWS services and vendors with DPA annexes, retention based on business need criteria, rights portfolio, complaint paths to CNIL or ICO, data origins, and detailed profiling info including collaborative filtering logic, feature weights, significance for product suggestions, and consequences like promotional targeting.
process_and_outcome: Amazon navigated 2022 French CNIL investigations successfully by demonstrating robust DSAR fulfillment, contributing to no fine imposition for access rights and earning mentions in EDPB best practices.

example_name: Facebook (Meta) Irish DPC GDPR Fine on Data Accuracy
example_background: In 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined Facebook's practices in verifying the accuracy of users' dates of birth and ages. The investigation found that Facebook did not implement sufficient technical and organizational measures to ensure age data was accurate, leading to minors being exposed to age-inappropriate content and advertising. Users attempting to rectify their age information faced challenges due to systemic inaccuracies in the platform's data processing for ad targeting and content delivery.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €91 million fine in January 2023. Facebook appealed aspects of the decision, but the company was required to adjust its data verification processes and enhance mechanisms for data subjects to update their information.

example_name: TikTok Dutch DPA GDPR Fine
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority investigated TikTok's handling of children's personal data, including age verification and accuracy of age-related information. The platform processed data of users under 16 without proper consent mechanisms, and there were issues with the accuracy of self-reported ages, which affected content recommendations and advertising. Data subjects, particularly parents, sought to rectify incomplete or inaccurate age data but encountered barriers in the app's processes.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the Dutch DPA fined TikTok €17 million for violations related to data accuracy and children's data processing. TikTok implemented changes to age gating and verification systems in response.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 - Conditions for Children's Consent

example_name: Vodafone Portugal GDPR Rectification Fine
example_background: Vodafone Portugal was investigated for failing to properly handle data subject requests to rectify inaccurate personal data. Customers reported issues where their contact details, billing information, and service preferences remained outdated despite multiple requests for corrections. The company's internal processes delayed or ignored these requests, leading to continued processing of inaccurate data for marketing and service provision purposes.
process_and_outcome: Portugal's National Data Protection Commission (CNPD) fined Vodafone €200,000 in 2021. The company was ordered to improve its data rectification procedures and report compliance measures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 12 - Transparent Information and Communication

example_name: H&M CNIL GDPR Fine on Employee Data
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center collected and stored extensive personal data on employees, including sensitive details about their private lives, health, and behaviors gathered through 'welcome meetings.' This data was used to predict employee performance but included inaccuracies that employees could not easily rectify. Affected employees raised concerns about the completeness and accuracy of this data, which was processed without proper mechanisms for correction.
process_and_outcome: France's CNIL fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2023 after a cross-border investigation initiated by the Bavarian DPA. H&M discontinued the practices and enhanced data governance.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 - Processing of Special Categories

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Injunction and Fine
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the internet to build a facial recognition database without consent. Individuals and privacy groups sought to exercise rights to rectify or delete inaccurate biometric data linked to their images, but the company lacked processes to handle such requests effectively, leading to persistent inaccuracies in identity associations.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA issued a 2022 order prohibiting processing and a €30.5 million fine, upheld by the CJEU in 2024. Multiple EU regulators banned the service and imposed penalties.

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp GDPR Fine by Ireland DPC
example_background: WhatsApp was investigated for transparency issues in its privacy policy updates, which impacted users' ability to understand and rectify personal data used for targeted advertising. Users faced difficulties in completing or correcting profile data shared across Meta services due to opaque processing purposes.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2022 after a multi-year probe. WhatsApp revised its policies and improved data subject rights portals.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 - Information to be Provided

example_name: Apple GDPR Compliance with Rectification Requests
example_background: Apple has established a dedicated GDPR portal allowing EU users to access, rectify, or complete their personal data across services like iCloud, App Store, and Apple ID. The process enables users to update inaccurate details such as addresses, phone numbers, or payment information promptly, with verification steps to ensure data accuracy for processing purposes like billing and support.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions recorded; Apple regularly publishes transparency reports showing high volumes of successful rectification requests processed without undue delay, demonstrating effective implementation of data subject rights.

example_name: Deloitte Croatia GDPR Enforcement
example_background: Deloitte d.o.o. for advising, a consulting firm in Croatia, processed personal data of 127 job applicants in 2017. When applicants requested copies of their personal data under GDPR Article 15, the company provided copies of CVs and correspondence but withheld internal assessment notes and evaluations. The decision to withhold was based on protecting the rights of other applicants and internal evaluators whose opinions and comparisons were embedded in the documents. This occurred during the transitional period after GDPR's enforcement began on May 25, 2018.
process_and_outcome: The Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) investigated following complaints and imposed a €200 fine in 2020, determining that the withheld data should have been provided in a manner that redacted third-party information. Deloitte was required to revise its data subject rights procedures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 15(4) (Manifestly unfounded or excessive requests)

example_name: Meta Platforms EDPB Decision on Access Rights
example_background: Multiple national data protection authorities coordinated an investigation into Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's handling of GDPR Article 15 requests. Users requesting copies received metadata and basic profile information but not full social graphs, friend lists, or algorithmic outputs due to the volume of data (sometimes terabytes) and the inclusion of data from non-requesting users in relational datasets like interactions and shared posts.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued binding decisions requiring Meta to provide more detailed copies, including timelines of data processing, while allowing pseudonymization or redaction of third-party data. Meta adjusted its processes accordingly.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Recital 63 (Rights and freedoms of others)

example_name: Norwegian DPPA WhatsApp Fine
example_background: WhatsApp shared user data with Meta companies for advertising without clear consent, prompting Article 15 requests from users seeking copies of shared data and behavioral profiles. Responses included aggregated insights but omitted detailed cross-platform data to avoid disclosing information about contacts and group members who had not consented to sharing.
process_and_outcome: Norway's Datatilsynet fined WhatsApp (Ireland) €10,000 initially in 2021, later increased via EDPB procedures. WhatsApp revised its consent mechanisms and data portability tools.

example_name: Vodafone Portugal Compliance Practice
example_background: Vodafone Portugal implemented a structured data subject rights portal allowing customers to request and receive copies of their personal data, such as call records, billing history, and location data summaries. The system automatically redacts third-party numbers, contact details, and data from shared accounts to ensure disclosures do not impact others' rights. This was developed post-GDPR to handle high volumes of telecom data requests efficiently.
process_and_outcome: Following audits by Portugal's CNPD, Vodafone's approach was recognized as aligned with GDPR requirements in 2022, with no fines issued. The company received positive feedback for its automated redaction tools and timely responses averaging under 30 days.

example_name: CNIL Enforcement Against Google (Right to Erasure)
example_background: Google LLC, as a data controller, received multiple requests from data subjects seeking erasure of personal data from search results under the right to be forgotten provisions. The company processed some requests but applied a global approach to delisting, making erasures visible worldwide rather than limited to EU domains as initially interpreted. This stemmed from data subjects invoking erasure grounds such as data no longer being necessary for original processing purposes or for compliance with privacy rights post-Costaja decision. CNIL investigated after data subjects complained about incomplete implementation, noting that Google's policy did not sufficiently restrict visibility outside the EU, potentially affecting the effectiveness of the erasure right for European users. The case highlighted tensions between global search engine operations and localized data protection requirements.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's restricted committee imposed a 100,000 euro fine on Google in 2022 for non-compliance with erasure obligations. The decision affirmed that delisting must consider EU users' access from non-EU domains via tools like VPNs. Google was ordered to adjust its processes within two months and demonstrate compliance.

example_name: ICO Fine on TikTok for Children's Data Erasure Failures
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of millions of UK users under 13 without adequate age verification, retaining videos and profiles after users requested deletion or after accounts became inactive. Data subjects, primarily children, withdrew consent or had data that was no longer necessary, but the platform failed to erase it promptly, sharing it with third parties. This occurred amid broader concerns over default public profiles and biometric data processing from videos. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) launched an investigation following complaints and its own inquiries into child data practices.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the ICO fined TikTok £12.7 million for multiple GDPR breaches, including failures to erase children's data without undue delay. TikTok committed to remediation steps, including enhanced age assurance and data deletion processes.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Action Against WhatsApp on Data Retention
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited retained user metadata and personal data beyond what was necessary for service provision, even after users deleted accounts or requested erasure. Users invoked rights citing withdrawal of consent and data no longer needed for original purposes. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) examined complaints where WhatsApp's policies led to prolonged storage, including IP addresses and device info, without clear erasure timelines, affecting millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, AEPD imposed a 6 million euro fine on WhatsApp for GDPR violations, specifically highlighting inadequate erasure processes. WhatsApp appealed but implemented policy updates.

example_name: Meta Platforms Fine by Irish DPC for Non-Erasure
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, operator of Facebook, faced issues with handling erasure requests for personal data linked to deleted profiles. Users who closed accounts found data remnants used for advertising profiles or retained indefinitely. Grounds included data no longer necessary post-account deletion and withdrawal of consent. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated as part of broader GDPR audits following user complaints.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion primarily for data transfers but included findings on erasure failures, ordering compliance improvements. Meta challenged aspects in court.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Erasure Violations
example_background: Clearview AI Inc. scraped billions of facial images from the web without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement. Data subjects, upon discovering their biometric data, requested erasure citing unlawful processing and lack of necessity. The company refused most requests, claiming US jurisdiction and no EU establishment initially. Multiple EU DPAs coordinated investigations after complaints.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Dutch, French, Greek, and Italian DPAs banned processing and fined Clearview cumulatively over €30 million. Orders enforced immediate data erasure for EU subjects.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Fine Involving Data Erasure Elements
example_background: British Airways faced a data breach exposing 400,000 customers' payment data. Post-breach, the company retained compromised data longer than necessary without notifying or erasing it promptly for affected subjects who requested deletion. ICO probed handling of erasure rights amid the incident.
process_and_outcome: ICO issued a £20 million fine in 2020 (reduced from £183m on appeal) for security and post-breach handling failures, including delayed erasure. BA enhanced data governance.

example_name: Apple's App Tracking Transparency and Erasure Compliance
example_background: Apple Inc. introduced App Tracking Transparency (ATT) in iOS 14.5, requiring apps to obtain explicit consent before tracking users across apps and websites. Upon withdrawal of consent, Apple enforces erasure of tracking identifiers like IDFA. This supports data subjects' rights by ensuring data no longer necessary for tracking is deleted, integrated into device-level privacy controls.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; praised by regulators and privacy advocates for proactive implementation exceeding GDPR minimums. Reduced industry tracking by 80% per studies.

example_name: ProtonMail's GDPR Compliance Framework
example_background: ProtonMail, a privacy-focused email service, processes personal data under strict erasure protocols. Users can request deletion of emails and accounts, triggering immediate erasure unless legally required to retain. Consent withdrawal leads to data purge, aligning with grounds like no longer necessary or consent-based processing.
process_and_outcome: Certified GDPR compliant with no major fines; audited successfully by Swiss and EU authorities. Serves as a model for end-to-end encrypted services.

example_name: CNIL Fine on Amazon France for Advertising Data Retention
example_background: Amazon Services Europe SARL retained vast amounts of personal data from Prime users for targeted advertising beyond stated purposes. Users requested erasure after withdrawing consent, but data persisted in logs and profiles, deemed no longer necessary.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon €32 million in 2020 for opaque consent and erasure failures. Amazon updated practices and transparency notices.

example_name: CNIL v Google LLC (French Right to be Forgotten Enforcement)
example_background: Following the 2014 Google Spain ruling, the French data protection authority (CNIL) received numerous complaints about Google's handling of delisting requests under the right to be forgotten. CNIL found that Google was only applying delistings to European Union country-specific domains (e.g., google.fr, google.de) but not to the global google.com domain, allowing access to the same personal data via non-EU versions from within the EU using VPNs or direct access. In 2015, CNIL issued a formal notice to Google requiring full implementation, including global delisting. Google argued that global delisting would undermine freedom of expression outside the EU and create enforcement challenges. CNIL conducted audits and assessed compliance, determining that partial delisting was insufficient to protect EU data subjects' rights. The case highlighted tensions between territorial scope of GDPR and global internet operations.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €100,000 fine on Google in 2019 for non-compliance. Google challenged the decision in French courts, but the Conseil d'État upheld CNIL's order in 2020, mandating global delisting where feasible. Google implemented 'geoblocking' measures to comply within the EU.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: TikTok €345 Million GDPR Fine (Ireland DPC)
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated TikTok's data processing practices for users under 16, focusing on features like push notifications and age verification. A key aspect involved children requesting deletion of their accounts and associated personal data, including videos, comments, and behavioral profiles. TikTok was found to retain data beyond necessity after deletion requests, using it for algorithmic training and targeted advertising. The investigation stemmed from a 2020 complaint by a child safety group, revealing systemic issues in handling erasure requests for minors' data collected via the platform's 'information society services.' TikTok processed billions of data points daily, complicating complete erasure, especially for publicly shared content replicated across the platform.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC fined TikTok €345 million for GDPR violations, including failures in right to erasure. TikTok agreed to enhanced deletion processes and audit mechanisms as remedial actions.
involved_parties: Other EU DPAs (co-lead: Netherlands and New Zealand DPA)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland €1.2 Billion Fine (Right to Erasure and Transfers)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland, as the lead EU entity for Facebook, faced scrutiny over international data transfers to the US and handling of user deletion requests. Users who deleted accounts found residual data retained in US servers for analytics and ad targeting, without effective erasure. The investigation by the Irish DPC, coordinated with other EU regulators, examined whether Meta ensured equivalent protection post-Schrems II invalidation of Privacy Shield, including obligations to propagate erasure requests across borders as per GDPR Article 17(2).
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the DPC imposed a record €1.2 billion fine on Meta and ordered cessation of unlawful transfers with a five-month suspension period. Meta is appealing.

example_name: British Airways €20 Million Fine (Deletion Request Handling)
example_background: A 2018 cyber-attack exposed personal data of 400,000 customers. Affected individuals submitted deletion requests post-incident, but British Airways retained data for internal investigations longer than necessary. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated broader GDPR compliance, including failures to erase data no longer needed for the original booking purposes or after consent withdrawal for marketing.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the ICO fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £24 million), citing issues with erasure processes among other violations. The airline implemented improved data lifecycle management.

example_name: OVHcloud €10.5 Million Fine (French Hosting Provider)
example_background: French regulator CNIL investigated OVHcloud, a major cloud hosting provider, after complaints about inability to fully delete customer data from backups and replicated systems. Users and clients requested erasure under GDPR grounds such as consent withdrawal, but technical retention persisted for service continuity, without adequate anonymization or justification under exemptions.
process_and_outcome: In May 2024, CNIL fined OVHcloud €10.5 million for multiple violations, including right to erasure failures, and ordered process improvements.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: H&M €35.25 Million Fine (Germany Data Processing and Erasure)
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg data center collected excessive employee data via 'welcome meetings' where staff shared personal details, which were stored and accessed post-employment without consent or necessity. Former employees requested erasure, but data remained accessible to managers, violating storage limitation and erasure rights.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, Hamburg DPA fined H&M Justice CV €35.25 million. H&M enhanced data minimization and deletion procedures.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Google Delisting Compliance Post-Costeja (Best Practice)
example_background: After the 2014 CJEU ruling, Google established a dedicated online form for EU users to submit right to be forgotten requests. By processing over 1.6 million requests as of 2024, Google implemented a multi-step review balancing privacy against public interest, using human reviewers and machine learning. Delistings are applied to all EU domains and geoblocked globally where public interest does not override, demonstrating structured adherence to erasure obligations for search results.
process_and_outcome: Google reports 45% average delisting rate, with transparency reports detailing processes. No major fines on this aspect since enhancements.

example_name: Apple GDPR Compliance Framework (Privacy Features)
example_background: Apple integrated GDPR-compliant features like 'Delete Account' in iCloud and App Store, enabling users to request erasure of personal data across services. Upon request, Apple purges data from backups, devices, and third-party processors within 30 days, with exemptions logged for legal holds. This includes propagation to partners as per Article 17(2), supported by privacy-preserving technologies like differential privacy.
process_and_outcome: Apple's 2023 Privacy Report highlights millions of deletion requests processed without regulatory fines, audited annually.

example_name: H&M Data Processing Fine 2020
example_background: H&M operated a service center in Nuremberg, Germany, where it collected and processed personal data of approximately 1,000 employees gathered from colleagues, ex-employees, and external sources. This indirectly obtained data included sensitive details on private lives, health, and finances. Employees were not provided with information on the controller's details, DPO contacts, processing purposes, legal basis, data categories, recipients, or international transfer safeguards as required when data is not directly from the data subject.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg's data protection authority investigated after a whistleblower complaint. In October 2020, a fine of €35.25 million was issued. H&M discontinued the practice and improved compliance measures.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Enforcement 2021
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from websites and social media without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement and others. The personal data was obtained indirectly from public sources. The company did not provide data subjects with controller identity, DPO contacts, processing purposes, legal basis (relied on legitimate interest), data categories (biometric), recipients (clients worldwide), or details on transfers to third countries like the US, including absence of adequacy decisions or safeguard references.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU authorities, led by the Dutch DPA, investigated. In 2021, a €30.5 million provisional fine was issued by the Dutch AP, with bans on processing EU data. Further fines followed from other regulators; Clearview appealed but largely ceased EU operations.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Article 14 Fine 2023
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited used data from Facebook and Instagram users for behavioral advertising. When data was combined from third-party sources or not directly obtained, Meta failed to provide users with information on the controller's identity and contacts, DPO details, purposes of processing, legal basis (contractual necessity and legitimate interests), categories of data (browsing behavior, interests), categories of recipients (advertisers), and transfers to the US including references to safeguards like Standard Contractual Clauses.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated following a cross-border procedure. In 2023, a €1.2 billion fine was imposed, the largest under GDPR, with orders to suspend data transfers to the US and implement safeguards. Meta appealed to the courts.

example_name: Whistleblower Platform Compliance Case Study
example_background: A European whistleblower platform collects reports from anonymous sources containing third-party personal data not directly from data subjects. The platform provides a detailed privacy notice to reporters upon submission, including operator identity and contacts, DPO email, purposes (investigation), legal basis (legitimate interest/public task), data categories (names, allegations), recipients (authorities), and EU-US transfers with SCCs and copy links, fulfilling Article 14 within one month.
process_and_outcome: During a 2022 DPA audit, the platform demonstrated full adherence, receiving no enforcement actions and recognition as a best practice example in transparency for indirectly obtained data.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Meta Platforms Ireland (Public Health Exemption)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland processed special category data for behavioral advertising purposes. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined whether processing complied with GDPR principles, particularly in light of exemptions for public interest in public health under Article 9(2)(h). The case involved large-scale processing of sensitive data without explicit basis, affecting EU users.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision mandating a fine of €1.2 billion against Meta for unlawful processing. Additional orders required suspension of data transfers to the US and implementation of compliant mechanisms.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Amazon France (Legal Claims Exemption)
example_background: Amazon France Logistique processed employee and third-party data via algorithms for performance monitoring and warehouse management. The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated data access practices and whether processing was necessary for the establishment, exercise, or defense of legal claims under GDPR exemptions. Concerns centered on the scope and proportionality of data retention and access.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, CNIL imposed a €32 million fine for breaches related to insufficient data minimization and security. Amazon was ordered to adjust its data processing practices.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: UK NHS COVID-19 Contact Tracing App (Public Health Exemption)
example_background: The UK's National Health Service (NHS) developed and deployed a contact tracing app during the COVID-19 pandemic to track potential exposures. The app processed location and proximity data centrally to alert users of risks. This was justified under public interest exemptions for public health under GDPR Article 9(2)(i), with data processing designed to balance privacy and health protection.
process_and_outcome: The ICO reviewed the app and confirmed compliance with exemptions in 2020. No enforcement actions were taken, and the app was rolled out successfully before transitioning to decentralized models.
involved_parties: UK Department of Health and Social Care

example_name: Italian Garante Decision on Janssen Cilag (Public Health Research)
example_background: Janssen Cilag, a pharmaceutical company, processed health data from Italian healthcare professionals for market research surveys. The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) assessed whether the processing qualified for exemptions under public interest in public health and scientific research per GDPR Article 9(2)(i) and Article 89.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Garante found the processing unlawful due to lack of proper safeguards and proportionality, issuing a €500,000 fine. The company was required to revise consent and anonymization processes.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: Danish DPA Clearance for Research Biobank (Article 89 Exemption)
example_background: The Danish Data Protection Agency reviewed the Inter99 biobank's processing of genetic and health data for scientific research on chronic diseases. The project involved longitudinal data collection from thousands of participants, relying on GDPR exemptions for scientific research purposes under Article 89(1), with technical and organizational measures to protect rights.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the DPA granted approval for continued processing, confirming the exemption applied appropriately with implemented pseudonymization and access controls. No further actions required.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Enforcement by Dutch AP
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement. The Dutch Data Protection Authority investigated processing of biometric data, evaluating claims of exemption for legal claims defense under Article 9(2)(f). The company argued public data availability justified unrestricted use.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Dutch AP imposed a €30.5 million fine, upheld despite appeals. Multiple EU authorities issued similar fines totaling over €40 million, with orders to cease unlawful processing.

example_name: Meta Schrems II Litigation (Freedom of Expression Claim)
example_background: In the Schrems II case, privacy activist Max Schrems challenged Meta's transatlantic data transfers to the US, where data was subject to surveillance laws. Meta argued processing was necessary for freedom of expression and information under GDPR exemptions, particularly for platform operations.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU invalidated the Privacy Shield in 2020 and required adequacy in transfer mechanisms. Subsequent EDPB decisions led to bans on Meta's transfers until Standard Contractual Clauses with supplements were implemented.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Right to be Forgotten Fine
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority investigated Google's handling of right to be forgotten requests under GDPR Article 17. Google had made de-listing requests public on its website, publishing details such as the names of individuals, search terms, and reasons for requests. This practice occurred after the European Court of Justice's 2014 ruling in Google Spain SL v. AEPD and Mario Costeja González, which established the right to de-listing. Google's transparency report included searchable databases of requests received from May 2014 to December 2018, covering thousands of cases where personal data had been made public via search results. The CNIL found that this public disclosure undermined the erasure obligation, as it informed third parties of the data subject's identity and the specific links targeted for removal, potentially perpetuating the availability of the information despite de-listing from EU search results. The case highlighted tensions between transparency for public interest and data subject rights when controllers process publicly available personal data.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a 15 million euro fine on Google Ireland Limited on December 10, 2020. The decision required Google to cease publishing such details within two months and delete existing data. Google appealed to the Council d'État, which referred questions to the CJEU in 2022 regarding balancing transparency and erasure rights. The fine stood as one of the early enforcement actions on Article 17(2) propagation obligations.

example_name: Meta Platforms €91 Million Fine for Public Photo Facial Recognition Data
example_background: Meta Platforms operated a facial recognition system on public photos uploaded to Facebook and Instagram from 2011 to 2021. When users requested erasure of their images or biometric data under GDPR, Meta deleted the data from its primary databases but did not take steps to notify or require third-party apps, websites, or scrapers that had accessed and replicated the public photos to remove copies or links. Public photos had been indexed by search engines and archived by third parties, with facial recognition templates derived from them persisting externally. The Irish DPC found Meta processed biometric data without adequate safeguards for propagation of erasure requests, affecting millions of EU users whose public profile pictures remained discoverable despite individual deletion requests.
process_and_outcome: In November 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd €91 million. Additional orders required Meta to cease unlawful processing and enhance erasure mechanisms. Meta indicated it would appeal the decision.

example_name: British Airways €22 Million GDPR Breach Fine
example_background: In 2018, hackers accessed personal data of 400,000 customers via a Magecart attack on British Airways' website, including public-facing booking data. Affected individuals requested erasure of compromised payment details and travel records that had been exposed publicly during the breach. British Airways notified data subjects but did not implement measures to inform search engines, data brokers, or other controllers who had indexed or copied the leaked data from dark web forums or public breach repositories. The exposed data included names, addresses, and card details that remained replicated across multiple platforms post-breach, despite erasure requests.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO issued a €22 million fine in October 2020 (reduced from £183 million proposed). British Airways remediated systems and improved breach response protocols, including better propagation for erasure in public data scenarios.

example_name: Marriott €18.4 Million Fine for Starwood Data Leak
example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a data breach affecting 339 million guest records from 2014-2018. Publicly exposed data included passport numbers and travel details requested for erasure by affected guests. Marriott informed individuals post-acquisition but took no technical steps to notify or compel third-party processors, travel aggregators, or dark web marketplaces holding replicated copies to erase the data. Public breach dumps persisted online, with copies circulating despite individual erasure requests.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined Marriott International €18.4 million in November 2020. Marriott enhanced data governance and implemented monitoring for public data replicas.

example_name: Twitter €450,000 Fine by Dutch DPA
example_background: Twitter (now X) suspended accounts of Dutch users for rule violations, making public tweets and profiles unavailable in the EU but leaving copies on third-party archives, scrapers, and user reposts. Suspended users requested erasure of their public data, but Twitter did not notify external controllers processing cached or mirrored content to remove links or replicas. The practice affected political and public figures whose data remained accessible via Google cache or Wayback Machine despite account suspension.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Twitter €450,000 in 2019 for failing to honor erasure requests fully. Twitter adjusted processes for better third-party notifications.

example_name: Vodafone €12.75 Million Italian GDPR Fine
example_background: Vodafone processed public marketing data for over 18,000 customers without consent, exposing phone numbers and addresses. Upon erasure requests, Vodafone removed data from its systems but did not inform data brokers or directories that had copied the public records to delist entries. This left personal data discoverable in public phone books and sales leads databases.
process_and_outcome: Italian DPA fined Vodafone €12.75 million in 2021. Remediation included contracts mandating erasure propagation with processors.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into WhatsApp Restriction Requests
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, operating WhatsApp, faced complaints from data subjects who requested restriction of processing of their personal data following objections to processing based on legitimate interests. The data subjects invoked their rights under Article 18 GDPR after contesting the lawfulness of data processing for behavioral advertising purposes. WhatsApp continued certain processing activities, including data transfers to the US, without adequately restricting access or use pending verification of overriding legitimate grounds. This occurred in the context of broader GDPR enforcement following the Schrems II judgment, where multiple requesters across EU member states sought to limit processing of metadata and user data collected via the service.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted an own-volition inquiry in 2021, culminating in a draft decision that proposed no finding of infringement on restriction rights. However, other EU supervisory authorities objected via the EDPB dispute resolution procedure. In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision instructing the DPC to impose corrective measures, including a formal reprimand and orders to bring processing into compliance within three months. Meta appealed aspects of related decisions.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 18 (Right to restriction of processing)

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Google for Failing to Honor Data Subject Rights Including Restriction
example_background: Google LLC processed personal data of French users for online advertising without fully transparent legal bases and ignored requests from data subjects exercising multiple rights, including restriction of processing under Article 18. Users contested the accuracy of data used for profiling and requested restriction while verification occurred, but Google continued behavioral analysis and targeted ads. Additional issues involved lack of response to objection rights under Article 21, where processing persisted pending assessment of legitimate interests. This stemmed from complaints filed by None of Your Business (NOYB) in 2018 across multiple EU countries.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL investigated in 2019 and issued a €50 million fine in January 2020 for violations including failure to comply with rights to access, rectification, restriction, and objection. Google was ordered to cease unlawful processing within two months and conform to GDPR principles. Google contested the fine in French courts, leading to ongoing appeals.

example_name: TikTok €345 Million Fine for Children's Data Processing Violations Including Rights Restrictions
example_background: TikTok Ireland Unlimited Company processed personal data of children under 16 without proper age verification and parental consent, leading to complaints where parents and children requested restriction of processing after objecting to profiling and data retention. The platform continued processing user-generated content and behavioral data without halting operations as required under Article 18(2) exceptions, particularly after data subjects contested accuracy and requested restriction pending verification. Issues arose from default public account settings and algorithmic recommendations using restricted data.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC concluded its inquiry in September 2023 with a €345 million fine for GDPR breaches, including infringement of children's data rights and failure to uphold restriction requests. TikTok was mandated to implement compliance measures, such as enhanced age assurance tools, and faced criticism for systemic failures.

example_name: Meta €1.2 Billion Fine for Unlawful Data Transfers and Rights Handling
example_background: Following Schrems II, data subjects requested restriction of processing for Meta's (Facebook) EU-US data transfers, objecting under Article 21 and seeking Article 18 restrictions due to unlawful processing lacking adequate safeguards. Meta continued transferring and processing data without restriction, using Standard Contractual Clauses invalidated for not addressing US surveillance risks. Users specifically invoked restriction for data no longer needed by Meta but retained for potential legal claims.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC imposed a record €1.2 billion fine in May 2023 after a 2020 inquiry, ordering Meta to suspend future transfers and delete transferred data within five months. Meta appealed to Irish courts, arguing procedural flaws.

example_name: British Airways £20 Million GDPR Fine Involving Data Subject Rights Failures
example_background: After a 2018 Magecart data breach exposing 400,000 customers' payment details, British Airways customers requested restriction of processing for compromised data, contesting accuracy and objecting to further use pending verification. The company failed to promptly restrict access to affected personal data, continuing internal processing for analytics without data subject consent or legal exceptions, and did not inform subjects before lifting any temporary restrictions.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO investigated and issued a £20 million fine in October 2020 (reduced from £183 million), citing failures in rights handling under Articles 15-22. British Airways implemented remediation including enhanced security and rights portals.

example_name: Marriott £18.4 Million Fine for Starwood Breach and Rights Non-Compliance
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a breach affecting 339 million records. Affected guests requested restriction of processing for breached passport and payment data, invoking Article 18 after objecting to retention no longer needed. Marriott delayed notifications and continued processing without restrictions, failing to verify accuracy claims or halt use pending legal claims by data subjects.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in October 2020 (reduced from £99 million), requiring audit and compliance improvements. Marriott enhanced global data protection measures.

example_name: Clearview AI €30.5 Million Dutch Fine for Rights Violations
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the web without consent, building a biometric database. Dutch data subjects, upon discovery, requested restriction of processing after objecting to the unlawful scraping and profiling, contesting accuracy and seeking halt pending verification. Clearview continued providing database access to clients without restricting queried data.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Clearview €30.5 million in September 2022 for multiple infringements, ordering cessation of offering services in the EU. Additional fines issued by other DPAs.

example_name: H&M €35.3 Million German Fine for Employee Data Mishandling
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg data center stored ex-employee personal data, including sensitive gossip from offboarding interviews, without erasure. Former employees requested restriction after objecting to processing no longer needed, but H&M continued internal sharing for team-building without consent or public interest justification, failing to inform before lifting restrictions.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined €35.3 million in October 2020 after a 2019 complaint, mandating data deletion and process changes. H&M discontinued the practice globally.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 05/2022 on Article 18 Best Practices
example_background: The European Data Protection Board issued non-binding guidelines in May 2022 providing examples of controllers properly implementing the right to restriction.
