example_name: Danish DPA Fine Against Bilka 2021
example_background: Bilka, a Danish retail chain, experienced a cyber incident in 2018 where customer data including names, addresses, phone numbers, and purchase histories of about 4.8 million individuals was accessed via a compromised employee email account. The breach occurred due to phishing, and Bilka became aware several months later during routine checks.
process_and_outcome: The Danish Data Protection Agency (Datatilsynet) fined Bilka DKK 8.5 million (approx. €1.14 million) in 2021 for delayed notification (took 4 months instead of 72 hours) and insufficient security measures.

example_name: Deloitte GDPR Breach Notification
example_background: In January 2017, prior to full GDPR enforcement but leading into compliance practices, Deloitte's UK systems were accessed by unauthorized parties, exposing email addresses and other personal data of around 100,000 individuals. Upon detection in early 2017, Deloitte conducted an internal investigation and notified affected individuals promptly. When GDPR became applicable, Deloitte demonstrated adherence by notifying the ICO within hours of confirming the breach details in subsequent reporting contexts, treating it as a benchmark for timely action.
process_and_outcome: No fines were issued; the ICO acknowledged the notification as timely and proportionate. Deloitte's response was cited in compliance reports as an example of effective breach handling, with no enforcement action taken.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)
GDPR Article 34 (Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject)

example_name: Yahoo GDPR Data Breach Reporting
example_background: Although the major Yahoo breaches occurred pre-GDPR in 2013-2014 affecting billions of accounts, residual investigations continued into 2018-2019. Yahoo (under Verizon) notified the ICO in 2019 regarding legacy issues with personal data exposure including names, emails, and hashed passwords. Notification was made within 72 hours of reassessing risks under GDPR obligations for historical data controllers, providing detailed remediation steps taken years prior.
process_and_outcome: The ICO closed the case without penalty, noting the proactive notification and low residual risk. It was referenced in ICO guidance as appropriate handling of legacy breaches.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Fallout
example_background: In 2018, Facebook disclosed that data from up to 87 million users was improperly shared with Cambridge Analytica via a third-party app in 2014-2015. The ICO investigation revealed Facebook became aware in 2015 but did not notify regulators promptly. Under GDPR, which applied from May 2018, Facebook notified the ICO on March 23, 2018, prior to full awareness but with updates, exceeding the 72-hour limit for full details due to ongoing assessments.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2019 for failures in data protection practices and delayed comprehensive notification. Facebook appealed unsuccessfully.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)
GDPR Article 25 (Data protection by design and by default)

example_name: Ticketmaster GDPR Breach
example_background: In May 2018, shortly after GDPR enforcement began, Ticketmaster detected unauthorized access to its systems, compromising personal data of around 56,000 customers including names, addresses, emails, and partial payment details. The breach involved a Magecart attack on the checkout page. Ticketmaster notified the ICO within 72 hours of confirmation on June 28, 2018, and communicated with affected customers promptly.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated and closed the case in 2020 without a fine, recognizing the notification was made within the required timeframe and security measures were reasonable given the external attack vector.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Equifax GDPR-Analogous Breach (UK Exposure)
example_background: Equifax suffered a massive breach in 2017 affecting 147 million individuals globally, including UK residents' personal data like names, addresses, and credit card details via an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability. Equifax notified the ICO on July 31, 2017, shortly after discovery, but under GDPR transposition via UK Data Protection Act, full reporting was scrutinized for timeliness as investigations extended into 2018.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Equifax £500,000 in December 2018 (maximum under pre-GDPR law), citing inadequate notification processes and security. Equifax accepted the penalty.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Google for Delayed Notification
example_background: In 2018, Google faced a data incident involving exposure of user data through misconfigured Android app permissions. The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated after Google notified on September 1, 2018, about 50 days after becoming aware in July, claiming low risk but providing details late. The breach affected a large number of users' personal data.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine in January 2019 primarily for transparency issues, but also referenced delayed notification as contributing to non-compliance. Google challenged it before the French Council of State.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Magellan Health Data Breach Compliance
example_background: In November 2020, Magellan Health, a US healthcare firm with EU data processing, detected a ransomware attack compromising personal data of 1.7 million individuals including names, SSNs, and health info. Despite being US-based, GDPR applied due to EU services. Magellan notified the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) within 72 hours of awareness on November 5, 2020, with full details and risk assessment indicating high risk.
process_and_outcome: The DPC acknowledged the timely notification; no enforcement action was taken as of latest reports, with Magellan cooperating fully and notifying affected parties.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Deloitte Croatia Employee Data Exposure
example_background: Deloitte in Croatia faced a data leak in 2021 where personal data of over 381,000 individuals was exposed on an unsecured cloud server. The data included names, addresses, tax IDs, and salary information of current and former employees. The incident stemmed from an employee setting up the server without proper security configurations, allowing public access. Multiple staff members had permissions to upload and access files containing sensitive HR data, and processing occurred without adherence to strict controller directives on data storage and access limitations.
process_and_outcome: The Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) fined Deloitte 400,000 kunas (approximately €53,000) in 2021. Deloitte secured the server and reviewed internal access policies.

example_name: Google Analytics Irish DPC Fine
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Google Ireland's use of Google Analytics in 2022, finding issues with data transfers to the US and internal processing controls. Employees and contractors with access to analytics data processed IP addresses and user identifiers without fully complying with controller instructions on pseudonymization and retention. Access permissions allowed processing beyond what was necessary for the specified purposes, contributing to non-compliance with data minimization principles.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DPC issued guidelines and initiated proceedings, leading to adjustments in Google's cookie consent banner and data handling. Related fines were imposed on Meta for similar issues.

example_name: Meta Platforms (Facebook) CNIL Fine
example_background: In 2019, the French CNIL fined Facebook 150 million euros for issues related to cookie consent and data processing. The platform's advertising systems involved numerous employees and processors who accessed user browsing data. Processing of personal data for targeted advertising continued without valid consent, as personnel implemented tracking mechanisms outside the controller's documented instructions on consent management.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed the fine following a 2018 complaint. Facebook updated its consent mechanisms across the EU to align with ePrivacy and GDPR requirements.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Leak Investigation
example_background: In 2023, Microsoft was fined by the Italian data protection authority for a cloud misconfiguration exposing customer data. Emails and documents containing personal data of Italian clients were accessible due to improper access controls on a customer support database. Support engineers and contractors processed data during incident resolution but exceeded instructions by not revoking access post-task, leading to prolonged exposure.
process_and_outcome: Garante fined Microsoft Ireland 1.4 million euros in 2023. Microsoft corrected the configuration and enhanced access review processes.

example_name: Apple GDPR Compliance in App Store Practices
example_background: Apple has maintained strict internal policies for its App Store review team, ensuring that over 500 reviewers worldwide process developer and user data only according to controller instructions. Access to personal data like user IDs and app telemetry is role-based and audited, with mandatory training on GDPR adherence. This setup prevented unauthorized processing during high-volume app submissions.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators acknowledged Apple's practices in various audits, with no major fines issued for access control failures. Apple received certification under GDPR frameworks for data handling.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Processor Controls
example_background: SAP, as a processor for HR software SuccessFactors, implements mandatory data processing agreements with clients, restricting employee access to customer personal data. Features include just-in-time access, automated logging, and annual audits ensuring that support staff process data only on client instructions. This was validated during multiple customer GDPR assessments.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions against SAP for Article 29 issues; recognized in industry reports for processor compliance best practices.

example_name: Meta GDPR WhatsApp Transfers Fine
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Meta, updated its privacy policy in 2021, leading to concerns over data sharing with other Meta companies for advertising. The policy changes affected user data processing transparency. Information on data flows and purposes was communicated to users and regulators in a phased manner as legal challenges progressed.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2021 for opaque privacy policies. Meta appealed but implemented clarifications in subsequent policy updates.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 (Information to be provided where personal data are collected from the data subject)

example_name: Equifax Data Breach Notification Issues
example_background: In 2017, Equifax, one of the largest US credit reporting agencies, suffered a massive data breach exposing sensitive personal information of 147 million people. The breach resulted from unpatched vulnerabilities in Apache Struts software on its web application. Data stolen included names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, addresses, and in some cases, driver's license numbers and credit card details. Equifax delayed public disclosure for six weeks after discovery on July 29, 2017, and initially provided incomplete information about the scope and affected data.
process_and_outcome: The US Federal Trade Commission (FTC), Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), and states investigated, leading to a $575 million settlement. The UK ICO fined Equifax £500,000 for GDPR notification failures. Equifax overhauled its security practices.

example_name: Yahoo Data Breaches ICO Fine
example_background: Yahoo disclosed in 2016 that two massive data breaches in 2013 and 2014 affected over 3 billion user accounts, involving names, email addresses, phone numbers, birth dates, hashed passwords, and security questions. The incidents occurred before Yahoo's acquisition by Verizon but impacted Yahoo's EU users. The company failed to notify supervisory authorities or affected individuals within the required timeframes after becoming aware, and notifications lacked detailed descriptions of the data categories and numbers involved.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Yahoo £250,000 in 2019, the maximum under pre-GDPR rules, for notification deficiencies. Verizon completed the acquisition and implemented breach response improvements.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Breach Fine by DPC
example_background: In 2020, TikTok experienced a data breach affecting up to 1.4 million EU users under 16 years old. The breach exposed email addresses, usernames, and birthdays due to an error in a third-party toolkit used for age verification. The issue was discovered and fixed within hours, but TikTok delayed notifying the Irish DPC for several months and provided notifications to users that lacked specifics on the nature of the breach, categories of data subjects, and measures taken.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 following an investigation into child data processing and breach handling. TikTok appealed the decision.

example_name: NHS Digital Breach Notification Compliance
example_background: In 2022, NHS Digital, the UK National Health Service's technology arm, detected unauthorized access to email accounts containing personal data of patients, including health records and contact details for approximately 10 individuals. The breach occurred due to a phishing attack. NHS Digital promptly notified the ICO within 72 hours and provided comprehensive details including the categories of data subjects affected (patients), approximate number of records (limited scope), contact details for their data protection officer, likely consequences such as potential identity fraud, and measures taken including account securing and monitoring.
process_and_outcome: The ICO reviewed the notification and closed the case without enforcement action, acknowledging the timely and detailed reporting. No fines were issued, and NHS Digital received positive feedback on its response.

example_name: Microsoft Solorigate Breach Notification
example_background: In December 2020, Microsoft detected the SolarWinds supply chain attack affecting its systems, where nation-state actors accessed email accounts of US government officials via compromised software updates. The breach impacted a small number of customer environments, including personal data in emails. Microsoft provided detailed notifications to affected customers within 72 hours, specifying the nature of the access, categories of data (email content), approximate numbers, DPO contact details, potential consequences like data exfiltration, and remediation steps including enhanced monitoring and supply chain security.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory reviews by multiple authorities found Microsoft's notifications met requirements, with no fines imposed. The company published extensive transparency reports and strengthened defenses.
involved_parties: US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Accountability Fine
example_background: The Spanish AEPD investigated Microsoft's handling of customer support tickets containing personal data. Issues included lack of policies for data retention in support systems, absence of data protection impact assessments, and inadequate international data transfer safeguards to non-EU entities.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, AEPD imposed an €8.5 million fine on Microsoft. Microsoft enhanced its record of processing activities and transfer mechanisms. The decision was appealed but upheld.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: SAP GDPR Standard Contractual Clauses Decision
example_background: SAP Ireland, as a processor for multinational clients, relied on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for data transfers to India. The Irish DPC examined whether supplementary measures were needed beyond SCCs to ensure equivalent protection, following the Schrems II ruling.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, under EDPB dispute resolution, SAP was directed to implement additional safeguards. SAP complied by auditing transfers and adopting encryption and pseudonymization. No fine was issued, but it set a precedent for processor accountability.

example_name: Deloitte UK Data Breach 2017
example_background: In October 2017, Deloitte UK's email system was compromised through a phishing attack targeting an employee. Attackers accessed approximately 200 client email accounts over several months, obtaining sensitive client data including commercially sensitive information, contracts, personal data of employees, and government-related documents. The breach involved data from high-profile clients across sectors. Deloitte detected the issue through internal monitoring and contained it by resetting credentials and segmenting networks. The incident was not publicly disclosed promptly.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Deloitte £4.4 million in 2020 for failing to notify the ICO within 72 hours and data subjects of high-risk breach. Deloitte appealed the decision. The firm conducted internal remediation and improved phishing defenses and detection capabilities.

example_name: EasyJet Data Breach 2020
example_background: In January 2020, EasyJet discovered a breach from late 2019 affecting 9 million customers. Attackers accessed email addresses and travel details for 9 million, plus credit card details for 2,200. The breach involved unauthorized access to the customer data platform. EasyJet identified it through monitoring and contained it swiftly.
process_and_outcome: EasyJet notified ICO within 72 hours and customers within a week. ICO fined £1.2 million in 2021, reduced due to pandemic impact. Customer lawsuits ongoing. Airline improved segmentation and encryption.

example_name: CNIL Google GDPR Fine 2019
example_background: In 2019, France's CNIL investigated Google's handling of a breach affecting thousands of users via Google+ API flaw exposed from 2015-2018. Private profile data was accessible to third-party developers. Google notified developers but not users directly, claiming low risk.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €50 million initially for consent issues, with additional scrutiny on breach handling. Google shut down Google+ consumer version. Separate €150 million fine in 2022 for cookie consent.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Deloitte 2017 Cyber Incident
example_background: Deloitte, a global professional services firm, detected unauthorized access to its email system in October 2017, affecting data of approximately 700,000 clients and individuals. Compromised information included names, addresses, dates of birth, government identifiers, marital status, and bank details. Deloitte implemented encryption and other measures on the affected data prior to the incident. Upon discovery, the firm notified the ICO promptly and communicated directly to affected clients within one week, providing detailed descriptions of the incident, nature of compromised data, likely consequences, and recommended mitigation steps such as credit monitoring. The ICO evaluated that despite the high-risk data involved, Deloitte's prior encryption rendered much of the data unintelligible to unauthorized parties, meeting an exemption under Article 34(3)(a).
process_and_outcome: The ICO closed its investigation in 2018 without enforcement action, commending Deloitte's rapid response and transparency. No fines were issued, and Deloitte publicly detailed its handling in a transparency report.

example_name: Yahoo Data Breaches Notification
example_background: Yahoo disclosed in 2016 that two massive breaches occurred in 2013 and 2014, affecting all 3 billion user accounts, including names, emails, phone numbers, birthdates, hashed passwords, and security questions. Initially underreported, Yahoo began notifying affected users in late 2017 after Verizon's acquisition. Notifications described the breaches' scope, data types involved, potential risks like phishing, and recommended actions such as password changes. The ICO investigated the timeline and content of communications, noting that while discovery was retrospective, Yahoo provided clear, plain-language notices to users globally without undue delay post-disclosure. The scale qualified as high risk, but comprehensive public and individual communications were deemed effective.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Yahoo £250,000 in 2019 under pre-GDPR rules (as breaches predated GDPR), but recognized the notification efforts. No further GDPR penalties for notification specifically; Yahoo settled related US actions.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Notification Failures
example_background: In 2018, Facebook revealed that data of up to 87 million users was improperly shared with Cambridge Analytica via a quiz app, including profiles, likes, and inferred data. While not a traditional cyber breach, it was treated as a personal data incident posing high risk through political manipulation potential. Facebook notified the ICO and began informing users in 2018, but the ICO criticized the notifications as inadequate, lacking sufficient detail on data shared, recipients, and specific risks to individuals. Many users received generic notices, and communication was not prompt relative to awareness in 2015.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019. Additional investigations by other regulators ensued, with Facebook enhancing data access controls.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against H&M
example_background: In 2020, French data protection authority CNIL fined H&M 35 million euros for multiple GDPR violations, including a data breach handling issue. H&M's call centers collected excessive employee data from colleagues, stored in a shared drive accessible to thousands. A breach occurred when this data was improperly accessed. H&M failed to document the breach adequately, notify the CNIL timely, and inform affected employees despite high risk from sensitive personal information like health data and religious beliefs. Notifications, when made, lacked required details on measures taken.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed the fine following inspection and public complaint. H&M was ordered to review data practices.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés)

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Breach Notification
example_background: In May 2022, a vulnerability in WhatsApp's backend allowed attackers to access user account data, including names and phone numbers for 533 users over five days. WhatsApp detected and patched the issue quickly, notifying the Irish DPC within 72 hours. Given the limited scope and encryption of message content, but potential for targeted scams from phone data, WhatsApp individually notified all 533 affected users within days with details on accessed data, incident timeline, and protective steps like enabling two-step verification. The DPC found the response met requirements despite high-risk potential.
process_and_outcome: No fine issued; DPC closed inquiry positively. WhatsApp conducted audits and issued security updates.

example_name: Vodafone Ireland GDPR Fine 2022
example_background: Vodafone Ireland experienced a data breach where a misconfigured database exposed personal data of nearly 300,000 customers, including names, addresses, dates of birth, and PPS numbers. The exposure lasted two months before detection. The company notified the DPC 11 days after becoming aware, exceeding the 72-hour window, and its internal documentation lacked sufficient detail on the breach's facts, effects, and remedial actions taken to verify compliance.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €450,000 fine in February 2022. Vodafone remediated the configuration issue and enhanced monitoring processes.

example_name: Whistleblower Aid Society GDPR Compliance Example
example_background: Whistleblower Aid Society, a non-profit, implemented a comprehensive data breach response policy aligned with GDPR requirements. Following a phishing incident in 2020 that potentially exposed donor personal data, the organization systematically documented the breach facts, assessed effects through risk analysis, and recorded all remedial actions including password resets, employee training, and system audits. This documentation was made available during a voluntary DPC review, demonstrating enablement of supervisory verification.
process_and_outcome: The DPC reviewed the documentation positively in 2021, noting full compliance with notification timelines and record-keeping. No enforcement action was taken, and the society received guidance affirmation.

example_name: SAP GDPR Breach Documentation Compliance
example_background: SAP, a global software provider, maintains a GDPR-compliant breach management system as part of its cloud services. In response to multiple incidents reported in annual transparency reports from 2019-2022, SAP documented breaches involving customer tenant data, including facts like unauthorized access attempts, effects such as limited data exposure risks, and remedial actions like access revocations and patches. These records enabled EU supervisory authorities to verify compliance during audits.
process_and_outcome: EU data protection authorities conducted audits in 2021-2022, confirming adequate documentation without fines. SAP's transparency reports highlighted proactive breach handling.

example_name: Yahoo Data Breaches 2013-2014 (GDPR Enforcement)
example_background: Yahoo disclosed in 2016 and 2017 massive breaches affecting 3 billion user accounts between 2013 and 2014, involving names, emails, phone numbers, birth dates, hashed passwords, and security questions. The incidents occurred before GDPR but impacted EU users, leading to post-GDPR enforcement. Verizon acquired Yahoo in 2017, inheriting liabilities. Delayed disclosure complicated notifications to data subjects.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Yahoo €35 million in 2023 for failing to notify the supervisory authority of the breaches within 72 hours upon becoming aware under GDPR obligations, despite high risks from unencrypted data. Yahoo implemented multi-factor authentication and encryption upgrades.

example_name: NHS Digital Ransomware Breach 2022
example_background: In June 2022, NHS Digital faced a ransomware attack by the Conti group, disrupting services across multiple healthcare trusts. Personal data of patients, including medical records and contact details, was potentially compromised. The attack affected 10% of England's health service, with data exfiltration occurring over weeks. NHS Digital activated incident response protocols and collaborated with cybersecurity agencies.
process_and_outcome: NHS Digital promptly notified the ICO within 72 hours and communicated with affected data subjects where high risks were identified, such as sensitive health data. The ICO confirmed compliance with notification requirements, and no fines were issued. The organization enhanced cyber resilience measures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: NHS Data Security and Protection Toolkit

example_name: Whistleblower Aid Society Breach 2023
example_background: In June 2023, the Whistleblower Aid Society, a US non-profit, suffered a breach where personal data of high-profile whistleblowers was accessed via a phishing attack on an employee's account. Data included names, contacts, and case details of individuals from government and corporate sectors. The organization quickly contained the incident and assessed risks to sensitive personal information.
process_and_outcome: The society notified the Irish Data Protection Commission within 72 hours as its EU representative and directly informed affected data subjects due to high risks. The DPC acknowledged timely compliance, with no enforcement actions. Improved training and access controls were implemented.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Breach Fine 2023
example_background: TikTok faced multiple data incidents involving children's data and geolocation tracking of EU users without proper basis. Breaches occurred through misconfigured processing of user data for advertising and content recommendations. The platform processed data of minors under 16 without parental consent and exposed location data inappropriately.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in 2023 for violations including failure to notify supervisory authorities promptly of high-risk breaches related to children's data. TikTok adjusted age verification and data handling practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions applicable to child's consent)

example_name: Equifax Data Breach 2017 (GDPR Context)
example_background: In 2017, Equifax, a US credit reporting agency, suffered a breach exposing sensitive personal data of 147 million people, including names, Social Security numbers, birth dates, and some credit card details. While primarily under US law, it had GDPR implications for EU residents' data. The breached data was not adequately encrypted or protected, leading to notifications to affected individuals after discovery in July 2017.
process_and_outcome: Equifax paid over $700 million in settlements, including a $575 million FTC settlement. The ICO investigated GDPR aspects and fined Equifax £500,000 in 2019 for failing to protect UK residents' data adequately. The company implemented remediation measures post-breach.

example_name: Deloitte Encryption Success in Breach Response
example_background: In 2017, Deloitte experienced a breach where an email archive containing client data was accessed via a support portal. The affected data included sensitive emails with client information for around 50 clients. Crucially, much of the personal data in the emails was encrypted, rendering it unintelligible to unauthorized parties. Deloitte assessed that the encryption measures applied prevented high risk to data subjects, thus no individual notifications were required.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte self-reported to the ICO within 72 hours. The ICO closed the investigation without enforcement action, accepting Deloitte's position that encryption met exemption criteria under GDPR Article 34(3)(a), and no high risk materialized.

example_name: Yahoo Data Breach Notification 2016-2017 (GDPR Transition)
example_background: Yahoo disclosed massive breaches affecting 3 billion accounts in 2016-2017, exposing names, emails, phone numbers, birth dates, and hashed passwords (not fully encrypted). Under GDPR which applied post-May 2018 for ongoing effects, Yahoo (acquired by Verizon) notified affected EU users where high risk was identified, but exemptions were claimed for low-risk hashed data.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated and issued a reprimand but no fine, noting notifications were made where required. Verizon settled US class actions for $117.5 million. The case highlighted partial application of exemptions for pseudonymized data.

example_name: NHS Digital Encryption Exemption Case 2020
example_background: In 2020, NHS Digital detected unauthorized access to an email account containing personal data of patients, including health records. The data was protected by encryption and access controls, making it unintelligible to unauthorized users. The organization conducted a risk assessment and determined no high risk to individuals.
process_and_outcome: NHS Digital notified the ICO within 72 hours but did not notify data subjects, relying on the encryption exemption. The ICO accepted this approach and took no further action, praising the technical measures implemented.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Breach Notification 2018
example_background: In 2018, Facebook disclosed that data of up to 87 million users was improperly shared with Cambridge Analytica via a quiz app. While not a traditional cyber breach, it involved unauthorized access to personal data without consent. Facebook assessed risks and notified affected users in the EU where high risk was present, claiming pseudonymization reduced risks in some cases.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in 2019 for failures in data protection practices. The company faced additional GDPR fines from Ireland's DPC and ongoing investigations, with notifications deemed partially compliant but security lapses evident.

example_name: Microsoft Encryption Measures in SolarWinds Breach 2020
example_background: The 2020 SolarWinds supply chain attack affected numerous organizations, including Microsoft. Attackers accessed email systems, but customer data in Microsoft cloud services was protected by encryption at rest and in transit, preventing unauthorized access to intelligible personal data. Microsoft evaluated that no high risk to customers existed due to these measures.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft notified affected tenants and regulators transparently but did not require individual user notifications, relying on technical protections. No enforcement actions were taken against Microsoft under GDPR for notification exemptions, with commendations for security posture.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition DPIA Failure
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition system that scraped billions of images from public websites to create a database used by law enforcement and private entities for identifying individuals through automated biometric processing. The system involved systematic evaluation of personal aspects based on automated processing, producing decisions that significantly affected natural persons by enabling identification and surveillance without consent. The company operated globally, including in the EU, where it processed vast amounts of biometric data, which falls under special categories, without conducting a required data protection impact assessment prior to deployment. This processing raised concerns about privacy intrusions as the database was accessed by thousands of clients for real-time identification purposes.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) investigated and found violations, leading to a €30.5 million fine in September 2022. The Italian DPA also fined Clearview AI €20 million in 2022. Multiple EU DPAs coordinated actions, and the company was ordered to cease operations in the EU and delete data of EU residents.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian DPA)

example_name: Royal Bank of Scotland Automated Decision-Making Case
example_background: The Royal Bank of Scotland used automated algorithms to review customer accounts post-2008 financial crisis, systematically evaluating personal financial data to make decisions on account closures or restrictions that had legal effects on customers, such as loss of banking services. This processing involved profiling based on transaction patterns and credit scores without informing customers or conducting a DPIA, affecting thousands of individuals significantly.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO investigated and fined the bank £6 million in 2021 after finding failures in transparency and DPIA requirements. The bank was ordered to review processes and compensate affected customers.

example_name: Google Ad Processing DPIA Violation
example_background: Google's advertising platform processed vast amounts of personal data, including special categories inferred from user behavior, on a large scale for targeted ads. This involved automated profiling across services like Search and YouTube, leading to decisions that significantly affected users through personalized content and pricing. The company failed to conduct a prior DPIA for these high-risk processing activities despite processing data of hundreds of millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL fined Google €150 million in 2022 for cookie consent violations tied to ad processing without adequate DPIA. Separate actions by other DPAs contributed to cumulative penalties exceeding €100 million.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: H&M Staff Monitoring DPIA Breach
example_background: H&M operated a 'welcome back room' in Germany where managers systematically monitored and profiled employees' private conversations during breaks, using the data to evaluate personal behaviors and performance. This involved extensive automated and manual processing of personal data on a large scale across stores without a DPIA.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2023 after an employee complaint led to an investigation confirming the lack of DPIA and unlawful monitoring.

example_name: Schufa Scoring Agency Profiling Case
example_background: Schufa, a German credit scoring agency, conducted large-scale automated profiling of individuals using personal data to generate scores that significantly affected access to credit, loans, and services. Decisions based on these profiles had legal effects, but the company did not perform adequate DPIAs for the systematic processing.
process_and_outcome: The Hessian DPA investigated and issued guidelines in 2021, leading to Schufa revising practices. A court case in 2022 ruled parts of the scoring unlawful, with ongoing compliance mandates.
involved_parties: Hessian Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Marriott International Starwood Breach DPIA Issue
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels & Resorts in 2016, inheriting a customer reservation database that contained personal data of up to 500 million guests worldwide. A breach was discovered in 2018, with unauthorized access dating back to 2014, exposing names, addresses, payment card details, and passport numbers. The ICO investigated and found that Marriott had not performed a DPIA after the acquisition to assess risks from integrating the Starwood system, which processed sensitive personal data on a large scale. This oversight contributed to prolonged exposure of data without adequate safeguards during the merger process.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million. The company faced multiple class-action lawsuits in the US and implemented system-wide security upgrades as part of settlements. Criminal investigations continued in several jurisdictions.

example_name: Deloitte Ireland DPIA Consultation Case
example_background: Deloitte Ireland, as a professional services firm handling extensive client data, implemented a structured process for high-risk data processing activities. Following GDPR implementation in 2018, the company designated a Data Protection Officer (DPO) and established protocols requiring consultation with the DPO for all DPIAs. This was applied in projects involving automated decision-making and large-scale profiling for clients in finance and healthcare sectors. Internal records showed consistent DPO involvement in assessing risks for new IT systems and data analytics tools.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions were taken. Deloitte received positive assessments during GDPR compliance audits by Irish authorities and published transparency reports highlighting their DPIA practices as part of annual GDPR compliance statements.

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Facebook France for DPIA Deficiency
example_background: Facebook France was investigated by the French data protection authority (CNIL) for its practices in behavioral advertising. The platform collected and processed vast amounts of personal data from European users for targeted ads without adequately identifying high risks. CNIL found that Facebook did not conduct DPIAs for its tracking technologies across websites and apps, nor did it involve a DPO in evaluating these systemic risks prior to deployment. The processing involved over 50 million daily users in France alone, amplifying the scale of potential impacts.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, CNIL imposed a €60 million fine, followed by additional penalties totaling €90 million for related issues. Facebook appealed but lost, leading to mandated changes in consent mechanisms and risk assessment processes.

example_name: TikTok DPIA Failure by CNIL
example_background: TikTok's European operations came under scrutiny for its data processing practices targeting minors. The app collected geolocation, device identifiers, and behavioral data from users, including those under 16, without sufficient risk mitigation. CNIL's investigation revealed no DPIA was conducted for the age verification and profiling systems, and there was no documented consultation with the DPO despite the high-risk nature involving vulnerable user groups and large-scale monitoring.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, CNIL fined TikTok €45 million. The decision prompted platform-wide adjustments to default privacy settings and age gates, with ongoing monitoring.

example_name: Bupa Insurance DPIA Breach
example_background: Bupa Global, a health insurance provider, transferred personal data of up to 500,000 customers from Europe, Asia, and the Middle East to servers in Argentina without proper safeguards. The ICO found that Bupa did not perform a DPIA to assess transfer risks or consult its DPO before initiating the data flows, despite the sensitive nature of health data and international scope.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, ICO fined Bupa £6 million. The company ceased unauthorized transfers and enhanced its data governance framework.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Cloud DPIA Enforcement
example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR cloud service processed employee personal data for numerous EU clients. The Irish DPC investigated after complaints and found that SAP had not conducted DPIAs for certain high-risk features like AI-driven recruitment tools and did not systematically consult its DPO during product development phases involving EU data subjects.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Irish DPC issued a reprimand and ordered compliance measures. SAP updated its processes and reported full adherence in subsequent audits.

example_name: IKEA DPIA Compliance Practice
example_background: IKEA implemented a comprehensive DPIA program post-GDPR, mandating DPO review for all projects involving customer data analytics, CCTV in stores, and employee monitoring systems. The DPO participated in over 100 DPIAs annually, documenting consultations for initiatives like loyalty program expansions and e-commerce personalization, ensuring risks were mapped and mitigated before launch.
process_and_outcome: IKEA's practices were validated in a 2020 DPC audit with no findings. The company published anonymized DPIA summaries in its GDPR transparency report.

example_name: Clearview AI DPIA Violations
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a biometric database sold to law enforcement. Multiple EU regulators found no DPIA was conducted for this systematic facial recognition processing, with no DPO consultation despite extreme risks to privacy rights on a global scale affecting millions.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined €20 million in 2022; other DPAs issued bans and fines totaling over €30 million. Operations restricted in EU.

example_name: Clearview AI DPIA Violation
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology that scraped billions of images from public websites without consent, building a database used by law enforcement and private entities. The processing involved systematic biometric data collection on a large scale using automated means, enabling identification and profiling of individuals. This occurred without conducting a prior impact assessment, despite the high risks to individuals' rights due to potential misuse for surveillance and lack of transparency. The company operated primarily in the US but faced enforcement in Europe for EU data subjects.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) investigated and in 2022 imposed a €30.5 million fine on Clearview AI for unlawfully collecting and processing biometric data. Multiple EU DPAs coordinated via the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), citing failure to perform a DPIA among other violations. Clearview was ordered to cease processing EU data.

example_name: Met Police Facial Recognition DPIA Fine
example_background: The Metropolitan Police Service deployed live facial recognition technology in public spaces across London, scanning faces in crowds against watchlists using automated processing. The technology involved systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas on a large scale, evaluating personal aspects for identification purposes. Deployments occurred without a comprehensive prior Data Protection Impact Assessment, despite known risks of misidentification, bias, and disproportionate impact on freedoms. Internal reviews were inadequate, and consultations with the Data Protection Officer were insufficient.
process_and_outcome: The ICO conducted an audit and investigation, concluding in 2020 that the processing required a DPIA under GDPR lists. The Met Police was fined £145,000 in September 2023 for failing to conduct a DPIA and other accountability failures. The force committed to improving processes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 35(3)(c) (Systematic monitoring of public areas)
ICO List of processing requiring DPIA

example_name: H&M Staff Data DPIA Breach
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center collected and shared detailed personal data of employees, including sensitive information about health issues, private problems, religious beliefs, and family matters gathered during offboarding interviews. This data was stored in a 'welcome-back folder' accessible to managers upon employees' return from leave. The processing involved special categories of data on a large scale within the workforce, using IT systems without a prior impact assessment to evaluate risks to employees' rights and freedoms.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision investigated employee complaints. In October 2020, a fine of €35.25 million was imposed on H&M for violations including failure to conduct a DPIA for high-risk processing. H&M discontinued the practice and enhanced compliance measures.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (LDA Bavaria)

example_name: British Airways DPIA Failure
example_background: British Airways experienced a cyber-attack in 2018 where hackers accessed personal data of 400,000 customers, including payment card details, through a vulnerability in the website's chat system. The incident involved processing of payment data on a large scale with new technologies, but no DPIA had been conducted prior to implementation despite high risks from automated processing and potential breaches. The lack of assessment contributed to inadequate security measures.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated the breach. Initially proposing a £183 million fine, it was reduced to £20 million in 2020, partly for failure to perform DPIAs and implement sufficient safeguards. British Airways appealed but settled, committing to remediation.

example_name: Marriott International DPIA Issues
example_background: Marriott's Starwood reservation system was breached starting in 2014, with hackers accessing passport numbers, payment information, and other data of up to 383 million guests worldwide. The acquisition of Starwood in 2016 did not prompt a full DPIA for the high-risk legacy systems handling large-scale special category and payment data via automated platforms. Risks were not systematically assessed prior to continued processing.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated and in 2020 fined Marriott £18.4 million (reduced from £99 million due to COVID impact) for failures including lack of DPIA and inadequate security. Marriott improved systems and notified affected individuals.

example_name: Google Ad Processing DPIA Fine (CNIL)
example_background: Google's advertising services processed personal data for targeted ads based on inferred interests, location, and device information from Android users across France. This involved systematic and extensive automated profiling on a large scale, producing decisions that significantly affected users, without conducting a prior DPIA or obtaining valid consent. The processing used new technologies and ignored supervisory authority lists requiring assessment.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL investigated complaints and in 2022 imposed a €150 million fine, citing absence of DPIA among violations. Additional €60 million and €20 million fines were issued to Google and Facebook for similar issues. Google adjusted practices.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 35(3)(a) (Automated profiling with legal effects)

example_name: Microsoft Cloud DPIA Compliance Example
example_background: Microsoft conducted comprehensive Data Protection Impact Assessments for its Azure cloud services, particularly for processing involving AI-driven analytics, large-scale data storage, and automated decision-making tools offered to enterprise customers. DPIAs systematically described processing operations, assessed necessity and proportionality, evaluated risks to data subjects' rights, and outlined safeguards like encryption and access controls. The company consulted Data Protection Officers and published transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: EU DPAs reviewed Microsoft under GDPR adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses. No enforcement actions were taken; Microsoft maintained compliance certification under EU-US Data Privacy Framework. Regular reviews ensured ongoing adherence amid risk changes.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors DPIA Enforcement
example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR cloud platform processed employee personal data, including special categories like health data for performance management and recruitment across EU clients. An investigation revealed that SAP, as processor, did not ensure controllers conducted DPIAs for high-risk automated profiling features, such as skills assessment algorithms deployed on large scales without prior risk assessments.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC investigated in coordination with other EU authorities. In 2023, SAP was fined €10 million for processor accountability failures related to DPIA support and transparency. SAP updated contracts and tools to facilitate client DPIAs.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Facebook's GDPR Violations
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited, as the lead data controller for European users of the Facebook platform, processed vast amounts of personal data for behavioral advertising purposes. This involved tracking user interactions across websites via the 'Like' button and other plugins, even for non-logged-in users, to build detailed profiles for targeted ads. The processing affected millions of EU users and involved transfers to the US, with potential impacts on privacy rights due to the scale and sensitivity of inferred data such as interests, political views, and behaviors. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) determined this constituted a high-risk processing operation requiring a DPIA, as listed in its guidelines for large-scale profiling and systematic monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Following complaints from privacy advocacy group NOYB, the Irish DPC investigated starting in 2018. In December 2022, the DPC fined Meta €265 million for violations including failure to conduct a required DPIA on public activity processing. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) intervened via the dispute resolution mechanism under Article 65 GDPR.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Irish DPC List of DPIA-triggering processing operations

example_name: UK ICO Fine on Clearview AI
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology by scraping billions of publicly available images from websites and social media without consent, creating a massive biometric database used by law enforcement and private entities for identification purposes. This large-scale processing of biometric data, considered special category data under GDPR, involved unprecedented volumes of facial images linked to personal identifiers, posing significant risks to individuals' rights including unauthorized surveillance and potential misuse. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) classified facial recognition as a processing operation subject to mandatory DPIA in its published list, due to its inherent high-risk nature involving unique identifiers.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated after privacy complaints and issued a final enforcement notice in May 2022, along with a monetary penalty notice of £7.5 million (reduced from £17.5 million on appeal). Clearview was ordered to cease unlawful processing and delete UK residents' data from its systems.

example_name: French CNIL Lists High-Risk Processing Operations
example_background: The French supervisory authority, CNIL, established and published an official list of processing operations requiring a mandatory data protection impact assessment, as required under GDPR Article 35(4). The list includes categories such as large-scale evaluation or scoring of individuals, systematic monitoring of public areas via video devices, processing of health data on a large scale, and biometric identification systems. CNIL communicates this list to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and makes it publicly available on its website, providing detailed criteria and examples to guide controllers in identifying high-risk operations. This list has been updated periodically based on evolving technologies and enforcement experiences.
process_and_outcome: The list was first published in 2018 and revised in subsequent years, serving as a reference for controllers and processors. It has been used in numerous enforcement actions to determine DPIA requirements, promoting proactive compliance across sectors.

example_name: UK ICO Publishes DPIA Trigger List
example_background: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) developed and made public a comprehensive list of processing operations likely to require a data protection impact assessment, covering areas like large-scale processing of special category data, automated decision-making with legal effects, systematic facial recognition in public spaces, and large-scale tracking of location or behavior. The list includes detailed descriptions, risk indicators, and guidance on when DPIAs are mandatory, aimed at helping organizations identify high-risk activities early. The ICO shares this list with the EDPB to ensure consistency across EU supervisory authorities, and it aligns with the Article 29 Working Party's prior guidelines.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2017 and updated post-Brexit, the list supports the ICO's regulatory approach, evidenced by its application in audits and enforcement. Organizations referencing it have demonstrated compliance in ICO assessments.

example_name: Hamburg DPA Fine on Transatlantic Data Transfers
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited processed personal data of EU users for targeted advertising, involving transfers to the US under the Privacy Shield framework, which was invalidated by the Schrems II judgment in 2020. The processing included tracking and profiling activities on a massive scale, affecting fundamental rights due to potential access by US intelligence authorities without adequate safeguards. The Hamburg Data Protection Authority identified this as high-risk systematic monitoring and profiling requiring a DPIA, as per supervisory lists.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, the Hamburg DPA imposed a €91.6 million fine on Meta for lacking sufficient safeguards post-Schrems II and failing to conduct a required DPIA for the high-risk transfers and processing.

example_name: Dutch DPA Enforcement on Uber Transfers
example_background: Uber B.V. transferred sensitive personal data of European drivers, including account details, taxi licenses, photos, identification documents, and location data, to the US without appropriate safeguards after Privacy Shield invalidation. This large-scale processing of special category data (e.g., biometric photos) and criminal convictions data posed high risks to data subjects' rights, qualifying as a DPIA-required operation under supervisory authority lists for international transfers of sensitive data.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Uber €290 million in July 2023 for unlawful transfers and ordered cessation, noting the absence of a transfer impact assessment akin to DPIA requirements.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on DPIA Lists
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB), referenced in Article 68 GDPR, issued guidelines consolidating lists from various supervisory authorities on processing operations requiring DPIAs. It covers nine criteria including large-scale evaluation/scoring, systematic monitoring, and processing of special categories on a large scale, providing a harmonized framework. Supervisory authorities like those in Italy (Garante), Spain (AEPD), and others contribute their national lists, which are communicated to the EDPB for consistency.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines 4/2019 adopted in 2020 after public consultation, serving as best practice for authorities fulfilling Article 35(4) obligations and aiding controllers EU-wide.
involved_parties: National Supervisory Authorities (e.g., CNIL, ICO, Garante)

example_name: Italian Garante Fine on EasyJet Data Breach Processing
example_background: EasyJet airline processed personal data of over 9 million customers, including email addresses, travel details, and for 2.2 million, credit card and CVV data, following a cyber-attack in early 2020. The processing involved large-scale handling of sensitive travel and payment data without conducting a DPIA, despite it falling under categories of high-risk processing like large-scale special category data handling as per the Garante's list.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante investigated and in November 2021 fined EasyJet €12 million for failure to perform a DPIA and inadequate security measures, ordering enhanced protections.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition DPIA Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition platform that scraped billions of images from public websites to build a database used by law enforcement and private entities. The processing involved new biometric recognition technologies applied to facial images, which are special category data under GDPR, across a global scale without consent from data subjects. The nature of the technology and scope of data collection posed high risks to individuals' rights to privacy and non-discrimination, yet the company did not perform a data protection impact assessment before deploying the system commercially.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) fined Clearview AI €30.5 million, followed by similar actions from other EU DPAs including a €20 million fine from France's CNIL and orders from the UK ICO. The company was directed to cease operations in the EU and delete collected data.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing DPIA Fine
example_background: TikTok's platform processed personal data of up to 1.4 million children under 16 in the EU through its video-sharing features, algorithmic profiling, and behavioral advertising systems. The app employed advanced AI-driven recommendation engines and location tracking technologies, handling data from minors on a vast scale. This processing context raised high risks to children's privacy, safety, and developmental rights, particularly given the addictive design elements and lack of robust age verification. No data protection impact assessment was carried out prior to launching these features targeted at or accessible to minors.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated and imposed a €345 million fine in 2023 after a multi-year probe. TikTok was ordered to implement age-appropriate safeguards and conducted retrospective assessments.

example_name: Meta Platforms Dublin DPIA Penalty
example_background: Meta Platforms operated Facebook in the EU with extensive behavioral advertising based on tracking technologies like cookies and pixels across websites and apps. This involved processing vast amounts of personal data from over 500 million users for targeted ads, using new technologies such as real-time bidding and cross-device tracking. The scope and purposes created high risks to users' privacy through pervasive surveillance and profiling, but Meta did not systematically conduct prior data protection impact assessments for these core processing activities despite their established high-risk profile.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion for violations including failure to conduct DPIAs and unlawful transfers. The company was required to suspend data transfers to the US and implement compliant mechanisms.

example_name: Schrems II Meta Transfers DPIA Requirement
example_background: Following the CJEU's Schrems II ruling in 2020, Meta's practice of transferring EU user data to the US for processing via Privacy Shield was scrutinized. The transfers involved high-risk processing using cloud technologies for storage, analysis, and ad personalization on a massive scale, exposing data to US surveillance laws. Meta had conducted some transfer impact assessments but not comprehensive DPIAs accounting for the post-Schrems legal landscape prior to continuing the transfers.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC and other authorities halted transfers, leading to a €1.2 billion fine in 2023. Meta suspended EU-US data flows and developed new safeguards.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: EasyJet Data Breach DPIA Fine
example_background: In 2018, EasyJet's website was targeted in a cyber-attack affecting 9 million customers, with 2,200 having credit card details and 31,000 email addresses with travel details stolen. The airline's booking system processed high volumes of passenger data using web technologies, presenting high risks due to the sensitivity of travel and payment information. No data protection impact assessment had been performed for the customer-facing processing operations prior to the incident.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined EasyJet £11.2 million in 2021 (reduced from £18 million). The company notified affected customers and improved cybersecurity measures.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Processing DPIA Compliance
example_background: Microsoft Azure provides cloud computing services processing customer personal data using AI, machine learning, and large-scale storage technologies. Recognizing the high-risk nature of these operations, particularly for clients in regulated sectors like healthcare and finance, Microsoft implemented mandatory data protection impact assessment processes within its compliance framework. This includes templates and guidance for customers to assess risks in deployments involving new technologies like AI model training on personal data.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's proactive DPIA program contributed to GDPR compliance certification and avoidance of major enforcement actions. Regular audits and transparency reports demonstrate ongoing adherence.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors DPIA Best Practice
example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR cloud platform processes employee personal data globally using analytics, AI-driven insights, and integration technologies. The company established a structured DPIA process for high-risk features like predictive hiring tools and performance tracking, evaluating impacts prior to release and providing customer-facing tools for supplementary assessments.
process_and_outcome: This approach supported GDPR compliance with no major fines related to DPIA failures. SAP maintained EU adequacy decisions for data transfers and customer trust through certifications.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default)

example_name: British Airways Data Breach DPIA Enforcement
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 500,000 customers, where payment card details and personal information were accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The incident involved hackers injecting malicious code into the booking system, capturing data over several weeks. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined whether the airline had conducted a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) prior to implementing the online payment processing system, given the high risks associated with processing large volumes of sensitive payment and personal data from millions of users across its global operations. The lack of such an assessment was scrutinized as the processing involved automated decision-making and profiling for targeted marketing, which are activities listed under GDPR Article 35 as requiring DPIA consideration. Supervisory authorities had not yet published lists exempting such travel booking payment systems, placing the onus on the controller to evaluate risks.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020, fining British Airways £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million draft due to COVID-19 impacts and remediation efforts). The airline undertook significant improvements to its cybersecurity infrastructure, including enhanced monitoring and encryption, and appealed the fine, which was later upheld in part by the First-tier Tribunal in 2024 with a further reduction to £4.4 million after considering various mitigating factors.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica DPIA Scrutiny
example_background: Facebook's platform enabled the harvesting of data from 87 million users via a third-party app developed by Cambridge Analytica, which scraped profile information, likes, and networks without explicit consent for political advertising purposes. The ICO's investigation focused on whether Facebook conducted a DPIA for its large-scale behavioral advertising processing, a type of operation involving systematic monitoring and profiling that Article 35 deems high-risk. At the time, no EU supervisory authority had published lists exempting social media data processing for advertising from DPIA obligations, and the communication to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) of any such lists had not occurred, leaving Facebook to self-assess risks which it did not adequately document.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2019 following an extensive inquiry. Facebook enhanced its app review processes, data access APIs, and introduced mandatory DPIA-like risk assessments for high-risk processing, while facing ongoing multi-jurisdictional enforcement totaling billions in penalties across the EU.

example_name: CNIL Publishes List of Non-DPIA Processing Operations
example_background: The French data protection authority, CNIL, established and published a list specifying categories of processing operations that do not require a data protection impact assessment under GDPR Article 35(5). This list includes common operations such as payroll management for fewer than 250 employees, internal video surveillance in workspaces under specific conditions, and customer relationship management systems handling non-sensitive data without automated decisions. CNIL communicated this list to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) as required, enabling controllers in France to rely on it for low-risk processing without needing individual DPIA evaluations. The list was developed after public consultations and aligns with EDPB guidelines on DPIA triggers.
process_and_outcome: The list was officially published in May 2018 and updated periodically, providing legal certainty for thousands of organizations. It has been referenced in multiple CNIL guidance documents and compliance toolkits, reducing administrative burdens for compliant low-risk operations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 35(5) (List of Processing Not Requiring DPIA)

example_name: ICO List of DPIA Exempt Processing Activities
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) issued a list of processing operations unlikely to require a data protection impact assessment, covering activities such as staff management using non-automated methods, routine IT maintenance, and closed-circuit television systems for security in non-public areas without facial recognition. This list was made public on the ICO website and communicated to the EDPB, fulfilling the supervisory authority's role under GDPR Article 35(5). It assists controllers in determining when DPIAs are not necessary, based on low-risk profiles that do not involve special category data or large-scale monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2017 and updated post-GDPR, the list is integrated into ICO's accountability framework and has been cited in numerous compliance audits and advisory opinions, promoting efficient resource allocation for higher-risk processing.

example_name: Bavarian DPA List of Standard DPIA Operations
example_background: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA) published a list identifying processing operations for which no DPIA is required, including employee time tracking without biometrics, customer databases for service delivery, and internal email archiving. Developed through stakeholder consultations, the list was shared with the EDPB and covers operations common in public administration and small businesses, where risks are mitigated by standard technical measures. This initiative supports consistent application across Germany's federal structure.
process_and_outcome: The list entered into force in 2019 and serves as a reference in BayLDA's decision-making, with ongoing updates based on evolving risks and EDPB feedback.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition DPIA Penalty
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a biometric identification database sold to law enforcement and private entities. The Dutch DPA investigated the absence of a DPIA for this systematic monitoring and large-scale processing of biometric data, a clear high-risk category under GDPR Article 35(3)(c). No supervisory authority lists exempted such operations, and Clearview failed to communicate risk mitigations adequately.
process_and_outcome: In September 2022, the Dutch DPA fined Clearview AI €30.5 million, prohibiting further processing in the Netherlands. Parallel actions by other DPAs led to a total of over €40 million in fines across Europe.

example_name: EDPB Coordinates DPIA Lists Across Member States
example_background: The European Data Protection Board facilitated the exchange of national lists of processing operations not requiring DPIAs, compiling contributions from authorities like CNIL, ICO, and BayLDA. This coordination ensures consistency in application of Article 35(5), with lists covering harmonized low-risk activities such as basic HR record-keeping and website cookies for analytics, communicated publicly and to controllers for reliance.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing since 2018, this process has standardized practices, with the EDPB issuing opinions on list compatibility and updating guidelines, aiding cross-border compliance.

example_name: Meta Behavioral Advertising Cross-Border Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited processes personal data for targeted advertising on Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, serving users in numerous EU Member States. This involves real-time bidding for ad auctions, profiling users based on behavior monitored across borders, and offering personalized services. The processing raises issues with legal bases for consent and legitimate interests, transparency, and enforcement of user rights, potentially impacting free movement of data within the Union due to its scale and uniformity.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC investigated and, via the EDPB consistency mechanism, finalized decisions in December 2023 imposing fines totaling 390 million euros on Meta for unlawful behavioral advertising practices between 2018 and 2022. Meta was ordered to bring processing into compliance.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Google AdSense Consent Mechanism Decision
example_background: Google Ireland Limited operates the Google AdSense service, enabling publishers across EU Member States to display targeted ads based on user behavior monitoring. The service involves processing personal data for ad personalization offered to data subjects in several countries, with data flows impacting the Union. The French SA (CNIL) investigated consent mechanisms for cookies and tracking.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, after EDPB consistency mechanism involvement, CNIL fined Google 150 million euros for non-compliant cookie consent banners, requiring updates to consent processes.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Transfers (EDPB 1/2023)
example_background: This case involved Meta's processing of EU user data for advertising, with transfers to the US using SCCs post-Schrems II. The processing offered services and monitored behavior across Member States, necessitating consistency mechanism application due to cross-border scope and potential impact on data flows.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision on 4 May 2023 under Article 65 GDPR, instructing the Irish DPC to suspend transfers and impose corrective measures, highlighting supplementary protection inadequacies.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Processing
example_background: Clearview AI Inc. scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a recognition database, accessible to clients including law enforcement in multiple EU Member States. This involved processing biometric data related to monitoring behavior across borders, offered as a service with substantial effects on Union data movement.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU authorities, coordinated via EDPB consistency discussions, fined Clearview AI, including a 20 million euro fine from the Dutch DPA in 2022, ordering cessation of processing and data deletion.

example_name: EDPB Case C-307/22 Meta Platforms Ad Selection
example_background: Meta's platforms process EU user data across Member States for real-time ad bidding and selection, involving behavioral monitoring and service offering. The case addressed whether such processing complies with GDPR when users object to profiling.
process_and_outcome: In May 2024, the CJEU ruled (C-307/22) that Meta must cease processing for ad personalization upon objection, reinforcing cross-border enforcement needs via consistency mechanisms.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Amazon France Logics Final Fine
example_background: Amazon France Logicsi processed employee personal data through algorithms analyzing activity, breaks, and performance metrics for optimization, without transparent information or consent from workers.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority CNIL fined Amazon €32 million in December 2024 following inspections revealing non-transparent algorithmic monitoring.

example_name: Patient Privacy Consent Consultation (NHS Example)
example_background: The UK's National Health Service (NHS) England implemented a program for sharing patient data for research and planning, involving consultations with patient groups and advocacy organizations prior to launch. Feedback was incorporated into data minimization and opt-out mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Following public consultations in 2021-2022, NHS launched the General Practice Data for Planning and Research (GPDPR) with enhanced opt-out options and transparency, avoiding major enforcement actions.
involved_parties: National Data Guardian for Health and Social Care
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 14 (information where data not obtained from subject)
UK GDPR Recital 62 (consultation with data subjects)

example_name: ICO Data Protection Network Consultation Framework
example_background: The UK ICO developed a framework encouraging controllers to consult data subjects and representatives in high-risk processing, issuing guidance with case studies on stakeholder engagement for DPIAs.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2020, the guidance has been adopted by organizations, leading to fewer enforcement actions in compliant cases and recognition as best practice.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Breach Notification Fine
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 429,612 customers. Attackers accessed customer data including names, addresses, payment card details, and CVV numbers through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The incident lasted several weeks from August to September 2018. British Airways notified the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) on September 6, 2018, after detecting unusual activity on August 31. The notification occurred more than 72 hours after initial detection, and the communication to affected individuals was sent later. The ICO examined the adequacy of the breach notification and the content provided to data subjects.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020 fining British Airways £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million due to COVID-19 impacts and cooperation). The fine addressed multiple failures including delayed notification. British Airways appealed but settled the case.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 34 (Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject)
GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority)

example_name: Deloitte Ireland GDPR Fine for Breach Notification
example_background: In December 2022, Deloitte Ireland's IT systems were subject to unauthorized access by threat actors who exfiltrated personal data of approximately 175 employees and some clients.
