The breach involved sensitive information such as payroll details and client contracts. Deloitte notified the Data Protection Commission (DPC) on January 11, 2023, claiming detection on January 9, but the DPC found notification was 4 days late. Communications to affected data subjects lacked sufficient detail on the nature of the breach and specific protective measures implemented.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €1.2 million fine in May 2024 for violations including untimely notification and inadequate communication content under Articles 33 and 34. Deloitte cooperated during the investigation.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Data Breach Fine
example_background: Between 2018 and 2019, approximately 29 million Facebook users' data was exposed due to a vulnerability in the View As feature, affecting names, contact details, and other profile information across regions including the EU. Meta notified the Irish DPC on September 28, 2018. The notifications to affected data subjects were criticized for lacking plain language descriptions of the breach nature and insufficient detail on measures to mitigate risks.
process_and_outcome: The DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion in May 2023 primarily for data transfers, but a separate €275 million fine in 2022 addressed breach notification deficiencies under Article 34, noting inadequate information provided to data subjects.

example_name: WhistleOut Australia PECR and GDPR Fine
example_background: In October 2021, comparison website WhistleOut sent 4.6 million marketing emails to users who had opted out. The company notified the ICO on October 28, 2021. Communications to affected recipients did not adequately explain the nature of the personal data misuse or provide clear recommended measures, and used technical language.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined WhistleOut £125,000 in December 2022 for violations including poor breach notification content. The company had limited resources but was found non-compliant.

example_name: Equifax GDPR-Equivalent Breach Notification Issues
example_background: Although primarily under US law, the 2017 Equifax breach affected 147 million individuals globally, including EU residents. Personal data including names, SSNs, birth dates, and addresses were compromised due to unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability. Equifax notified US authorities promptly but delayed EU notifications beyond 72 hours and provided communications to EU data subjects that lacked detailed plain language on data categories and mitigation steps.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Equifax £4 million in 2019 for notification failures and security lapses. Equifax settled US FTC claims for $575 million and faced multiple class actions.

example_name: Virgin Media Ireland Data Breach Fine
example_background: In April 2023, threat actors accessed Virgin Media Ireland's systems, exfiltrating personal data of over 850,000 customers including names, addresses, dates of birth, and PPS numbers. The company detected the breach on April 3 but notified the DPC on April 22, exceeding the 72-hour limit. Subsequent communications to customers were found to use unclear language and omit details on breach nature and protective measures.
process_and_outcome: The DPC fined Virgin Media Ireland €10 million in December 2023 for delayed notification and inadequate data subject communications.

example_name: Accenture Ireland GDPR Breach Communication Fine
example_background: In July-August 2021, Accenture Ireland's systems were breached by Lockbit ransomware, affecting personal data of over 60,000 Irish employees. Data included HR records and identification details. Notification to DPC was on September 7, 2021, post-initial detection. Data subject notifications lacked comprehensive plain language details on the breach scope and recommended actions.
process_and_outcome: DPC issued a €1.2 million fine in July 2024, citing failures in timely notification and clear communication under GDPR Articles 33 and 34.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach DPIA Fine
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 429,612 customers. Attackers accessed customer payment card details, names, addresses, and travel bookings through a vulnerability in the Magecart JavaScript skimming technique on the company's website. The breach occurred between August 22 and September 5, 2018, and was publicly disclosed on September 6, 2018. The incident involved personal data processing for booking and payment purposes, but the company did not conduct a prior Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) to evaluate the necessity, proportionality, risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms, and measures to address those risks, despite the high-risk nature of processing payment data on a public-facing website.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the incident and in October 2020 issued a monetary penalty notice of £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million proposal following appeal considerations). British Airways, part of International Airlines Group, accepted the penalty and implemented remediation measures including enhanced security protocols.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica DPIA Deficiency
example_background: In 2014-2015, the Facebook platform allowed third-party app 'This Is Your Digital Life' to collect data from 87 million users without adequate consent mechanisms. Data was shared with Cambridge Analytica for political advertising purposes. Facebook's processing operations involved profiling users based on likes, shares, and networks for targeted advertising. The company failed to perform a DPIA documenting the purposes, legitimate interests in advertising revenue, necessity and proportionality of data sharing with third parties, risks to users' privacy rights, and mitigation measures like granular consent or data minimization.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated and fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2019. Additional actions included a €1.2 billion EU fine in 2023 for unrelated transfers, but the Cambridge case highlighted DPIA gaps. Facebook implemented new privacy tools and faced ongoing US litigation.

example_name: Metropolitan Police Live Facial Recognition DPIA Fine
example_background: The Metropolitan Police deployed live facial recognition (LFR) technology at public events from 2016-2020, scanning faces against watchlists to identify suspects. Processing involved real-time biometric analysis of attendees' data for public safety purposes. A post-deployment DPIA was conducted, but regulators found it deficient in systematically describing processing operations, assessing necessity and proportionality against less intrusive alternatives, evaluating risks to rights like freedom of assembly, and detailing safeguards such as bias audits or retention limits prior to deployment.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined £33,395 in September 2023 for breaches, determining inadequate DPIA and legal basis. The police appealed aspects but implemented improved policies.

example_name: Google EU-US Data Transfers Fine
example_background: Google transferred personal data of EU users to the US for advertising and analytics purposes using standard contractual clauses without conducting a prior transfer impact assessment equivalent to DPIA, failing to document risks from US surveillance laws (e.g., Cloud Act), necessity for global operations, proportionality, and supplementary measures like encryption or pseudonymization to protect data subjects' rights.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL fined €50 million in 2019; further Schrems II implications led to suspension orders. Google updated clauses but faced ongoing challenges.
involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Deloitte GDPR DPIA Best Practice Implementation
example_background: Deloitte, as a professional services firm, processes vast amounts of client and employee personal data for consulting, audit, and HR purposes. Following GDPR implementation, the company systematically integrated DPIAs into its privacy program. For high-risk projects like AI-driven client analytics or employee monitoring tools, DPIAs include detailed descriptions of processing (e.g., data flows, legitimate interests in efficiency), necessity/proportionality assessments (e.g., data minimization), risk evaluations to rights (e.g., privacy, equality), and measures (e.g., encryption, audits, DPO oversight), reviewed annually.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte achieved ISO 27701 certification and avoided major enforcement actions. The approach was recognized in industry reports as enabling compliance and business innovation without incidents.

example_name: IKEA Employee Monitoring DPIA Success
example_background: IKEA implemented employee performance monitoring systems involving CCTV, badge swipes, and digital tracking across European stores. Prior to rollout, comprehensive DPIAs were conducted, describing processing for safety and efficiency, legitimate interests balanced via LIA, necessity/proportionality with alternatives considered, risks to employee privacy and union rights assessed, and measures like notice postings, data retention limits (30 days), access logs, and grievance mechanisms implemented, with regular reviews.
process_and_outcome: The DPIA process prevented regulatory challenges and employee complaints. IKEA's transparency was praised in a 2020 Dutch DPA review, serving as a model for retail sector compliance.

example_name: Google Analytics and Ad Tech DPIA Fine 2022
example_background: The French data protection authority CNIL investigated Google Ireland's use of Google Analytics and ad personalization tools on websites. The processing involved transferring personal data to the US without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II, affecting French users' data through cookies and tracking technologies. CNIL found that Google's transfer impact assessments did not sufficiently account for US surveillance laws and lacked supplementary measures, despite references to standard contractual clauses.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL issued a €150 million fine to Google for non-compliance with GDPR transfer rules. Similar actions followed against Meta and others. Google modified its tools to include consent banners but continued facing enforcement across Europe.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to Third Countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Biometric Data Scraping 2023
example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) was found to have scraped millions of UK users' biometric data from public posts without explicit consent or impact assessments. The processing involved automated analysis for AI training, raising concerns over special category data handling in large-scale social media environments. The ICO examined Meta's practices post-breach disclosures and user complaints.
process_and_outcome: In November 2023, the ICO issued a £12.5 million fine (proposed, under appeal) for processing biometric data without DPIA or lawful basis. Meta committed to enhanced assessments for future AI projects amid ongoing EU scrutiny.

example_name: Amazon E-Commerce Personalization Fine 2021
example_background: Amazon's EU e-commerce platform used automated profiling for targeted advertising based on vast user data without transparent assessments of risks to consumer rights. The processing involved real-time bidding and behavioral analysis, scrutinized for lack of DPIA documentation despite high-risk nature.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in July 2021 (largest GDPR fine at the time) for transparency and consent failures. Amazon appealed to the Court of Justice of the EU, with proceedings ongoing.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD)

example_name: SAP GDPR Processor Compliance
example_background: SAP, as a major cloud processor, adheres to GDPR Article 40 by participating in the EU Cloud Code of Conduct, requiring DPIAs for processing operations. This includes assessments for ERP systems handling HR and financial data, with approved codes integrated into contracts with controllers like Siemens and others.
process_and_outcome: SAP achieved certification under the EU Cloud Code in 2020 and maintains annual audits, enabling clients to factor compliance into their DPIAs. No major enforcement actions against SAP for GDPR processor failures.
involved_parties: EU Cloud Code of Conduct Supervisory Authorities

example_name: Deloitte HR Platform GDPR Breach
example_background: In early 2017, Deloitte's HR application on Google Workspace platform was misconfigured, exposing data of 200 employees including names, addresses, salaries, and tax details. The issue persisted until 2020 when discovered. Processing was based on Article 6(1)(c) legitimate interests for HR management, subject to UK DPA 2018 implementing GDPR. The ICO noted absence of DPIA for cloud-based HR data processing despite high risks from third-party services.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Deloitte £4,000 (reduced from £80,000) in 2021, considering cooperation. Deloitte fixed configurations, conducted DPIAs, and improved vendor oversight.

example_name: Amazon EPrivacy GDPR Penalty
example_background: Amazon implemented cookie banners without granular consent options, pre-ticking trackers, and processing behavioral data for advertising without proper basis under Article 6(1)(a) consent or (f) interests. The Italian DPA noted absence of DPIA for large-scale tracking across millions of users, despite high-risk profiling.
process_and_outcome: Italian Garante fined Amazon €28 million in 2021. Amazon adjusted cookie mechanisms and conducted impact assessments.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italy)

example_name: Meta Platforms Transfers Invalidated
example_background: Meta (Facebook) transferred EU personal data to the US using Privacy Shield and later Standard Contractual Clauses without supplementary measures. Processing relied on Article 6(1)(b) and (f). The Irish DPC and CJEU found inadequate risk assessment equivalent to lacking DPIA for high-risk international transfers post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated Privacy Shield (2020); Irish DPC ordered suspension of transfers (2023), upheld by EDPB. Meta appealed; fined €1.2 billion in 2023. Implemented new safeguards.

example_name: French Public Sector DPIA Exemption Case
example_background: French law (Loi de Programmation et de Réforme pour la Sécurité Intérieure) provided legal basis under Article 6(1)(c) for video surveillance processing by public authorities. A general impact assessment was integrated into the legislative adoption process, regulating specific operations like ANPR systems.
process_and_outcome: CNIL confirmed exemption from Article 35(1-7) DPIA requirements, allowing processing without additional prior assessment as Member State deemed general assessment sufficient.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach DPIA Review Failure
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 400,000 customers, where payment card details and personal information were accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The breach occurred over several weeks without detection. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) later determined that British Airways had not conducted a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for its web processing operations, nor performed any review of processing activities despite ongoing risks from third-party integrations and known cybersecurity threats in the aviation sector. The company's data processing involved high-risk elements such as tracking technologies and payment processing, which should have triggered periodic risk reassessments under GDPR requirements, but no evidence of such reviews was found prior to the incident.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020, fining British Airways £20 million for infringements of GDPR Articles 5(1)(f), 24, 25(1), 32(1), 32(2), 32(4), and 33. The penalty was reduced from an initial proposed £22 million due to the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on the airline industry. British Airways appealed the decision, but the fine was upheld after review.

example_name: DPO FINE by CNIL for DPIA Non-Review
example_background: A French data protection officer (DPO) of an unnamed company was fined in 2021 by the CNIL after the organization processed sensitive employee health data for HR purposes without a DPIA. Following a complaint, the CNIL investigation revealed that although a DPIA was eventually conducted after the fact, the controller had not reviewed processing risks prior to implementation despite changes in data handling practices, such as new software integrations that altered data flows and retention periods. The processing involved high-risk categories under GDPR, including health data, and no periodic reassessment occurred even after initial setup.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €300 fine directly on the DPO for personal failure to ensure compliance. The company was not fined separately but ordered to regularize practices.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data DPIA Violation
example_background: TikTok's Irish subsidiary processed personal data of up to 1.4 million EU children under 16 without adequate age verification or parental consent mechanisms. The processing involved behavioral profiling and targeted advertising, classified as high-risk. The Irish DPC found that TikTok failed to conduct a DPIA before launching features and did not review risks when introducing changes like the 'Duet' and 'Stitch' functions, which increased data sharing and processing intensity among minors.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC issued a €345 million fine following a three-year investigation. Additional orders required enhanced child protections and DPIA processes.

example_name: Clearview AI DPIA Absence Fine
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites to build a biometric database used for facial recognition services sold to law enforcement and private clients. Processing began without a DPIA, despite involving systematic monitoring and biometric data on an unprecedented scale. Even after regulatory scrutiny emerged in 2020, representing a change in risk level due to enforcement actions across jurisdictions, no review was conducted to assess ongoing processing alignment with data protection requirements.
process_and_outcome: In October 2022, the Dutch DPA fined Clearview €30.5 million; similar fines from Italian, Greek, and French DPAs totaling over €40 million. Banned from offering services in the Netherlands.

example_name: Meta GDPR-Writing Assistance DPIA Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited developed an AI-powered tool to assist EU public bodies in drafting GDPR-related correspondence using public data. The processing involved personal data extraction and analysis without prior DPIA, despite high risks from AI decision-making and novel technology. No review was performed when the project scope expanded to include more data sources, altering the risk profile.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the Irish DPC fined Meta €265 million and ordered cessation of processing.

example_name: H&M Staff Data Monitoring DPIA Breach
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg data center collected and processed detailed notes on over 1,000 employees' private lives, including health issues, family matters, and personal behaviors, shared among managers without employee knowledge. Processing continued for years without a DPIA for this high-risk surveillance activity. When the Hamburg DPA investigated following complaints, it was found that no risk review had been conducted despite changes in monitoring practices, such as digital storage expansions.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, Hamburg DPA fined H&M Justice CV €35.25 million. H&M discontinued the practices and implemented compliance reforms.

example_name: Whistleblower Platform DPIA Success Case
example_background: A European whistleblower platform implemented a system for anonymous reporting of corporate misconduct, involving high-risk processing of sensitive personal data. Prior to launch, a comprehensive DPIA was conducted assessing risks like data breaches and re-identification. Following deployment of new encryption features and increased user volumes in 2022, which changed the risk profile, the controller performed a mandatory review, updating safeguards and residual risk evaluations, ensuring continued alignment with GDPR.
process_and_outcome: The platform underwent a voluntary DPC audit in 2023, which confirmed compliance with DPIA obligations. No enforcement actions were taken, and the review process was highlighted as a model in regulatory guidance.
involved_parties: Whistleblower Platform Operator (e.g., similar to Whistlelink)

example_name: ICO DPIA Review Guidance Compliance Example
example_background: The UK's ICO published detailed case studies of organizations that integrated DPIA reviews into change management processes. One example involved a healthcare provider that conducted initial DPIAs for patient data processing apps. When adopting cloud migration in 2021, representing a risk change due to third-party dependencies, the provider reviewed all active processing operations, reassessed risks, and implemented additional pseudonymization measures.
process_and_outcome: ICO's 2022 accountability audit praised the provider's systematic review process, noting it as aligned with GDPR best practices. No issues identified.

example_name: TikTok GDPR DPIA Fine by ICO
example_background: TikTok, a social media platform operated by ByteDance, processed personal data of up to 1.4 million child users under 13 years of age in the UK without obtaining parental consent or conducting appropriate data protection impact assessments. The platform's practices included age verification methods that were inadequate, allowing children to create accounts easily and access content without safeguards. Data processing involved profiling for targeted advertising and content recommendation algorithms, which raised concerns due to the vulnerability of minors and the scale of data collection. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office identified multiple failures in compliance with data protection principles, particularly around high-risk processing activities involving children's data, where impact assessments were either absent or insufficient to address identified risks.
process_and_outcome: The ICO conducted an investigation following complaints and its own proactive monitoring, culminating in a monetary penalty notice of £12.7 million issued in April 2023. TikTok accepted the findings and committed to implementing changes, including enhanced age assurance measures and DPIA processes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Age verification for information society services)

example_name: Clearview AI DPIA Violation by CNIL
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology by scraping billions of images from public websites without consent, building a database used by law enforcement and private entities. The processing involved biometric data of EU residents, constituting high-risk activities due to the sensitive nature of facial recognition and lack of transparency. The French data protection authority, CNIL, noted that no data protection impact assessment was conducted prior to processing, nor were consultations made with supervisory authorities despite the evident high risks associated with mass surveillance capabilities and potential for misuse.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued injunctions in 2021 ordering cessation of data processing for French residents, followed by a €20 million fine in 2022 for ongoing violations. Similar actions were taken by other EU authorities, leading to broad enforcement across multiple jurisdictions.

example_name: Meta DPIA Failures in Behavioural Advertising
example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook and Instagram, engaged in extensive behavioural advertising based on tracking user data across websites via tools like the Like button and pixel trackers. This involved processing sensitive personal data categories for over 500 million EU users, identified as high-risk due to large-scale profiling and potential discrimination. Internal documents revealed awareness of risks, yet no prior consultations with supervisory authorities occurred despite DPIAs indicating high residual risks after mitigation attempts, such as consent mechanisms deemed invalid.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission issued a €1.2 billion fine in May 2023 after a multi-year investigation coordinated with other EU DPAs under the One-Stop-Shop mechanism. Meta was ordered to suspend data transfers to the US and implement compliance measures.

example_name: H&M Data Processing DPIA Breach
example_background: H&M operated a 'welcome-back room' in its Nuremberg store where employees gathered and shared detailed personal information about colleagues' private lives, including health data, family issues, and vacation details obtained during absences. This data was stored in a central Excel sheet and used for interactions upon return, involving around 100 employees over several years. The processing was deemed high-risk due to the inclusion of special categories of data and lack of employee consent, with no evidence of a data protection impact assessment or consultation with the supervisory authority to mitigate identified risks.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA investigated following employee complaints, imposing a €35.25 million fine in October 2020. H&M discontinued the practice and enhanced data governance training.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: British Airways DPIA and Breach Notification Failure
example_background: British Airways experienced a cyber-attack between June and September 2018, where hackers accessed payment card data of approximately 429,612 customers through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack exploitation. The incident involved high-risk processing of financial data without adequate safeguards. The ICO found that British Airways did not conduct a proper data protection impact assessment for its payment processing systems prior to the breach, failing to identify and mitigate foreseeable risks, and delayed notification to the supervisory authority beyond the 72-hour requirement.
process_and_outcome: ICO issued a £20 million fine in October 2020 after investigation, later reduced on appeal. The airline implemented enhanced security measures and DPIA protocols.

example_name: Marriott International Starwood Breach DPIA Issues
example_background: Marriott acquired Starwood Hotels and integrated its reservation system, which had been compromised since 2014, exposing passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal data of up to 383 million guests worldwide, including 300,000 EU residents. The integration overlooked high-risk data processing elements without conducting a DPIA or consulting authorities, despite the sensitive nature of travel-related data and known vulnerabilities in legacy systems.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 following investigation into the breach notification and risk assessment failures. Marriott enhanced its global privacy program.

example_name: Google AdWords Analytics DPIA Fine by CNIL
example_background: Google placed advertising cookies via its AdWords and Analytics services on third-party websites without valid consent from French users, affecting millions through widespread tracking for personalized ads. The processing was large-scale and involved profiling, qualifying as high-risk, but Google did not perform a data protection impact assessment or seek prior consultation with the supervisory authority, relying instead on insufficient cookie banners.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €150 million in 2022 after investigations prompted by complaints from privacy organizations. Orders were issued for compliance improvements.

example_name: Vodafone DPIA Consultation Success Case
example_background: Vodafone Germany implemented a large-scale customer profiling system for marketing purposes, involving analysis of usage patterns and location data from millions of subscribers. Recognizing high-risk elements due to the scale and potential for intrusive profiling, Vodafone conducted a DPIA which indicated residual high risks post-mitigation, leading to proactive consultation with the Bavarian DPA before full deployment.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian DPA reviewed the consultation, provided recommendations on additional safeguards like enhanced consent and anonymization, which Vodafone implemented. Processing proceeded without enforcement action, serving as a model for compliance.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Amazon France LogFiles DPIA Violation
example_background: Amazon collected and processed IP addresses and other technical data via logfiles on its French website without a legal basis or transparency, impacting all visitors. The activity was deemed high-risk profiling for advertising, but no DPIA was conducted nor was there consultation with authorities despite the extensive data processing infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €32 million fine in 2020 following a complaint-driven investigation. Amazon adjusted its practices in response.

example_name: H&M Data Protection Impact Assessment Fine
example_background: H&M operated a service centre in Germany where over 1,000 employees handled customer service tasks including processing personal data from customer interactions such as complaints, returns, and inquiries. Employees memorized detailed customer information during calls and shared it informally with sales staff in stores to assist with sales processes. This practice involved storing notes on customer preferences, issues, and personal details in a shared system accessible across the company. The data protection authority determined that this processing presented high risks to customer privacy due to the extensive collection and intra-company sharing of sensitive personal information without adequate safeguards. The company conducted a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) but proceeded with processing without prior consultation with the supervisory authority despite the identified high risks, as required when mitigation measures were deemed insufficient.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information investigated following employee reports and media coverage. In October 2020, H&M was fined €35.3 million for multiple GDPR infringements including failure to conduct proper prior consultation after a high-risk DPIA. The authority noted insufficient risk mitigation and ordered changes to data handling practices.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (HmbBfDI)

example_name: Clearview AI Prior Consultation Failure
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology that scraped billions of images from public websites and social media platforms worldwide to create a massive database used by law enforcement and private entities for identification purposes. The processing involved biometric data of millions of individuals without their knowledge or consent, enabling real-time and retrospective identification. European data protection authorities identified this as high-risk processing under GDPR due to the sensitive nature of biometric data and lack of transparency. Despite conducting a DPIA that highlighted significant risks, Clearview AI did not consult supervisory authorities prior to deploying the technology in the EU, nor did it implement sufficient mitigation measures such as data minimization or purpose limitation.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU supervisory authorities coordinated investigations leading to bans and fines. The Dutch DPA banned processing in 2021 and fined €30 million; the French CNIL imposed €20 million in 2022; Greek and Italian authorities added further penalties totaling over €50 million. Clearview was ordered to cease operations in the EU and delete EU residents' data.

example_name: Meta WhatsApp PEPPOL Project Consultation Breach
example_background: Meta's WhatsApp planned to launch a Business Messaging service in Europe integrated with PEPPOL, an e-invoicing network used by public administrations. This involved processing personal data including names, addresses, and invoice details for millions of users to facilitate B2B and B2G communications. The Italian Data Protection Authority identified high risks due to the scale of processing, involvement of public sector entities, and potential for profiling. WhatsApp conducted a DPIA but initiated processing without prior consultation with the supervisory authority, despite the assessment indicating residual high risks after proposed mitigations like encryption and access controls.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante initiated proceedings in 2023 and imposed a €3.3 million fine in 2024 for failure to consult prior to high-risk processing. The authority required suspension of data processing for the project and implementation of additional safeguards.

example_name: British Airways DPIA and Consultation Failure
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber-attack where hackers accessed payment card details, names, addresses, and travel booking information of approximately 400,000 customers through a vulnerability in the website's chat functionality. The breach occurred due to unencrypted transmission of credit card data and weak authentication. The UK ICO later found that British Airways had identified high risks in a DPIA for its payment processing but failed to consult the supervisory authority prior to implementation, as residual risks remained high despite some security measures like firewalls.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO investigated and initially proposed a £183 million fine in 2020, reduced to £22 million in 2021 after appeal considerations. The authority cited failures in DPIA consultation and risk mitigation as key factors.

example_name: Marriott International Hotel Booking Breach
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a reservation database that had been compromised since 2014. The breach exposed passport numbers, payment card details, and travel history of up to 383 million guests worldwide, including 7 million EU residents. The processing involved high-risk elements like special categories of data (passport info) and large-scale profiling for loyalty programs. Marriott's DPIA indicated high risks but no prior consultation occurred with supervisory authorities before continuing operations post-merger.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 (reduced from £99 million on appeal) for failing to perform adequate DPIA consultation and security measures. Similar actions by other EU DPAs followed.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on High-Risk Processing Consultation
example_background: The European Data Protection Board issued guidelines on GDPR Article 36 prior consultation, providing examples where controllers must consult supervisory authorities. One scenario involved large-scale processing of special categories of data for AI training datasets, where DPIAs showed persistent high risks despite mitigations like pseudonymization. The EDPB emphasized the obligation to submit DPIAs and other required information for review when high risks to rights and freedoms remain.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted the guidelines in 2023 after public consultation, establishing best practices. Supervisory authorities reference this in enforcement, promoting proactive consultation to avoid violations.

example_name: Dutch DPA Meta Biometric Data Case
example_background: Meta Platforms processed biometric data through Facebook's photo tagging feature 'DeepFace' across the EU, analyzing facial geometries of hundreds of millions of users without explicit consent or sufficient transparency. The Dutch DPA assessed this as high-risk processing involving special category data on a large scale. Meta's internal DPIA identified risks but the company did not consult the lead supervisory authority (Irish DPC) prior to rolling out the feature, relying on terms acceptance instead of adequate mitigations.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Meta €275 million in 2022 as part of coordinated EDPB actions. Orders included ceasing unlawful processing and enhancing compliance programs.

example_name: Vodafone Italy Age Verification Consultation Success
example_background: Vodafone Italy developed a new customer onboarding platform using AI for age verification and data processing for service provision. Recognizing high risks due to sensitive ID data and automated decision-making, Vodafone conducted a comprehensive DPIA and proactively consulted the Italian Garante before launch, providing details on responsibilities, safeguards like encryption, and DPO contacts.
process_and_outcome: The Garante reviewed within the 8-week period, provided written advice approving the processing with minor adjustments. Launch proceeded in 2022 without enforcement actions, demonstrating effective compliance.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Consultation Rejection
example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology that scraped billions of images from public websites worldwide to build a database used by law enforcement. Prior to launching services in the EU, the company engaged in prior consultation with the French data protection authority (CNIL) under Article 36 GDPR regarding the high-risk processing involved in biometric data handling. The consultation highlighted concerns over the lack of legal basis for scraping images, insufficient risk mitigation measures for fundamental rights impacts, absence of data minimization, and potential for mass surveillance. The processing was deemed to present unacceptable risks due to the irreversible nature of biometric identification and the company's failure to adequately assess proportionality or implement necessary safeguards like purpose limitation and transparency. CNIL received the consultation request and conducted a detailed review, consulting other EU supervisory authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) cooperation mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Within the eight-week period, CNIL issued written advice rejecting the processing plan and prohibiting deployment in France. The authority exercised powers under Article 58, including issuing a formal decision and referring the matter for enforcement. Subsequent EDPB binding decision confirmed the prohibition across EU member states, leading to ongoing investigations and fines totaling over €30 million imposed on Clearview AI by multiple authorities including CNIL (€20m), UK ICO (€7.5m), and others. The company continued operations outside EU compliance, prompting further cross-border enforcement.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Consultation
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, planned to process personal data of minors under 16 through its social media platform features including algorithmic profiling, targeted advertising, and content recommendations. Recognizing the high-risk nature due to vulnerability of children and large-scale profiling, TikTok initiated prior consultation with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) as lead authority under GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism. The consultation submission detailed proposed processing operations, data protection impact assessment (DPIA), risk mitigations like age verification gates, parental consent mechanisms, and default privacy settings. However, the DPC identified gaps in risk identification, particularly around accuracy of age assurance tools, potential for harmful content exposure, insufficient mitigation for psychological impacts, and non-compliance with children's data processing standards.
process_and_outcome: The DPC extended the consultation period by six weeks due to complexity, informing TikTok within one month with reasons including need for additional information from the company. After suspending periods to obtain clarifications, the DPC provided written advice within the total timeframe, advising against the processing as proposed due to unmitigated risks. The authority used Article 58 powers to issue reprimands and ordered suspension of certain features. EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2023 criticizing DPC's initial draft, leading to €345 million fine for children's data violations and mandated redesign of processing activities.

example_name: Meta Public Sector Use of Biometric Data
example_background: Meta Platforms sought to expand its use of biometric recognition technologies for public sector clients, involving processing of facial images from publicly available sources for identity verification purposes. As a high-risk processing under GDPR Annex 1, Meta submitted a prior consultation request to the Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens, AP) alongside its DPIA. The submission outlined data flows, security measures, risk assessments for bias and discrimination, and mitigations including human oversight and audit trails. The AP reviewed the proposal in coordination with other DPAs, noting concerns over legal basis under public task exception, adequacy of risk mitigations for fundamental rights, data retention periods, and cross-border transfers.
process_and_outcome: The AP informed Meta of a six-week extension within one month, citing complexity and need for EDPB input. After obtaining requested information, the authority issued written advice finding insufficient risk mitigation and prohibiting the processing under Article 36(3). Meta was advised to revise its DPIA and resubmit. No fine was issued at consultation stage, but the decision contributed to broader scrutiny leading to temporary bans on similar facial recognition uses.

example_name: UK ICO Consultation on Police Facial Recognition
example_background: South Wales Police planned deployment of live facial recognition technology at public events to identify persons of interest from CCTV feeds, involving real-time biometric processing on a large scale. Recognizing high risks to privacy and non-discrimination rights, the force conducted a DPIA and sought prior consultation with the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) under UK GDPR Article 36 equivalent. The consultation detailed technical specifications, deployment protocols, watchlist curation processes, retention policies, and mitigations such as oversight boards, impact assessments for bias, and transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: The ICO accepted the consultation, conducted a thorough review including site visits and stakeholder input, and provided written advice within eight weeks confirming that risks were sufficiently identified and mitigated through robust safeguards. No Article 58 powers were exercised, and the processing proceeded with ICO monitoring recommendations. This was noted as an example where consultation led to strengthened compliance without prohibition.

example_name: CNIL Rejection of Facial Recognition in Nice Airport
example_background: Société Aéroports du Grand Ouest planned to implement a facial recognition system at Nantes Atlantique Airport for passenger boarding verification, processing biometric data of travelers against passport photos. Deemed high-risk, a prior consultation was submitted to CNIL with DPIA covering data capture, matching algorithms, error handling, and mitigations like opt-out options and data deletion post-processing. CNIL's review revealed inadequate risk assessment for false positives/negatives, lack of necessity justification, insufficient security for biometric templates, and non-compliance with proportionality principles.
process_and_outcome: CNIL extended the period by six weeks, suspended it for additional info, then issued advice prohibiting the processing due to unmitigated risks. The authority exercised Article 58 powers including a formal prohibition decision in 2022. The project was halted, and CNIL published anonymized consultation details for guidance.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Consultation on Health Data Processing
example_background: A consortium of hospitals planned a large-scale AI system for predicting patient health outcomes using electronic health records from multiple EU countries, involving sensitive health data processing. Prior consultation was filed with the lead DPA (Spanish AEPD), detailing pseudonymization techniques, federated learning models, risk mitigations for re-identification, and governance structures. The consultation triggered cross-border cooperation due to multi-state data flows.
process_and_outcome: AEPD coordinated with EDPB; periods were extended and suspended for input. Final advice required enhancements to mitigations but approved the processing with conditions. No prohibition; project proceeded with monitored implementation.

example_name: H&M Staff Monitoring DPIA Consultation
example_background: H&M operated a 'welcome-back room' where HR staff monitored and profiled returning employees' private conversations to gather insights on morale and performance. After internal DPIA, consultation was sought with Hamburg DPA (HmbBfDI) as lead, but review found failure to identify risks to employee privacy, insufficient mitigations for psychological harm, lack of transparency, and disproportionate surveillance.
process_and_outcome: DPA issued advice highlighting infringements and used Article 58 powers post-consultation, resulting in €35.25 million fine in 2023. H&M discontinued the practice and implemented remediation.

example_name: Dutch DPA COVID-19 App Consultation
example_background: Dutch government developed a COVID-19 exposure notification app processing location and proximity data on a large scale during the pandemic. Prior consultation with Dutch DPA included DPIA addressing pseudonymization, voluntary use, data minimization, and end-to-end encryption mitigations for public health risks.
process_and_outcome: DPA reviewed within timeframe, provided advice confirming sufficient mitigations despite high risks, allowing deployment with recommendations for transparency. App launched successfully without further enforcement.

example_name: Italian Garante Rejection of School Facial Recognition
example_background: An Italian school institute planned facial recognition for attendance tracking of students, processing minors' biometrics. Consultation with Garante included DPIA, but authority identified unmitigated risks to children's rights, inaccuracy issues, and lack of parental involvement.
process_and_outcome: Garante prohibited processing via Article 36 advice and Article 58 decision in 2022, halting deployment.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Decision 2022
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated the use of Google Analytics by websites in France. Several organizations embedded Google Analytics cookies without adequate consent mechanisms, leading to transfers of IP addresses and user IDs to Google LLC in the US. The processing involved tracking user behavior for analytics purposes. CNIL found that organizations did not sufficiently inform users about data transfers or obtain valid consent, and failed to provide DPIAs for high-risk tracking activities. Controllers relied on Google as a processor for data analytics within their web services.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL issued formal notices to non-compliant websites, ordering cessation of data transfers without safeguards within one month. Several entities adjusted their configurations to comply. No immediate fines were imposed, but ongoing monitoring continued.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés, supervisory authority)

example_name: European Data Protection Board Binding Decision on Meta 2023
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland conducted a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for its behavioral advertising model, processing vast amounts of EU user data for personalized ads. The DPIA identified high residual risks to users' rights and freedoms. Meta consulted the Irish DPC as required. However, the DPC determined the risks were not sufficiently mitigated by proposed safeguards like pseudonymization and access controls. Other EU supervisory authorities challenged the DPC's initial draft decision via the dispute resolution procedure. Meta's processing involved joint controllership with Facebook entities.
process_and_outcome: In April 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision requiring Meta to suspend processing personal data for behavioral advertising until compliance. A separate €1.2 billion fine was imposed for transfers. Meta appealed and adjusted practices.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, supervisory authority)

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine for Employee Monitoring 2023
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center monitored employees' private conversations, including sick leave discussions and personal issues, which were noted in knowledge databases used for performance management. The monitoring occurred from 2014 to 2019. Bavarian DPA investigated after employee complaints, finding the processing lacked lawful basis and involved high risks without DPIA. H&M Sweden AB was the controller, with local HR as processors. The company did not consult the authority proactively.
process_and_outcome: In October 2023, the Bavarian DPA fined H&M €35.25 million. The company discontinued the practice and deleted the data.
involved_parties: H&M Software Services Eastern Europe SRL (processor)
Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA, supervisory authority)

example_name: Deutsche Telekom GDPR Corrective Measure 2021
example_background: Deutsche Telekom processed customer data for marketing without proper consent and conducted high-risk processing chains involving multiple affiliates without comprehensive DPIA. The company consulted the BfDI after internal risk identification for targeted advertising using geolocation and browsing data.
process_and_outcome: The BfDI issued a reprimand and corrective order in 2021, requiring DPIA revisions and safeguard implementations. No fine was levied due to proactive consultation. Telekom updated its group-wide processing policies.
involved_parties: Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI, supervisory authority)

example_name: Vodafone Germany DPIA Consultation Success
example_background: Vodafone conducted a DPIA for a new IoT platform processing location data from millions of devices for fleet management and smart city services. Identifying high risks, the company consulted the BfDI, providing details on joint controller responsibilities with device manufacturers, processing purposes, encryption safeguards, DPO contacts, and the full DPIA report.
process_and_outcome: The BfDI authorized the launch in 2022 after reviewing submissions, confirming adequate mitigations. The platform rolled out without further incidents.
involved_parties: Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz (BfDI, supervisory authority)

example_name: French CNIL Consultation on National Data Processing Law
example_background: In 2018, the French National Assembly prepared a legislative proposal for a law on personal data processing in the context of national security and public order. The proposal included provisions for data retention and access by authorities. Prior to adoption, the French data protection authority, CNIL, was formally consulted to review the measures' alignment with data protection principles. CNIL provided a detailed opinion highlighting potential issues with proportionality, data minimization, and individual rights safeguards in the proposed processing activities. This consultation process involved multiple rounds of feedback between the legislative drafters and CNIL experts, ensuring that the final law incorporated adjustments to processing scopes and enhanced oversight mechanisms. The engagement allowed for early identification of compliance gaps and integration of privacy by design principles into the legislative framework.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a formal opinion in March 2018, recommending amendments. The National Assembly revised the proposal accordingly, and the law was adopted in June 2018 with incorporated changes. No enforcement actions followed as the consultation led to compliant adjustments.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: German Conference of Data Protection Authorities Consultation on Federal Police Data Law
example_background: Germany's Federal Government drafted a 2021 amendment to the Federal Police Act introducing new automated processing capabilities for passenger data and vehicle tracking to combat crime. Before parliamentary submission, the federal and state data protection authorities were consulted under GDPR requirements. The authorities conducted a comprehensive review, issuing a joint statement that analyzed the necessity, proportionality, and technical safeguards of the proposed processing operations. Their input addressed concerns over bulk data collection, retention periods, and access controls, influencing the legislative text through proposed modifications to limit processing scopes and mandate stricter logging requirements.
process_and_outcome: Joint opinion delivered in May 2021 led to revisions in the draft. The Bundestag adopted the amended law in July 2021, incorporating authority recommendations. Subsequent monitoring by authorities ensured ongoing compliance.
involved_parties: German Conference of Data Protection Authorities (DSK)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Consultation on Organic Law for Data Protection Adaptation
example_background: Following GDPR adoption, Spain prepared the Organic Law 3/2018 to adapt national legislation to EU requirements. The Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) was consulted extensively during the drafting phase in 2018. The consultation covered processing rules for public administrations, health data, and biometric processing. AEPD provided input on aligning national measures with GDPR, suggesting enhancements to rights enforcement mechanisms, impact assessments, and supervisory powers. This process involved workshops, written submissions, and iterative reviews, ensuring the law supported GDPR's framework while addressing Spain-specific contexts like regional autonomies.
process_and_outcome: AEPD's contributions were integrated into the final text. The law was approved by Congress in December 2018 and entered into force in 2019, facilitating smooth GDPR transposition without identified gaps.
involved_parties: AEPD (Agencia Española de Protección de Datos)

example_name: Italian Garante Consultation on Public Security Data Processing Decree
example_background: In 2020, the Italian Council of Ministers prepared a decree-law on public security measures involving extensive processing of personal data for immigration and crime prevention. The Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali was consulted prior to parliamentary review. The authority examined the decree's provisions on data collection from online platforms and retention practices, providing observations on legal basis adequacy, purpose limitation, and international transfers. Feedback included recommendations for pseudonymization techniques and periodic reviews, shaping the decree's final processing parameters.
process_and_outcome: Garante opinion issued in July 2020 prompted clarifications. The decree was converted into law in September 2020 with modifications. No subsequent challenges arose from the process.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: Dutch CBP Consultation on Passenger Name Record Directive Implementation
example_background: The Netherlands implemented EU Directive 2016/681 on PNR data through national legislation in 2018. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (CBP, now part of AP) was consulted during preparation. The consultation focused on processing large-scale travel data for law enforcement, reviewing retention periods, filtering mechanisms, and depersonalization processes. CBP's analysis led to adjustments ensuring compatibility with GDPR, including mandatory data protection impact assessments and enhanced oversight by the authority.
process_and_outcome: Consultation report issued in 2017 influenced the legislative proposal. The law was enacted in May 2018, with CBP appointed as supervisory body.
involved_parties: Dutch Ministry of Justice and Security

example_name: Polish UODO Consultation on Video Surveillance in Public Spaces Regulation
example_background: Poland's Ministry of Interior drafted a 2022 regulation on video surveillance systems operated by public authorities, detailing processing rules for CCTV footage. The President of the Personal Data Protection Office (UODO) was consulted beforehand. The review covered data collection scopes, storage durations, and access rights, with UODO recommending privacy notices, access logs, and DPIA requirements. This input refined the regulation to balance security needs with data subject protections.
process_and_outcome: UODO opinion in March 2022 led to amendments. Regulation adopted in June 2022, enabling compliant deployment.

example_name: Swedish IMY Consultation on Health Data Processing Legislation
example_background: Sweden prepared amendments to its health data laws in 2021 to facilitate secondary processing for research. The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) was consulted on the proposal, which involved pseudonymized patient data sharing across institutions. IMY assessed legal bases, consent alternatives, and security measures, suggesting governance structures and audit requirements. The consultation ensured the measures met GDPR standards for sensitive data handling.
process_and_outcome: IMY provided input in October 2021, incorporated into the bill. Legislation passed in 2022.

example_name: Austrian DSB Consultation on eGovernment Data Processing Act
example_background: Austria's Federal Ministry for Digital and Economic Affairs developed the 2020 eGovernment Act expansion, introducing new processing for digital services. The Austrian Data Protection Authority (DSB) was consulted, reviewing provisions on data interoperability, profiling, and citizen data portals. DSB's feedback addressed transparency, purpose binding, and rectification rights, leading to refined technical standards and supervisory roles.
process_and_outcome: Consultation in 2019 resulted in adjustments. Act entered force in 2020.
involved_parties: Austrian Federal Ministry for Digital Affairs

example_name: German Federal Commissioner Investigation of Deutsche Wohnen
example_background: Deutsche Wohnen, one of Germany's largest real estate companies, manages properties and processes personal data of millions of tenants, including sensitive data such as health-related information for accessibility accommodations and criminal record checks for tenant screening. Its core activities involve large-scale processing of special categories of data under GDPR Article 9 and data relating to criminal convictions under Article 10. In 2019, the Berlin Data Protection Commissioner investigated the company following complaints and found no designated DPO despite the mandatory requirement due to the scale and nature of data processing, which included regular monitoring of tenant behaviors through smart building systems and extensive databases.
process_and_outcome: The authority ordered Deutsche Wohnen to appoint a DPO immediately and imposed administrative measures. No monetary fine was publicly detailed, but the company complied by designating a DPO and reporting contact details.
involved_parties: Berlin Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 37(1)(b) and (c) (Designation of DPO for large-scale processing)

example_name: CNIL Fine Against Facebook France
example_background: Facebook France, part of Meta Platforms, processes vast amounts of personal data from hundreds of millions of users in the EU, involving regular and systematic monitoring on a large scale through behavioral advertising and platform analytics. In 2021, the French data protection authority CNIL conducted an investigation into compliance with GDPR obligations, including DPO designation. Although Meta had appointed a group DPO, the French subsidiary had not properly published accessible contact details for each establishment nor ensured the DPO's effective involvement in French-specific processing operations, as required for groups of undertakings.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €60 million fine in December 2021, partly for DPO-related failures alongside cookie consent issues. Meta updated its DPO communications and structures across EU entities.

example_name: DPO Appointment by Siemens Healthineers
example_background: Siemens Healthineers, a division of Siemens AG specializing in medical technology, processes large-scale special categories of personal data including health data from patient records, medical imaging, and diagnostic systems across EU operations. Recognizing its core activities under GDPR Article 37(1)(c), the company proactively designated a group DPO in 2018 prior to GDPR enforcement, ensuring the DPO had expert knowledge in data protection law and healthcare-specific practices. The DPO's contact details were published on the company website and communicated to all relevant EU supervisory authorities, with provisions for accessibility from each establishment.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions were taken; the company passed multiple GDPR audits and was cited in industry reports as a compliance leader. The DPO structure supported successful data breach responses and privacy by design implementations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 37(1)(c) (DPO for large-scale special categories data)

example_name: Vodafone Group DPO Compliance Structure
example_background: Vodafone Group, a multinational telecommunications company, handles massive volumes of personal data from customer subscriptions, location tracking, and usage analytics, involving systematic monitoring on a large scale. In preparation for GDPR, Vodafone appointed a single group DPO in 2017, based on professional expertise in telecom data protection. The DPO's details were published centrally and on local EU subsidiary websites, ensuring easy accessibility from each establishment, and communicated to all relevant supervisory authorities. The structure supported compliance across 22 countries.
process_and_outcome: Vodafone received no major GDPR fines related to DPO issues and was recognized in compliance benchmarks. The DPO played a key role in privacy program management and regulatory engagements.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors GDPR DPO Designation
example_background: SAP SE, through its SuccessFactors HR cloud service, processes personal data of millions of employees worldwide for clients, including special categories like health data in HR contexts. As a processor whose core activities involve large-scale processing, SAP designated a group DPO with expertise in cloud data protection and SaaS models before GDPR took effect. Contact details are published and accessible via service contracts and websites, with communication to authorities.
process_and_outcome: SAP maintained compliance through EDPB audits and client certifications like EU Cloud Code of Conduct. No enforcement actions for DPO failures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 37(1)(c) (Processor DPO for special data)

example_name: Deloitte Ireland GDPR DPO Designation
example_background: Deloitte Ireland, as a professional services firm, processes large volumes of personal data in its core consulting, audit, and advisory activities, including client employee data and sensitive information for GDPR compliance services. Recognizing the scale and systematic nature of its data processing operations, the firm proactively designated a Data Protection Officer to oversee compliance across its European operations.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte published details of its DPO on its website and integrated the role into its data governance framework, enabling effective handling of data subject requests and breach responses without regulatory enforcement.

example_name: Bavarian Data Protection Authority v. Planet49
example_background: Planet49, a German online services company, operated consent management platforms processing personal data from website visitors on a large scale through cookie banners and tracking for marketing purposes. Its core activities involved systematic monitoring of data subjects across multiple client websites. The Bavarian authority investigated DPO designation adequacy amid broader consent practices.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on consent validity in 2019, and the authority imposed a €20,000 fine in 2020 for related violations, highlighting the need for proper DPO oversight in large-scale monitoring.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Deutsche Telekom DPO Compliance
example_background: Deutsche Telekom, a major telecommunications provider, engages in core activities of processing large-scale personal data for customer communications, billing, and network usage analytics, including regular monitoring of data subjects' online and mobile activities across millions of subscribers. The company designated a DPO to manage these operations in line with GDPR requirements.
process_and_outcome: Deutsche Telekom published DPO contact details and demonstrated effective data protection through internal audits and cooperation with authorities, avoiding enforcement actions related to DPO absence.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI)

example_name: Accenture GDPR DPO Appointment
example_background: Accenture, a global consulting firm, handles large-scale processing of special categories of personal data, such as health and financial information, in its core HR, client advisory, and technology services across Europe. The firm appointed a DPO to oversee compliance with processing requirements for sensitive data.
process_and_outcome: Accenture integrated the DPO into its global privacy program, enabling successful GDPR audits and transparent data handling practices without regulatory penalties.

example_name: French Public Authorities DPO Sharing Arrangement
example_background: Several French regional public authorities, including departments and municipalities, established a shared data protection officer service to handle GDPR compliance across multiple entities. This setup was designed to optimize resources given the similar organizational structures and sizes of these public bodies. The arrangement was formalized through inter-authority agreements, allowing a single DPO to oversee data protection activities such as processing personal data for citizen services, administrative records, and public health initiatives. The DPO provided advice on data processing operations, monitored compliance, and acted as the point of contact for data subjects and supervisory authorities. This model was implemented starting from the GDPR's enforcement date in May 2018 and has been maintained to address budget constraints in smaller public administrations while ensuring consistent application of data protection principles across the entities.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority CNIL reviewed and approved the shared DPO designation as compliant with GDPR provisions. No enforcement actions were taken, and the arrangement has been cited as a model for other public bodies. Periodic audits confirmed effective functioning of the shared DPO role.
involved_parties: Multiple French regional departments and municipalities

example_name: Italian Municipalities Shared DPO Initiative
example_background: A consortium of small municipalities in Italy's Lombardy region designated a single data protection officer to serve 15 local public authorities. These entities process personal data for services like resident registration, social welfare, and local taxation. The decision was based on their comparable sizes (populations under 10,000 each) and aligned organizational structures under regional governance. The shared DPO was appointed in 2019, conducting risk assessments, training staff, and handling data breach notifications for all participating bodies. This approach reduced individual costs from approximately €50,000 per year to a shared €150,000 annually, while maintaining dedicated support through scheduled visits and a centralized helpdesk.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) validated the arrangement following a formal notification and conducted follow-up inspections in 2021, confirming adequate coverage and no gaps in responsibilities. The model was expanded to additional municipalities.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: UK NHS Trusts Joint DPO Designation
example_background: Several National Health Service (NHS) trusts in England, operating hospitals and community health services, appointed a single data protection officer for their group. These public bodies handle vast amounts of sensitive health data for patient care, research, and administrative purposes. The designation accounted for their integrated organizational structure within the NHS framework and similar operational scales. Implemented post-GDPR transition via the Data Protection Act 2018, the DPO oversaw data protection impact assessments, staff training programs reaching over 5,000 employees, and coordination with the National Data Guardian for health data flows.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) acknowledged the joint appointment as appropriate during routine compliance checks. The trusts received guidance endorsements and no corrective measures were required.

example_name: Spanish Autonomous Communities DPO Consolidation
example_background: Public administrations in Spain's Andalusia region consolidated data protection officers for various regional departments, including education, health, and social services. This single DPO served multiple bodies due to their hierarchical structure under the regional government and proportional sizes. The setup processes personal data for public registries, citizen portals, and service delivery. Established in 2018, the DPO managed over 200 data processing activities annually, provided legal advice on transfers, and liaised with the Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD).
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued a positive opinion on the designation and included it in guidance materials for public sector entities. Annual reports demonstrated effective monitoring without incidents.

example_name: German Länder Public Bodies Shared DPO
example_background: In the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia, several state-level public authorities such as ministries of interior and finance designated one data protection officer for coordinated oversight. These entities process data for public administration, law enforcement support, and citizen services, with structures aligned under state government. The shared DPO, appointed considering size and organization, handles tasks like DPIAs for digital services and training for 10,000+ public servants.
process_and_outcome: The state data protection authority reviewed and approved the model, issuing no findings in subsequent audits.

example_name: Belgian Local Governments DPO Pooling
example_background: A group of 20 Belgian municipalities formed a joint data protection service with a single DPO to cover their administrative data processing for civil registry, urban planning, and public welfare. Their similar small-to-medium sizes and federated structure justified the pooling. Launched in 2019, the DPO conducted joint awareness campaigns, managed a shared incident response protocol, and advised on GDPR-compliant digital transformations.
process_and_outcome: The Belgian Data Protection Authority (APD) confirmed compliance in a 2020 opinion and highlighted it as efficient practice.

example_name: Swedish Municipalities DPO Collaboration
example_background: Ten Swedish municipalities in the Stockholm area designated a shared data protection officer for handling personal data in education, elderly care, and local governance. The decision reflected their comparable organizational setups and population sizes. The DPO supported implementation of e-services, data minimization in records, and coordination with the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish DPA approved the arrangement and noted it in public sector guidance documents.

example_name: Polish Voivodeship Offices Single DPO
example_background: Poland's regional voivodeship offices (provincial administrations) implemented a single data protection officer across 16 offices, considering their uniform structure under the central government and similar staffing levels. The DPO oversees data for administrative decisions, subsidies, and public registries, providing centralized training and policy development.
process_and_outcome: The President of the Personal Data Protection Office endorsed the model as fitting the regulation.

example_name: H&M Data Protection Officer Accessibility Fine
example_background: In 2020, the Portuguese data protection authority investigated H&M's data processing operations in Portugal as part of a group-wide structure where a single data protection officer was appointed for multiple undertakings across Europe. The investigation revealed that the designated data protection officer, based outside Portugal, was not easily accessible to the local establishment. Employees in Portugal faced difficulties in contacting the DPO due to time zone differences, language barriers, and lack of a local contact point or dedicated communication channels. The DPO's contact details were listed on the company website, but practical accessibility was limited, with response times exceeding several days and no provision for urgent inquiries during local business hours. This setup affected approximately 500 employees in the Portuguese subsidiary who processed personal data for HR and customer management purposes. The authority conducted an on-site audit and reviewed internal records, emails, and employee feedback, confirming that the arrangement did not meet operational accessibility requirements despite the group-level appointment.
process_and_outcome: The Portuguese National Data Protection Commission (CNPD) issued a decision fining H&M €456,000 for non-compliance with GDPR requirements on DPO accessibility. The company was ordered to appoint a locally accessible DPO contact and implement improved communication protocols within 30 days. H&M complied with the remediation measures without appeal.

example_name: British Airways GDPR DPO Fine
example_background: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined British Airways' data protection structure following a major data breach in 2018 affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details. As part of the group's compliance framework, British Airways had appointed a single data protection officer for its international operations, including subsidiaries in multiple EU countries. The DPO was located at the UK headquarters, and while contact details were publicly available, the ICO found that accessibility from non-UK establishments was inadequate. Subsidiary offices in Ireland and other locations reported delays in DPO responses due to centralized handling without regional deputies. Internal logs showed average response times of 72 hours for data subject requests from overseas teams, impacting timely handling of rights exercises under GDPR. The investigation involved interviews with 200 staff members and review of 5,000 internal tickets.
process_and_outcome: The ICO imposed a £20 million fine primarily for the breach but included findings on DPO accessibility issues, requiring British Airways to establish regional DPO access points. The airline enhanced its structure and avoided further penalties.

example_name: Vodafone Portugal DPO Fine
example_background: Vodafone Group appointed a central DPO for its Portuguese operations in 2019, with the officer located in Spain. The Portuguese CNPD initiated an inquiry after complaints from employees about inaccessible DPO support for data processing activities involving millions of customer records. The DPO was reachable only via a shared email and a Spanish phone line, leading to language issues and response delays averaging 4-5 days. No local office hours or video call provisions were available, affecting compliance with data subject access requests processed by 2,000 Portuguese staff. The authority inspected premises, analyzed 1,200 support tickets, and interviewed department heads.
process_and_outcome: CNPD fined Vodafone €400,000 in 2021 and mandated appointment of a locally accessible DPO within 60 days. Vodafone implemented a hybrid model with local deputies.

example_name: IKEA Belgium DPO Accessibility Decision
example_background: IKEA's Ingka Group designated a single DPO for its Belgian and Dutch stores, located in the Netherlands. The Belgian Data Protection Authority (GBA) reviewed the setup during a 2022 routine audit of retail data processing for loyalty programs involving 10 million customer profiles. Findings indicated that Belgian store managers and HR teams experienced challenges accessing the DPO due to cross-border logistics, with no dedicated Belgian contact number and email responses taking 3-7 days. This delayed handling of 15% of data subject requests. The audit included site visits to 20 stores and review of internal compliance metrics.
process_and_outcome: GBA imposed a €200,000 fine and required IKEA to ensure easy accessibility through local channels by Q3 2022. IKEA added regional support lines.

example_name: Siemens Group DPO Best Practice
example_background: Siemens AG, a multinational conglomerate, implemented a group-wide single DPO model in 2018 for its 300+ EU establishments. To ensure accessibility, the Germany-based DPO was supplemented by 15 regional coordinators, each assigned to clusters of countries, providing local language support, dedicated phone lines, and office hours aligned with local time zones. Monthly training sessions and a 24/7 ticketing portal enabled response times under 24 hours.
