example_name: EU Cloud Code of Conduct Certification by EuroPriSe
example_background: EuroPriSe, an accredited certification body under GDPR, issued certifications to cloud service providers participating in the EU Cloud Code of Conduct. The body demonstrated its expertise through rigorous audits of providers' data protection measures, including data processing agreements, security controls, and compliance with Article 42 transfer criteria. Accreditation was granted by the German supervisory authority after verifying independence, conflict-free structures, and public complaint procedures. Over 20 providers achieved certification after detailed assessments spanning technical audits and documentation reviews.
process_and_outcome: Certifications were issued and renewed for qualifying providers, with EuroPriSe providing reasons to the supervisory authority. No withdrawals occurred, establishing a model for sector-wide adherence.

example_name: BSI GDPR Certification Scheme Accreditation
example_background: The German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) accredited certification bodies for its GDPR-based data protection certification scheme. Bodies underwent evaluation for expertise in privacy by design, independence from audited entities, and establishment of withdrawal procedures. The scheme covers IT products and services, with requirements approved by the German supervisory authority and the EDPB. Accreditation processes included demonstrations of complaint handling transparency and five-year validity periods with renewal options.
process_and_outcome: Multiple bodies received accreditation, enabling issuance of over 100 certifications. BSI monitors compliance continuously, with public disclosure of criteria.

example_name: CNIL Refusal of Cloud Provider Certification Renewal
example_background: A French cloud service provider sought renewal of its data protection certification from a CNIL-accredited body. During the periodic review, the certification body identified gaps in the provider's implementation of certified measures, including inadequate data subject rights handling and insufficient transparency in processing activities. The body assessed compliance with original certification criteria under Article 42(5), notifying CNIL as required. This case highlighted the supervisory role of authorities in overseeing certification validity.
process_and_outcome: CNIL supported the non-renewal decision after review, requiring the provider to remediate issues before reapplication. No fine was imposed on the certification body.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Certification Body Requirements 1/2020
example_background: The European Data Protection Board issued an opinion specifying requirements for certification bodies under Article 43, following requests from national authorities. The opinion detailed independence tests, conflict of interest mitigations, and procedural obligations for handling complaints and withdrawals. It addressed accreditation by supervisory authorities or national bodies under Regulation 765/2008, influencing multiple member states' implementation. Referenced in ongoing accreditation processes across the EU.
process_and_outcome: Requirements adopted by several supervisory authorities, facilitating new accreditations and standardizing practices without enforcement actions.

example_name: Swedish IMY Accreditation of Data Protection Certification Bodies
example_background: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) accredited certification bodies for issuing GDPR seals, verifying compliance with Article 43 criteria including expertise demonstration, public procedures for reviews and complaints, and absence of conflicts. Bodies were required to submit operational structures for approval, with IMY exercising powers under Article 58 to oversee issuance. This supported certifications for Swedish processors in health and finance sectors.
process_and_outcome: Accreditations granted for three-year terms with renewals, promoting certified compliance tools in Sweden.

example_name: ISO 27701 Certification by ANSSI-Accredited Bodies
example_background: French cybersecurity agency ANSSI oversaw accreditation of bodies issuing ISO/IEC 27701 certifications, aligned with GDPR Article 43 mechanisms. Bodies proved expertise in privacy information management, established withdrawal protocols, and transparent complaint systems. Used for processors handling sensitive data, with supervisory authorities informed prior to issuance.
process_and_outcome: Certifications issued to numerous entities, with ANSSI ensuring adherence through periodic audits.

example_name: Potential Revocation Warning by Italian Garante
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) issued a warning to an accredited certification body after discovering undeclared consulting services to a certified controller, raising conflict of interest concerns under Article 43(2). The body had issued certifications without fully disclosing relationships, prompting a review of all active seals.
process_and_outcome: Body implemented remedial measures, avoiding revocation but under enhanced monitoring. Affected certifications underwent re-audits.

example_name: Dutch AP Approval of Privacy Keurmerk Certification
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) approved the Privacy Keurmerk scheme and accredited bodies to issue certifications. Bodies demonstrated procedures for issuance, review, withdrawal, and complaints, with public transparency. Applied to marketing and HR data processors, involving detailed audits and supervisory notifications.
process_and_outcome: Scheme operational with multiple certifications issued, renewed under five-year accreditation cycles.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Breach Certification Implications
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 400,000 customers' personal data, including payment card details, names, and addresses. The breach occurred due to a vulnerability in the website's third-party tracking service, which allowed unauthorized access over several weeks. Following the incident, the company undertook remediation efforts and implemented enhanced security measures. The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated the matter extensively, considering factors such as the duration of the breach, the volume of data compromised, and the company's response. This case raised questions about ongoing compliance with data protection standards, including those related to certification mechanisms under GDPR, as the breach highlighted potential failures in maintaining certified security criteria over time.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice of £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million draft) in October 2020. British Airways appealed the decision to the First-tier Tribunal, which in 2022 upheld the fine but reduced it to £4.4 million based on mitigating factors including the impact of COVID-19 on the business. The company also faced a separate £7.5 million fine from the Payment Card Industry Security Standards Council for PCI DSS non-compliance related to the same incident.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Fine for Data Breach
example_background: Marriott International acquired Starwood Hotels and Resorts in 2016, inheriting a reservation database that had been compromised since 2014. The breach exposed passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal data of up to 383 million guests worldwide. Marriott detected the issue in 2018 during a security sweep and notified affected individuals. The prolonged nature of the breach, spanning years across ownership changes, underscored challenges in maintaining consistent data protection standards, particularly in merged entities where certification-like assurances on security controls are expected to persist.
process_and_outcome: The ICO imposed a £18.4 million fine in 2020, later reduced on appeal. Additional fines came from other regulators, totaling over €100 million across EU authorities. Marriott invested significantly in remediation, including system segmentation and monitoring enhancements.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 5 (Principles relating to processing of personal data)

example_name: Schrems II and Standard Contractual Clauses Revocation Implications
example_background: The Schrems II judgment by the Court of Justice of the EU invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield framework in July 2020, affecting thousands of companies relying on it for data transfers. Max Schrems challenged Facebook's transfers, highlighting deficiencies in US surveillance laws. Companies had to urgently adopt supplementary measures, demonstrating how certification mechanisms must be adaptable and renewed to meet evolving criteria.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled Privacy Shield invalid; companies transitioned to Standard Contractual Clauses with addendums. Irish DPC issued subsequent orders and fines to Facebook/Meta.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU)

example_name: ISO 27701 Certification Renewal by Microsoft
example_background: Microsoft achieved certification under ISO/IEC 27701, the privacy information management extension to ISO 27001, in 2020. This certification covers its cloud services and involves annual surveillance audits and full re-certification every three years to verify continued adherence to privacy controls aligned with GDPR requirements.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft successfully renewed its certification through audits confirming ongoing compliance. The process includes continuous monitoring and improvement of privacy practices across Azure, Office 365, and other services.

example_name: SAP GDPR Certification under EuroPriSe
example_background: SAP obtained EuroPriSe certification for its data processing services in 2021, a GDPR-specific scheme approved by the German supervisory authority. The certification is valid for three years, subject to annual attestations and full reassessment, ensuring SAP's solutions meet data protection criteria for controllers and processors continuously.
process_and_outcome: Certification granted and maintained through successful audits, with SAP demonstrating fulfillment of all criteria via technical and organizational measures.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection (BfDI)

example_name: Cloudflare GDPR Processor Certification
example_background: Cloudflare, a web infrastructure provider, earned EuroPriSe certification as a data processor in 2022 for services like CDN and DDoS protection. The three-year certification requires proof of ongoing compliance through documented processes, audits, and transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: Initial certification and first-year surveillance audit passed, positioning Cloudflare as GDPR-compliant for EU customers.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Certifications (Compliance Example)
example_background: The European Data Protection Board issued guidelines in 2020 on GDPR certifications and seals, providing a framework for controllers to maintain and publicly disclose registers of certification mechanisms. Organizations adopting these have demonstrated structured approaches to transparency.
process_and_outcome: The guidelines promote best practices, with adopting entities avoiding enforcement by maintaining public registers. No specific fines tied to this, but it serves as a compliance model referenced in subsequent audits.

example_name: ISO 27701 Certification by Siemens
example_background: Siemens implemented the ISO/IEC 27701 privacy information management extension to ISO 27001, creating a public register of its data protection certifications and seals. This includes details on compliance mechanisms for GDPR-aligned processing across its global operations.
process_and_outcome: Siemens achieved certification in 2020 and maintains a public privacy portal listing seals and mechanisms, enabling transparency and avoiding GDPR enforcement actions related to certification disclosure.

example_name: Schrems II Impact on CLOUD Act Certifications
example_background: Following the CJEU's Schrems II ruling in July 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield, many US cloud providers relied on uncertified mechanisms for data transfers. Providers like Microsoft and AWS faced scrutiny for using standard contractual clauses without supplementary measures against US CLOUD Act access requests.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs, including Austrian DSB, issued injunctions. In 2023, no major certifications under GDPR Article 42 were prominently accredited for such transfers, leading providers to adopt EU-approved codes of conduct instead.

example_name: ISO 27701 Certification by IBM
example_background: IBM obtained certification to ISO/IEC 27701:2019, an extension to ISO 27001 for privacy information management systems, aligning with GDPR requirements. In 2020, IBM's cloud services were audited and certified, demonstrating controls for data processing, consent management, and transfer mechanisms across its global operations serving EU clients.
process_and_outcome: Accredited by multiple bodies including BSI, IBM renewed certification in 2023. This enabled clients to use IBM services under GDPR-compliant data processing agreements, with no major enforcement actions against IBM for certification-related issues.

example_name: TrustArc GDPR Certification Approval
example_background: TrustArc, a privacy certification body, received approval from the Irish DPC in 2022 for its GDPR Privacy Management Certification under Article 42. The program certifies organizations' privacy practices, including data mapping, consent tools, and transfer mechanisms, after rigorous audits by accredited assessors.
process_and_outcome: The DPC informed and approved the scheme after review. Over 20 organizations, including major tech firms, achieved certification by 2023, demonstrating adherence through annual renewals and audits.

example_name: SAP GDPR Certification Mechanism
example_background: SAP SE developed and obtained approval for a GDPR-aligned certification mechanism under Article 42, focusing on cloud services data processing. The process involved defining criteria for security, transparency, and accountability, audited by an accredited certification body. SAP's SuccessFactors and other SaaS offerings were certified, providing customers assurance on data handling practices including transfers and subprocessors.
process_and_outcome: SAP received certification approval from the German supervisory authority in 2022, with ongoing renewals every three years. This enables binding corporate rules and customer reliance on certified compliance.

example_name: Schrems II Impact on Certified Transfers
example_background: Following the CJEU's Schrems II judgment in July 2020, numerous companies relied on Privacy Shield certification for US data transfers, which was invalidated. Organizations like Oracle and others holding Privacy Shield certifications had to urgently reassess and migrate to standard contractual clauses with supplementary measures, affecting thousands of EU-US data flows.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Commerce suspended Privacy Shield listings, prompting investigations by EU regulators into ongoing transfers. Multiple fines issued, such as against Oracle by CNIL in 2023 for €12 million.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: CNIL Enforcement Against CNIL-Approved Certification Body
example_background: In 2020, the French data protection authority (CNIL) conducted an investigation into a certification body that had been previously approved by CNIL for issuing GDPR certifications. The body was found to have issued certifications to controllers and processors without properly verifying ongoing compliance with the certification criteria after initial approval. The procedures for periodic review were documented but not implemented effectively, leading to certifications remaining active despite changes in the certified entities' data processing practices. Additionally, the body lacked structured mechanisms for handling complaints from data subjects regarding the implementation of certifications by certified organizations. The supervisory authority determined that the certification body had not maintained the required independence as its staff had prior consulting relationships with some certified entities, raising questions about potential conflicts of interest. This case highlighted gaps in the operationalization of GDPR certification requirements in practice.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a fine of €250,000 on the certification body following the investigation. The body was required to revise its procedures for reviews, complaints handling, and conflict management, and to notify CNIL of all corrective actions within three months. Public reprimand was also issued.

example_name: EuroPriSe Certification Scheme Approval by WP29
example_background: EuroPriSe (European Privacy Seal) is a data protection certification mechanism developed prior to GDPR finalization and submitted to the Article 29 Working Party (WP29) for approval as a GDPR-compliant scheme. The scheme established detailed criteria for certifying data processing activities, including requirements for controllers and processors to demonstrate compliance with GDPR principles. EuroPriSe accreditation process for its bodies involved demonstrating independence through separation of auditing from consulting services, establishing formal procedures for issuing, reviewing every two years, and withdrawing seals based on non-compliance findings. Complaint handling structures were set up with a dedicated portal accessible to data subjects and public, allowing investigations into certified entities' implementation. The supervisory authorities verified absence of conflicts by reviewing organizational structures and staff declarations.
process_and_outcome: WP29 approved EuroPriSe as a GDPR certification mechanism in 2018. Multiple certification bodies have been accredited under it, with ongoing certifications issued to companies across Europe without reported major supervisory interventions.

example_name: BSI GDPR Certification Approval by German DPA
example_background: BSI (German Federal Office for Information Security) developed a GDPR-based certification scheme for cloud computing services, focusing on data protection in cloud environments. The scheme was submitted to BayLDA (Bavarian DPA) for approval. BSI demonstrated expertise through its established IT security standards and independence by maintaining separate divisions for certification from any consulting arms. Procedures were established for initial certification audits, annual surveillance audits, and recertification every three years, including withdrawal if criteria were not met. A public complaints portal was created for data subjects to report issues with certified cloud providers' data handling. Conflict of interest policies required auditors to disclose relationships and rotate assignments.
process_and_outcome: BayLDA approved the BSI C5:2020 GDPR certification scheme in 2020. Over 50 cloud service providers have obtained certifications, with periodic reviews confirming adherence.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Action on Privacy Seals Pre-GDPR
example_background: Prior to full GDPR implementation, the Spanish DPA (AEPD) investigated several entities offering privacy seals and certifications claiming GDPR alignment. One prominent case involved a certification body that issued seals to numerous websites without accreditation from a supervisory authority. The body advertised GDPR compliance but lacked demonstrated independence, as its certification process was funded directly by the certified entities without arm's-length oversight. No formal procedures existed for periodic reviews or withdrawal of seals upon non-compliance discoveries. Complaints from users about data breaches in certified sites were not addressed systematically, and transparency to the public was limited to promotional materials. The AEPD found that the body's operations created inherent conflicts as it provided both certification and compliance consulting.
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued public statements declaring the seals invalid under GDPR and ordered the body to cease operations. Affected certified entities were notified to remove seals, with follow-up audits on some.

example_name: Cloud Security Alliance CCDPSE Accreditation
example_background: The Cloud Security Alliance (CSA) launched the Certificate of Cloud Data Privacy Security Expertise (CCDPSE) and related certification programs for cloud services under GDPR frameworks. CSA sought accreditation for its certification bodies by engaging with European DPAs. The process included demonstrating expertise via technical working groups, independence through governance prohibiting commercial influences on certification decisions, and commitments to GDPR Article 42(5) criteria. Procedures covered multi-stage audits for issuing certifications, biennial reassessments, and automated withdrawal for audit failures. A global registry and public dashboard provided transparency on complaints handling, allowing data subjects to file issues against certified providers directly with escalation to DPAs.
process_and_outcome: CSA received recognition from multiple DPAs including Italian Garante and German authorities in 2022. Dozens of certifications issued with zero major revocation cases reported.

example_name: Dutch DPA Investigation into Unaccredited Certifiers
example_background: In 2019, the Dutch DPA (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) probed several organizations offering GDPR compliance certifications and seals without proper accreditation. These bodies claimed to certify SMEs for data protection but failed to demonstrate independence to any supervisory authority, as their certification panels included representatives from industry associations with financial ties to applicants. No evidence was provided of procedures for handling data subject complaints or for periodic reviews beyond self-reported annual questionnaires. Structures for withdrawal were absent, with seals remaining on websites even after public data leak incidents involving certified entities. The DPA noted undisclosed conflicts where certifiers offered paid remediation services post-certification.
process_and_outcome: The DPA publicly warned against using such certifications and fined one body €100,000. Certified organizations were instructed to discontinue seal usage.

example_name: Schrems II Related Certification Review by Irish DPC
example_background: Following the Schrems II CJEU ruling in 2020, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) reviewed certification bodies involved in EU-US data transfers, including those under Privacy Shield (pre-invalidated). One body continued issuing post-Schrems certifications without updating criteria to address CJEU concerns on equivalence. Independence was questioned due to US-based governance potentially conflicting with EU supervisory oversight. Complaint procedures existed but were not transparent or accessible to EU data subjects, routing issues to internal US panels. Periodic reviews did not incorporate supplementary measures as required, leading to ongoing certifications without withdrawal despite known transfer risks.
process_and_outcome: DPC issued guidance invalidating prior Privacy Shield certifications and imposed administrative measures on the body, requiring scheme overhaul. No direct fine but part of broader enforcement.

example_name: Trusted Cloud Framework Approval in Germany
example_background: Germany's Trusted Cloud Framework, initiated by the BSI, establishes a certification ecosystem for cloud services compliant with GDPR and national sovereignty requirements. Participating certification bodies undergo rigorous accreditation by BSI and DPAs, proving expertise in data protection through pilot audits, independence via strict no-consulting rules for auditors, and adherence to Article 42(5) via standardized criteria. Bodies implement digital platforms for certification lifecycle management, including automated periodic audits every 12-24 months and immediate withdrawal protocols. Publicly accessible complaint mechanisms integrate with DPA reporting, ensuring transparency for data subjects.
process_and_outcome: Framework launched in 2023 with first accreditations granted. Multiple bodies certified, enabling over 100 cloud services to achieve Trusted Cloud status.

example_name: Google Analytics and Consent Mode Sanctions by CNIL
example_background: In 2022, the French data protection authority (CNIL) identified issues with Google Analytics usage by multiple websites that transferred personal data to Google LLC in the US without adequate safeguards. The processing involved IP addresses and user identifiers collected via cookies for analytics purposes, potentially exposing data to US surveillance laws without valid transfer mechanisms like Standard Contractual Clauses properly implemented or supplemented.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued formal sanctions against eight websites, including public notices and injunctions to cease processing, following public complaints and investigations into cookie consent banners and data flows. The authority emphasized the lack of transparency and legal basis for transfers.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (transfers to third countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Fines
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for EU Facebook services, faced scrutiny over data processing practices including behavioral advertising based on personal data profiling. Multiple investigations revealed insufficient legal bases for processing, inadequate transparency in data use, and non-compliance with purpose limitation principles across user interactions on the platform.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) imposed fines totaling €405 million in 2022 for violations related to handling children's data on Instagram, following a cross-EU cooperation process with other DPAs. Additional proceedings were ongoing for other aspects.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Transfers
example_background: Meta's reliance on Standard Contractual Clauses for EU-US data transfers post-Schrems II was challenged, as the company continued transferring data despite identified risks from US surveillance laws without supplementary measures ensuring equivalent protection.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision directing the Irish DPC to impose corrective measures, including potential suspension of transfers and fines up to 4% of global turnover. Proceedings continued into 2024.

example_name: Irish DPC Guidelines on Article 42(5) Criteria Publication
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission developed and published detailed guidelines specifying the criteria used for imposing administrative fines under Article 83 GDPR, as required by Article 42(5). These guidelines outline factors such as the nature, gravity, and duration of the infringement, as well as whether it was intentional or negligent, the categories of data affected, the manner of processing, and mitigating or aggravating circumstances. The document provides comprehensive explanations with examples and references to relevant case law from the Court of Justice of the European Union. It was made available on the DPC's website in a structured PDF format with clear navigation, ensuring accessibility to data controllers, processors, and the public. The guidelines were also transmitted to the European Data Protection Board for consistency across member states. This publication occurred as part of the DPC's ongoing efforts to promote transparency in enforcement practices following the entry into force of the GDPR in May 2018.
process_and_outcome: The guidelines were finalized and published on 19 December 2023, serving as a reference for future fining decisions and contributing to harmonized application of GDPR enforcement across the EU.

example_name: CNIL France Publication of Fine Imposition Criteria
example_background: The French data protection authority, Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), issued and publicly released a comprehensive framework detailing the requirements and criteria for determining administrative fines under GDPR Article 83. This 28-page document categorizes infringements by severity (minor, intermediate, major), assigns base fine amounts ranging from €100 to €20 million or 4% of global turnover, and lists adjustment criteria including cooperation with the authority, prior infringements, and economic benefits gained. It includes practical examples for different types of violations such as lack of legal basis for processing or insufficient security measures. The framework was published on the CNIL website in both French and English, with searchable PDF and web formats for easy access. CNIL transmitted this to the EDPB to support consistent enforcement methodologies across Europe.
process_and_outcome: Published on 8 March 2022, the framework has been applied in subsequent enforcement actions, providing predictability for organizations subject to CNIL investigations.

example_name: EDPB Consistency Mechanism on Fine Criteria Transmission
example_background: The European Data Protection Board facilitated the exchange of national supervisory authorities' criteria for fine imposition by establishing a dedicated section in its register of decisions. Following GDPR Article 42(5), various DPAs submitted their methodologies, which the EDPB compiled and made publicly accessible through its website. This included criteria from multiple member states focusing on factors like infringement scale, data subjects affected, and remedial actions taken. The register provides detailed summaries of cross-border case decisions, incorporating fine calculations and rationales, ensuring transparency. The process began with the EDPB's adoption of guidelines on consistent application, urging all 27 DPAs to publish and transmit their approaches promptly after GDPR implementation.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing register updates since 2018 have incorporated over 1,000 decisions, promoting uniformity in fine assessments across the EU.
involved_parties: National Supervisory Authorities (e.g., DPC Ireland, CNIL France)

example_name: Italian Garante Criteria for GDPR Fines Disclosure
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali) published specific criteria and requirements for administrative fines on its official website, addressing Article 42(5) obligations. The document details a tiered fine structure based on violation gravity, with examples for breaches like unlawful data transfers or failure to conduct DPIAs. It covers calculation parameters such as turnover percentages, number of affected individuals, and duration of non-compliance, alongside adjustment factors for voluntary disclosures or certifications. Available in multiple formats including HTML and PDF with keyword searchability, it was shared with the EDPB. This initiative followed internal deliberations post-GDPR to standardize enforcement transparency.
process_and_outcome: Released in 2019 and updated periodically, used as basis for fines in cases like the 2020 €11.8M penalty against Vodafone Italy.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italy)

example_name: UK ICO Penalty Framework Publication
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released a detailed regulatory action policy outlining criteria for monetary penalties under UK GDPR, equivalent to EU GDPR Article 42(5). The 2021 policy specifies fine categories from £100 to £17.5 million or 4% of global annual turnover, with criteria including seriousness of harm, culpability, and compliance history. It provides case studies and decision trees for accessibility. Published prominently on the ICO website with interactive tools, it was adapted post-Brexit but transmitted to relevant bodies for alignment. The framework supports public understanding of enforcement thresholds.
process_and_outcome: Published 25 May 2021, applied in subsequent fines including £20M against Clearview AI.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Fine Criteria Accessibility Initiative
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) made public its criteria for imposing fines under GDPR Article 83 via a dedicated web section with downloadable guides, infographics, and videos. Criteria cover infringement typology, proportionality, and legal maximums, with real anonymized case examples illustrating applications. Ensured easy access through multilingual support and mobile optimization, transmitted to EDPB. Developed after 2018 GDPR audits to enhance stakeholder awareness.
process_and_outcome: Launched 2020, integrated into AEPD's annual enforcement reports with over 500 decisions referenced.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 04/2022 on Fining Criteria
example_background: The EDPB adopted guidelines specifying common criteria for administrative fines, urging supervisory authorities to publish and transmit them per Article 42(5). The 45-page document details factors like intentionality, cooperation, and economic scale, with illustrative scenarios. Adopted after public consultation involving 200+ stakeholders, available on EDPB site in 23 languages with annexes. Serves as model for national DPAs.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 12 October 2022, endorsed by all EU DPAs, influencing national publications.

example_name: Dutch AP Publication of Sanction Framework
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) published a sanction policy detailing fine imposition criteria, including base amounts, multipliers for severity, and caps at 4% turnover. Covers 12 infringement categories with examples like cookie violations. Placed in easily accessible online location with FAQs, transmitted to EDPB.
process_and_outcome: Published 2019, updated 2022, used in fines like €725K against Funda real estate platform.

example_name: Finnish DPA Fine Meter Tool Launch
example_background: The Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman released an interactive 'fine meter' tool and accompanying criteria document for GDPR fines, publicly accessible online. Details calculation based on violation type, data volume, and recidivism, with sliders for simulations. Transmitted to EDPB, enhances public engagement.
process_and_outcome: Launched 2021, featured in EU best practices compilations.

example_name: Amazon EDPB Decision on Advertising
example_background: Amazon's use of personal data for targeted advertising on its EU platform involved combining shopping data with third-party sources without clear consent. The practice encompassed behavioral advertising based on inferred interests from purchase history and browsing.
process_and_outcome: In July 2024, the EDPB issued a binding decision following the Luxembourg DPA's draft, requiring Amazon to suspend personalized ads until compliant. No final fine amount specified yet, but ongoing proceedings.

example_name: ISO 17065 Accreditation for GDPR Certification Bodies
example_background: Certification bodies seeking to offer GDPR compliance certifications under Article 42 must be accredited by national authorities based on requirements complementing Regulation (EC) No 765/2008. The EU Commission approved schemes like EuroPriSe, where bodies like BayLIND are accredited to certify data processing services.
process_and_outcome: BayLIND received accreditation in 2021 from the Bavarian authority to certify under the EuroPriSe scheme. Multiple certifications have been issued to companies demonstrating GDPR adherence through audited processes.

example_name: ARD IT-Sicherheit Certification Scheme
example_background: ARD IT-Sicherheit GmbH operates an accredited GDPR certification scheme approved by the German Federal Network Agency (BNetzA). The scheme certifies IT service providers' data protection management systems, ensuring alignment with GDPR security requirements and technical standards.
process_and_outcome: Accredited since 2019 under ISO 17065, the scheme has certified numerous providers, with ongoing audits ensuring continued compliance. Recognized by the EDPB as meeting Article 42 standards.

example_name: UK ICO Enforcement Against British Airways
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach where personal data of approximately 400,000 customers was accessed by unauthorized actors through a vulnerability in the company's website. The breach involved credit card details, names, and addresses being skimmed over several weeks. The incident came to light after the company detected suspicious activity and notified the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO). The company's handling of the breach raised questions about the adequacy of its security measures and compliance with data protection requirements under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which had recently come into force. The ICO investigated whether the airline's data protection practices met the standards required for processing large volumes of personal data in its booking systems.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020, fining British Airways £20 million (reduced from an initial proposed £183 million due to COVID-19 impacts and cooperation). The regulator determined that the company failed to process personal data securely. British Airways appealed the decision to the First-tier Tribunal, but the fine was upheld with the amount confirmed.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on WhatsApp Ireland
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission initiated an own-volition inquiry in 2020 into WhatsApp's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising. WhatsApp shared user data with its parent company, Meta (formerly Facebook), for purposes including targeted ads. The investigation examined whether the company's privacy policy provided sufficient transparency about data flows and processing purposes, and if users were given adequate control over their data under GDPR requirements. This case highlighted challenges in intra-group data transfers within large tech ecosystems.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp Ireland Limited €225 million for opaque processing operations and insufficient information provided to users. Other EU supervisory authorities participated via the One-Stop-Shop mechanism, issuing additional fines totaling over €400 million. WhatsApp accepted the decision without appeal.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 (Information to be provided to data subjects)
GDPR Article 14 (Information to be provided where data not obtained from the data subject)

example_name: Revocation of Accreditation - UKAS Action Against ANSI
example_background: The United Kingdom Accreditation Service (UKAS) reviewed the accreditation status of the American National Standards Institute (ANSI) as a certification body under the EU Accreditation Regulation, which aligns with GDPR requirements for certification mechanisms. Concerns arose regarding ANSI's oversight of certified bodies, including instances where certified entities failed to maintain consistent data protection standards in their audits. This led to questions about whether ANSI continued to meet the impartiality, independence, and competence criteria required for accreditation in the context of GDPR Article 43 certifications.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, UKAS revoked ANSI's accreditation for certain GDPR-related certification schemes due to persistent non-conformities identified in surveillance assessments. ANSI was required to notify affected clients and transition services, impacting its operations in the EU market.

example_name: Danish DPA Fine Against Nordic Maritime
example_background: Nordic Maritime, a shipping company, was investigated following a personal data breach in 2020 where employee and customer data was exposed due to insecure cloud storage configurations. The Danish Data Protection Agency examined the company's role as a data controller and its contracts with a certification body that had previously audited its GDPR compliance. The probe revealed that the certification body's assessment overlooked significant gaps in technical and organizational measures, leading to reliance on flawed accreditation assurances.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Datatilsynet fined Nordic Maritime DKK 8 million and separately addressed the certification body's inadequacies, contributing to scrutiny on the accrediting national body. This prompted a review of the certification process.

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica Fallout - ICO Fine
example_background: The 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal involved the harvesting of data from up to 87 million Facebook users without proper consent, used for political advertising. The ICO investigated Facebook's (now Meta) platform design, data sharing policies with third-party apps, and overall compliance framework. Although not directly an accreditation case, it exposed weaknesses in third-party certifications and audit processes that Facebook relied on for GDPR readiness.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2019 for practices that were unjustified, excessive, and lacked transparency. The company accepted the penalty, and subsequent EU-wide actions followed under GDPR enforcement.

example_name: Luxembourg DPA Fine Against Amazon
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. faced investigation by the Luxembourg Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CNPD, now CNIL equivalent) starting in 2018 into its data processing for advertising and personalization on its platform. The inquiry covered user profiling based on browsing history, purchase data, and location information, affecting tens of millions of EU customers. Issues included lack of transparency in data combination practices and validity of consent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, the CNPD fined Amazon €746 million, one of the largest GDPR fines, for violations in personalized ads processing. Amazon appealed to the Luxembourg courts, with proceedings ongoing as of 2023.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) Luxembourg

example_name: Spanish AEPD Action Against Vodafone
example_background: Vodafone España was investigated by the Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) after a 2019 breach exposed customer data including phone numbers and addresses. The case also scrutinized Vodafone's use of GDPR certification from an accredited body, questioning the thoroughness of the certification audit in identifying vulnerabilities in customer management systems.
process_and_outcome: AEPD imposed a €8.15 million fine in 2021. The certification body faced separate review for its accreditation status, leading to enhanced oversight requirements.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: ENISA GDPR Certification Pilot Success - Example Body
example_background: Under the EU GDPR Article 42, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) piloted certification mechanisms in 2020-2022, involving accredited bodies like those from DIN CERTCO. One participating certification body demonstrated sustained compliance through rigorous audits of client data processing operations, maintaining accreditation by meeting ongoing competence and impartiality standards set by national bodies.
process_and_outcome: The body retained its accreditation following successful surveillance audits, enabling continued issuance of GDPR-compliant certifications to controllers in cloud services, serving as a model for adherence.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Data Fine
example_background: SAP SE provided a cloud-based HR platform where employers uploaded employee personal data, including sensitive information like health data. The service lacked sufficient technical measures to prevent unlawful access, such as inadequate encryption and access controls. A 2021 vulnerability exposed data of thousands of users across EU organizations.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined SAP €74.25 million in March 2025, one of the largest GDPR penalties, after a cross-border procedure initiated by Belgian DPA. SAP patched vulnerabilities and improved security certifications. The decision is subject to appeal.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: TikTok GDPR Transfers Fine by Dutch DPA
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform, transferred personal data of European users, including children under 16, to servers located in China and other non-EEA countries for processing purposes such as content recommendation algorithms, user profiling, and moderation. The data encompassed behavioral data, video interactions, device information, and IP addresses. Transfers occurred without sufficient safeguards to ensure an equivalent level of protection, particularly given Chinese laws potentially mandating access by authorities, and without valid transfer mechanisms in place during the relevant period.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) investigated following complaints and imposed a €750,000 fine in 2022 for unlawful transfers among other issues. TikTok appealed but implemented additional transfer controls, including data localization efforts in Europe.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland SCC Inquiry Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited conducted transfers of EU user personal data to the United States using Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), involving data of over 500 million Facebook and Instagram users. The transfers included sensitive information like political views, religious beliefs, health data inferred from behavior, and location data, processed on US servers for ad targeting and analytics. Following the Schrems II ruling, the company did not implement adequate supplementary measures to address risks from US surveillance laws, such as those under Section 702 of FISA, leading to potential undermining of EU protection levels.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted a cross-border inquiry, concluding in 2023 that the transfers violated GDPR. It issued a €1.2 billion fine, the largest under GDPR, and ordered suspension of data transfers from EU servers to the US, with appeal pending.

example_name: Amazon AWS Transfer Investigation by CNIL
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) Ireland Limited provided cloud storage and processing services to French clients, resulting in transfers of personal data of millions of French citizens to AWS data centers in Virginia, US, and other locations. Data included health records, financial information, and HR data processed for various controllers. The transfers relied on SCCs, but supplementary measures were deemed insufficient against US intelligence access risks, with data accessible via US cloud infrastructure without equivalent protections.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated and in 2022 ordered Amazon to cease unlawful transfers within two months, following EDPB binding decision. No fine was imposed at that stage, but compliance was mandated with ongoing monitoring.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Transfers Compliance with EU Data Boundary
example_background: Microsoft implemented the EU Data Boundary for its commercial cloud services, ensuring that personal data of EU customers remains within the European Economic Area (EEA) or specific approved regions. For services requiring processing outside, it uses approved transfer mechanisms like SCCs supplemented by technical measures such as encryption and pseudonymization. This applies to Azure, Microsoft 365, and Dynamics 365, processing data for enterprise clients while maintaining protection levels.
process_and_outcome: Following Schrems II, Microsoft committed to and launched the EU Data Boundary in 2023, gaining approval from EU regulators. No enforcement actions occurred; it serves as a model for compliance, with transparency reports detailing measures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Standard Contractual Clauses with Supplementary Measures

example_name: Oracle Cloud Transfers Fine by CNIL
example_background: Oracle France provided cloud services involving transfers of French customer personal data to US data centers in Arizona and Virginia for storage and processing. Data included commercial and HR information processed under SCCs, but without additional measures to mitigate risks from US surveillance legislation, affecting multiple French controllers using Oracle Cloud Infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: CNIL investigated and imposed a €300,000 fine in 2024, ordering cessation of non-compliant transfers. Oracle adjusted its practices to include encryption and other safeguards.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Transfers Decision
example_background: SAP SE's SuccessFactors HR cloud service transferred EU employee personal data to US data centers for payroll, performance management, and analytics processing. The service relied on SCCs, but an EDPB decision highlighted insufficient supplementary measures against third-country access risks.
process_and_outcome: The Belgian DPA investigated a complaint, leading to an EDPB binding decision in 2022 requiring SAP to suspend transfers. SAP implemented additional protections like data residency options.

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers by CNIL
example_background: Multiple French websites using Google Analytics transferred EU user data to Google LLC in the US for web analytics, including IP addresses anonymized post-transfer, browsing behavior, and device data. The tool embedded US servers via cookies and scripts, effecting transfers without adequate supplementary measures beyond SCCs.
process_and_outcome: CNIL declared such use unlawful in 2022 after investigations, ordering controllers to stop transfers. Similar actions by Austrian and Italian DPAs followed, prompting Google to update tools with EEA data residency.

example_name: Google Analytics and Consent Mode CNIL Enforcement
example_background: In 2022, the French data protection authority (CNIL) identified issues with Google Analytics implementation on websites of various organizations, including major French media outlets. The configuration involved data transfers to the US without adequate safeguards, using standard contractual clauses that were deemed insufficient post-Schrems II due to US surveillance laws. CNIL's web sweeping exercise revealed non-compliance with consent requirements for cookies and analytics tools, where users were not properly informed or given granular choices before data collection and transfer began. Several entities continued using the tools despite public warnings issued by CNIL in June 2022.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued formal notices and subsequently fined Google €150 million in 2023, with additional fines against non-compliant websites. Affected parties were given time to comply but faced penalties for persistence. Google adjusted its services in response.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (transfers to third countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Schrems II Violations
example_background: Following the 2020 Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) Schrems II ruling invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield, Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook) continued data transfers from EU users to the US for processing on its servers. The transfers involved behavioral advertising data including IP addresses and device identifiers. Max Schrems' NOYB organization filed complaints across multiple EU countries alleging invalid transfer mechanisms, as standard contractual clauses alone were insufficient without supplementary measures to protect against US government access under laws like FISA 702.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated and in 2023 fined Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful transfers. Meta was ordered to suspend future transfers and bring existing EU data back from the US. The company appealed but faced enforcement actions.

example_name: H&M Personal Data Misuse
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center from 2014 to 2019 gathered non-anonymized sensitive employee data, including health conditions, family issues, religious beliefs, and personal life details obtained during 'welcome meetings' where returning employees shared gossip. This data informed performance predictions, shift assignments, and HR decisions, stored in Excel sheets without explicit consent or necessity assessment. An internal whistleblower prompted the discovery.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA investigated and fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2020. H&M cooperated, ceased the practices, and enhanced compliance training.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Schrems II EU-US Data Transfers Coordinated Actions
example_background: Post-Schrems II (July 2020), NOYB filed 101 strategic complaints across EU member states against organizations using Google Analytics and Facebook Connect without valid transfer mechanisms to the US. Tools transmitted personal data like IP addresses immediately upon website load, without consent or supplementary safeguards against US intelligence access.
process_and_outcome: National DPs issued resolutions; e.g., Austrian DPA fined a site €100 in 2023, Brandenburg DPA fined €2200. Google suspended services in targeted countries. Ongoing proceedings shaped transfer practices.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Transfers Inquiry
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited provided cloud services like Azure and Office 365 to EU customers, involving transfers of customer personal data to US data centers for storage, backup, and analytics. Following Schrems II, concerns arose over US CLOUD Act access risks despite use of SCCs and transparency reports.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted a major inquiry concluding in 2023 with no fine but required Microsoft to implement additional contractual and technical measures. Similar probes in other EU countries led to commitments for data localization options.

example_name: Oracle Cloud Transfers Correction
example_background: Oracle Corporation provided cloud hosting services to EU customers, transferring personal data to US and other third-country data centers for processing without initially conducting full transfer impact assessments post-Schrems II. Data included customer CRM records and analytics.
process_and_outcome: NOYB complaints led to a 2023 EDPB binding decision requiring Oracle to suspend non-compliant transfers and adopt supplementary measures. Oracle complied by updating contracts and implementing encryption.

example_name: SAP International Transfers Remediation
example_background: SAP SE, a German software firm, transferred EU customer and employee data to US entities for ERP processing and support. Post-Schrems II, audits revealed gaps in risk assessments for government access.
process_and_outcome: Following NOYB complaints, the Austrian DPA in 2023 required SAP to halt transfers and implement TIAs. SAP complied swiftly, adopting EU-US Data Privacy Framework certifications and local processing options.

example_name: Accenture Data Localization Compliance
example_background: Accenture, a global consulting firm, serves EU clients with data processing services and shifted strategies post-Schrems II by establishing EU-based data centers and using binding corporate rules (BCRs) for any necessary third-country flows, ensuring equivalent protection levels.
process_and_outcome: Audits by client DPAs and internal reviews confirmed adherence, with no enforcement actions. Accenture published transparency reports on transfer mechanisms.

example_name: EU-US Data Privacy Framework Adoption by Cisco
example_background: Cisco Systems, Inc. certified under the new EU-US Data Privacy Framework in 2023, enabling compliant transfers of EU personal data to the US for networking and software services. Implemented safeguards including TIAs, encryption, and audit rights.
process_and_outcome: US Department of Commerce listed Cisco as compliant; EU adequacy decision in July 2023 validated the framework. No regulatory challenges reported.

example_name: TikTok French CNIL GDPR Fine for Transfers
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated TikTok's data processing practices, focusing on international transfers of EU users' personal data to China and other countries. Findings indicated inadequate safeguards for transfers, insufficient assessment of third-country protections under laws governing public security and national security access, lack of effective data subject rights enforcement abroad, and no independent supervisory authority oversight in recipient countries equivalent to EU standards. Transfers relied on Standard Contractual Clauses without supplementary measures to address deficiencies in rule of law and judicial redress in those jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CNIL imposed a fine of 20 million euros on TikTok France for multiple GDPR violations, including unlawful data transfers.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Google Analytics Banned in Austria for US Transfers
example_background: The Austrian Data Protection Authority (DSB) addressed complaints about use of Google Analytics by noserbste.com, finding transfers of personal data to the US inadequate post-Schrems II. Google Analytics involved IP addresses and other data sent to US servers without sufficient supplementary measures. The authority assessed US laws allowing government access without adequate safeguards, lack of effective judicial redress for EU data subjects, and no equivalent independent supervisory authority, failing GDPR adequacy elements like data protection rules implementation and human rights standards.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, DSB ordered the website operator to stop using Google Analytics, declaring transfers unlawful.

example_name: EU Adequacy Decision for UK
example_background: Post-Brexit, the European Commission granted the UK an adequacy decision in 2021, recognizing its data protection framework under the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 as providing adequate protection. The UK retained the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) as an independent supervisory authority with enforcement powers, maintained effective data subject rights and judicial redress, and ensured limitations on public authority access aligned with EU standards. Commitments included cooperation with EU authorities and adherence to ECHR human rights protections.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy decision adopted 28 June 2021, extended indefinitely in 2023 after review, allowing continued data flows.

example_name: Meta Standard Contractual Clauses Challenged by NOYB
example_background: The None of Your Business (NOYB) organization filed complaints in 2021 against Meta (Facebook) in multiple EU countries regarding data transfers to the US using Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). Post-Schrems II, assessments showed SCCs alone insufficient due to US surveillance laws (FISA 702, EO 12333) enabling bulk access without proportionality requirements, limited redress for non-US persons, and no independent EU-equivalent supervisory authority. This highlighted failures in third-country rule of law, effective rights enforcement, and security measures for onward transfers.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC found transfers unlawful in 2023; other authorities suspended transfers. Meta faced ongoing proceedings and potential fines.

example_name: EU Adequacy Decision for Switzerland
example_background: The European Commission renewed Switzerland's adequacy status in 2023, originally granted in 2000. Switzerland's Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP), revised in 2023, ensures protection through independent Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC), enforceable rights, judicial remedies, and restrictions on public authority data access for security purposes. Alignment with Council of Europe Convention 108 and cooperation protocols addressed GDPR criteria including case-law and international commitments.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy decision extended for 10 years from 27 July 2023.
involved_parties: Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Swiss FADP (Federal Act on Data Protection)

example_name: European Commission Adequacy Decision for Japan
example_background: The European Commission assessed Japan's data protection framework, including the Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI), to determine if it provided an adequate level of protection for personal data transferred from the EU. This evaluation considered Japan's rules on onward transfers, data subject rights, and oversight by the Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC). Supplementary rules were adopted by Japan to align with EU standards, addressing issues like access by public authorities. The decision reflected monitoring of developments in Japan that could impact the functioning of prior transfer decisions.
process_and_outcome: In January 2019, the Commission adopted an adequacy decision recognizing Japan as providing adequate protection. This was renewed in 2024 following a review, enabling data flows without additional safeguards. Regular reviews are scheduled every four years.

example_name: Meta EU-US Data Transfers Suspension
example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling, the Irish DPC ordered Meta (Facebook) to suspend data transfers to the US using SCCs due to insufficient protections against US surveillance laws. Meta's practices involved transferring vast amounts of EU user data to the US for processing. Noonan, the DPC's lead investigator, highlighted risks from US laws. This case underscored the need for continuous monitoring of third-country developments affecting transfer decisions.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the Irish DPC directed Meta to cease transfers within five months. Meta challenged this in court. Subsequently, the EU-US Data Privacy Framework was adopted in July 2023 as a replacement for Privacy Shield.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Fine - Magecart Attack Transfers
example_background: British Airways experienced a data breach via a Magecart attack where payment card details of approximately 400,000 customers were skimmed from its website. Investigations revealed that data was exfiltrated to servers outside the EEA. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined compliance with data protection principles, including security and international transfers, noting failures in monitoring third-party scripts and data flows.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the ICO fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £183 million after appeal). Remediation steps included enhanced security measures and transfer compliance reviews.

example_name: Adequacy Decision for the Republic of Korea
example_background: The European Commission evaluated South Korea's Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) and enforcement by the Personal Information Protection Commission. This included assessments of data subject rights, onward transfers, and limitations on public authority access, with supplementary rules ensuring EU-equivalent protections amid evolving Korean laws.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy decision granted in December 2020, enabling seamless data flows. Reviewed and maintained in 2023.

example_name: Schrems II - Invalidating EU-US Privacy Shield
example_background: The Privacy Shield framework was adopted in 2016 as a mechanism to ensure an adequate level of data protection for personal data transfers from the EU to the US following the invalidation of the Safe Harbour arrangement by the Court of Justice of the European Union in the Schrems I case. The framework involved self-certification by US companies and commitments to comply with EU data protection principles enforced by the US Department of Commerce and Federal Trade Commission. Concerns arose regarding ongoing US government surveillance programs, including Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333, which allowed bulk collection of data involving non-US persons without sufficient safeguards equivalent to those in the EU. Advocate General Saugmandsgaard Øe issued an opinion highlighting deficiencies in redress mechanisms for EU citizens. This led to a review process where available information revealed that the US no longer ensured an adequate level of protection.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU in Case C-311/18 ruled on 16 July 2020 that the Privacy Shield decision was invalid with immediate effect and without retroactive effect. Data transfers continued under standard contractual clauses were permitted but subject to additional safeguards where necessary. The Commission was directed to monitor third-country protections more rigorously.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union

example_name: Safe Harbour Invalidated (Schrems I)
example_background: The Safe Harbour arrangement was established in 2000 via Commission Decision 2000/520 to facilitate data transfers from the EU to the US by allowing self-certification of US companies to adhere to EU-equivalent data protection principles. Revelations from Edward Snowden in 2013 exposed extensive US NSA surveillance programs, including PRISM, which accessed data of EU citizens through Safe Harbour participants without adequate protections or judicial redress. The Irish Data Protection Commissioner refused to suspend transfers but Max Schrems challenged this, arguing the framework no longer provided adequate protection due to systemic surveillance deficiencies.
process_and_outcome: On 6 October 2015, the CJEU in Case C-362/14 annulled the Safe Harbour decision, stating it failed to ensure an adequate level of protection and suspended its application immediately without retroactive effect. This prompted the Commission to negotiate the successor Privacy Shield.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: EU-Japan Adequacy Decision Suspension Review
example_background: In 2019, the European Commission adopted an adequacy decision for Japan following extensive negotiations and reviews to align Japan's data protection framework, amended by the Act on the Protection of Personal Information, with GDPR standards. The decision included supplementary rules for Japan to protect EU data subjects' rights. Periodic reviews under Article 45(3) GDPR assessed ongoing compliance amid changes in Japan's legal environment and data transfer practices.
process_and_outcome: The first periodic review in 2022 confirmed continued adequacy, with no repeal or suspension needed. The Commission issued a report detailing monitoring mechanisms and Japan's cooperation on data protection enforcement.

example_name: Suspension of Data Transfers to Russian Entities
example_background: Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, several EU data protection authorities identified risks to personal data transferred to Russian entities due to sanctions, potential state access to data, and erosion of rule of law. Available information from geopolitical developments indicated that Russia no longer ensured adequate protection levels.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA suspended transfers to Gazprom on 28 February 2022 under Article 66 GDPR. The French CNIL issued orders to suspend transfers to Yandex and VK on 8 March 2022. The EDPB coordinated responses, leading to multiple national suspensions without Commission-level repeal but highlighting urgency procedures.

example_name: Canadian Adequacy Decision Renewal
example_background: Canada received its first adequacy decision in 2001, renewed multiple times based on reviews of PIPEDA and sector-specific laws. The 2021 renewal involved detailed assessments of commercial data transfers, government access rules under the CSIS Act, and redress mechanisms like the Office of the Privacy Commissioner.
process_and_outcome: On 11 November 2021, the Commission renewed adequacy via Implementing Decision C(2021) 7720 following a review confirming sustained protection levels, with monitoring commitments.
involved_parties: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

example_name: New Zealand Adequacy Confirmation
example_background: New Zealand's Privacy Act 1993, updated in 2020, was assessed for GDPR equivalence, covering data handling practices, enforcement by the Privacy Commissioner, and limitations on public authority access. The decision facilitated seamless data flows for business and public sectors.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy granted on 27 November 2023 after review, with no issues prompting suspension, ensuring continued validity.
involved_parties: Office of the Privacy Commissioner (New Zealand)

example_name: EDPB Recommendations on US Data Transfers Post-Schrems II
example_background: After Schrems II invalidated Privacy Shield, the EDPB reviewed transfer tools and found many third-country destinations, particularly the US, lacked systemic adequacy due to surveillance laws. This informed supplementary measures for SCCs.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued Recommendations 01/2020 on 21 June 2021, adopted after public consultation, guiding controllers on assessing third-country protections without adequacy decisions, leading to widespread adoption of additional safeguards.

example_name: South Korea Adequacy Renewal Amid Review
example_background: South Korea's Personal Information Protection Act was deemed adequate in 2021 after amendments strengthening enforcement and rights. Ongoing reviews monitored compliance with data localization and cross-border transfer rules.
process_and_outcome: The 2023 review confirmed adequacy, with the Commission issuing a report on sustained protection levels and no need for amendment or suspension.

example_name: Schrems I Invalidates Safe Harbour
example_background: The Safe Harbour framework, established by a 2000 EU-US Commission decision, allowed EU companies to transfer personal data to US counterparts by self-certifying adherence to seven principles covering notice, choice, data integrity, and enforcement. Over time, revelations about US NSA surveillance programs raised doubts about the framework's ability to protect EU data subjects' rights. Max Schrems filed a complaint with the Irish Data Protection Commissioner against Facebook Ireland for transferring his data to the US under Safe Harbour, arguing it exposed data to disproportionate surveillance without adequate remedies. The Irish High Court referred questions to the CJEU on the Commission's adequacy decision.
process_and_outcome: On 6 October 2015, the CJEU invalidated Commission Decision 2000/520, finding it failed to ensure adequate protection due to US surveillance practices and lack of judicial redress. This led to immediate suspension of Safe Harbour transfers, prompting over 4,000 companies to seek alternatives, and spurred the creation of Privacy Shield.

example_name: UK Adequacy Decisions Post-Brexit
example_background: Following Brexit on 31 January 2020, the UK became a third country under GDPR. The UK had implemented the UK GDPR mirroring EU GDPR, with the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) as supervisor. The Commission assessed the UK's data protection framework, including adequacy for general transfers, law enforcement processing (via separate decision), and derogations. This involved reviewing UK laws, surveillance practices under the Investigatory Powers Act, and ICO enforcement record.
process_and_outcome: On 28 June 2021, the Commission adopted two adequacy decisions (EU) 2021/915 and 2021/914, recognizing the UK as providing adequate protection until 27 June 2025, subject to periodic review every four years. A sunset clause allows revocation if UK standards diverge, with the first review initiated in 2023 amid concerns over UK data bill proposals.

example_name: South Korea Adequacy Decision
example_background: South Korea's Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA), amended in 2020 and 2023, provides protections including consent requirements, data subject rights, breach notifications, and restrictions on cross-border transfers. The EU granted initial adequacy in 2021 after verifying alignment with GDPR essentials, including the role of the Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC). The decision covers all sectors except air passenger data, with binding supplementary rules for onward transfers.
process_and_outcome: Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/915 was adopted on 14 June 2021, with a review confirming adequacy in 2024. Periodic reviews are set every four years, ensuring ongoing monitoring of PIPC enforcement and legislative changes.
involved_parties: South Korea Personal Information Protection Commission

example_name: Canadian Adequacy Status Renewal
example_background: Canada's adequacy under the former Data Protection Directive was based on PIPEDA and sector-specific laws. Post-GDPR, the Commission reviewed updates including the Digital Charter Implementation Act proposals, enforcement by the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC), and limitations from laws like the Patriot Act affecting commercial data. The assessment confirmed safeguards for EU data subjects.
process_and_outcome: On 11 December 2023, Commission Implementing Decision 2023/2732 renewed adequacy with limitations on federal public sector transfers, specifying OPC oversight and four-year review cycles. This maintains seamless data flows with Canada.
involved_parties: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

example_name: Meta Fined for Lacking Adequate Transfer Safeguards
example_background: Following Schrems II, Meta Platforms Ireland continued transferring EU user data to the US using SCCs without additional measures to address CJEU-identified risks from US surveillance. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp transfers, finding reliance on SCCs insufficient post-invalidation of Privacy Shield. The probe examined volume of data (billions of users), nature of transfers, and absence of encryption or pseudonymization to mitigate access risks.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the DPC imposed a 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta, the largest GDPR penalty to date, ordering suspension of US transfers and deletion of affected data. The decision followed EDPB binding instructions after other DPAs objected.

example_name: Switzerland Adequacy Renewal
example_background: Switzerland's Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) was revised effective September 2023 to enhance GDPR equivalence, including mandatory DPIAs, breach reporting within 72 hours, and restrictions on international transfers. The EU's initial adequacy from 2000 was reviewed in 2023, confirming the Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC) independence and enforcement powers.
process_and_outcome: Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2023/1909 adopted on 27 October 2023 renews adequacy status with four-year reviews, covering all sectors and specifying FDPIC as supervisory authority.
involved_parties: Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner

example_name: Meta Platforms Schrems III Proceedings
example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling, Max Schrems filed new complaints against Facebook Ireland, WhatsApp Ireland, and Instagram for continuing to transfer EU user data to the US using updated standard contractual clauses and other mechanisms. The transfers involved vast amounts of personal data for advertising and content moderation purposes, with concerns raised over persistent US surveillance laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333, which allow warrantless access to data by US authorities without equivalent protections for EU residents.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission issued final decisions prohibiting data transfers to the US and requiring Meta to cease such transfers within five months, resulting in operational suspensions until alternative compliant mechanisms were implemented.
involved_parties: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited (Facebook, WhatsApp, Instagram)

example_name: Adequacy Decision for Japan
example_background: The European Commission evaluated Japan's data protection framework, including the Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI), supplemented by a mutual adequacy arrangement with enforceable data subject rights and limitations on onward transfers. Japan implemented supplementary rules to align with GDPR standards, enabling Japanese organizations to receive personal data from the EU without further authorization.
process_and_outcome: In January 2019, the Commission adopted an adequacy decision for Japan, periodically reviewed and renewed, facilitating trade and data flows between the EU and Japan with minimal friction.
involved_parties: Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC) of Japan

example_name: Adequacy Decision for UK Post-Brexit
example_background: After the UK's departure from the EU, the European Commission assessed the UK's data protection regime, including the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018, confirming ongoing alignment with EU standards, independent supervisory authority, and effective enforcement mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: On June 28, 2021, an adequacy decision was adopted for the UK, extended indefinitely in 2023 after review, allowing seamless data flows between the EU and UK.

example_name: H&M German DPA Data Transfers Fine
example_background: H&M relied on standard contractual clauses for transferring EU employee and customer data to affiliates in non-adequate third countries, including the US and India, without supplementary measures to address identified risks from foreign access laws.
process_and_outcome: In October 2023, the Bavarian DPA fined H&M Justice 35.25 million euros for multiple GDPR infringements including unlawful international transfers, mandating process overhauls.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Invalidation of Safe Harbor Framework
example_background: The Safe Harbor arrangement, established in 2000 under Directive 95/46/EC Article 25(6), allowed self-certifying US companies to transfer EU personal data based on a Commission adequacy decision. Revelations from Edward Snowden in 2013 about US NSA surveillance programs, including PRISM, raised doubts about the adequacy of US protections, particularly regarding indiscriminate access by public authorities without adequate safeguards or redress for EU citizens.
