example_background: H&M's Nuremberg service center collected and stored detailed notes on employees' private lives, including health data, family situations, and personal beliefs, gathered during offboarding interviews with former colleagues. This data was used to evaluate rehired employees and create employee profiles, processing sensitive personal data without explicit consent or necessity.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2020. H&M discontinued the practice, deleted the data, and enhanced employee privacy training.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: Schrems II Facebook-US Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems challenged Facebook's data transfers from the EU to the U.S., arguing that U.S. surveillance programs like PRISM and Upstream did not provide adequate protection for EU personal data. Following the invalidation of Safe Harbour and Privacy Shield, the case examined whether Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) alone sufficed for transfers.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled in July 2020 that Privacy Shield was invalid and SCCs required supplementary measures for adequacy. This led to multiple enforcement actions, including the €1.2 billion Meta fine.

example_name: SAP Binding Corporate Rules Certification
example_background: SAP SE developed comprehensive Binding Corporate Rules for its worldwide group companies to manage HR and client personal data transfers. The BCRs incorporated data protection principles, risk assessments, transparency obligations, and technical measures like encryption, addressing post-Schrems II transfer requirements through supplementary protections.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU authorities, led by the Dutch DPA, approved SAP's BCRs in 2021 after thorough review and mutual recognition. SAP maintains ongoing compliance through annual reporting and third-party audits.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EBA Guidelines on outsourcing and cloud policy

example_name: Google Ireland BCR Challenges
example_background: Google maintains a complex global data processing ecosystem with transfers of European user search, location, and advertising data to US-based entities. While Google has implemented binding corporate rules, these faced scrutiny during investigations into data transfer practices post-Schrems II. The BCRs purported to cover group structure details, transfer scopes including categories like IP addresses and device IDs, processing for service personalization, data subjects as users and partners, third countries primarily the US, internal binding commitments, principle applications including limited retention and pseudonymisation, rights facilitation with court access, liability acceptance, user notification methods, DPO oversight, complaint resolution paths, audit mechanisms reporting to authorities, update processes, cooperation duties, conflict reporting, and staff training. Regulators questioned adequacy given US surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC audited Google's BCR implementation in 2020-2022, leading to recommendations for enhancements. Google updated its rules and supplemented with additional safeguards. No formal invalidation occurred, but it highlighted BCR verification needs.

example_name: Amazon Europe BCR Framework
example_background: Amazon operates extensive e-commerce and cloud services with data centers worldwide, transferring EU customer purchase history, payment data, and AWS metadata to US and other non-EU entities. Amazon's binding corporate rules, approved by the Luxembourg DPA, encompass the corporate group structure with entity contacts, detailed transfer mappings for categories like order details and browsing behavior, processing activities for fulfillment and analytics, purposes of service delivery, data subjects as consumers and businesses, identified third countries, enforceable commitments, GDPR principles enforcement with data minimisation techniques, comprehensive rights mechanisms including automated decision opt-outs and compensation paths, EU entity liability for global breaches, privacy notice integrations, compliance officer tasks for audits and training, intra-group complaint escalation, verification audits with board reporting and DPA access, rule amendment protocols, authority collaboration, third-country requirement notifications, and mandatory data protection training across the organization.
process_and_outcome: CNPD Luxembourg approved Amazon's BCRs in 2020, with ongoing monitoring. This supported continued operations amid transfer mechanism reviews post-Schrems II.

example_name: British Airways BCR Audit Findings
example_background: British Airways, part of International Airlines Group, uses BCRs for passenger data transfers to non-EU group entities for loyalty programs and operations. During a 2019-2021 review linked to a data breach incident, regulators examined BCR compliance including structure details, transfer specs for booking and frequent flyer data, processing types, purposes, subjects, countries, binding nature, principles, rights, liability, info provision, monitoring, complaints, audits, changes, cooperation, reporting, training. Shortcomings were noted in audit frequency and third-country reporting.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO investigated post-breach and required BCR enhancements in 2020, imposing a £20m fine primarily for security lapses but noting transfer mechanism improvements needed. BA remediated and updated BCRs.

example_name: HP Inc. BCR Approval
example_background: HP Inc. manages global IT services with transfers of customer support and employee data from EU to US and Asia-Pacific entities. BCRs cover group contacts, data categories like support tickets, processing for maintenance, purposes, subjects, countries, all required elements including audits, training, and DPA cooperation.
process_and_outcome: CNIL approved HP's BCRs in 2015 with GDPR-aligned updates, maintaining compliance through verified mechanisms.

example_name: British Airways €20 Million Fine (Transfer Elements)
example_background: British Airways, operated by British Airways PLC, experienced a data breach in 2018 affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details and personal data. Data was processed by a third-party provider with servers outside the EEA. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) examined the transfer mechanisms, finding inadequate safeguards for data sent to non-EEA countries without adequacy decisions or proper derogations.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the ICO issued a £20 million fine (reduced from £183 million after appeal). BA was required to enhance security and transfer controls.

example_name: DPC Investigation into Transfers to Russia
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated multiple companies transferring data to Russia post-2022 Ukraine conflict, where Russian laws mandate government access to data processors. Transfers relied on SCCs without assessing risks from Federal Law No. 152-FZ, affecting EU data subjects' rights.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DPC issued reprimands and ordered suspensions for companies like LinkedIn Ireland, requiring supplementary measures or cessation of transfers.

example_name: Orange Romania €50,000 Fine
example_background: Orange Romania transferred customer personal data to non-EEA affiliates for marketing without adequacy decisions, SCCs, or valid derogations like explicit consent. The transfers involved telecom subscriber data shared with group companies in countries lacking adequate protection.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the Romanian National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing fined Orange €50,000 and mandated cessation of unlawful transfers.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Yahoo Transfers
example_background: Yahoo Europe (Oath Europe) transferred EU user data to the US under Privacy Shield. Post-Schrems II invalidation, the EDPB addressed ongoing reliance on derogations without proper assessments.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the EDPB issued binding decisions leading to fines and compliance orders, emphasizing Article 49 derogations' strict application.

example_name: Microsoft EU Cloud Transfers Compliance Post-Schrems II
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations transferred Azure and Office 365 customer data from EU to US data centers. Following Schrems II, Microsoft conducted Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs) and implemented supplementary measures including encryption and pseudonymization to address US surveillance risks.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft published transparency reports and TIA methodologies, enabling continued transfers under GDPR without enforcement actions to date. Adopted EU Data Privacy Addendum and ICCP framework.
involved_parties: EU Cloud Code of Conduct participants

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Binding Corporate Rules Implementation
example_background: SAP SE implemented Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) approved by EU authorities for intra-group transfers of HR data via SuccessFactors platform from EU to non-EEA subsidiaries, including US and Asia. BCRs included audits, transparency, and risk mitigation clauses.
process_and_outcome: BCRs approved in 2019 by Irish DPC as lead authority, allowing compliant group-wide transfers without individual derogations.

example_name: Clearstream Banking Luxembourg €5.4m Fine
example_background: Clearstream Banking transferred client personal data to non-EEA entities for securities services without adequate safeguards or derogations, relying on outdated model clauses.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Luxembourg CNPD fined €5.4 million and ordered remediation of transfer mechanisms.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Article 49 Derogation Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the European subsidiary of Facebook, engaged in multiple instances of transferring personal data of European users to the United States between 2020 and 2023. The transfers occurred in the context of providing social media services, where user data was processed on servers located in the US. Following the Schrems II judgment in 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield framework, Meta relied on derogations under Article 49 GDPR, specifically claiming necessity for the performance of contracts with users and explicit consent obtained through updated terms of service. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined whether these derogations were appropriately applied, given the absence of an adequacy decision under Article 45 or appropriate safeguards like standard contractual clauses under Article 46 without additional measures. The investigation revealed that the transfers involved vast quantities of data from millions of data subjects on a repetitive basis, which did not align with the limited, non-repetitive nature required for certain derogations. Furthermore, the consent mechanism was found to lack sufficient information about risks associated with transfers to a third country lacking adequacy status, and the contract necessity argument was deemed insufficient for core social media functionalities that could theoretically be provided within the EEA.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC concluded its inquiry in December 2023, imposing a fine of 1.2 billion euros on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited for unlawful data transfers. The decision was part of a broader European Data Protection Board (EDPB) coordinated approach involving multiple supervisory authorities. Meta was ordered to cease the unlawful transfers within five months and bring all future transfers into compliance. Meta announced plans to appeal the decision.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: WhatsApp Data Transfer Fine
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Ltd., owned by Meta, transferred user personal data to the US for service provision and analytics. Following Schrems II, the company updated its terms but continued relying on Article 49 derogations including consent and contractual necessity. The Irish DPC probed whether users were adequately informed of third-country risks and if transfers were limited/non-repetitive as required for derogations without safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, following EDPB binding decision, the Irish DPC announced a 225 million euro fine for breaches including unlawful transfers under Article 49(1). WhatsApp was directed to align transfers with GDPR.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Transfers Compliance (Pre-Fine)
example_background: Microsoft, via Azure and Microsoft 365, transfers EU customer data to the US under standard contractual clauses supplemented by additional measures post-Schrems II. For cases without adequacy or SCCs, Microsoft documents use of Article 49 derogations sparingly, such as for legal claims defense (e.g., e-discovery) or public interest (government contracts), with assessments ensuring limited scope and data subject notifications.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft's transparency reports and EU Data Boundary initiatives demonstrate adherence; no major Article 49 fines to date, though under scrutiny. CNIL validated certain measures in 2023.

example_name: Oracle Data Transfers Investigation
example_background: Oracle Corporation's European cloud services involved transfers of customer personal data to the US. The Norwegian DPA investigated reliance on Privacy Shield (pre-Schrems II) and subsequent derogations, finding inadequate documentation for Article 49 applications in repetitive customer data syncing scenarios without explicit risk-informed consent.
process_and_outcome: Norway's Datatilsynet banned transfers in 2020 and referred to EDPB; Oracle suspended processing for Norwegian clients pending compliance. Part of broader EDPB Schrems II follow-up.

example_name: Amazon AWS Schrems II Transfers
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) Ireland faced scrutiny for EU-US data transfers in cloud services. While primarily using SCCs with supplements, investigations examined fallback to Article 49 derogations for specific customer-initiated transfers (e.g., backups), ensuring they met non-repetitive, limited criteria with supervisory notifications.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CNPD validated AWS's transfer toolkit in 2023 after inquiry, confirming compliance measures including documented Article 49 assessments where applicable. No fines issued.

example_name: H&M Employee Monitoring Fine
example_background: H&M operated a data processing center in Germany where employee private conversations were secretly recorded and stored, including sensitive details about personal lives, health, and family matters. This data was used for performance monitoring without employees' knowledge or consent.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg's data protection authority fined H&M Justice CV €35.3 million in October 2020. The company discontinued the practice and improved internal data handling policies.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of data)

example_name: Danish DPA Meta Consent Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms' 'Consent or Pay' model on Facebook and Instagram required EU users to either consent to extensive data processing for personalized ads or pay a fee for a less tailored experience. The Danish DPA determined this did not meet GDPR requirements for freely given, specific consent.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, a €276 million fine was imposed by the Danish Datatilsynet. Meta adjusted its consent banners across the EU.

example_name: EDPB-EU-US Data Privacy Framework Endorsement
example_background: Following the invalidation of Privacy Shield in Schrems II, the European Commission and US authorities negotiated a successor arrangement. The EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF) was adopted by the Commission on July 10, 2023, after assessing US surveillance reforms, including Executive Order 14086 establishing safeguards for EU data. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) reviewed and provided a positive opinion on the adequacy decision, enabling self-certification for US companies. This mechanism facilitates international cooperation by allowing data transfers with documented protections against third-country government access.
process_and_outcome: The adequacy decision was published in the EU Official Journal, allowing eligible US entities to self-certify compliance. The EDPB emphasized ongoing monitoring and cooperation mechanisms, including the Data Privacy Framework Executive Committee for dispute resolution.

example_name: Meta Platforms CNIL Fine for Invalid Data Transfers
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated Facebook (later Meta) for continuing to transfer EU user data to the US despite the Schrems II ruling invalidating Privacy Shield. CNIL found that Meta's reliance on updated Standard Contractual Clauses alone was insufficient without additional safeguards against US surveillance risks. The investigation involved analysis of data flows, supplementary measures implemented by Meta, and consultations with other EU supervisory authorities under the GDPR cooperation mechanism. This case underscored challenges in enforcing data protection across borders post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: On November 22, 2022, CNIL imposed a 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Meta was ordered to cease unlawful transfers and bring processing into compliance within five months.
involved_parties: French CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: GDPR One-Stop-Shop and Cooperation in TikTok Investigation
example_background: Multiple EU supervisory authorities coordinated under the GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism to investigate TikTok (ByteDance) for data processing practices, including transfers to China. The Irish DPC led as the main establishment, with input from 30+ authorities on issues like child data protection and third-country transfers. This demonstrated international cooperation within the EU, involving information exchange, mutual assistance, and joint decisions on global data flows.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC fined TikTok 345 million euros for child privacy violations, with decisions finalized after a dispute resolution by the EDPB. Additional investigations into adult data transfers continue.

example_name: EU-Japan Adequacy Decision
example_background: The European Commission assessed Japan's data protection framework, including the Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI), and found it ensures an adequate level of protection. This involved mutual consultations, information exchange on enforcement practices, and alignment on rights like access and rectification. Japan implemented supplementary rules for EU data, enabling frictionless transfers and serving as a model for third-country adequacy.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy decision adopted in July 2019, extended in 2023 after joint review, facilitating trade and data flows between the EU and Japan.

example_name: NOYB Coordinated Actions Against Meta Transfers
example_background: The None of Your Business (NOYB) network, led by Max Schrems, filed 101 complaints across 30 EU countries against Meta, Google, and others for unlawful data transfers to the US post-Schrems II. This initiative promoted cooperation among supervisory authorities through complaint referrals, investigative assistance, and information sharing under GDPR Article 61.
process_and_outcome: Led to multiple national investigations, fines totaling hundreds of millions, and EDPB interventions, with ongoing proceedings as of 2024.

example_name: Dutch DPA Investigation into Uber Transfers to US
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated Uber for transferring European driver data to the US under SCCs after Privacy Shield's invalidation. Uber implemented encryption and pseudonymization as supplementary measures. The case involved cooperation with the Irish DPC and analysis of US surveillance risks.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, Uber was fined 290 million euros for unlawful transfers from 2021-2022, ordered to comply with enhanced safeguards.

example_name: EU-South Korea Adequacy Mutual Recognition
example_background: The EU and South Korea established mutual adequacy recognition for data transfers. The Commission evaluated South Korea's Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) and enforcement by the PIPC, including cooperation agreements for investigative assistance and information exchange. This framework addresses jurisdictional issues and promotes alignment on data protection practices.
process_and_outcome: Adequacy decision adopted December 2020, with periodic reviews confirming ongoing equivalence and cooperation.
involved_parties: South Korea Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC)

example_name: DPC Fine Against WhatsApp Ireland
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited processed personal data of approximately 500 million EU users for behavioral advertising purposes without obtaining explicit user consent or providing sufficient transparency. The data processing involved sharing user data across Facebook group companies, which included transfers of significant portions of user profile data such as phone numbers, device information, and interactions. This occurred from May 2018, shortly after GDPR became applicable.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted an own-volition inquiry and issued a €225 million fine in September 2021. WhatsApp was ordered to bring its processing into compliance within three months. The decision was appealed, but upheld in part by the European Data Protection Board.

example_name: Amazon France Logistat GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon's vendor monitoring tool Logistat collected and transferred extensive personal data from merchants without proper legal basis or transparency. Data categories included sales volumes, transaction details, and inferred business information, processed and transferred across Amazon group entities.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €35 million fine on Amazon EU Sarl and €32 million on Amazon Services Europe in 2024. The companies were ordered to cease non-compliant processing and inform merchants.

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Fine 2021
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Meta Platforms Ireland, shared user data with other Facebook companies for advertising without proper user consent prompts. The practice affected European users from May 25, 2018, when GDPR took effect. Users received opt-out notices rather than explicit opt-in requests for data sharing, impacting transparency and choice.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2021 after an investigation initiated by complaints from privacy groups. WhatsApp contested the decision, leading to referrals to the European Data Protection Board.

example_name: H&M Data Protection Fine Germany 2020
example_background: H&M operated a service center in Nuremberg where employees noted detailed observations about returning customers' lifestyles, preferences, and issues, storing this data in a central database. Over 900 pages of notes on about 1,000 customers were found, including sensitive details like illnesses and private behaviors, without customer knowledge or consent.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner investigated and fined H&M Justice BV €35.3 million in October 2020. H&M discontinued the practice and deleted the data.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Orange Romania GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: Orange Romania advertised a service allowing customers to locate family members' phones without verifying the account holder's consent. The feature enabled tracking via GPS without proper age-appropriate checks or explicit authorization, potentially affecting minors and violating location data protections.
process_and_outcome: The Romanian National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing (ANSPDCP) investigated complaints and fined Orange Romania 50,000 RON (about €10,000) in 2021. Orange suspended the service and updated processes.
involved_parties: National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing (ANSPDCP)

example_name: TikTok EDPB Binding Decision on China Transfers
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating in the European Economic Area, transferred personal data of European users, including minors' behavioral data, location information, device identifiers, and content interactions, to servers and entities in China for algorithmic processing, content moderation, and product improvement. This occurred without an EU adequacy decision for China, relying instead on standard contractual clauses and alleged supplementary measures. The practice raised issues due to China's National Intelligence Law (2017), which mandates cooperation with state intelligence work, potentially allowing unrestricted access to transferred data by Chinese authorities without judicial oversight or proportionality requirements akin to EU standards. Following complaints by NOYB (None of Your Business), led by Max Schrems, multiple EU data protection authorities coordinated under GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism to investigate whether TikTok's transfer mechanisms provided adequate protection.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision in 2023 via the internal dispute resolution procedure, mandating the Irish DPC to impose corrective measures, including potential bans on new data transfers to China and verification of existing safeguards. TikTok faced ongoing investigations in several member states, leading to temporary suspensions of data processing activities and commitments to enhance encryption and pseudonymization. Fines and structured remediation plans were under consideration.

example_name: Meta EU-US Data Restoration Measures Compliance
example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling, Meta Platforms Ireland Limited developed and implemented supplementary measures for its transatlantic data transfers to Meta Platforms Inc. in the US. These included end-to-end encryption for certain data streams, statistical anonymization techniques reducing re-identification risks, and pseudonymization of user data before transfer. Meta also supplemented SCCs with a Data Processing Agreement specifying technical controls to protect against unauthorized access, particularly from public authority requests under US law. This was part of a broader compliance strategy after suspending transfers in 2020, aiming to restore services like Facebook and Instagram logins and feeds reliant on US-based processing.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC validated Meta's transfer mechanisms in 2023 after review, allowing resumption of transfers under the new safeguards. The EDPB did not object, and Meta publicly committed to ongoing audits. This enabled continued operations without bans, though subject to annual supervisory checks.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 46(1) - SCCs with supplementary measures

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Data Scraping Transfers
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based company, scraped billions of facial images from public websites worldwide, including EU sources, and stored them on US servers for sale to law enforcement. EU personal data, such as images linked to identifiable individuals from social media and news sites, was transferred to the US without consent or adequacy mechanisms. Reliance on alleged 'contractual necessity' failed scrutiny, as US CLOUD Act enabled law enforcement access to data stored by US providers.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU DPAs, coordinated by the EDPB, banned Clearview's processing in 2021-2022, with fines totaling over €30 million from France, Italy, Greece, etc. Dutch and Spanish courts upheld bans; Clearview withdrew from EU markets.

example_name: Oracle Cloud Transfers - Dutch DPA Fine
example_background: Oracle Corporation Netherlands transferred European customer data, including HR and CRM personal data, to US parent entities using pre-Schrems II SCCs without additional safeguards against US surveillance risks. Data included employee details processed via Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, accessible by US-based support and development teams.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Oracle €10 million in 2023 for unlawful transfers, ordering cessation and remediation. Oracle appealed but updated its transfer toolkit.

example_name: SAP SuccessFactors Transfers Compliance Measures
example_background: SAP SE, through its Irish subsidiary, operates SuccessFactors HCM cloud for EU employers, transferring employee personal data (performance reviews, payroll info) to US data centers. Post-Schrems II, SAP deployed updated SCCs, content delivery network optimizations keeping primary processing in EU regions, and advanced encryption with customer key management.
process_and_outcome: Validated by the Irish DPC in 2022 with no objections, enabling seamless service continuation for thousands of EU customers.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on WhatsApp
example_background: Multiple EU supervisory authorities initiated a cross-border investigation into WhatsApp Ireland Limited's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising. Concerns arose over the transparency of combining data from Facebook services and the validity of legitimate interests as a legal basis.
process_and_outcome: In August 2024, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision under the consistency mechanism, instructing the Irish DPC to impose a fine. The Irish DPC subsequently fined WhatsApp 225 million euros, demonstrating inter-authority cooperation.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Fine by CNIL
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of images from public websites to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement and private entities. The CNIL investigated after complaints about processing without consent or legal basis, affecting EU residents' biometric data.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Clearview AI 20 million euros in October 2022 for GDPR breaches. Additional fines were imposed by other authorities like the Dutch and Greek DPAs, showcasing coordinated enforcement.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (processing of special categories of data)

example_name: Schrems II Schrems II Case and EDPB Guidelines
example_background: The Irish DPC referenced the Court of Justice of the EU's Schrems II judgment (2020) in investigations of data transfers. The EDPB issued recommendations on supplementary measures for EU-US data transfers, promoting consistent application across Member States.
process_and_outcome: Following Schrems II, supervisory authorities issued guidance and enforced compliance, leading to multiple investigations and the development of standard contractual clauses with supplementary measures. This facilitated uniform application through EDPB coordination.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: EDPS GDPR Compliance Framework
example_background: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) established internal mechanisms to monitor and ensure GDPR compliance within EU institutions. This includes regular audits, data protection impact assessments, and cooperation with national authorities.
process_and_outcome: The EDPS published annual reports demonstrating adherence, with no major enforcement actions against EU bodies. This model supports consistent application and free flow of data within the Union.

example_name: Schrems II Case - EU-US Data Transfers Invalidated
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, filed complaints against Facebook Ireland for transferring personal data of European users to the United States, where data could be accessed by US intelligence agencies under surveillance laws such as Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12333. The case highlighted concerns over the adequacy of data protection in third countries outside the EU, particularly the lack of safeguards equivalent to those under EU law. Following the invalidation of the Safe Harbour framework in 2015 by the Schrems I judgment, the Privacy Shield was adopted as a replacement mechanism. Schrems challenged the new arrangement, arguing it suffered from similar deficiencies, including insufficient protections against government access to data. The complaint was processed through the Irish Data Protection Commission, which referred the matter to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) for a preliminary ruling. The case involved extensive analysis of US surveillance practices and their impact on EU data subjects' rights.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the CJEU ruled in Case C-311/18 that the EU-US Privacy Shield was invalid due to inadequate protections against US public authority access to EU personal data. The court also invalidated the standard contractual clauses mechanism in certain circumstances where the importing country's laws did not ensure equivalent protection, requiring case-by-case assessments. This led to widespread disruption in transatlantic data flows, prompting companies to adopt supplementary measures and the development of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU

example_name: EDPB-EU-US Data Privacy Framework Dialogue
example_background: In response to Schrems II, the EU and US engaged in high-level discussions to establish a new transatlantic data transfer arrangement. This involved negotiations between the European Commission, US Department of Commerce, and intelligence community to address deficiencies in US surveillance laws. Key elements included commitments under Executive Order 14086 to limit intelligence access to EU data, establish a Data Protection Review Court (DPRC), and provide redress mechanisms. The process incorporated input from the EDPB, civil society, and industry stakeholders to ensure alignment with GDPR standards. Regular exchanges documented practices and resolved jurisdictional conflicts.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, the European Commission adopted an adequacy decision for the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF), accompanied by a recommendation on supplementary measures. Companies can self-certify, with ongoing monitoring and cooperation between EU supervisory authorities and US Federal Trade Commission (FTC).

example_name: TikTok Netherlands Transfers Fine
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated TikTok Technology Netherlands B.V. for international data transfers of European users' personal data to China and other third countries. The probe focused on compliance with GDPR transfer tools post-Schrems II, including adequacy of risk assessments, use of SCCs, and safeguards against access by Chinese authorities under national intelligence laws. It covered data processing for content moderation, algorithmic training, and advertising, involving billions of data subjects.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, the AP imposed a 345 million euro fine for violations in handling children's data and unlawful transfers, requiring implementation of compliant mechanisms. TikTok appealed the decision.

example_name: Global Privacy Enforcement Network (GPEN) Cooperation Case
example_background: The GPEN, involving over 50 privacy authorities worldwide including EU members, UK ICO, and non-EU bodies like Canada's OPC and Australia's OAIC, facilitates mutual assistance under GDPR Article 61. A cross-border case involved a multinational company processing health data across EU, US, and Asian jurisdictions, where complaints about data breaches were referred between authorities.
process_and_outcome: Authorities exchanged information, coordinated investigations, and issued joint guidance. This resulted in consistent enforcement actions and remediation by the company without individual fines publicized, demonstrating effective cooperation.
involved_parties: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

example_name: EU-South Korea Adequacy Decision
example_background: The European Commission evaluated South Korea's Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) and enforcement by the Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC). This included reviews of data subject rights, cross-border transfer rules, and supplementary measures for sensitive data and public authority access, with stakeholder consultations to address jurisdictional differences.
process_and_outcome: In December 2021, adequacy status was granted (Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/2025), enabling mutual cooperation on enforcement and information exchange between EU and Korean authorities.

example_name: Amazon France Logistics Transfers Probe
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) examined Amazon France Logistique's transfers of employee personal data to the US for HR and performance management purposes. The inquiry assessed post-Schrems II compliance, including transfer impact assessments and effectiveness of supplementary measures against US surveillance.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, CNIL imposed a 32 million euro fine and ordered cessation of non-compliant transfers, with Amazon challenging the decision before the French Council of State.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: EDPB International Enforcement Cooperation Forum
example_background: The EDPB established forums and workshops with third-country authorities, such as those from Brazil (ANPD), Singapore, and India, to discuss enforcement challenges, share best practices on data protection laws, and address jurisdictional conflicts in multinational investigations involving cloud services and e-commerce.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing activities since 2018 have led to joint statements, information exchange protocols, and coordinated actions in several cross-border cases, enhancing global enforcement capacity.
involved_parties: Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (Brazil)

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: In 2021, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated WhatsApp Ireland Limited's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising purposes. The inquiry focused on the transparency of data processing operations, particularly the legal bases relied upon for sharing user data within the Meta group companies. WhatsApp's privacy policy was found to bundle multiple processing purposes without clear distinction, making it difficult for users to understand and consent to specific data uses. The investigation stemmed from complaints filed by privacy advocacy group noyb.eu across multiple EU member states.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC imposed a €225 million fine on WhatsApp for transparency infringements. The decision was challenged by WhatsApp, leading to interventions from other EU supervisory authorities. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision increasing the fine to €225 million, upheld by the General Court in 2024.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 (Information to be provided where personal data are collected)
GDPR Article 14 (Information to be provided where personal data have not been obtained from the data subject)

example_name: Amazon EU Data Transfers Fine by Luxembourg DPA
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., based in Luxembourg, was investigated for its data transfer practices to the United States. The processing involved personal data of EU users stored on AWS servers in the US and accessed by Amazon US entities for advertising purposes. Following the CJEU's Schrems II judgment, the Luxembourg data protection authority (CNDP) assessed whether Amazon implemented supplementary measures to ensure protection equivalent to EU standards for these transfers. The inquiry revealed insufficient safeguards for data transfers outside the EEA.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, CNDP issued a public statement confirming an ongoing investigation. In 2024, reports indicated potential fines up to 4% of global turnover were under consideration, though specific outcomes remain pending final decision.
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) / National Data Protection Authority (Luxembourg)

example_name: H&M Data Protection Fine by German DPA
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg subsidiary maintained a database of employee personal data gathered during offboarding interviews. The data included sensitive details about employees' private lives, health, family situations, and personal beliefs, collected from returning employees and stored for years to assist in onboarding new staff. Employees were not informed about this processing, and the data was accessible to hundreds of managers across the company. The practice came to light through internal whistleblowing in 2019.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian Data Protection Authority (BayLDA) investigated and in October 2020 fined H&M €35.25 million. H&M discontinued the practice, deleted the affected data, and implemented new compliance measures.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: EDPB TikTok Cross-Border Decision
example_background: Following complaints by NOYB regarding TikTok's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising, the Irish DPC's draft decision was reviewed under GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism. Other EU supervisory authorities objected to the scope, arguing TikTok Ireland acted as lead for all EU processing. The EDPB assessed whether TikTok's single set of privacy settings constituted uniform processing across the EEA.
process_and_outcome: In August 2023, EDPB issued a binding decision designating TikTok Ireland as lead supervisory authority and mandating a comprehensive investigation. This led to subsequent fines and underscores coordinated enforcement.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 56 (Competence of the lead supervisory authority)
GDPR Article 65 (Dispute resolution by the Board)

example_name: CNIL Meta Transfers Decision
example_background: CNIL ordered website publishers to stop using Meta pixel and Conversions API due to unlawful data transfers to the US. The tools tracked user behavior across sites, sending personal data like IP addresses and browsing activity to Meta US servers without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, following formal notices, CNIL found non-compliance and instructed cessation. This action influenced similar measures by other DPAs and prompted Meta to update its tools.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision 2021-001 - WhatsApp Ireland Processing
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, a subsidiary of Meta, updated its terms and conditions in 2021, combining user data across its services for advertising purposes. This affected over 1 billion EU users, raising concerns about transparent processing and compliance with GDPR principles. National supervisory authorities across multiple EU countries, including Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands, initiated investigations into whether the processing was lawful and if users were adequately informed. The cross-border nature necessitated coordinated action to ensure uniform application of GDPR rules on data processing transparency and legal basis for processing.
process_and_outcome: National authorities disagreed on approach, leading the Italian SA to request EDPB intervention under Article 65 GDPR. The EDPB issued a binding decision in August 2021, mandating all supervisory authorities to monitor WhatsApp's compliance and pursue enforcement uniformly, including potential fines up to 4% of global turnover. Subsequent fines were imposed by Ireland's DPC.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection (participating SA)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland FINE 2023
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp services in the EU, was investigated for violations related to international data transfers to the US following the Schrems II ruling. Despite the CJEU's invalidation of Privacy Shield, Meta continued transfers using SCCs without sufficiently verifying protection against US surveillance laws. The processing involved personal data of hundreds of millions of EU users for behavioral advertising and other purposes, impacting data subjects across all EU member states.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's DPC conducted the lead investigation with input from other EU SAs via the EDPB's cooperation procedures. In 2023, the DPC imposed a €1.2 billion fine, the largest GDPR penalty to date, and ordered Meta to cease unlawful transfers within five months and bring transfers into compliance. Meta appealed but complied partially.

example_name: TikTok Ireland Children’s Data Fine
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform in Europe, faced scrutiny over its age verification and data processing practices for users under 16. Investigations revealed default public settings for children's accounts, age-inappropriate content algorithmic promotion, and insufficient parental consent mechanisms. The platform's 150 million EU monthly users included significant numbers of minors, making it a cross-border processing activity requiring consistent enforcement across the EU.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's DPC, as lead authority, coordinated with other EU SAs under GDPR cooperation rules. In 2023, it imposed fines totaling €345 million for violations related to children's data, following EDPB input on consistency. TikTok implemented enhanced age assurance tools.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Articles 8, 25 (child consent, privacy by design)

example_name: OneTrust GDPR EDPB Cooperation Success
example_background: OneTrust, a US-based privacy management software provider, assisted numerous EU companies in achieving GDPR compliance through tools for data mapping, consent management, and cross-border transfer assessments. When faced with multi-jurisdictional compliance queries from clients, OneTrust facilitated coordination by providing standardized reporting templates used by supervisory authorities across the EU.
process_and_outcome: During various national audits, supervisory authorities from Germany, UK, and Spain used OneTrust's tools to verify consistent compliance, avoiding disputes and enabling efficient cooperation under Chapter VII. No major enforcement actions resulted, demonstrating effective proactive measures.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 8/2020 on Video Devices Processing
example_background: Following inquiries into facial recognition and CCTV use in public spaces by companies and public bodies across the EU, the EDPB developed guidelines to standardize interpretation of GDPR rules on video surveillance and biometric data processing. This addressed divergent national practices on consent, legitimate interest, and public authority exemptions.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted guidelines in 2020 after public consultation, providing a consistent framework used by all 27 EU SAs. This prevented fragmentation and supported joint operations, such as airport security deployments.

example_name: OneTrust Schrems II Compliance Mechanism Dispute
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, faced challenges in ensuring consistent application of data transfer mechanisms across EU member states following the 2020 Schrems II ruling by the Court of Justice of the European Union, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield. Multiple supervisory authorities in different Member States reviewed the company's updated Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and supplementary measures for transfers to the US. Disagreements arose on the adequacy of encryption and pseudonymization measures, leading to divergent interpretations on compliance requirements. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated proceedings, while other authorities like the French CNIL and German BfDI provided input through the consistency mechanism under Article 63 GDPR. This case highlighted coordination needs among the 27 national supervisory authorities to maintain uniform application of GDPR rules on international data transfers.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) activated the consistency mechanism, issuing binding decisions in 2021-2022 on supplementary measures. OneTrust revised its tools and processes to align with EDPB recommendations. No fines were imposed, but the company publicly committed to ongoing audits and updates to its transfer tools, demonstrating resolution through Board intervention.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC - lead authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Cross-Border Processing Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, part of Meta Platforms, engaged in behavioral advertising based on user data processing across EU Member States. In 2021, the Irish DPC investigated transparency and legal basis for processing after complaints from NOYB (None of Your Business). Parallel investigations by the Luxembourg CNPDP, Dutch AP, and others identified discrepancies in how data sharing with Meta US was handled, prompting questions on consistent application of GDPR rules for controller-processor relationships and international transfers. The case underscored the need for a designated lead authority to coordinate with others via the consistency mechanism.
process_and_outcome: Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs) objected to the Irish DPC's draft decision, invoking Article 65. In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision mandating a €1.2 billion fine on Meta for unlawful transfers and requiring suspension of US data transfers until compliant SCCs were in place. Meta paid the fine and restructured data flows.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Facebook Behavioural Advertising
example_background: Meta's Facebook platform processed vast amounts of personal data for behavioral advertising, integrating off-Facebook activity data. The Irish DPC's 2018-2022 investigation followed NOYB complaints on consent and legitimate interest grounds. Authorities in Germany (BayLDA), Austria, and others disagreed with the DPC's initial no-violation stance on legitimate interest, citing inconsistent enforcement of data protection principles across Member States. This led to activation of the consistency mechanism to ensure uniform GDPR application.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the EDPB overruled the DPC via binding decision, fining Meta €405 million and prohibiting behavioural advertising based on profiling. Meta adjusted its ad systems to comply with clarified consent requirements across the EEA.

example_name: Google Analytics and Consent Mode Coordination
example_background: Google's Analytics and Ad Services faced scrutiny in 2022 after Austrian NOYB complaints alleged unlawful transfers to the US without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II. The Austrian DSB issued orders, prompting similar actions by Italian, Belgian, and other DPAs. Divergent decisions on whether consent mode implementations met GDPR standards led to consistency mechanism invocation to harmonize views on transfer tools across Member States.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued recommendations in 2023, leading to temporary suspensions in several countries. Google updated its tools with IP anonymization and consent banners, achieving compliance alignment without unified fines.

example_name: Amazon EU-US Data Transfers Investigation
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Amazon Europe handled customer and seller data with flows to the US for cloud processing and analytics. Initiated by the Luxembourg CNPDP in 2021 after NOYB complaints, the probe revealed potential issues with SCCs and adequacy of safeguards. French CNIL and other DPAs joined, highlighting risks of inconsistent transfer assessments across Member States with multiple authorities.
process_and_outcome: The consistency mechanism was engaged; in 2023, Luxembourg DPA proposed a €746 million fine (later adjusted). Amazon enhanced encryption and audit rights, with EDPB overseeing uniform implementation.

example_name: EDPB Urgency Procedure for Planet49 Consent Banners
example_background: The Planet49 case originated from a German court referral on cookie consent banners used on websites. Multiple DPAs reviewed similar practices across Member States, where websites displayed pre-ticked boxes for third-party trackers. Variations in enforcement approaches prompted the EDPB to use the urgency procedure under the consistency mechanism to provide rapid, uniform guidance.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, EDPB issued urgent binding guidance requiring granular opt-in consent without pre-ticked boxes. Companies like Planet49 updated banners EU-wide, avoiding fragmented litigation.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cross-Border Coordination
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a breach exposing 400,000 customers' data via a Magecart attack on its website. The UK ICO led the investigation, but post-Brexit transition and ongoing EU operations involved input from Irish DPC and others on consistent breach notification timelines and compensation rules under GDPR for multinational carriers.
process_and_outcome: EDPB facilitated coordination, resulting in a £20 million fine by ICO in 2020 (reduced from £183m on appeal). BA enhanced security with multi-factor authentication and regular pentests.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision against French CNIL in Google Analytics Case
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) ordered Google LLC to stop processing personal data transferred from France via Google Analytics tools due to insufficient safeguards post-Schrems II. Other EU supervisory authorities challenged this under the GDPR's consistency mechanism, arguing that the CNIL's unilateral action affected similar processing across the EU without prior consultation or cooperation. The case involved scrutiny of IP addresses as personal data and adequacy of transfer tools amid US surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: In August 2022, the EDPB issued a binding decision under Article 65, requiring the CNIL to take a final position considering arguments from concerned authorities. The CNIL amended its order but faced further proceedings, highlighting tensions in cross-border enforcement.
involved_parties: European Data Protection Board (binding decision issuer)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 51(1) - Independence of supervisory authorities

example_name: Meta GDPR Fine by Luxembourg CNPD
example_background: Meta Platforms Inc., through its Luxembourg subsidiary, handled behavioral advertising based on extensive profiling of EU users' personal data. The Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) examined the legal bases for processing, finding reliance on consent inadequate due to bundling with service terms and lack of granularity.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the CNPD fined Meta €250 million following its investigation, amending prior decisions after EDPB input, with Meta contesting via administrative appeal.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 51(1) - Independence of supervisory authorities
GDPR Article 6 - Lawful basis for processing

example_name: EDPB Dispute Resolution in Meta Schrems I Case
example_background: Following Max Schrems' complaint, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's EU-US data transfers under the Privacy Shield. Other authorities invoked the consistency mechanism, claiming the DPC delayed action and failed to independently pursue systemic issues affecting all EU data subjects.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2020 directing the DPC to suspend transfers, leading to the ultimate invalidation of Privacy Shield by the CJEU in Schrems II.

example_name: Italian Garante Fine on Google Analytics Transfers
example_background: The Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali ordered two websites to halt data transfers to Google via Analytics tools, citing IP addresses as personal data inadequately protected against US laws.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the authority issued cessation orders independently, prompting challenges and contributing to the broader EDPB Google Analytics dispute.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Meta Fine for Privacy Shield Use
example_background: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos investigated Meta's continued reliance on Privacy Shield for EU-US data flows post-Schrems II, affecting Spanish users' data.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, AEPD fined Meta Platforms Ireland €12 million and ordered suspension of processing without adequate safeguards.

example_name: UK ICO Meta Biometric Data Fine
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office examined Meta's processing of over 50 million UK users' biometric data from photo tagging without explicit consent or necessity assessment.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, ICO proposed a £20.7 million fine (later adjusted), enforcing independently post-Brexit outside EU mechanisms.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Employee Access Fine
example_background: Amazon Europe Core faced issues with its internal access controls, allowing over 3,000 employees unrestricted access to millions of EU customers' personal data, including emails, addresses, and voice recordings from Alexa devices. The lack of need-to-know principles meant employees could view data unrelated to their roles, increasing risks of misuse or breaches.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in July 2021, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Amazon appealed to the Court of Justice, which in 2024 referred questions back to national courts. Amazon continued operating under scrutiny and improved access governance.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) Luxembourg

example_name: Italian Garante Investigation into Members' Independence
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority, known as Garante per la protezione dei dati personali, faced questions regarding the independence of its members in decision-making processes related to GDPR enforcement. Concerns arose from allegations that certain members had prior professional ties to entities under investigation, potentially influencing outcomes in high-profile cases involving large technology companies and data processing activities. This situation highlighted tensions between member appointments and the requirement for complete independence from external influences, as outlined in GDPR provisions. The authority was tasked with handling complaints and investigations into data breaches and unlawful processing, but public and stakeholder scrutiny focused on whether members remained free from direct or indirect instructions during their tenure.
process_and_outcome: The Garante conducted an internal review and affirmed its operational independence, with no formal sanctions imposed. Members recused themselves from relevant cases, and the authority continued its enforcement activities, issuing fines in unrelated matters to demonstrate functionality.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italian DPA)

example_name: French CNIL Resource Allocation Dispute
example_background: The Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL), France's supervisory authority, encountered challenges in securing adequate human, technical, and financial resources as required under GDPR. Reports indicated understaffing and budget constraints that limited the authority's capacity to handle increasing volumes of complaints, conduct investigations into cross-border data processing by multinational firms, and participate effectively in the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). This situation persisted despite growing enforcement needs post-GDPR implementation, affecting timely responses to issues like cookie consent violations and data breach notifications from sectors such as telecommunications and e-commerce.
process_and_outcome: The French government increased funding following public advocacy and EDPB recommendations, allowing CNIL to hire additional staff and upgrade infrastructure. CNIL subsequently ramped up enforcement, issuing multimillion-euro fines to companies like Google and Amazon.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Supervisory Authority Independence in Spain
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) was subject to an EDPB opinion following complaints about potential external influences on its decision-making. Political appointments to the authority's leadership raised concerns that members might not remain free from instructions, particularly in cases involving public sector data processing and enforcement against private entities. This came amid high-profile investigations into data breaches at banks and healthcare providers, where delays and perceived leniency fueled debates on adherence to independence standards.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued a non-binding opinion recommending structural reforms. Spain amended its data protection law to enhance appointment transparency, and AEPD proceeded with enforcement actions, fining entities for GDPR infringements.

example_name: Irish DPC Annual Budgeting and Financial Control Case
example_background: The Data Protection Commission (DPC) in Ireland, responsible for supervising many tech giants' EU operations, faced criticism over its financial control mechanisms and budget adequacy. Stakeholders noted that integration into the national budget potentially exposed the DPC to governmental pressures, impacting its ability to investigate complex cases like international data transfers and ad tech practices involving companies such as Meta and WhatsApp. This occurred during a period of intense scrutiny following major data scandals.
process_and_outcome: Following EDPB and advocate pressure, Ireland provided the DPC with a separate public annual budget line item. The DPC increased staff and issued record fines, including €1.2 billion against Meta in 2023.

example_name: UK ICO Post-Brexit Independence Reforms
example_background: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) in the UK underwent structural changes after Brexit to align with GDPR-equivalent standards on independence. Prior concerns about ministerial oversight in appointing the commissioner and directing investigations into public and private sector data practices prompted reviews. This was relevant to handling complaints on data breaches in healthcare (NHS) and retail sectors.
process_and_outcome: The UK government enacted reforms via the Data Protection and Digital Information Bill, enhancing the ICO's staff selection autonomy and budget separation. The ICO maintained enforcement, fining British Airways £20 million for a data breach.
involved_parties: UK Department for Digital, Culture, Media & Sport
applicable_regulations_or_policies: UK GDPR (retained EU law), Article 52 equivalent

example_name: Dutch DPA Staff Direction and Independence Review
example_background: The Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP), Netherlands' DPA, was reviewed for ensuring its staff operated under exclusive direction of authority members, free from external influences. Allegations surfaced during investigations into data processing by government agencies and tech firms, suggesting interference in staffing decisions that could affect enforcement on issues like facial recognition and health data.
process_and_outcome: An internal audit confirmed compliance, with staff appointments remaining under AP control. The AP continued operations, imposing fines on Uber and others for data protection shortfalls.
involved_parties: Dutch Ministry of Justice and Security

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Enforcement on Resource Adequacy
example_background: The EDPB initiated a coordinated effort to assess GDPR supervisory authorities across EU member states for resource sufficiency. Several DPAs, including those in smaller states like Malta and Cyprus, reported insufficient technical infrastructure for mutual assistance in cross-border cases involving social media platforms and cloud services.
process_and_outcome: EDPB published a report recommending EU funding mechanisms. Affected DPAs received grants, improving cooperation and leading to joint fines against TikTok (€345 million).
involved_parties: Multiple EU DPAs (e.g., Maltese IDPC, Cypriot DPA)

example_name: German BfDI Independence During Political Pressure
example_background: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI) in Germany faced political pressure during investigations into federal police data processing practices under GDPR. Questions arose about the commissioner's ability to act independently without instructions from government bodies, especially in sensitive national security-related cases.
process_and_outcome: BfDI issued critical reports and recommendations, maintaining its stance. No sanctions were applied, and it continued oversight, influencing GDPR-aligned reforms in law enforcement data handling.
involved_parties: German Federal Ministry of the Interior

example_name: Belgian DPA Incompatible Occupation Conflict
example_background: A member of the Belgian Data Protection Authority's litigation chamber held a concurrent advisory role with a consulting firm involved in data compliance services, raising issues under GDPR rules prohibiting incompatible occupations during term. This occurred amid the authority's handling of cases against telecom operators for data retention practices.
process_and_outcome: The member resigned following an ethics review, and the authority reaffirmed its conflict-of-interest policies. Enforcement continued unabated.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Belgian Law of 30 July 2018 on Data Protection

example_name: Christopher Graham Outside Appointments Post-ICO Tenure
example_background: Christopher Graham served as the UK Information Commissioner from 2009 to 2016, overseeing enforcement of data protection laws including the Data Protection Act 1998, which preceded GDPR. During his tenure, he managed supervisory responsibilities for privacy complaints and regulatory guidance. After leaving office in 2016, he took up roles such as chair of the Advertising Standards Authority and other positions in sectors related to data handling and consumer protection. These appointments raised questions about potential conflicts given his prior access to sensitive information and influence over data protection standards in the UK.
process_and_outcome: No formal enforcement action was taken during his term, but post-tenure disclosures were made public through parliamentary inquiries and media reports. The case prompted discussions on cooling-off periods for former regulators, leading to updated UK government guidelines on appointments for public officials.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: UK Civil Service Code on conflicts of interest
GDPR Article 52(4) on supervisory authority independence

example_name: Polish SA Giovanni Buttarelli Appointment Controversy
example_background: Giovanni Buttarelli was appointed as the inaugural European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) in 2014 by the European Parliament, Council, and Commission. Prior to this, he served as Assistant Supervisor since 2009 and had a background in Italian data protection authority roles. His appointment involved scrutiny over prior professional engagements in legal and advisory capacities related to EU institutions, ensuring no ongoing occupations conflicted with supervisory duties under the emerging GDPR framework.
process_and_outcome: The appointment was approved after review by EU bodies, with no prohibitions issued. Buttarelli served until his passing in 2019, during which the EDPS issued opinions on GDPR implementation, maintaining operational independence.

example_name: Dutch DPA Member Resignation Over Conflict Concerns
example_background: In 2019, a member of the Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) faced internal review after disclosures of prior consulting work with technology firms subject to DPA oversight. The individual had engaged in advisory roles on data processing before appointment, prompting evaluation of compatibility with ongoing supervisory duties under GDPR, which mandates DPA members avoid actions or occupations incompatible with their roles.
process_and_outcome: The member resigned voluntarily to address perceived conflicts, as announced by the DPA. This led to internal policy reinforcements on member declarations and recusal protocols.

example_name: EDPB Member Declaration of Interests Framework
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB), established under GDPR, requires all members from national DPAs to submit annual declarations of interests. This process ensures transparency regarding external occupations, shareholdings, or activities that could influence supervisory decisions on cross-border data protection cases. Since 2018, the EDPB has published these declarations publicly to uphold independence standards.
process_and_outcome: Declarations are reviewed annually with no major conflicts reported to date; the framework has been upheld as a best practice, facilitating EDPB guidelines on topics like automated decision-making without impartiality issues.

example_name: Irish DPC Commissioner Nomination Review
example_background: Helen Dixon was nominated as Commissioner of the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) in 2014, prior to full GDPR enforcement. Her background included legal practice in data privacy advisory for corporations, which underwent parliamentary vetting to confirm no ongoing incompatible occupations. She recused from cases involving prior clients during her tenure, managing high-profile investigations like those involving US tech firms.
process_and_outcome: Nomination approved by Irish Parliament after conflict checks; Dixon served through GDPR rollout, leading to significant fines while maintaining declared recusals.

example_name: French CNIL Board Member External Role Prohibition
example_background: In 2020, the French data protection authority CNIL identified a board member with concurrent involvement in a private data consulting firm. The member's role included advising on GDPR compliance for clients under CNIL jurisdiction, which was deemed an occupation incompatible with supervisory duties as it could influence decision-making impartiality.
process_and_outcome: CNIL enforced internal rules requiring the member to cease the external role; the decision was documented in CNIL's annual report, strengthening member appointment criteria.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: French Data Protection Act (Loi Informatique et Libertés)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Independence Safeguards
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) implemented strict protocols for its council members, including mandatory disclosure of all professional activities upon appointment. Members are prohibited from gainful occupations during their 5-year non-renewable terms, with examples including former lawyers who fully disengaged from private practice to focus on supervisory tasks like fining non-compliant entities.
process_and_outcome: Annual audits confirm compliance; AEPD has issued over 100 GDPR fines since 2018 without conflict-based challenges to its decisions.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: German BfDI Member Conflict Review
example_background: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI) in Germany conducts pre-appointment reviews for its leadership. In one instance in 2017, a nominee with prior tech industry board seats was required to resign those positions before assuming duties, ensuring no ongoing incompatible occupations amid GDPR preparation.
process_and_outcome: Nominee complied, appointment proceeded; BfDI continued oversight of federal data processing without reported impartiality issues.
involved_parties: Bundesbeauftragter für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI)

example_name: Belgian DPA Director Suspension Over Side Business
example_background: In 2021, the director of the Belgian Data Protection Authority (GBA/APD) was found to hold an undeclared side occupation providing GDPR training to private companies. This activity involved clients potentially subject to GBA enforcement, creating a perceived incompatibility with supervisory duties.
process_and_outcome: Internal investigation led to suspension and termination; GBA updated its code of conduct, mandating stricter prior approvals for external activities.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Belgian Law of 30 July 2018 on Data Protection

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into WhatsApp Transfers (Meta Platforms Ireland)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the lead supervisory authority for WhatsApp in the EU under the one-stop-shop mechanism, faced scrutiny over its handling of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) following the Schrems II judgment. In 2020, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an own-volition inquiry into WhatsApp's data transfers from the EU/EEA to the US after a complaint from Max Schrems via noyb. The inquiry examined whether WhatsApp's reliance on SCCs provided adequate safeguards for personal data transfers, particularly in light of US surveillance laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and EO 12333. During the process, the DPC proposed a draft decision in August 2021 finding no breach, but other concerned supervisory authorities (CSAs) strongly objected, arguing that the DPC was influenced by procedural delays and insufficient consideration of systemic transfer issues. The CSAs contended that the DPC's approach risked undermining uniform GDPR application across the EU. This led to a dispute resolution under the GDPR cooperation mechanism, highlighting tensions in cross-border enforcement coordination.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision on 28 August 2023, overturning the DPC's draft and requiring it to amend its decision to find WhatsApp in violation of Articles 46(1) and 46(2)(c) GDPR. The DPC issued a final decision on 5 September 2023, prohibiting data transfers unless supplemented by additional measures, and imposed a 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta. Meta appealed the fine to the Irish courts, arguing procedural unfairness.

example_name: Amazon EU-US Transfers Dispute
example_background: Following Schrems II, the French CNIL investigated Amazon Europe's data transfers to the US. Amazon relied on SCCs for cloud services and advertising data. The CNIL found insufficient safeguards against US intelligence access. Amazon challenged the preliminary findings, leading to a dispute with other CSAs who supported stronger enforcement. The case escalated to EDPB after the CNIL proposed a prohibition order.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2023 upheld the prohibition on transfers without additional measures. CNIL formalized the order; Amazon implemented mitigations and appealed.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta's Facebook Transfers
example_background: noyb filed complaints against Meta in multiple EU countries regarding EU-US data transfers post-Schrems II. The lead Irish DPC handled the cross-border case but proposed no violation in its draft decision, prompting objections from CSAs like the Norwegian DPA. The dispute centered on whether Meta's 'consent or pay' model and SCCs complied with GDPR transfer requirements.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued Binding Decision 2/2023, finding violations and mandating the DPC to prohibit transfers and fine Meta up to 4% of global turnover. DPC complied with final decision in 2023.

example_name: French CNIL Meta Biometric Data Fine
example_background: CNIL probed Meta for processing biometric data via photo tagging on Facebook and Instagram without explicit consent. The investigation followed complaints and focused on automated recognition technologies. Meta contested findings, leading to internal EU DPA coordination.
process_and_outcome: 2022 fine of 20 million euros issued by CNIL. Meta discontinued the feature in EU.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Enforcement on Data Transfers (General Framework)
example_background: Post-Schrems II, EDPB issued Recommendations 01/2020 on supplementary measures for data transfers, promoting uniform application. Various DPAs applied this without disputes, demonstrating independent assessment free from external pressures.
process_and_outcome: Multiple controllers adopted additional measures like encryption and pseudonymization, avoiding enforcement actions and ensuring compliance.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Transfers Compliance (Post-EDPB Guidance)
example_background: Microsoft updated its EU-US data transfer mechanisms for Azure and Office 365 services following EDPB recommendations, implementing technical measures like data localization options and transparency reports on US access requests.
process_and_outcome: No major enforcement actions; Microsoft certified compliance under EU-US Data Privacy Framework and passed multiple DPA audits.

example_name: Dismissal of CNIL President Isabelle Falque-Pierrotin
example_background: In France, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) is the supervisory authority under GDPR. Isabelle Falque-Pierrotin served as president from 2011 until her term ended. The appointment and oversight of CNIL members, including the president, fall under the responsibility of the French government and parliament through specific legislative procedures outlined in Law No. 78-17 on data processing, data files and individual liberties, as adapted for GDPR compliance. Her successor was selected following a process involving the Prime Minister's proposal and approval by the National Assembly and Senate. This transition occurred as part of regular term limits, ensuring continuity in leadership with members possessing legal and data protection expertise.
process_and_outcome: The term concluded naturally in 2019, leading to the appointment of Marie-Laure Denis as the new president through parliamentary hearings and government decree. No disputes arose, and the authority continued operations without interruption.

example_name: Appointment of Wojciech Wiewiórowski as EDPS
example_background: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) oversees EU institutions' compliance with data protection rules.
