The ministry justified the processing as required by national legislation for identification and border control, but the supervisory authority examined whether the data retention periods and access controls aligned with data minimization principles.
process_and_outcome: The Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali conducted an investigation following complaints and its own inspections. It determined that the processing exceeded what was strictly necessary under the legal basis and imposed a fine of 2 million euros on the Ministry of Interior. The authority ordered the ministry to revise its data retention policies and implement stricter access logs. The ministry appealed the decision, but it was upheld.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (supervisory authority)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Decision on Local Police Body
example_background: A local police department in Spain processed CCTV footage and personal data of individuals in public spaces for crime prevention, relying on Article 6(1)(c) GDPR as mandated by regional public security laws. The processing involved continuous video surveillance without clear signage or defined retention periods, capturing data of passersby not suspected of any wrongdoing. The authority assessed whether the public body's actions as a state entity under point (c) correctly invoked national supervisory competence, excluding cross-border mechanisms under Article 56.
process_and_outcome: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) investigated after public complaints. It found the processing lacked adequate legal basis specification and transparency, issuing a warning and ordering the deletion of unlawfully retained footage. No fine was imposed due to corrective actions taken during the probe.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Organic Law 3/2018 on Personal Data Protection and Guarantee of Digital Rights (LOPDGDD)

example_name: Dutch DPA Guidance on Municipal Tax Authority Processing
example_background: A Dutch municipality's tax authority processed residents' financial data for property tax assessments, basing the processing on Article 6(1)(c) GDPR due to obligations under the Municipal Taxes Act. The authority handled large volumes of income, property ownership, and family composition data shared between departments. The Dutch DPA reviewed the setup to ensure competence remained national, as the public authority operated under domestic legal mandates without triggering one-stop-shop rules.
process_and_outcome: The Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (DPA) audited the municipality as part of broader public sector compliance checks. It confirmed proper application of the competence rule and provided guidance on data sharing protocols, noting no breaches. The municipality enhanced its data protection impact assessments (DPIAs) voluntarily.

example_name: French CNIL Sanction on Regional Health Agency
example_background: A French regional health agency processed patient data for public health monitoring during the COVID-19 pandemic, invoking Article 6(1)(c) GDPR for legal obligations under public health codes. This included contact tracing app data integration and vaccination record management. The processing by the public body raised questions on data minimization and cross-border applicability, with the CNIL asserting sole competence as the relevant Member State authority.
process_and_outcome: The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) investigated privacy complaints regarding data retention beyond pandemic needs. It imposed a 75,000 euro fine for excessive storage periods and mandated protocol updates, reinforcing national supervisory competence.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: French Public Health Code (Code de la Santé Publique)

example_name: German BfDI Ruling on Federal Police Data Processing
example_background: The German Federal Police processed passenger name record (PNR) data for border security, relying on Article 6(1)(c) and (e) GDPR as a public authority under the Federal Police Act. This involved automated analysis of travel data for risk assessment. The Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI) evaluated whether the processing correctly fell under national competence, excluding lead authority mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Following a court referral and complaints, the BfDI ruled the processing partially unlawful due to inadequate proportionality assessments. It ordered suspension of certain automated tools and data deletion, but upheld the competence framework. No direct fine was issued.

example_name: Irish DPC Case on Revenue Commissioners
example_background: Ireland's Revenue Commissioners, a public tax authority, processed taxpayer data for compliance checks and audits, grounding activities in Article 6(1)(c) GDPR via the Taxes Consolidation Act. This included sharing data with other public bodies for debt recovery. The Data Protection Commission reviewed the framework to confirm Irish supervisory competence applied without Article 56 cross-border provisions.
process_and_outcome: The DPC conducted a sector inquiry and found the processing aligned with requirements. It issued non-binding guidance praising the authority's transparency measures, such as public registers of processing activities, serving as a compliance model.

example_name: Polish UODO Fine on Border Guard
example_background: The Polish Border Guard processed biometric and personal data of third-country nationals at border crossings, based on Article 6(1)(c) GDPR and national border security laws. The system stored fingerprints and photos for entry refusals. The President of the Personal Data Protection Office examined competence and compliance during increased migration flows.
process_and_outcome: Urzędnik Ochrony Danych Osobowych (UODO) investigated data breaches reported in 2022. It fined the Border Guard 1 million PLN for insecure storage leading to unauthorized access and ordered system upgrades, applying national competence rules.

example_name: Austrian DSB Approval for Social Welfare Agency
example_background: An Austrian provincial social welfare agency processed sensitive data on benefit recipients, including health and financial details, under Article 6(1)(c) and (e) GDPR as a public body tasked with social assistance. Processing involved case management systems shared with partner agencies. The Datenschutzbehörde (DSB) assessed the setup for national competence.
process_and_outcome: The DSB reviewed a DPIA submission and approved the processing activities, confirming adherence to principles and providing recommendations for pseudonymization. The agency implemented enhancements, demonstrating effective compliance.

example_name: Belgian APD Investigation of Municipal Registry Office
example_background: A Belgian municipal civil registry office processed birth, marriage, and death records, relying on Article 6(1)(c) GDPR for legal public record-keeping duties. Issues emerged with third-party data sharing for commercial purposes without proper basis. The authority confirmed competence lay with the Belgian supervisory body.
process_and_outcome: The Autorité de Protection des Données (APD) probed following a complaint. It issued an administrative fine of 25,000 euros for improper sharing and required cessation of non-essential disclosures, upholding Article 55 competence.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Court Data Processing (2020)
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission received a complaint regarding the processing of personal data by the Courts Service of Ireland in the context of judicial proceedings. The complainant raised concerns about the handling of sensitive personal information during court case management systems. The investigation focused on whether the supervisory authority had jurisdiction over data processing activities conducted by courts when acting in their judicial capacity. The Courts Service maintained that their operations fell under judicial functions protected from external supervision. This case arose from broader implementation challenges of GDPR across EU member states where judicial independence intersects with data protection requirements. Detailed examination included review of court IT systems used for case tracking, access logs, and data retention practices during active judicial processes.
process_and_outcome: The DPC determined that it lacked competence to supervise the processing operations of the courts acting in their judicial capacity, closing the investigation without further action. No enforcement measures were applied, affirming the exemption under GDPR Article 55(3).

example_name: French CNIL Ruling on Judicial Data Systems (2019)
example_background: A data subject filed a complaint with the French data protection authority (CNIL) concerning the processing of personal data within the French Ministry of Justice's case management platform used by courts. The platform handled data for criminal and civil proceedings, including biometric and health information. The complainant sought access and rectification rights. CNIL's preliminary assessment involved analyzing the scope of judicial activities, such as scheduling hearings, evidence storage, and judgment dissemination. This highlighted tensions between individual data rights and the operational autonomy of judicial bodies in processing data integral to legal proceedings across France's court network.
process_and_outcome: CNIL declared itself incompetent to supervise the processing due to the courts' judicial capacity, referring the matter to internal judicial oversight mechanisms. No fines or orders were issued.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: UK ICO Guidance on Courts Exemption (2021)
example_background: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) issued guidance following inquiries about data processing in court administration. Multiple stakeholders questioned the application of data protection rights to court records, including judgments published online and data shared between courts and law enforcement. The guidance detailed scenarios where courts process data for judicial decision-making, such as trial preparations and sentencing, emphasizing structural independence. This was informed by post-Brexit adaptations of GDPR into UK law, with examples from family courts handling child welfare data and criminal courts managing offender records.
process_and_outcome: ICO confirmed supervisory authorities cannot oversee courts' judicial processing, providing public guidance and resolving related complaints without intervention.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Case on Judicial Processing (2022)
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) received a complaint about personal data handling in a regional court's digital filing system. The system processed data for ongoing litigation, including plaintiff and defendant details, witness statements, and expert reports. The investigation reviewed access controls and data sharing with judicial police. Context included Spain's decentralized court structure where regional tribunals manage high volumes of civil, administrative, and penal cases, raising questions on supervisory oversight amid digital transformation initiatives.
process_and_outcome: AEPD ruled it lacked competence over the court's judicial processing activities, dismissing the case and noting internal judicial remedies available.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: German BfDI Statement on Court Data (2020)
example_background: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (BfDI) addressed queries regarding data processing by German federal courts in constitutional matters. Cases involved processing of personal data in landmark rulings on privacy rights, where court databases stored submissions, hearings transcripts, and ancillary documents. The context stemmed from increased digitization of court proceedings under the German judicial digital strategy, prompting clarity on supervisory boundaries.
process_and_outcome: BfDI affirmed no supervisory competence over courts' judicial capacity processing, issuing a formal statement reinforcing the exemption.
involved_parties: Bundesbeauftragter für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI)

example_name: Italian Garante Decision on Judicial Exemption (2021)
example_background: Italy's Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali investigated a complaint about data retention in court archives for resolved cases. The archives included digitized files from civil disputes involving personal financial data. The probe examined workflows from case initiation to archival storage, within Italy's court system handling millions of proceedings annually.
process_and_outcome: Garante declared incompetence to supervise judicial processing, upholding the court's autonomy and closing the matter.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 1/2020 on Court Competence (2020)
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) developed guidelines following coordinated questions from national authorities on the scope of Article 55(3). Examples included processing by supreme courts in appeals, administrative tribunals in public law disputes, and specialized courts handling labor or immigration matters. The guidelines provided criteria to distinguish judicial from administrative functions, drawing from diverse EU court structures.
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted guidelines confirming supervisory authorities' lack of competence over judicial processing, adopted by consensus without enforcement actions.

example_name: Dutch AP Ruling on Raad van State (2022)
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) assessed a complaint concerning data processing by the Council of State, the highest administrative court. The case involved environmental permit appeals where personal data of applicants was processed in public hearings and decisions. Context included the court's role in reviewing government decisions, with digital platforms for submissions.
process_and_outcome: AP determined no supervisory competence due to judicial capacity, archiving the complaint.

example_name: Belgian APD Dismissal of Court Complaint (2021)
example_background: Belgium's Data Protection Authority (APD) handled a complaint about personal data in a criminal court database shared with probation services. The database tracked offender rehabilitation data during judicial oversight periods. The investigation considered Belgium's federal court system and data flows in penal enforcement.
process_and_outcome: APD ruled itself incompetent for judicial processing, directing to judicial channels.

example_name: French CNIL Action against Google LLC on Transfers
example_background: Google LLC conducted data transfers from France to the United States for its advertising services, processing personal data of European users. The transfers used Standard Contractual Clauses post-Schrems II. The French data protection authority, CNIL, received complaints from NOYB regarding the sufficiency of safeguards against US surveillance laws. CNIL assessed its competence as the supervisory authority for processing activities affecting French residents, despite Google LLC being based outside the EU. The investigation focused on whether the lead supervisory authority mechanism under Article 56 applied or if CNIL had independent competence for urgent action.
process_and_outcome: On 21 June 2022, CNIL issued formal notice to Google to provide details on transfer safeguards. After non-compliance, CNIL fined Google €150 million on 10 October 2022 for cookie consent violations but continued the transfer probe. CNIL imposed a €60 million fine on 25 March 2024 for inadequate transfer protections, exercising its competence independently. Google was ordered to comply within two months.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 66 (urgent action by supervisory authority)

example_name: Dutch DPA Fine on Clearview AI Transfers
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based company, offered facial recognition services involving the processing and transfer of biometric data scraped from public websites worldwide, including EU residents' images. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) investigated complaints about the lawfulness of offering these services in the Netherlands, where data of Dutch residents was processed and transferred to the US. The company had no EU establishment, raising questions of competence. The processing occurred on Dutch territory via availability of services to Dutch controllers.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA determined competence under Article 3(2)(b) GDPR for monitoring processing offered in the Netherlands. On 21 September 2022, it fined Clearview AI €30.5 million for unlawful processing and transfers without adequate safeguards. Similar fines were issued by other EU DPAs, demonstrating coordinated competence assertions.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on TikTok Children's Data (Ireland Lead)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of children under 13 in the EU, including location data and profiling for advertising. Default privacy settings allowed processing without age verification or parental consent. The Irish DPC initiated investigation as lead authority due to TikTok's EU establishment in Ireland. Processing occurred across multiple Member States, triggering the one-stop-shop mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC's draft decision was contested by other authorities. The EDPB issued a binding decision on 17 April 2023 under Article 65, finding infringements and ordering TikTok to pay €345 million fine for children's data violations and implement corrective measures. The DPC adopted the decision on 5 September 2023.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Fine on WhatsApp Transfers
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland processed and transferred user data to the US for Meta group entities. Following Schrems II, the Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) received complaints from AEPD and individuals about transfer safeguards. AEPD assessed its competence for Spanish residents' data, despite Ireland as lead authority.
process_and_outcome: On 6 September 2024, AEPD imposed a €225 million fine on WhatsApp for insufficient transfer measures, exercising powers under Article 66 for urgent preliminary action affecting Spanish data subjects. This complemented the Irish proceedings.

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Decision on Amazon Transfers (Meta Case Analogy)
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., established in Luxembourg, handled data transfers to the US for its services. The Luxembourg CNPD investigated compliance with transfer requirements post-Schrems II, as Luxembourg was the lead authority for Amazon's EU operations.
process_and_outcome: CNPD found issues with transfer tools and issued a draft decision. Under cooperation procedures, it coordinated with other authorities. In related Meta Luxembourg case, CNPD handled intra-group transfers.

example_name: EDPB Case C-132/21 on Competence for Non-Establishment Processing
example_background: A preliminary reference from a German court questioned the competence of a Member State's supervisory authority over a non-EU controller offering services to data subjects in that state without an establishment. The case involved Meta Platforms Inc. processing German residents' data.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled on 4 May 2023 that the supervisory authority of the Member State where data subjects reside has full competence under Article 55(1)(a) GDPR, independent of lead authority rules for establishments.

example_name: Italian Garante Fine on Facebook Ireland Transfers
example_background: Facebook Ireland transferred Italian users' data to the US. The Italian Garante investigated post-Schrems II, asserting competence for local data subjects despite Irish lead status.
process_and_outcome: Garante fined Facebook Ireland €10 million in 2021 for transfer violations, using urgent powers, later challenged but upheld in demonstrating layered competence.

example_name: One-Stop-Shop Application in Apple Irish DPC Probe
example_background: Apple's EU subsidiary in Ireland faced investigations into App Store data processing and transfers. The Irish DPC acted as lead authority for cross-border processing.
process_and_outcome: DPC coordinated with other EU authorities under Article 56, issuing decisions on tracking and transfers without disputes requiring EDPB intervention.

example_name: Greek DPA Competence Assertion against Facebook (Pre-OSS)
example_background: Prior to full GDPR implementation, the Greek DPA handled Facebook processing affecting Greek users, later transitioning to Irish lead under OSS.
process_and_outcome: Greek DPA fined €1.2 million in 2018, illustrating territorial competence before establishment-based rules solidified.

example_name: OneStop Medical Case
example_background: OneStop Medical provided online medical consultations and issued digital sick notes to customers across multiple EU countries including Ireland, the UK, Germany, and others. The company collected personal data such as names, email addresses, phone numbers, dates of birth, addresses, and health information from users in various member states. Processing activities involved automated decision-making for issuing fit notes without human medical review in many instances. The company was established in Ireland and targeted users throughout the EU via its online platform.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted an own-volition inquiry in 2020, determining OneStop Medical as the sole establishment in the EU, thus acting as lead supervisory authority. The DPC imposed a fine of €300,000 in April 2022 for infringements related to data processing transparency and automated decisions. The company appealed aspects of the decision.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland (Facebook) GDPR Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, with its main EU establishment in Ireland, engaged in cross-border processing of personal data for Facebook services used by approximately 530 million monthly active users in the EU/EEA. Data processing included behavioral advertising based on user interactions across member states. Multiple supervisory authorities received complaints about violations of GDPR principles such as lawfulness and transparency in data handling.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the Irish DPC issued a draft decision as lead authority, which was disputed by other EU authorities. Following the Article 65 dispute resolution procedure by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), the DPC issued a final decision in 2023 fining Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers to the US and ordering cessation of transfers.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Google LLC Android Case
example_background: Google LLC, with operations involving Android devices across the EU, processed personal data for advertising purposes by scanning installed apps on approximately 2.5 billion devices belonging to users in multiple EU countries. The main establishment for these processing activities was determined to be in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC served a draft decision as lead authority, which was subject to objections from the French SA (CNIL) and others. The EDPB adopted a binding decision under Article 65 in 2022, leading to a €50 million fine by the French CNIL upheld as consistent with the lead authority process, though primarily handled via cooperation.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Case
example_background: British Airways, a UK-based airline with operations involving passenger data processing across the EU, experienced a data breach in 2018 where personal and payment data of approximately 400,000 customers was accessed by unauthorized parties over several weeks. The breach affected customers from multiple EU countries.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), as the supervisory authority for the controller's main establishment post-Brexit but handled under GDPR cooperation, investigated and in October 2020 fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £183 million following appeal). Coordination occurred with other EU authorities.

example_name: Twitter International Unlimited Company Fine
example_background: Twitter International Unlimited Company, with its EU main establishment in Ireland, processed personal data of EU users for services including content moderation and analytics, affecting users across multiple member states. Issues arose with compliance in data access rights fulfillment.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC, as lead authority, concluded a cross-border investigation in December 2022 with a €450,000 fine for delays in responding to data subject access requests, following cooperation with concerned authorities.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Children's Data Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited processed personal data of children on Instagram across the EU, involving over 23 million child users under 13. Processing occurred through age estimation tools and handling of data from underage accounts in multiple member states.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC as lead authority investigated with input from 31 supervisory authorities. In September 2024, it issued fines totaling €382 million for failures in child data protections, including default private accounts.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica ICO Enforcement
example_background: In 2018, revelations emerged that data from up to 87 million Facebook users worldwide, including 414,477 in the UK, was harvested via the Cambridge Analytica app without adequate consent. The data processing involved UK and EU users substantially, with activities centered around political advertising.
process_and_outcome: The ICO conducted an investigation and fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2019 for failures in data protection practices, including processing data without transparency. Facebook appealed but later withdrew, accepting the penalty.

example_name: Profi Credit Slovakia Fine
example_background: Profi Credit Slovensko, a non-bank consumer credit provider, processed personal data for debt collection purposes. The processing involved contacting debtors and third parties in ways that affected data subjects primarily in Slovakia.
process_and_outcome: The Slovak Data Protection Authority (Úrad na ochranu osobných údajov) investigated complaints and in 2020 fined the company €200,000 for unlawful processing and inadequate security measures.

example_name: H&M Germany Data Processing Fine
example_background: H&M operated a service center in Nuremberg, Germany, where approximately 1,000 employees' personal data was collected and stored in shared drives. Managers accessed colleagues' sensitive information, including health data and private issues, substantially affecting German employees.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg's data protection authority investigated following employee complaints. In October 2020, a €35.25 million fine was issued for violations including lack of legal basis for processing. H&M implemented remediation steps.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Irish DPC WhatsApp Investigation
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland processed personal data for behavioral advertising by sharing user data across its services. The processing affected users across the EU but was handled by the Irish DPC as WhatsApp's lead authority, with substantial effects noted in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated starting in 2020. In September 2021, a €225 million fine was imposed for transparency failures. WhatsApp appealed to the courts.

example_name: CNIL Google Analytics Prohibition
example_background: Google LLC and Google Ireland used Google Analytics on websites, transferring personal data from France to the US without adequate safeguards. The processing involved French users' data substantially through cookie-based tracking.
process_and_outcome: France's CNIL ordered Google to stop processing data via Analytics in June 2022 after an investigation prompted by complaints. Subsequent fines were issued to non-compliant websites, reinforcing the decision.

example_name: OneStopPay GDPR Fine by Dutch DPA
example_background: OneStopPay, a Dutch payment service provider, processed personal data of over 2.5 million consumers across multiple EU countries through its platform used by various webshops. The company maintained its main establishment in the Netherlands but engaged in cross-border data processing without properly coordinating with other EU supervisory authorities. Following complaints from consumers in Germany regarding unauthorized charges and data handling issues, the Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens, AP) was designated as the lead supervisory authority due to the location of the main establishment. However, the process revealed discrepancies in how local complaints were escalated and handled, involving multiple national authorities. The case involved large-scale processing of payment data, including names, addresses, IP addresses, and transaction details, across borders, which triggered the one-stop-shop mechanism under GDPR. The company had not fully established clear protocols for informing and involving concerned supervisory authorities promptly, leading to delays in resolution.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch AP conducted an investigation starting in 2018, coordinated with the German DPA (BayLDA), and in 2022 imposed a fine of 1.25 million euros on OneStopPay for violations related to cross-border processing competence and cooperation failures. The decision emphasized the need for proper lead authority designation and timely information sharing. OneStopPay implemented corrective measures including updated compliance procedures.
involved_parties: Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP, Dutch DPA, lead authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 56 (Competence of lead supervisory authority)

example_name: H&M Data Retention Fine by Hamburg DPA
example_background: H&M, the Swedish fashion retailer, operated data centers in Germany where employee data from multiple EU countries was processed. The Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information identified issues with excessive retention of sensitive employee data, such as performance notes and private conversations, collected at various international locations but stored centrally in Germany. Although H&M's main establishment was in Sweden, making the Swedish DPA (IMY) the presumptive lead authority for cross-border processing, the Hamburg DPA handled the case locally because the processing occurred exclusively at the German establishment and primarily affected German data subjects. This triggered the derogation under GDPR Article 56(2), allowing the local authority to proceed independently after informing the lead.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, after investigation, Hamburg DPA imposed a 35.25 million euro fine. The Swedish IMY was informed within the required timeframe and decided not to take over, confirming Hamburg's competence. H&M was ordered to delete unlawfully retained data and enhance retention policies.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Lead Authority Designation
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the main establishment for Meta's EU operations including Facebook and Instagram, was designated as the lead supervisory authority for cross-border processing affecting hundreds of millions of EU users. Multiple complaints arose across EU member states concerning data transfers to the US, tracking technologies, and consent mechanisms. Local DPAs from countries like Ireland, France (CNIL), and the UK (ICO, pre-Brexit) lodged objections and initiated procedures under the GDPR cooperation framework. The Irish DPC acted as the sole interlocutor for Meta, handling draft decisions and coordinating with other authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB).
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a final decision in 2023 after EDPB binding instructions, resulting in a 1.2 billion euro fine for unlawful data transfers. The process exemplified the one-stop-shop mechanism with the lead authority managing cross-border cases despite multi-state impacts.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 60 (Mutual assistance and joint operations)

example_name: Google Android Fine EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: Google Ireland Ltd., lead establishment in Ireland, processed Android users' location, search, and advertising data across the EU. French CNIL initiated proceedings over lack of consent for data combinations affecting French users but with cross-border scope. Disagreement arose on the Irish DPC's draft decision, leading to EDPB involvement. The case highlighted tensions in lead authority competence where local authorities pushed for stricter enforcement against the lead's more lenient approach.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, EDPB issued a binding decision instructing Irish DPC to impose a 50 million euro fine (upheld and increased). This enforced the cooperation procedure under Article 60 and lead authority's role.

example_name: Amazon EU Lead Authority Coordination Failure
example_background: Amazon Services Europe S.a.r.l., based in Luxembourg, served as the lead establishment for EU operations involving vast consumer data processing for e-commerce, recommendations, and advertising. Luxembourg DPA (CNPD) was the lead, but complaints in France, Germany, and Italy about targeting ads led to parallel investigations. Local DPAs criticized slow coordination, with Luxembourg not adequately accounting for draft decisions from concerned authorities.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, CNPD fined Amazon 746 million euros primarily for transparency violations, after consultations. EDPB later intervened in related ad tech cases, reinforcing lead authority procedures.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Lead Authority Case
example_background: British Airways, part of International Consolidated Airlines Group with complex EU establishments, experienced a Magecart attack in 2018 exposing 400,000 customers' payment data. The UK ICO (pre-Brexit) acted as lead authority due to the main establishment, coordinating with other EU DPAs as the breach affected cross-border passengers. The case tested the one-stop-shop for breach notifications and remediation across member states.
process_and_outcome: ICO investigated and in 2020 fined 20 million pounds (later reduced on appeal). Coordination with EU authorities proceeded smoothly via the lead mechanism.

example_name: CNIL vs WhatsApp French Derogation Attempt
example_background: WhatsApp LLC, with main EU establishment in Ireland, updated terms in 2021 linking data to Facebook for business purposes, prompting widespread EU complaints. French CNIL considered the changes substantially affected French users due to localized impacts and sought to handle independently under Article 56(2), informing Irish DPC which disagreed, leading to EDPB arbitration.
process_and_outcome: EDPB in 2023 upheld Irish DPC as lead, fining WhatsApp 225 million euros after binding instructions, clarifying derogation limits for truly local matters.

example_name: Clearview AI Multi-DPA Cooperation
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition firm processing EU biometric data scraped from public sources, lacked an EU establishment, complicating lead authority determination. Multiple DPAs (French CNIL, UK ICO, Italian Garante) initiated actions, eventually coordinating via EDPB without a single lead, but applying consistency mechanisms for cross-border enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Various fines imposed: CNIL 20 million euros (2022), others followed. Demonstrated handling absent clear lead via joint operations.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Cross-Border Enforcement (Ireland-Netherlands)
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, processed personal data of European users through its platform headquartered in Dublin, Ireland, under the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) as the lead supervisory authority. Multiple concerns arose regarding data processing practices, including child privacy protections and data transfers. In late 2023, the Dutch supervisory authority (AP) identified issues with TikTok's handling of children's data in the Netherlands and notified the Irish DPC promptly. The Irish DPC, as lead, was required to assess whether to handle the case centrally considering TikTok's main EU establishment in Ireland. This notification occurred within the framework of GDPR cooperation mechanisms amid ongoing scrutiny of TikTok's compliance across member states.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch AP informed the Irish DPC without delay. The Irish DPC decided to handle aspects of the case under Article 60 procedures. TikTok faced investigations leading to a €345 million fine in September 2023 from the Irish DPC for child data violations, with additional cross-border coordination resulting in commitments for platform-wide changes.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms EDPB Binding Decision (Multiple Member States)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, with its main EU establishment in Ireland, faced complaints in several member states including Belgium, Germany, Ireland, and others regarding its behavioral advertising practices under GDPR. Local supervisory authorities, such as the Belgian DPA, notified the Irish DPC of cases falling under its lead competence due to Meta's establishment. Notifications were made promptly, and the Irish DPC was tasked with deciding within three weeks whether to handle the cases centrally, considering Meta's Irish base. This process highlighted tensions in one-stop-shop mechanism application.
process_and_outcome: After notifications, the Irish DPC decided not to handle some cases centrally, prompting concerned authorities to refer the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). In April 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision under Article 65, finding Meta's practices non-compliant and requiring uniform enforcement. Meta received fines totaling over €1.2 billion from the Irish DPC in related actions.
involved_parties: Belgian DPA, German BfDI (notifying supervisory authorities)

example_name: Clearview AI Cross-Border Disputes (Multiple Authorities)
example_background: Clearview AI, a facial recognition company without a main EU establishment, scraped billions of images from the internet for its database, accessible to European law enforcement and private clients. Supervisory authorities in France (CNIL), Greece, Italy, and others initiated enforcement, notifying potential lead authorities or coordinating under GDPR's decentralized competence rules. The French CNIL notified other authorities, including those in states where processors operated, leading to discussions on lead authority designation.
process_and_outcome: Multiple authorities issued fines independently (e.g., CNIL €20 million, Italy €20 million) due to lack of EU establishment. The EDPB coordinated via Article 61, but no single lead was designated, resulting in parallel proceedings. Clearview was ordered to cease operations in the EU in several jurisdictions.
involved_parties: Italian Garante, Greek DPA (other supervisory authorities)

example_name: Google Analytics Cross-Border Investigation (Austria-Other States)
example_background: Google's analytics tools were used by websites across the EU, raising data transfer concerns to the US post-Schrems II. The Austrian DPA initiated scrutiny on a local health institute's use, notifying other member states' authorities, including Ireland (Google's lead) and Luxembourg. Notifications were sent without delay, with the lead authority required to decide on handling within three weeks, factoring in Google's Irish establishment.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC was informed and decided to handle the controller aspects centrally. Several authorities (e.g., France, Italy) issued bans or fines on websites using the tool. Coordinated outcomes led to Google's suspension of analytics services in the EU until new safeguards.

example_name: Amazon EU Lead Authority Coordination (Luxembourg-Lead)
example_background: Amazon Services Europe S.à r.l., established in Luxembourg, served as controller for EU operations. The French CNIL investigated Amazon France Logistique for data processing practices, notifying the Luxembourg CNPD as lead authority without delay. The notification detailed the case scope, and Luxembourg had three weeks to decide on central handling, given Amazon's main establishment there.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CNPD took the case under Article 60. In 2023, it proposed no infringement, but CNIL and others objected, leading to EDPB referral. Pending EDPB decision, Amazon faced ongoing audits and implemented data processing enhancements.

example_name: WhatsApp Privacy Policy Case (Ireland-Multiple States)
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, under Meta, updated its privacy policy in 2021, prompting complaints in Germany, Italy, and other states about transparency and data sharing with Meta. Local authorities like the Hamburg DPA notified the Irish DPC promptly as lead, providing case details for the three-week decision period on handling, based on WhatsApp's Irish establishment.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC handled the case centrally, fining WhatsApp €225 million in 2021 for transparency failures. Cross-border cooperation ensured consistent application across notified states.
involved_parties: Hamburg DPA, Italian Garante (notifying supervisory authorities)

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Coordination (UK Post-Brexit Analogue)
example_background: Although post-Brexit, the British Airways breach in 2018 involved 400,000 customers' data. Pre-Brexit, the UK's ICO notified the Irish DPC as potential lead for BA's parent IAG establishment considerations. This illustrated pre-GDPR full effect cooperation similar to Article 60 procedures.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined BA £20 million after coordination. Irish DPC assessed no lead role. BA enhanced security measures.

example_name: EDPB Case on Meta Transfers (Austria Lead Coordination)
example_background: Austrian NOYB filed complaints against Meta in 11 member states over US data transfers post-Schrems II. The Austrian DSB as lead for the local case notified other authorities, including Ireland, which had to decide within three weeks on handling Meta's broader practices given its establishment.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC handled Meta's transfer practices, suspending them in 2023. EDPB binding decision upheld Schrems III challenges. Fines exceeded €1 billion.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Services Investigation (Ireland Lead)
example_background: Concerns over Microsoft Ireland's cloud services data processing for EU clients led French CNIL to notify the Irish DPC as lead authority without delay. The notification covered processing activities, with Ireland deciding within three weeks whether to centralize under Article 60, considering Microsoft's Dublin establishment.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC conducted the inquiry, leading to corrective measures and commitments from Microsoft on data localization and transfers in 2023.

example_name: Italian DPA WhatsApp Fine
example_background: WhatsApp shared user data with Meta companies for advertising purposes without transparent consent. Users were not adequately informed about data combination across services, affecting millions of Italian users who believed they had opted out.
process_and_outcome: The Italian DPA (Garante) fined WhatsApp €10 million in 2021 after investigating complaints, with injunctions to revise transparency notices. Further fines followed for non-compliance.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italian DPA)

example_name: British Airways GDPR Enforcement Case
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach where personal data of approximately 400,000 customers and staff members was accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's payment processing system. The breach involved Magecart attackers injecting malicious code that skimmed credit card details and other personal information over several weeks. The incident was reported to the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) in the UK, which began preliminary investigations. Due to the centralized nature of the company's data processing across the EU, the Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens, AP) was designated as the lead supervisory authority under GDPR Article 56 because the main establishment for data processing was located in the Netherlands. The UK ICO had been the initial supervisory authority to notify due to the breach occurring in the UK.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch AP handled the case as lead authority and initiated a cross-border procedure under Articles 61 and 62. After investigation, a draft decision was shared with concerned supervisory authorities, including the ICO. In October 2020, the AP imposed a fine of 22 million euros on British Airways. The ICO, as the initial notifying authority, had cooperated but deferred to the lead authority's handling.
involved_parties: Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, notifying supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Articles 61-62 (mutual assistance and joint operations)

example_name: Facebook (Meta) EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited faced scrutiny over its processing of personal data from European users for behavioral advertising, following complaints lodged in Belgium about tracking via the 'like' button even from non-users. The Belgian Data Protection Authority (DPA) initiated the case in 2015, pre-GDPR but transitioned under GDPR. Due to Facebook's main EU establishment in Ireland, the Irish DPC was lead supervisory authority. Five concerned authorities, including Belgium, objected to the DPC's draft decision to dismiss the case.
process_and_outcome: Under Article 65 (not directly 61-62 but related cooperation), the EDPB issued a binding decision in December 2020, upheld by the CJEU in 2023. The Belgian DPA, as initial authority, cooperated via mutual assistance (Articles 61-62). The process exemplified deferral to lead but with objection mechanisms leading to joint resolution. Facebook was fined separately but this highlighted cooperation.

example_name: WhatsApp Processing Transparency Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland was subject to complaints in Germany about lack of transparency in data processing for service improvement and targeted advertising. The Bavarian BayLDA initiated the investigation in 2019 as local authority for a German subsidiary, but WhatsApp's main establishment was Ireland, making DPC the lead.
process_and_outcome: BayLDA informed the Irish DPC under Article 61 mutual assistance. The DPC, as lead, handled the cross-border case. In 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp 225 million euros. BayLDA deferred after cooperation, aligning with the rule that the notifying authority handles only if lead declines.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection (BayLDA, notifying authority)

example_name: Amazon EU Data Use for Advertising
example_background: Amazon Europe Core was investigated by Luxembourg CNPD (lead authority due to establishment there) following a 2018 complaint by the French NOYB group about using data from purchases, searches, and views for targeted ads without proper basis. French CNIL had coordinated initial aspects.
process_and_outcome: CNPD handled as lead under one-stop-shop, with mutual assistance from French CNIL under Articles 61-62. In 2021, CNPD fined Amazon 746 million euros, the largest GDPR fine at the time. Concerned authorities like France deferred to lead's decision after consultations.

example_name: Meta Platforms Cookie Banner Case
example_background: NOYB filed 99 complaints against Meta (Facebook) in Austria for misleading cookie banners that nudged users to accept all cookies. The Austrian DPA was initial authority but, as Meta's lead is Irish DPC, it informed under Article 61.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC, as lead, handled the cross-border cases after notification from Austrian DPA. In 2022-2023, DPC fined Meta Platforms Ireland 1.2 billion euros for data transfers (related proceedings), with cookie issues integrated. Austrian DPA stepped back per the rule.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR One-Stop-Shop Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead supervisory authority representative for Facebook's European operations, managed cross-border data processing for over 500 million EU users. Following complaints about data practices, multiple national data protection authorities initiated investigations into processing activities that occurred across EU member states. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) was designated as the lead supervisory authority under the GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism due to Meta's establishment in Ireland. Other concerned supervisory authorities from countries such as Luxembourg, Netherlands, and France submitted their views on the case, leading to a coordinated process where the lead authority handled communications and decision-making for the controller.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a decision in 2023 fining Meta €1.2 billion for violations related to international data transfers, following input from concerned supervisory authorities through the dispute resolution mechanism. Meta appealed the decision, and compliance remediation steps were required, including changes to data transfer practices.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: WhatsApp Irish DPC Processing Fine
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited serves as the main establishment for the messaging service's EU-wide operations, handling cross-border data processing for hundreds of millions of users. In 2021, the Irish DPC led an investigation into WhatsApp's transparency regarding data sharing with Meta companies, following complaints from privacy groups in Germany and other states. As the sole interlocutor, the DPC managed the inquiry despite interventions from concerned authorities in Austria, France, Greece, Italy, Ireland, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, and Spain.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2021 for incomplete privacy policy information. An appeal was filed, and WhatsApp updated its practices. The European Data Protection Board issued a binding decision to ensure consistency.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Google Analytics Cookie Consent Case (Italy)
example_background: Google Ireland Limited processes analytics data across EU websites using Google Analytics tools. In 2022, the Italian Garante identified issues with cookie consent banners on Italian sites but engaged Google Ireland as the lead controller under one-stop-shop, rather than pursuing purely national actions, highlighting coordination needs.
process_and_outcome: The Italian authority ordered blocking of Google Analytics processing temporarily in 2022, prompting EU-wide discussions. Google updated tools, and the case underscored lead authority roles.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud EU Investigation
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited manages Azure and Office 365 data processing for EU customers across borders. The Irish DPC led a 2020-2023 inquiry into data retention practices in Microsoft 365, involving public sector and enterprise data from multiple EU states, acting as sole point of contact.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, the DPC issued a €20 million reprimand (no fine) and ordered compliance adjustments. Microsoft committed to product changes.

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Breach 2018-2020
example_background: Marriott International, operating the Starwood Hotels chain, suffered a data breach affecting up to 383 million guest records worldwide, including passport numbers, payment card details, and travel information. The breach originated from a Starwood system compromised as early as 2014 but only detected by Marriott in September 2018 after its acquisition of Starwood in 2016. The incident spanned multiple jurisdictions due to Marriott's global operations, with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) acting as lead supervisory authority given Marriott's main EU establishment in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted the investigation under Article 60 procedures, consulting other concerned authorities. In November 2020, the DPC imposed a fine of €20 million on Marriott. Other authorities submitted draft decisions, which the DPC took into account. Marriott appealed aspects but ultimately settled related litigation.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority
Other EU supervisory authorities via EDPB

example_name: Facebook (Meta) Cambridge Analytica GDPR Enforcement
example_background: Facebook faced scrutiny over the 2014-2015 Cambridge Analytica scandal where data of up to 87 million users, mostly from the US but including millions of EU residents, was improperly harvested via a third-party app. In the EU context, this led to investigations into data processing practices, consent mechanisms, and transfers. The Irish DPC was the lead supervisory authority for Facebook's EU operations based in Ireland, handling complaints from privacy advocates.
process_and_outcome: Following a multi-year investigation under Article 60, the Irish DPC issued draft decisions which were subject to disagreement among supervisory authorities. The EDPB intervened with a binding decision in April 2023, resulting in fines totaling €1.2 billion for data transfers to the US, plus additional penalties for violations of transparency and consent rules. The lead authority finalized the decision incorporating other authorities' inputs.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority

example_name: WhatsApp GDPR Processing Transparency Case
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Facebook (Meta), updated its terms in 2021 to share user data with other Meta companies for commercial purposes, prompting complaints across Europe about lack of transparency in data processing. As WhatsApp's main EU establishment is in Ireland, the Irish DPC served as lead supervisory authority, receiving drafts from other authorities like the Hamburg DPA which initiated complaints.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC investigated and prepared a draft decision under Article 60(3), taking utmost account of submissions from other authorities. The EDPB issued a binding decision in September 2022 due to disagreements, leading to a €225 million fine in September 2023 for transparency violations. WhatsApp accepted the penalty.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority
Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Data Case
example_background: TikTok faced investigations over its handling of children's data, including age verification, default privacy settings, and profiling of users under 16 without parental consent. The platform operates globally, with its EU entity in Ireland, making the Irish DPC the lead supervisory authority. Complaints originated from multiple EU countries regarding systemic issues discovered in 2020 audits.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted a cross-border investigation under Article 60, sharing drafts with concerned authorities. In September 2023, it imposed a €345 million fine for violations related to children's data processing. Other authorities' inputs were incorporated into the final decision, which TikTok did not contest.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority

example_name: Google GDPR Ad Personalization Fine
example_background: Google's practices for personalized advertising based on user data across Android devices and services came under scrutiny starting from 2018 complaints by privacy group None of Your Business (NOYB). As Google's lead establishment in the EU is in Ireland, the Irish DPC handled the cross-border case, involving data processing consent and transparency for millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a draft decision in 2021, which was challenged by other authorities like CNIL (France). The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2022 under Article 65, resulting in a €50 million fine upheld by the lead authority, with Google appealing to courts.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 60 (draft decision taking account of submissions)

example_name: Amazon GDPR Data Processing Case
example_background: Amazon's use of personal data from its marketplace sellers for targeted advertising on its platform raised concerns about transparency and lawful basis for processing. NOYB filed complaints in 13 EU countries in 2018. Amazon's EU lead establishment in Luxembourg made the Luxembourg DPA (CNPD) the lead supervisory authority.
process_and_outcome: The CNPD conducted the investigation under Article 60, considering draft inputs from other authorities. In 2021, it imposed a €746 million fine for violations of GDPR principles, marking one of the largest penalties. Amazon appealed the decision to Luxembourg courts.
involved_parties: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. (data controller)
Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) Luxembourg - lead supervisory authority

example_name: Meta GDPR Data Transfers to US (Schrems II Follow-up)
example_background: Following the Schrems II court ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, Meta's ongoing transfers of EU user data to the US for processing were investigated. The Irish DPC, as lead for Meta's EU operations, examined compliance with new Standard Contractual Clauses amid concerns from other EU authorities.
process_and_outcome: Under Article 60, the DPC prepared drafts taking account of other authorities' submissions. Disagreements led to EDPB binding decisions, culminating in a €1.2 billion fine in 2023 for unlawful transfers. Meta is challenging this in courts.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority

example_name: Instagram GDPR Child Data Processing
example_background: Instagram's practices allowing accounts for children under 13 and exposing their data publicly led to complaints. As part of Meta, with lead authority in Ireland, the DPC investigated cross-border processing issues related to age assurance and default settings.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a €405 million fine in September 2022 after Article 60 procedures, incorporating inputs from nine other authorities' draft decisions on child data handling.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) - lead supervisory authority

example_name: CNIL Cookie Consent Enforcement
example_background: The French CNIL conducted sweeps on major websites, finding widespread non-compliance with cookie consent rules. Many sites used cookie walls forcing acceptance for access and did not provide granular refusal options, processing data without valid consent. This followed CNIL's formal notice to 100+ sites in prior years.
process_and_outcome: In 2020-2021, CNIL issued fines totaling millions, including €150 million against Google and €35 million against Facebook for cookie practices. Sites updated banners to allow easy refusal. CNIL published compliance guidelines.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Transatlantic Transfers Fine
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated Meta's use of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for transferring Facebook user data from the EU to the US. Post-Schrems II ruling in 2020, which invalidated Privacy Shield, Meta continued transfers without sufficient supplementary measures to protect data from US surveillance laws. The inquiry covered transfers from May 2020 onwards.
process_and_outcome: In May 2023, the DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion and ordered suspension of US data transfers. Meta appealed to the courts.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers of personal data to third countries)

example_name: ICO Complaint Handling Report 2023
example_background: The UK's ICO published its annual report on complaint handling under GDPR. The ICO received over 190,000 complaints in 2022-2023, processing them through online forms, phone, email, and post. The report detailed how the ICO facilitates accessible submission channels, including a prominent online complaint form guiding users through data provided and expected outcomes. Caseworkers triage complaints within set timeframes, referring complex cases to investigations.
process_and_outcome: The ICO closed 82% of complaints within 6 months, upholding 19% where organizations mishandled data subject rights. The process demonstrated structured facilitation of complaints.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 77 (Right to lodge a complaint)

example_name: CNIL Online Complaint Portal
example_background: France's CNIL maintains an online complaint submission platform accessible via its website, allowing individuals to file complaints electronically about data protection issues. The form collects details on the organization involved, nature of the issue, evidence, and contact information. CNIL also accepts complaints by mail, email, and phone. In 2023, CNIL received over 15,000 complaints, with the online form being the primary channel, ensuring ease of access without mandating specific formats.
process_and_outcome: CNIL processes complaints by acknowledging receipt within one month, conducting preliminary assessments, and escalating to formal procedures if warranted. This system supports efficient handling aligned with regulatory duties.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Dutch DPA Klachtenloket Complaint Portal
example_background: The Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP), Netherlands' DPA, operates the 'Klachtenloket' (complaints desk) as an online gateway for submitting GDPR complaints. Launched to simplify access, it features a step-by-step digital form available 24/7 in Dutch and English, covering data subject rights requests, breaches, and other issues. Users receive immediate confirmation and tracking numbers. The AP supplements this with phone support and postal options.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the AP handled over 20,000 complaints via the portal, resolving most through advice or mediation, with 10% leading to investigations and enforcement.

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine 2020
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg data center unlawfully processed employee personal data from 2014 to 2018, including sensitive details gathered from sick leave conversations and stored in shared drives accessible to managers. Over 1,000 employees were affected, with data used for performance monitoring without consent or notification, discovered during an employee data subject request.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision fined H&M €35.3 million in October 2020. The company enhanced employee data policies and training programs globally.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Deutsche Wohnen GDPR Investigation 2024
example_background: Deutsche Wohnen, a major German real estate company, was probed for charging tenants fees for data subject access requests related to rental contracts and personal information from 2018 to 2023. Tenants requested copies of data processed for tenancy management, but the company imposed administrative charges, delaying responses.
process_and_outcome: The Berlin Data Protection Authority investigated and issued a warning with a potential fine in early 2024, requiring the company to process requests free of charge and refund fees. No final penalty was imposed after compliance assurances.
involved_parties: Berlin Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: ICO Decision on Manifestly Unfounded SAR - Mr. X v Organisation Y (2020)
example_background: An individual submitted multiple subject access requests to an organisation over a short period, seeking personal data held in various formats including emails and documents. The requests followed previous complaints about data handling and included demands for data from specific time periods and departments. The organisation initially responded to some requests but later declined further action, classifying them as manifestly unfounded due to the repetitive nature and lack of genuine purpose beyond ongoing disputes. The individual complained to the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), arguing that the organisation failed to provide evidence supporting their classification and did not charge a fee as an alternative. The case highlighted tensions between data subject rights and administrative burdens on controllers under GDPR provisions allowing supervisory authorities to refuse excessive requests.
process_and_outcome: The ICO investigated and upheld the organisation's decision, determining that the requests were manifestly unfounded based on evidence of repetition and ulterior motives linked to litigation. No fee was charged, but the refusal to act was deemed proportionate. The complainant was advised on appeal rights, and the organisation's procedures were noted as adequate.

example_name: Irish DPC Case on Repetitive DSARs - Company A (2021)
example_background: A data subject directed a series of data subject access requests to an Irish company over several months, each requesting copies of all personal data processed, including unstructured data from IT systems, emails, and third-party communications. The requests were submitted via different channels and often reformulated slightly, despite prior comprehensive responses providing over 10,000 pages of data. The company responded to initial requests but subsequently informed the subject that further identical requests would incur a fee based on administrative costs or be refused due to their excessive and repetitive character. The subject escalated to the Data Protection Commission (DPC), contending that no fee schedule was provided and that the company bore no burden to demonstrate excessiveness.
process_and_outcome: The DPC reviewed correspondence and response logs, concluding the requests were excessive. The company was instructed to formalise its fee structure but the refusal was validated, with the DPC bearing the demonstration burden through analysis of request patterns. No fine was issued, and the subject received guidance on reasonable request limits.

example_name: CNIL Deliberation on Excessive Access Requests (SAN-2020-006, 2020)
example_background: An individual lodged repeated demands for access to personal data with a French company, including full database extracts and real-time monitoring data, after a commercial dispute. Over six months, 15 similar requests were made, each requiring extensive searches across CRM systems, call logs, and employee emails. The company provided initial responses but then charged a reasonable administrative fee for subsequent ones, citing the repetitive nature and high resource demands. When the subject refused payment and complained to the CNIL, the authority examined whether the company adequately demonstrated the excessive character through logs and proportionality assessments.
process_and_outcome: CNIL confirmed the requests as excessive, approved the fee application, and dismissed the complaint. The decision reinforced that supervisory authorities must verify evidence like request frequency and scope before intervening, with no further action required from the company.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés, supervisory authority)

example_name: ICO Monetary Penalty on British Airways - Improper DSAR Handling (2020)
example_background: While primarily a security breach case, British Airways faced scrutiny for its overall data rights handling, including delays in responding to post-breach subject access requests from affected customers. Numerous individuals submitted DSARs seeking details on compromised data, but responses were delayed beyond one month, with some repetitive requests from the same subjects not addressed proportionately. The ICO examined whether these could have been classified as excessive amid high volumes, but found systemic failures in triage processes. The company did not invoke fees or refusals, leading to overload.
process_and_outcome: ICO issued a £20 million penalty notice, reduced from £22 million, primarily for the breach but noting deficiencies in DSAR management that exacerbated issues. No specific excessive request ruling, but highlighted need for robust processes to identify and handle repetitive requests appropriately.

example_name: Swedish IMY Decision on Repetitive Requests (DI-2021-123, 2021)
example_background: A data subject sent 22 nearly identical access requests to a public authority over 18 months, each demanding all personal data in electronic and paper formats, despite receiving full disclosures multiple times. The authority initially complied but later refused action on additional requests, stating they were manifestly excessive and providing evidence via request logs and prior response summaries. The subject challenged this to the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY), claiming lack of demonstration of unfoundedness.
process_and_outcome: IMY upheld the refusal after reviewing timelines and content similarities, confirming the authority met the burden of proof. The decision was published as guidance, with no sanctions as procedures were followed correctly.

example_name: DPA Decision on TikTok DSAR Failures (2023)
example_background: TikTok received thousands of DSARs from users, including repetitive ones post-privacy concerns, requesting video data, metadata, and algorithmic profiles. The company struggled with volume, delaying responses and not systematically identifying or charging for excessive repetitive requests from the same users. An Irish DPC inquiry revealed inadequate processes to assess manifestly unfounded requests, leading to inconsistent handling and complaints.
process_and_outcome: DPC imposed a €345 million fine primarily for child data processing but included findings on DSAR shortcomings. TikTok was ordered to improve request management, including mechanisms for fees or refusals on excessive cases.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 01/2022 Implementation - Company Z Case (2022)
example_background: Following EDPB guidelines on DSARs, a German company faced repeated access requests from a former employee seeking email archives and HR files across multiple departments. The requests spanned a year, with each iteration expanding scope slightly. The company charged an administrative fee after the third request, supported by time logs and prior disclosures, and refused further without payment. The former employee complained to the Bavarian DPA, prompting review of compliance with the burden of demonstration.
process_and_outcome: The DPA validated the approach, aligning with EDPB guidance, and closed the case without enforcement. The decision was cited in supervisory communications on handling repetitive requests.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)

example_name: Meta Platforms SAR Overload Fine by ICO (2023)
example_background: Meta faced a surge in subject access requests after Cambridge Analytica, with many users submitting repetitive or broad requests for Facebook data exports. The company extended response times without invoking fees or refusals for clearly excessive cases, citing technical challenges. The ICO investigated complaints about non-responses to repeated requests from the same individuals.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined Meta £27.5 million for separate issues but in parallel proceedings criticised DSAR handling, requiring process enhancements for identifying and addressing manifestly excessive requests under Article 12(5).

example_name: Dutch AP Ruling on Excessive Journalism-Related Requests (2022)
example_background: A journalist submitted multiple DSARs to a municipality for personal data related to public interest reporting, repeating requests for updated datasets quarterly. The municipality provided initial responses but refused further ones as excessive, offering a fee-based service instead, backed by resource expenditure records. The journalist appealed to the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP).
process_and_outcome: AP confirmed the excessive nature despite public interest context, upholding the fee option and refusal mechanism, emphasising the supervisory burden to demonstrate via evidence.

example_name: CNIL Approval of Standard Contractual Clauses for Orange
example_background: Orange S.A., a major French telecommunications company, sought to transfer personal data of its customers outside the European Economic Area to group entities in non-adequate countries. The company submitted draft standard contractual clauses to the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL) for review as part of its data processing operations involving international subsidiaries. This process involved detailed documentation on data flows, security measures, and safeguards to ensure the level of protection of natural persons was not undermined. The submission was made in line with requirements for controllers handling large-scale personal data transfers in the telecom sector, where customer information such as contact details, usage data, and billing information is routinely shared across borders for operational purposes like customer support and network management.
process_and_outcome: CNIL reviewed the submitted clauses, issued a favorable opinion, and formally adopted them as standard data protection clauses on October 21, 2016, enabling Orange to proceed with the transfers while ensuring compliance with transfer requirements.

example_name: Irish DPC Authorization of BCRs for Accenture
example_background: Accenture plc, a global professional services company headquartered in Ireland, implemented Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) to govern the transfer of employee and client personal data across its worldwide network of over 500,000 employees in more than 120 countries. The BCRs covered categories such as HR data, financial information, and project-related personal data, specifying data protection principles, rights of data subjects, and accountability measures. The process was initiated prior to GDPR's full enforcement but aligned with its requirements, involving consultations with various EU supervisory authorities to establish a consistent framework for intra-group transfers.
process_and_outcome: The Data Protection Commission (DPC) authorized the BCRs on May 11, 2018, after review and approval, allowing Accenture to legally transfer data to non-EU entities under a unified set of rules.

example_name: CNIL Accreditation of AFNOR Certification Body
example_background: AFNOR Certification, a subsidiary of the French standardization organization Association Française de Normalisation, applied for accreditation to issue GDPR-compliant data protection certifications. This involved demonstrating technical competence, independence, and adherence to certification standards for schemes like Privacy by Design. The accreditation process supported controllers and processors in various sectors seeking third-party validation of their data protection practices, including compliance with principles like data minimization and accountability.
process_and_outcome: CNIL accredited AFNOR as a certification body on July 5, 2018, enabling it to conduct audits and issue certifications under GDPR frameworks.

example_name: EDPB Adoption of Standard Contractual Clauses
example_background: Following the Schrems II judgment by the Court of Justice of the EU in 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) coordinated the update of standard contractual clauses for international data transfers. Supervisory authorities across EU member states participated in this initiative to provide controllers and processors with updated tools ensuring appropriate safeguards for transfers to third countries, incorporating requirements for assessing third-country laws and supplementary measures like encryption.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued an executive summary and recommendations in June 2021, leading to the European Commission's adoption of new standard contractual clauses on June 4, 2021, which national authorities then applied and referenced.

example_name: Hamburg DPA Approval of BCRs for Siemens
example_background: Siemens AG, a German multinational conglomerate operating in energy, healthcare, and manufacturing sectors, developed Binding Corporate Rules to manage personal data transfers across its global operations involving millions of employee and customer records. The BCRs detailed governance structures, risk assessments, and data subject rights enforcement mechanisms, submitted for review to establish a group-wide compliance standard.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information approved the BCRs on December 20, 2012, with updates aligned to GDPR, authorizing their use for intra-group transfers.

example_name: CNIL Approval of BCRs for Capgemini
example_background: Capgemini, a French multinational IT services and consulting company, established Binding Corporate Rules for its international group entities to handle personal data of clients and employees transferred to countries without adequacy decisions. The rules covered data processing activities in areas like cloud services and IT outsourcing, including provisions for audits, training, and breach notification across borders.
process_and_outcome: CNIL approved the BCRs on September 26, 2017, following a mutual recognition procedure with other EU authorities, permitting ongoing group data transfers.

example_name: Italian Garante Accreditation of Bureau Veritas
example_background: Bureau Veritas Italia, part of the global testing and certification firm, sought accreditation to certify organizations against GDPR privacy management frameworks.
