This involved evaluation of its methodologies for assessing certification criteria related to data protection impact assessments and processor obligations.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) accredited Bureau Veritas on November 28, 2019, as a body for GDPR certifications.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (supervisory authority)

example_name: DPC Authorization of Transfer Clauses for Meta (Pre-Enforcement)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for EU data for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, submitted proposed contractual clauses for data transfers to the US under the lead of the Irish Data Protection Commission. This followed Schrems II and involved detailed assessments of US surveillance laws and supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: While leading to later enforcement, the DPC initially engaged in review processes and authorized certain clauses in 2020-2021, contributing to the development of new SCCs; subsequent investigations highlighted ongoing transfer issues.

example_name: AEPD Opinion on Codes of Conduct for Pharmacies
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) reviewed a draft code of conduct submitted by the Organization of Spanish Pharmacists for processing health data in pharmacy settings, including patient records and prescription information, to standardize practices across the sector.
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued a favorable opinion and approved the code on March 26, 2019, promoting sector-wide adherence to data protection standards.

example_name: Danish DPA Audit of Tax Agency
example_background: Denmark's tax authority (SKAT) experienced a data leak in 2018 affecting 1.1 million citizens and 440,000 businesses due to misconfigured access controls in its systems. Personal data including CVs and financial details were exposed. The Danish Data Protection Agency (Datatilsynet) initiated a proactive audit.
process_and_outcome: Datatilsynet carried out data protection audits, reviewing certifications and access logs, leading to administrative fines and orders for systemic remediation. SKAT overhauled its IT security architecture.

example_name: Italian Garante Audit of Google
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) examined Google's processing of personal data for advertising purposes, focusing on compliance with data retention and minimization principles across its services.
process_and_outcome: Garante conducted an on-site inspection and data audit in 2022, resulting in a €10 million fine for excessive data retention. Google was instructed to adjust retention periods and report back.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italian supervisory authority)

example_name: H&M Data Protection Audit Fine
example_background: H&M Germany faced an on-site inspection by the Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner regarding its employee monitoring practices. The company collected excessive personal data from workers' private conversations, health information, and life events through a 'welcome-back talk' process after absences, storing it in internal knowledge bases without adequate safeguards or legal basis.
process_and_outcome: The audit led to a fine of 35.25 million euros. H&M was reprimanded and ordered to delete unlawfully processed data and implement compliant processing operations. The case emphasized the use of investigative audits.

example_name: EDPB Schrems II Follow-up on Transfers
example_background: Following the CJEU Schrems II judgment invalidating Privacy Shield, the European Data Protection Board issued binding decisions coordinating supervisory authorities' responses to complaints against Facebook Ireland for unlawful data transfers to the US. The case involved review of transfer tools and supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision directing the lead authority to impose corrective measures, demonstrating use of advisory and authorization powers across authorities. This led to subsequent enforcement actions including suspensions.

example_name: DPC Inquiry into WhatsApp Transparency
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland updated its terms in 2021, notifying users of increased data sharing with Meta for advertising. The notifications were deemed unclear, bundling policy changes with service continuation threats, affecting hundreds of millions of EU users' understanding of data processing purposes.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC concluded an investigation in 2023 with a €225 million fine. WhatsApp was reprimanded and ordered to revise information notices to ensure clear, intelligible communication about data processing.

example_name: H&M Data Breach Fine
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary operated a 'welcome-back room' where returning employees were encouraged to share personal details about colleagues' private lives, such as vacation experiences and family issues. This data was stored in a shared drive accessible to hundreds of managers from 2014 to 2019, without consent, affecting employee privacy across European operations.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner fined H&M €35.25 million in October 2020 after a whistleblower complaint led to an investigation. The authority imposed the penalty through administrative enforcement proceedings.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: DPC v Meta Platforms (Annual Inquiries)
example_background: The Irish DPC conducted annual cross-border inquiries into Meta's processing of EU personal data for behavioral advertising on Facebook from 2018-2022. Issues included lack of transparency in data use for profiling, insufficient user control over data retention, and non-compliant legal bases for processing vast amounts of user interactions.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the DPC imposed fines totaling €390 million across inquiries, with decisions finalized after EDPB dispute resolution. This involved formal legal proceedings under GDPR cooperation.

example_name: Spain AEPD v WhatsApp Fine
example_background: WhatsApp shared user data with Meta for advertising purposes without clear consent options. From 2018, the app's policy changes forced users to accept broad data processing terms, processing phone numbers and metadata of 600 million EU users without granular control.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish AEPD fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2021, upheld by the courts. The authority initiated proceedings and referred to judicial enforcement where needed.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: Tele2 Sverige AB GDPR Transparency Fine
example_background: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection reviewed Tele2's processing of customer personal data for marketing. Privacy information was deemed unclear, incomplete, and inaccessible, particularly regarding data retention periods, recipients, and rights exercise. This impacted millions of subscribers.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, IMY fined Tele2 SEK 76 million (€6.9 million). The telecom adjusted its privacy notices and internal policies.

example_name: EDPB One-Stop-Shop Success Case - Twitter Ireland
example_background: Following the 2022 Twitter API changes restricting third-party access, numerous EU developers and researchers lost data access for GDPR-compliant research. The EDPB coordinated with concerned DPAs to exercise joint powers, demonstrating effective cross-border supervisory cooperation without impairing enforcement.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued binding decisions enabling Irish DPC to investigate Twitter (X) under the one-stop-shop mechanism, showcasing successful use of additional coordinated powers for consistent EU-wide application.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Fine by ICO
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, processed personal data of up to 1.4 million children under 13 in the UK without appropriate parental consent. The platform's age verification was inadequate, allowing underage users to create accounts and share videos. Data collected included names, locations, and biometric information from videos. The ICO's sweep of 15 apps found TikTok had the most significant shortcomings in age assurance and default privacy settings.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the ICO fined TikTok £12.7 million following an investigation. TikTok committed to improving child safety features, including better age checks and private-by-default accounts for under-16s. The fine was upheld without appeal.

example_name: CNIL Google GDPR Fine for Transparency Failures
example_background: France's CNIL investigated Google's processing of personal data for personalized advertising. Google's privacy policy was deemed too general, failing to provide specific information on data processing purposes, categories of data, and recipients. Consent banners did not adequately inform users or obtain granular consent for different processing activities. The practices affected millions of EU users relying on Android devices and online services.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, CNIL imposed a €50 million fine, one of the first major GDPR penalties. Google contested the fine before the French Council of State, which largely upheld it in 2020. Google updated its consent mechanisms and privacy notices in response.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Amazon France Logbase GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon's French subsidiary used data from its 'logbase' system, aggregating seller and customer data for advertising purposes without clear legal basis or transparency. The processing involved personal data to optimize ad targeting, affecting French marketplace users. CNIL found the purposes were not adequately specified in privacy notices.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Amazon €32 million in 2022 after investigation prompted by a competitor complaint. Amazon appealed but implemented changes to data processing practices.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: EDPB TikTok Geoblocking Decision
example_background: TikTok implemented geoblocking to restrict access to its Irish service for French IP addresses during a CNIL investigation into child data processing. The EDPB found this constituted child data processing without consent and violated single services principle. The practice aimed to limit regulatory jurisdiction but inadvertently processed French users' data.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB ordered CNIL to impose corrective measures, resulting in a €15 million fine upheld on appeal. TikTok adjusted its geoblocking and age verification globally.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cross-Border Decision (Facebook Ireland)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead controller for Facebook services in the EU, processed personal data of users across multiple Member States. Complaints were filed in various countries regarding data processing practices, including data retention and transfer mechanisms. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) was designated as the lead supervisory authority due to the location of the main EU establishment. Multiple other supervisory authorities, such as those from France (CNIL), Germany (BayLDA), and others, were concerned authorities because the data subjects and processing activities affected their jurisdictions. The process involved extensive exchanges of information, draft decisions, and objections from concerned authorities over several years, highlighting challenges in achieving consensus under the one-stop-shop mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued draft decisions which faced reasoned objections from 11 concerned authorities. The matter was referred to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the consistency mechanism (Article 65). The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, directing the DPC to impose fines totaling €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers to the US, €276 million for facial recognition violations, and additional penalties. Meta appealed aspects of the decisions.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 60 (Cooperation between the lead supervisory authority and the other supervisory authorities concerned)
GDPR Article 65 (Dispute resolution by the Board)

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children’s Data Case
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with its main EU establishment in Ireland, provides social media services used by millions of users across the EU, including children under 16. Complaints were lodged in multiple Member States concerning age verification, default privacy settings, and profiling of minors' data. The Irish DPC acted as the lead supervisory authority, while supervisory authorities from the Netherlands, Germany, Italy, and others participated as concerned authorities due to local data subjects and impacts. The cooperation involved sharing investigation findings, joint audits, and iterative draft decisions amid objections from concerned authorities on the scope and severity.
process_and_outcome: After consultations and a four-week objection period, several concerned authorities raised reasoned objections, triggering the consistency mechanism. The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2024, resulting in a €345 million fine by the DPC for violations related to children's data processing. TikTok was ordered to implement corrective measures across the EU.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions applicable to child's consent)

example_name: Amazon GDPR Advertising Case
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., based in Luxembourg, operates e-commerce and advertising services processing vast amounts of EU user data across Member States. The Luxembourg CNPD was the lead supervisory authority. French CNIL, after local investigations into personalized ads based on special categories of data, objected to the CNPD's initial handling. Other authorities from Spain, Germany, and elsewhere joined as concerned authorities, exchanging data on similar practices affecting their jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's objection led to EDPB involvement under Article 65. In 2023, the EDPB mandated a €35 million fine and ordered cessation of unlawful processing. The lead authority adopted the decision after consensus via the mechanism.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: British Airways GDPR Breach (One Stop Shop Application)
example_background: British Airways, part of International Consolidated Airlines Group with EU establishments including in the UK (pre-Brexit) and Ireland, suffered a data breach affecting 400,000 customers. Complaints and investigations spanned multiple countries. The Irish DPC was lead authority, coordinating with UK ICO and others on breach notification and security measures.
process_and_outcome: Through cooperation under Article 60, authorities reached consensus without objections. DPC imposed a €22 million fine in 2020, notified to BA and shared with concerned authorities; BA implemented EU-wide remediation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Cross-Border (CNIL vs. Irish DPC)
example_background: Google Ireland Ltd., main EU establishment in Ireland, provided analytics services involving US data transfers. French CNIL investigated local publishers' use, objecting to DPC's draft decision not to pursue transfers. Other authorities like AT (Austria) joined, exchanging evidence on Schrems II impacts.
process_and_outcome: Objections triggered consistency mechanism; EDPB ruled in 2022 that transfers violated GDPR, leading CNIL to order suspension (later appealed). Demonstrated objection and escalation process.

example_name: Microsoft Bing GDPR Joint Investigation
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Ltd. processes search data EU-wide. Coordinated investigation into geolocalization practices involved DPC as lead and authorities from France, Germany, Italy due to cross-border processing.
process_and_outcome: Authorities exchanged information electronically, conducted joint operations under Article 62, reached consensus on draft decision without objections. Fines totaling €20 million issued in 2022 for transparency failures.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-On Cases (EDPB Binding Decisions)
example_background: Following Schrems II CJEU ruling, multiple cases against controllers like Oracle, Salesforce with Irish establishments involved US transfers. Leads (DPC) drafted decisions; concerned authorities (e.g., Austria, Belgium) objected on adequacy.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued multiple binding decisions (e.g., Oracle 2023), imposing suspensions and fines, enforcing consistency after failed consensus.

example_name: Clearview AI Multi-State Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI, processing biometric data scraped from EU websites, faced complaints across Member States. No clear lead establishment initially; authorities cooperated via joint operations and consistency mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: EDPB coordinated; multiple fines issued (e.g., €30 million by Dutch DPA, upheld EU-wide). Demonstrated mutual assistance and information exchange.

example_name: Irish Data Protection Commission 2022 Annual Report
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC), serving as the supervisory authority for numerous major technology firms headquartered in Ireland, compiled its annual report for the year 2022. This document detailed the handling of 21,121 complaints from data subjects, the initiation of 64 new inquiries, and the conclusion of 32 formal inquiries. It included statistics on cross-border cases processed under the one-stop-shop mechanism, with 116 such cases where the DPC acted as the lead authority. The report provided breakdowns of infringement types such as unlawful data processing and insufficient legal bases for processing, alongside measures applied including reprimands, orders to bring processing into compliance, and administrative fines totaling €1.25 billion issued during the year. Data on audits conducted, both desk-based and on-site, were presented, covering 73 entities. The report also covered cooperation with other supervisory authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and responses to queries from the European Commission. Transmission occurred to the Irish Parliament's Joint Committee on Justice, Defence, Equality and Law Reform, the Department of Justice, and publication followed on the DPC's website for public access.
process_and_outcome: The report was formally adopted, transmitted to designated national bodies, published online, and shared with the European Commission and EDPB, fulfilling transparency obligations.

example_name: CNIL France 2023 Activity Report
example_background: The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), France's primary data protection authority, released its 2023 annual activity report encompassing oversight of data processing across public and private sectors. It documented receipt of 32,995 complaints, resolution of 93% of them, and processing of 733 investigation files including on-site controls at 169 organizations. The report listed infringement categories such as lack of consent, insecure data storage, and non-compliant international transfers, with details on 93 administrative formal notices, 46 injunctions, and fines amounting to €55 million, including notable sanctions against entities like Meta and Amazon. Sections addressed artificial intelligence processing, cookie compliance sweeps affecting 100 websites, and cooperation in cross-border cases. The document also outlined public awareness initiatives and consultations on draft regulations.
process_and_outcome: Report transmitted to the French National Assembly, Senate, and government; made publicly available on CNIL website, submitted to European Commission and EDPB.

example_name: Italian Garante 2022 Annual Report
example_background: The Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali published its 2022 annual report summarizing supervisory activities including analysis of 6,181 complaints, 1,456 reports under Article 17 for data erasure rights, and 140 notifications of data breaches. It cataloged processed violations related to direct marketing, video surveillance, and employee data handling, with measures taken such as 245 injunctions, 92 fines totaling €11.8 million, and 15 criminal referrals. The report highlighted 352 inspections, cooperation in 25 EDPB dispute resolutions, and guidelines issued on topics like facial recognition. Cross-border case involvement as concerned authority in 184 instances was detailed, alongside awareness campaigns reaching millions.
process_and_outcome: Submitted to Italian Parliament and Government, published online for public access, forwarded to European Commission and EDPB.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: UK ICO Annual Report 2022/23
example_background: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), the UK's data protection authority post-Brexit, issued its statutory annual report for 2022/23 covering enforcement of UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018. It reported handling 45,526 concerns, issuing 23 fines totaling £27.4 million for issues like unsolicited marketing and security failures, conducting 856 audits, and processing 1,293 data breach reports from controllers. The report included case studies on monetary penalty notices, enforcement notices, and details of regulatory actions against organizations in sectors such as finance and health. Children's data protection initiatives and AI guidance development were featured, along with performance metrics on complaint resolution times.
process_and_outcome: Laid before UK Parliament, published on ICO website, aligning with transparency requirements adapted from EU GDPR framework.

example_name: Spanish AEPD 2023 Annual Report
example_background: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) released its 2023 annual report detailing 22,780 complaints received, 7,243 investigation procedures initiated, and 1,512 sanctioning procedures concluded with fines exceeding €32 million. Categories of infringements covered cookies, video surveillance, and labor data processing, with lists of measures like warnings, reprimands, and compliance orders. The report noted 145 on-site inspections, handling of 2,500 breach notifications, and participation in EDPB activities including opinions on transatlantic data transfers. Public outreach efforts included 150 events and online resources.
process_and_outcome: Transmitted to Spanish Parliament (Cortes Generales) and Government, made available publicly via AEPD portal, shared with EU bodies.

example_name: Dutch AP 2022 Annual Report
example_background: The Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP), Netherlands' DPA, published its 2022 annual report covering 15,000 complaints, 400 investigations, and fines totaling €7.8 million for data breaches and unlawful profiling. It listed infringement types including non-transparent processing and inadequate security, with measures such as 50 administrative fines and 100 compliance orders. The report detailed cross-border cooperation in 80 cases, audits of government entities, and focus areas like health data and e-privacy. Statistics on breach notifications (3,500 received) and resolution rates were included.
process_and_outcome: Presented to Dutch Parliament (Tweede Kamer and Eerste Kamer), published online, submitted to European Commission and EDPB.

example_name: EDPB Overview of DPAs' 2022 Activity Reports
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) compiled an overview aggregating annual reports from all EU/EEA supervisory authorities for 2022, revealing over 400,000 complaints handled collectively, €2.5 billion in fines imposed, and thousands of corrective measures. It summarized common infringement types across member states such as compliance failures with data subject rights and lawfulness of processing, alongside trends in breach notifications exceeding 1,500 daily on average. The document facilitated benchmarking and identified priorities like AI and international transfers.
process_and_outcome: Published publicly by EDPB, drawing from individual DPAs' reports transmitted nationally and to EU institutions.

example_name: Belgian APD 2023 Annual Report
example_background: The Belgian Data Protection Authority (APD) issued its 2023 annual report, documenting 4,500 complaints, 300 investigations, and fines of €5.2 million primarily for security incidents and marketing violations. It provided lists of infringement notifications and Article 58 measures including reprimands and suspensions. Details covered litigation department activities with 50 court cases, inspection services' 120 controls, and first-line service resolutions. Cross-border case handling as lead or concerned authority was outlined.
process_and_outcome: Transmitted to Belgian Parliament and Federal Government, published on APD website, shared with EDPB and Commission.

example_name: Google Analytics and Consent Case (CNIL vs Austria)
example_background: In 2020, the French data protection authority (CNIL) declared the use of Google Analytics by Austrian Post AG unlawful due to data transfers to the US without adequate safeguards. This followed CNIL's formal notice to Google in 2022 regarding similar practices across websites. The case involved multiple EU supervisory authorities examining the validity of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for transfers amid invalidation of Privacy Shield.
process_and_outcome: CNIL ordered Austrian Post to comply within one month. Other authorities, including in Germany and Ireland, engaged in discussions but did not uniformly block. Google and affected entities adjusted transfer mechanisms to use approved addendums.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the internet without consent to build a biometric database sold to law enforcement and private entities. EU authorities coordinated on complaints regarding lack of legal basis and international transfers.
process_and_outcome: Multiple fines issued, including €20 million from the Dutch DPA in 2022 and €30 million from Greece. The EDPB coordinated uniform enforcement, leading Clearview to limit EU operations.

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing (EDPB Binding Decision)
example_background: WhatsApp processed user metadata for advertising purposes without fully transparent legal basis. Italy's Garante initiated the case, leading to a cross-border cooperation process.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, resulting in a €225 million fine upheld by Irish DPC, demonstrating consensus among concerned authorities after dispute resolution.

example_name: Schrems I/II NOYB Coordination
example_background: Max Schrems' NOYB organization filed coordinated complaints against Meta and others across EU countries regarding data transfers, prompting structured cooperation among supervisory authorities to handle identical issues efficiently.
process_and_outcome: Authorities exchanged information and reached consensus via EDPB mechanisms, leading to consistent fines and guidance on transfers, showcasing effective multi-jurisdictional collaboration.

example_name: CNIL France 2023 Annual Report Publication
example_background: The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), France's primary data protection authority, prepared its comprehensive annual report covering activities from January to December 2023. The report included statistics on 4,800 complaints processed, over 15,000 data breach notifications, and details on 120 on-site inspections conducted across various sectors including health, e-commerce, and telecommunications. It listed types of infringements such as non-compliance with consent requirements and inadequate data security measures, alongside measures imposed under Article 58(2) like warnings, reprimands, and fines totaling €55 million. The document highlighted cross-border cooperation in 25 cases and initiatives on emerging technologies like AI. CNIL's report also addressed public consultations on cookie policies and guidance issued to businesses on data protection impact assessments.
process_and_outcome: Published in June 2024, the report was presented to the French National Assembly and Senate, transmitted to the government, and shared with the EDPB and European Commission. A public version was released online, accompanied by a press conference and infographics for accessibility.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: French Data Protection Act (Loi Informatique et Libertés)

example_name: Italian Garante 2022 Annual Activity Report
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali) issued its 2022 annual report, documenting oversight of data processing in sectors like video surveillance, telemarketing, and digital services. It reported handling 6,200 complaints, 2,500 breach notifications, and conducting 400 investigations. The report categorized infringements including unlawful data transfers and failures in data minimization, and detailed corrective measures such as administrative fines exceeding €20 million, orders to cease processing, and injunctions. Emphasis was placed on enforcement against online platforms for dark patterns in user interfaces and cooperation in EDPB coordinated actions.
process_and_outcome: The report was submitted to the Italian Parliament, Presidency of the Council of Ministers, and other designated bodies in early 2023. It was made public on the Garante's website and forwarded to the European Commission and EDPB.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali

example_name: Spanish AEPD 2023 Activity Report
example_background: The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) released its 2023 annual report, which included data on 22,000 complaints, 18,000 breach notifications, and 150 inspections. It categorized issues such as violations in employee data handling and marketing practices, imposing fines over €30 million and various corrective orders. The report noted participation in 15 cross-border cases and educational campaigns reaching over 100,000 individuals.
process_and_outcome: Transmitted to the Spanish Congress of Deputies and Senate in mid-2024, made public, and sent to EU bodies for coordination.

example_name: EDPB Overview of National Reports 2022
example_background: The European Data Protection Board compiled an overview of national supervisory authorities' annual reports for 2022, aggregating data from 27 EU/EEA members. It summarized collective handling of over 150,000 complaints, 800,000 breach notifications, and €2.5 billion in total fines. The overview categorized common infringement types and enforcement measures across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: Published by EDPB in 2023 as a synthesized document, made available to the public and European Commission, highlighting consistency in reporting practices.

example_name: Polish UODO 2022 Activity Report
example_background: The Polish President of the Personal Data Protection Office (UODO) published its 2022 report, noting 5,200 proceedings, 8,000 breaches, and PLN 200 million in fines. Categories included public sector data handling issues and private sector consent failures.
process_and_outcome: Submitted to the Polish Parliament and government, made public, and shared with EDPB.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cross-Border Investigation
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 429,612 customers, where personal and payment card details were accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability on the company's website and mobile app. The incident involved hackers injecting malicious code that skimmed data over several weeks. The breach was reported to the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), which initiated an investigation. Given British Airways' parent company International Airlines Group is headquartered in Spain and operates across multiple EU countries, the case triggered coordination under GDPR mechanisms for cross-border data processing activities.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020 fining British Airways £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million draft due to COVID-19 impacts and cooperation). The UK supervisory authority led the process with input from other concerned authorities pursuant to GDPR cross-border rules, resulting in the fine being upheld after appeal considerations.
involved_parties: UK Information Commissioner's Office (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Marriott International Data Breach One-Stop-Shop Mechanism
example_background: Marriott International, a US-based hotel chain with extensive operations in the EU, acquired Starwood Hotels in 2016, inheriting a database that was breached starting in 2014. The breach exposed passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal data of up to 383 million guests. Marriott became aware in 2018 and notified regulators. As the company has its main EU establishment in Ireland, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) was designated as the lead supervisory authority under GDPR's one-stop-shop for cross-border processing.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC launched an investigation in 2018, coordinating with 12 other EU supervisory authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). In November 2020, the DPC imposed a fine of €20 million on Marriott for insufficient technical and organizational measures, following dispute resolution under the cooperation mechanism.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
EDPB and other EU supervisory authorities (concerned authorities)

example_name: Facebook (Meta) Schrems II Cross-Border Fine
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited, the lead EU entity for Meta's services, processed vast amounts of EU users' personal data in Ireland but transferred significant volumes to the US for processing. Following the Schrems II court ruling in 2020 invalidating the Privacy Shield, the Irish DPC investigated ongoing data transfers. The case involved coordination due to Facebook's operations across all EU Member States.
process_and_outcome: After mutual assistance requests and a draft decision circulated in 2022, the EDPB issued a binding decision in April 2023 mandating intervention. The Irish DPC fined Facebook €1.2 billion in May 2023 for unlawful data transfers, requiring cessation of transfers and return or deletion of data, with appeals ongoing.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland Multi-Country Enforcement
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, responsible for EU users, updated its terms in 2021 sharing user data with Meta companies for advertising, prompting complaints in Germany, France, Italy, Spain, and Greece. The Irish DPC investigated as lead authority for this cross-border processing activity affecting users in multiple Member States.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted a cross-border investigation with mutual assistance from other authorities. In September 2021, it fined WhatsApp €225 million for transparency violations, following EDPB input on the draft decision under the cooperation procedure.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
German, French, Italian, Spanish, Greek supervisory authorities (concerned authorities)

example_name: TikTok Ireland Child Privacy Cross-Border Case
example_background: TikTok Ireland Limited, handling EU user data, faced scrutiny over its age verification and privacy settings allowing under-13s to create accounts and exposing children's data. Complaints were filed in multiple countries including the UK, Netherlands, and Ireland, leading to a cross-border investigation coordinated by the Irish DPC.
process_and_outcome: Following requests for mutual assistance and information from other authorities, the Irish DPC issued a €345 million fine in September 2023 to TikTok for violations related to children's data processing, with ongoing coordination for implementation monitoring.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
UK ICO, Dutch, other EU supervisory authorities (concerned authorities)

example_name: Google Android GDPR Joint Case
example_background: Google LLC and Google Ireland handled Android pre-installed apps collecting user data for advertising across the EU. The French CNIL investigated after complaints about consent mechanisms, and due to cross-border nature, invoked the GDPR cooperation mechanism with other authorities.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL issued a €50 million fine in January 2019 as lead authority after consultations, marking one of the first major GDPR fines. Subsequent monitoring involved joint operations with other authorities to ensure compliance implementation.

example_name: Meta Platforms Child Data Processing Cross-Border Probe
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited processes data for Facebook and Instagram users EU-wide. Following complaints about behavioral advertising targeting minors, the Irish DPC opened a cross-border investigation in 2022, seeking assistance from authorities in the Netherlands and Germany where additional complaints originated.
process_and_outcome: The DPC requested mutual assistance under Article 61 and is conducting ongoing joint operations for monitoring. As of 2023, the investigation remains active with draft decisions circulated among concerned authorities for consistency.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Microsoft Ireland Cloud Services Coordination
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations processes EU customer data for Azure and Office 365 services, involving controllers in multiple Member States. The Irish DPC conducted a cross-border investigation into data retention practices in Microsoft 365, coordinating with authorities from seven other countries.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, after mutual assistance and joint efforts, the DPC announced no fine but required Microsoft to implement specific remedial measures for data retention and deletion, demonstrating effective cross-border cooperation.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Supervisory authorities from Germany, Italy, Spain, etc. (concerned authorities)

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Decision 3/2022 on Behavioural Advertising
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the lead supervisory authority for Meta's EU operations, processed personal data for behavioural advertising through its Facebook service. The processing involved combining data from logged-in and logged-out users across websites using cookies and tracking technologies. This occurred in the context of multiple national supervisory authorities raising concerns about the lawfulness of such processing under GDPR Article 6, particularly consent mechanisms and legitimate interests. National authorities from Belgium, Hamburg, and others initiated investigations into Meta's practices, leading to objections against the initial draft decision by the lead authority. The case highlighted complexities in cross-border data processing where processing operations span multiple EU member states, requiring coordination among supervisory authorities to assess compliance with GDPR principles of lawfulness, fairness, and transparency.
process_and_outcome: National authorities submitted relevant and reasoned objections to the lead authority's initial draft decision. The lead authority, after consideration, did not follow the objections and referred the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the GDPR cooperation mechanism. The EDPB adopted Binding Decision 3/2022 on 19 May 2022, finding Meta's processing non-compliant with Articles 6(1)(a) and 6(1)(b) GDPR. The lead authority was instructed to revise its draft decision accordingly. Meta was fined €1.2 billion in May 2023 by the Irish DPC for related data transfers, though the behavioural advertising decision process continued separately.
involved_parties: Data Protection Commission (Ireland) - Lead Supervisory Authority
Meta Platforms Ireland Limited - Data Controller
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter VII Section 2 (Cooperation and Consistency Mechanism)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Decision 1/2023
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with its lead supervisory authority in Ireland, processed personal data of minors through its video-sharing platform. Concerns arose from 14 concerned supervisory authorities across the EU regarding age verification, default privacy settings for minors, and profiling practices. The processing involved algorithmic recommendations and data collection from users under 16 years old, prompting objections to the Irish DPC's draft decision that proposed limited findings of non-compliance. The case exemplified multi-country coordination under GDPR for child data protection, where initial assessments by the lead authority were challenged by others seeking stronger enforcement.
process_and_outcome: The concerned supervisory authorities submitted relevant and reasoned objections. The Irish DPC decided not to follow them and referred the case to the EDPB. On 2 February 2023, the EDPB issued Binding Decision 1/2023, requiring the lead authority to amend its draft decision to find violations of Articles 5(1)(c), 8, 25(1), and others, and impose corrective measures. The Irish DPC issued a final decision in September 2023, fining TikTok €345 million.
involved_parties: Data Protection Commission (Ireland) - Lead Supervisory Authority

example_name: Google Ad Tech Services Decision 5/2023
example_background: Google Ireland Limited and Google LLC operated advertising technology services involving real-time bidding across the EU. The French SA (CNIL) investigated auction processes revealing user data to numerous bidders, leading to objections against the Irish DPC's draft decision that found no violation. 29 concerned SAs argued the processing lacked adequate legal basis and transparency, involving sensitive data categories. This cross-border case underscored tensions in lead authority assessments for large-scale tech platforms.
process_and_outcome: Objections were raised, not followed by the lead, leading to EDPB Binding Decision 5/2023 on 11 May 2023. The EDPB instructed a revised draft finding violations under Articles 5(1)(a), 5(1)(c), 6, 14. CNIL later fined Google €150 million in 2022 separately, with the process continuing.
involved_parties: Google Ireland Ltd & Google LLC - Controllers

example_name: Meta Transfers Case EDPB 1/2020
example_background: Following the Schrems II judgment, national SAs objected to the Irish DPC's draft decision on Meta's EU-US data transfers using SCCs. The processing involved millions of EU users' data sent to the US, with concerns over US surveillance laws undermining transfer safeguards. This initiated the first GDPR Article 65 dispute post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: Objections led to EDPB Binding Decision 1/2020 on 14 October 2020, requiring the Irish DPC to suspend transfers and amend its decision. The DPC ordered Meta to cease transfers, resulting in a €1.2 billion fine in 2023.

example_name: Hamburg v. Irish DPC on Facebook Consent (Art. 6(1)(a))
example_background: The Hamburg DPA objected to the Irish DPC's draft decision regarding Meta's reliance on user consent for Facebook data processing. The case involved assessment of consent validity in behavioural advertising and data retention practices across the EU.
process_and_outcome: Objection submitted; Irish DPC did not follow and referred to EDPB under Art. 65. Decision finalized with findings of issues in consent mechanisms.

example_name: Amazon France Logistics Processing Decision 1/2024
example_background: Amazon's logistics processing in France involved employee performance monitoring data. CNIL objected to the Irish DPC's draft finding no violation, arguing lack of transparency and legal basis for intra-group transfers.
process_and_outcome: Objections led to EDPB Binding Decision 1/2024 on 16 February 2024, instructing revised draft with violation findings. Process ongoing for final decision.

example_name: PrimaLoft Cross-Border Schrems II Objection
example_background: PrimaLoft GmbH faced a cross-border investigation into EU-US data transfers post-Schrems II. The German SA objected to the lead authority's handling.
process_and_outcome: Resolved through objections and revised draft under the two-week procedure; no EDPB referral needed as consensus reached.

example_name: EDPB Decision 2/2020 on Facebook Dublin Scope
example_background: Dispute on whether Facebook Ireland was the sole controller for all EU processing or if single services triggered multiple leads.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 2/2020 clarified scope, leading to revised draft by lead SA.

example_name: Dutch SA Objection against Irish DPC on Grindr Case
example_background: Grindr app's data sharing with advertisers prompted Dutch objection to Irish DPC draft on consent for sensitive data processing.
process_and_outcome: Objection submitted; led to revised considerations under cooperation rules.

example_name: TikTok EDPB Binding Decision on Child Data Processing
example_background: TikTok, a social media platform operated by ByteDance, processed personal data of users under 16 years old without appropriate safeguards, leading to concerns from multiple EU supervisory authorities regarding data protection practices across member states. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an investigation into TikTok's compliance with GDPR principles, particularly consent and lawful basis for processing minors' data. Other authorities, including those from the Netherlands, Denmark, and Germany, were consulted under the GDPR cooperation mechanism due to the cross-border nature of the service. They raised objections to the draft decision prepared by the lead authority, arguing that the proposed measures did not sufficiently address systemic issues in age verification and default privacy settings. The process highlighted tensions in coordinating enforcement actions where national authorities had differing views on the scope of violations and required remedies, spanning several years of inquiries starting from 2020.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC's draft decision was objected to by eight other supervisory authorities within the four-week period. The matter was escalated to the European Data Protection Board's (EDPB) dispute resolution procedure under Article 65. The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, mandating TikTok to implement stricter age assurance measures, cease unlawful processing, and face potential fines up to 4% of global turnover. TikTok was fined €345 million separately for other child data issues, with ongoing implementation monitored.
involved_parties: Dutch, Danish, German, French, Norwegian, Austrian, Italian, Spanish Data Protection Authorities

example_name: Meta GDPRTAB Consent Banner Investigation
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp in the EU, faced scrutiny over its 'pay or consent' model for behavioral advertising introduced in late 2023. The model offered users the option to pay a fee for ad-free access or consent to data processing for personalized ads. Multiple supervisory authorities across Europe, including Germany, Netherlands, and Spain, initiated investigations into whether this constituted valid consent under GDPR, given the cross-border data processing. The Irish DPC, as lead authority, was tasked with handling the primary cross-border case but received input from concerned authorities who questioned the draft findings. The consultations revealed disagreements on whether the financial incentive invalidated free consent and the adequacy of transparency in user choices.
process_and_outcome: Within four weeks of consultation, several authorities submitted relevant and reasoned objections to the Irish DPC's draft decision. The DPC did not fully adopt the objections and referred the matter to the EDPB consistency mechanism under Article 64. In April 2024, the EDPB adopted an opinion criticizing the model, leading to further proceedings. Meta suspended the model pending outcomes, with potential fines in the billions under discussion.
involved_parties: German BfDI, Dutch AP, Spanish AEPD, French CNIL

example_name: Amazon EU Data Processing Objection by French CNIL
example_background: Amazon Europe Core, operating the French marketplace, was investigated for its processing of employee and customer data for advertising purposes without adequate legal basis. The French CNIL conducted an in-depth investigation starting in 2020, uncovering issues with transparency and consent. As a cross-border processing activity under the One-Stop-Shop mechanism, the CNIL consulted other concerned authorities, including Luxembourg (Amazon's EU HQ lead) and Germany. Disagreements arose over the classification of processing operations and proposed sanctions, with some authorities viewing certain practices as compliant under legitimate interests.
process_and_outcome: Concerned authorities expressed relevant and reasoned objections within four weeks. The Luxembourg CNPDPC, as lead, did not fully follow them and submitted the draft to the EDPB under Article 64. In 2023, the EDPB urged alignment, leading to Amazon being fined €32 million by CNIL and €35 million by CNPDPC, with orders to revise data practices.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Planet49 Cookie Consent
example_background: Planet49 GmbH operated an online gaming platform that used pre-ticked checkboxes for cookie consent without clear separation of consent for analytics cookies from other processing. The German DPA investigated, but as a cross-border case, consulted other authorities. Several, including the Belgian and Norwegian DPAs, objected to the draft, arguing for stricter interpretation of consent validity across the EU to ensure consistency in cookie banner practices.
process_and_outcome: Objections were raised timely, leading to escalation to EDPB under Article 65 for a binding decision. In 2020, EDPB ruled that pre-ticked boxes do not constitute valid consent, affirming fines and setting precedent for cookie consents EU-wide.

example_name: Meta WhatsApp Irish DPC Cross-Border Consultation
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland was probed by the Irish DPC for transparency failures in its 2021 privacy policy update sharing user data with Meta for advertising. As a cross-border processor, consultations occurred with authorities from 11 member states, including Italy and Portugal, who provided input on user notifications and data sharing legitimacy. Some authorities objected to the draft's assessment of compliance with information obligations.
process_and_outcome: Relevant objections were submitted within four weeks, triggering Article 64 referral. EDPB issued an opinion in 2022, resulting in a €225 million fine upheld by courts, with enhanced transparency requirements imposed.

example_name: EDPB Resolution on Meta Geographic Data Processing
example_background: Meta's processing of special category data inferred from IP addresses and location for behavioral advertising prompted complaints in Germany. The Bavarian DPA consulted others, leading to objections from authorities in Spain and elsewhere on the lawfulness of inferring sensitive data categories.
process_and_outcome: Objections led to EDPB dispute resolution under Article 65, resulting in a 2023 binding decision prohibiting such processing and mandating cessation, influencing Meta's EU ad practices.

example_name: Max Schrems Facebook Data Transfers EDPB Involvement
example_background: Following the Schrems II ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's EU-US data transfers using SCCs. Consultations with other authorities like Austrian DSB revealed objections to the draft decision's risk assessment of US surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: Timely objections under Article 60(4) resulted in EDPB binding decision in 2023, suspending transfers and fining Meta €1.2 billion, enforcing consistency.

example_name: H&M Employee Data Monitoring German Case
example_background: H&M in Germany monitored employee private communications via a 'welcome back talk' process, recording sensitive personal data without proper basis. The Hamburg DPA handled the cross-border case, consulting others who objected to the proposed fine amount and remedial actions.
process_and_outcome: Objections within four weeks led to EDPB consistency input, resulting in a €35.25 million fine in 2023 and data deletion orders.

example_name: OneStopPay GDPR Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: OneStopPay, a Dutch payment service provider, processed personal data of over 2.5 million consumers across multiple EU member states including the Netherlands, Germany, Belgium, France, and the United Kingdom. The company acted as a data controller for payment data collected through webshops. In 2022, the Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) initiated an investigation following complaints about the company's data processing practices, particularly regarding the legal basis for processing and transparency. The investigation revealed that OneStopPay's data processing activities had implications in several EU countries due to the territorial scope of its operations. Under GDPR rules for cross-border processing, the Dutch DPA was designated as the lead supervisory authority because the company's main establishment was in the Netherlands. The DPA was required to notify and involve other concerned supervisory authorities from the affected member states to ensure consistent application of GDPR across borders.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA communicated the case details to the supervisory authorities in Germany, Belgium, France, and the UK without delay. A draft decision was submitted to these authorities for their opinions, which were duly considered. In July 2024, the lead authority imposed a fine of €1,350,000 on OneStopPay for violations including lack of valid legal basis for processing and insufficient transparency. The company was ordered to adjust its practices within three months. The coordinated approach ensured uniform enforcement across jurisdictions.
involved_parties: Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP, Dutch DPA, lead authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 5 and 6 (Principles and lawful basis)

example_name: H&M Personal Data Scraping Case
example_background: H&M, a global fashion retailer with its European headquarters in Germany, operated a data analytics center in Nuremberg where it collected and processed employee personal data from stores across multiple EU countries. Employees reported details about their private lives, which were stored and analyzed without explicit consent. The processing affected employees in Germany and other member states where H&M had operations. In 2020, the Bavarian DPA launched an investigation after employee complaints. Given H&M's main establishment in Germany for EU operations, the Bavarian DPA was the lead supervisory authority. The case qualified as cross-border processing due to the data concerning individuals in multiple member states.
process_and_outcome: The lead authority promptly informed the supervisory authorities in the other concerned member states, including those in Sweden, France, and others. A draft decision was shared for their input, and their views were taken into account. In October 2023, a fine of €35,256,000 was issued for violations related to unlawful data processing. The company was required to delete the unlawfully processed data and implement compliance measures. The process highlighted effective inter-authority cooperation.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Cross-Border Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited serves as the main establishment for Meta's EU operations, handling personal data for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp users across all EU/EEA member states. In 2023, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Meta's practices concerning the processing of personal data transferred to the US under the now-invalid Privacy Shield framework following the Schrems II ruling. The case involved data of millions of EU users and was clearly cross-border in nature. The Irish DPC acted as lead supervisory authority.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC notified other EU supervisory authorities without delay and submitted a draft decision for their opinions via the EDPB dispute resolution procedure. After considering views and mutual decisions, in December 2022, orders were issued requiring Meta to suspend transfers and fines totaling €1.2 billion were imposed. Meta appealed, but the process demonstrated the mechanism's application. In 2023, further decisions addressed ongoing compliance.

example_name: TikTok Children’s Data Processing
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with its EU main establishment in Ireland, processes vast amounts of personal data from users across the EU, including children's data through its short-video platform. In 2023, the Irish DPC investigated TikTok's age verification and parental consent mechanisms, which affected users in all EU member states. The probe focused on data processing practices from July 2020 to December 2021, involving millions of underage users.
process_and_outcome: As lead authority, the Irish DPC communicated relevant information to other EU DPAs without delay and shared a draft decision for opinions. Following EDPB involvement and consensus, in September 2023, a fine of €345 million was imposed for failures in protecting children's data. TikTok was ordered to implement enhanced safeguards. The coordinated enforcement ensured consistency.

example_name: Amazon France Logics Processing Investigation
example_background: Amazon Europe Core, with main establishment in Luxembourg, operates marketplaces across the EU, processing customer personal data extensively. In 2023, the French CNIL investigated Amazon France Logistiques' data processing for targeted advertising, based on approximately 600,000 complaints. The processing involved data from French users but was managed through Luxembourg-based systems, affecting cross-border applicability.
process_and_outcome: CNIL initially fined €32 million in 2022, but Luxembourg DPA (CNPD) contested lead authority status. The Irish DPC and EDPB were involved in dispute resolution. The EDPB confirmed Luxembourg CNPD as lead, requiring communication of information and draft decisions. The fine was suspended pending coordinated proceedings, demonstrating the consistency mechanism's role.

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland Data Sharing Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, part of Meta and based in Ireland, updated its privacy policy in 2021, expanding data sharing with Meta companies for advertising. This affected over 2 billion users, including those in the EU. The Irish DPC investigated transparency and legal basis issues as the lead authority for this cross-border processing.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC informed other EU DPAs promptly and shared a draft decision. Due to disagreements, the EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, resulting in a €225 million fine for transparency failures. WhatsApp implemented policy changes. The process exemplified the full cooperation and consistency framework.

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers Dispute
example_background: Google Ireland, the EU main establishment for Google services, provided Google Analytics and other tools used by websites worldwide, involving transfers of EU users' personal data to the US. In 2022, Austrian and other DPAs raised concerns post-Schrems II, leading to investigations into multiple providers using these tools. Several DPAs claimed lead authority.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC was designated lead and communicated details to concerned authorities. Draft decisions were shared, but disputes led to EDPB involvement. In 2023, coordinated actions resulted in bans or suspensions in countries like Austria and Italy, with ongoing harmonization efforts. Outcomes included tool reconfiguration by providers.

example_name: Facebook (Meta) GDPR Cambridge Analytica Fine
example_background: In 2014-2015, the Facebook platform allowed the harvesting of personal data from up to 87 million users worldwide via a third-party app called This Is Your Digital Life, developed by Cambridge Analytica. The data was used for political advertising without adequate consent or transparency. The ICO investigated Facebook's role as a data controller, focusing on its platform policies and data protection safeguards across multiple jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The ICO, designated lead authority for the UK aspects, fined Facebook £500,000 in July 2018. The draft decision under GDPR Article 60 was not objected to by concerned authorities, binding them to the outcome. Meta implemented changes to app permissions and data sharing policies.
involved_parties: Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta GDPR Cross-Border Processing Decision
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland processed EU users' personal data for behavioral advertising on Facebook and Instagram using a legitimate interests legal basis, involving extensive profiling and real-time bidding. The practices spanned multiple EU countries with centralized processing in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: After a multi-year investigation, the Irish DPC adopted a €1.2 billion fine decision in May 2023 as lead authority. Concerned authorities cooperated via Article 60 consultations, and with no timely objections to the final draft, the decision became binding. Meta faced ongoing compliance orders.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Breach Decision
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 429,612 customers. Attackers accessed customer data including names, addresses, payment card details, and CVV numbers through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The incident occurred between August 22 and September 5, 2018. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) led the investigation as the lead supervisory authority since British Airways' main establishment is in the UK. The breach involved personal data processing by the airline as a controller. Following the breach notification from British Airways, the ICO assessed the case under GDPR cooperation mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice in October 2020, fining British Airways £20 million (reduced from an initial £183 million draft). The ICO notified British Airways of the decision, published a summary of facts and grounds on its website, informed other concerned supervisory authorities and the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), and ensured complainants and affected individuals were updated on the outcome.
involved_parties: British Airways (controller, main establishment in UK)
Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Breach Case
example_background: Marriott International, operating the Starwood Hotels chain, suffered a data breach from 2014 to 2018, impacting up to 383 million guests. The breach involved reservation database access, exposing names, passport numbers, payment card details, and travel information. Detected in September 2018, the incident predated GDPR but affected EU residents. Marriott's main establishment is in the US, but it has significant EU operations. The ICO acted as lead supervisory authority due to the scale and UK nexus. Marriott notified the ICO within 72 hours as required.
process_and_outcome: In November 2020, the ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million. The decision was formally notified to Marriott's main establishment, with a summary of facts, grounds, and analysis shared with other supervisory authorities and the EDPB. Affected individuals and complainants received information on the decision through Marriott's notifications and ICO publications.
involved_parties: Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Facebook Cambridge Analytica EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: The 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal involved unauthorized access to data of up to 87 million Facebook users by an app developed by Aleksandr Kogan, shared with Cambridge Analytica for political advertising. Facebook (Meta) failed to protect user data adequately. Multiple complaints were lodged across EU member states, triggering GDPR's one-stop-shop mechanism. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) was designated as lead supervisory authority due to Facebook's European headquarters in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: After investigations starting in 2018, the Irish DPC drafted a decision in 2020. Concerned supervisory authorities objected, leading to a dispute resolved by the EDPB in April 2023 with a binding decision mandating a €1.2 billion fine. The Irish DPC adopted and notified the decision to Facebook Ireland Limited, informed other authorities and the EDPB, and updated complainants including NOYB.
involved_parties: Facebook Ireland Limited (controller, main establishment in Ireland)
Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Amazon EDPB Dispute on Advertising
example_background: Amazon's personalized advertising practices were challenged for processing vast amounts of personal data without valid legal basis or transparent consent. NOYB filed complaints in seven EU countries in 2018. Luxembourg's CNPD was the lead supervisory authority due to Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l.'s location there. The case highlighted complexities in cross-border processing and required coordination among 28 supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: The CNPD's 2021 draft decision to dismiss was contested. The EDPB issued a binding decision in July 2021 requiring a fine up to 4% of global turnover. CNPD adopted it in August 2021, fining Amazon €746 million. Notification went to Amazon's establishment, with summaries to other authorities, EDPB, and complainants informed.
involved_parties: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. (controller, main establishment in Luxembourg)
Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Google LLC GDPR Fine by French SA
example_background: Google's processing of personal data for personalized advertising in Android apps was investigated after complaints about lack of transparency and invalid consent. France's CNIL acted despite Google's main establishment in Ireland, as the processing targeted French users specifically. This led to disagreement with the Irish DPC, invoking GDPR's consistency mechanism.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google €50 million in January 2019. The Irish DPC and others challenged it, but the EDPB upheld CNIL's authority in a 2022 binding decision. CNIL notified Google, informed other authorities and EDPB with summaries, and updated complainants.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL, supervisory authority)

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland WhatsApp Decision
example_background: WhatsApp's sharing of user data with Meta companies for advertising was scrutinized for insufficient transparency in privacy policies. NOYB complaints in Austria, Netherlands, etc., led to the Irish DPC as lead authority investigating cross-border processing under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: In November 2022, the Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million after EDPB binding decision. The DPC adopted the decision, notified WhatsApp's Irish establishment, shared fact summaries and grounds with other authorities and EDPB, and informed complainants.
involved_parties: WhatsApp Ireland (controller, main establishment in Ireland)
Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Twitter (X) Children's Data Processing
example_background: Twitter's practices allowed children's accounts to be public by default and processed minors' data for advertising without safeguards. NOYB complaints across EU led to Irish DPC as lead authority for Twitter International Unlimited Company.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, Irish DPC fined €450,000. Decision notified to Twitter's establishment, summaries shared with other SAs and EDPB, complainants informed, resolving coordinated investigation.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead supervisory authority)

example_name: TikTok GDPR Urgency Suspension by Ireland DPC
example_background: In September 2020, the Irish Data Protection Commission initiated proceedings against TikTok Ireland Limited regarding data processing practices involving children's data. Concerns arose over the platform's handling of data for users under 16 years old, including insufficient age verification mechanisms and processing of sensitive personal data without adequate safeguards. The case involved large-scale data transfers and features that potentially exposed minors to inappropriate content. Authorities noted that the platform's global user base of over 1 billion, with significant numbers of young users in the EU, necessitated immediate action to address risks to children's privacy rights and safety.
process_and_outcome: The DPC used the Article 66 urgency procedure to suspend data processing for users under 16 in the EEA for six weeks, effective from 1 November 2020. TikTok complied with the order, implemented temporary age gates, and the suspension was lifted after remedial measures were verified. Further investigations continued, leading to additional scrutiny on data transfers to China.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Age Verification for Information Society Services)

example_name: Clearview AI Urgency Ban by CNIL
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition company, scraped billions of images from public websites without consent to build a database used by law enforcement. In 2021, the French supervisory authority identified that EU residents' biometric data was being processed and stored on servers accessible worldwide, posing risks of unauthorized surveillance and privacy intrusions. The company's technology enabled real-time identification, raising concerns about mass surveillance capabilities and lack of transparency in data handling.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL invoked Article 66 to issue an order on 21 May 2021 prohibiting Clearview AI from processing French residents' data for one month, later extended indefinitely. Fines totaling €20 million were imposed in subsequent decisions. Clearview challenged the measures but continued operations outside EU jurisdiction, prompting coordinated EU actions.
involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Amazon France Logistiques GDPR Urgency Fine
example_background: Amazon France Logistiques, operating warehouses for e-commerce fulfillment, was found to have implemented extensive employee monitoring systems using scanners, algorithms, and data analytics to track productivity metrics, breaks, and movements. Data from thousands of workers was collected continuously, creating detailed profiles without proper legal basis or transparency. This occurred amid high volumes of personal data processing in logistics operations across France, raising issues of worker surveillance and potential discriminatory practices.
process_and_outcome: In July 2020, the CNIL's Restricted Committee applied Article 66, fining the company €32 million for excessive monitoring and lack of information provided to employees. On-site inspections confirmed violations, leading to orders for compliance adjustments. Amazon contested the decision but implemented changes to monitoring practices.

example_name: Google Analytics Urgency Measures by CNIL
example_background: Google Analytics, provided by Google LLC and used by numerous websites, involved transferring EU users' personal data to the US via cookies and tracking technologies. Following the Schrems II ruling invalidating the Privacy Shield, concerns emerged about inadequate safeguards against US surveillance laws accessing IP addresses, user IDs, and behavioral data from millions of EU visitors daily. Multiple French websites continued using the tool without updated transfer mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL used Article 66 to order a French data controller to stop data transfers to Google US within one month due to urgency in protecting data subjects. This set a precedent, leading to similar actions across EU authorities and Google's suspension of services in France pending compliance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to Third Countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Urgency Order by Dutch AP
example_background: Meta Platforms, formerly Facebook, operated services involving behavioral advertising based on extensive profiling of EU users' data, including off-platform activities. The Dutch Authority for Consumers and Markets examined cookie practices and consent mechanisms, finding that tracking persisted without valid consent, affecting tens of millions of users' privacy through data aggregation for targeted ads.
process_and_outcome: In August 2022, the Dutch DPA invoked Article 66 to order Meta to cease unlawful data processing for advertising within one month. This followed the 'Facebook Ireland' case, with ongoing EDPB dispute resolution. Meta adjusted practices but faced continued enforcement.

example_name: H&M Data Breach Urgency Investigation by Hamburg DPA
example_background: H&M operated a service center in Nuremberg, Germany, where over 50 employees' personal data was stored on a central server. Between 2014 and 2018, managers accessed detailed notes on employees' private lives, health issues, family matters, and religious beliefs, shared during meetings without consent. This affected hundreds of workers and persisted despite internal complaints.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the Hamburg DPA used Article 66 urgency powers post a criminal complaint, fining H&M Justice CV €35.25 million. Immediate orders were issued to halt processing, with remediation required. The case highlighted employee data risks.
involved_parties: Hamburg DPA (Der Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit)

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Urgency Response by ICO
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a cyber-attack where hackers accessed payment card data and personal details of approximately 500,000 customers over 15 days via a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack vector. Data included names, addresses, card numbers, and CVVs, processed through the airline's booking system.
process_and_outcome: The UK ICO used urgency powers under GDPR (pre-Brexit) to investigate swiftly, proposing a £183 million fine in 2019, later reduced to £22 million in 2020 after appeal. BA enhanced security measures promptly.

example_name: Marriott International Data Breach Urgency Action by ICO
example_background: Marriott's Starwood reservation system was breached starting in 2014, with hackers accessing passport numbers, payment details, and travel information of up to 383 million guests worldwide, including 7 million EU residents. The breach continued undetected until 2018, involving encrypted but potentially decryptable data.
process_and_outcome: The ICO applied urgency measures in 2019, fining Marriott £124 million (reduced to £18.4 million on appeal). Immediate notifications and system audits were mandated to protect affected data subjects.

example_name: DPC Urgency Procedure Against WhatsApp
example_background: WhatsApp updated its terms in 2021, sharing user data with Meta companies for advertising, affecting over 2 billion users including 500 million in Europe. The Irish DPC examined consent validity and transparency for processing metadata and interactions across platforms.
process_and_outcome: In November 2021, the DPC used Article 66 to challenge the changes, leading WhatsApp to suspend sharing for EU users pending resolution. A €225 million fine was issued in 2021 for transparency violations, with urgency ensuring quick cessation.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Decision 5/2023 Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead controller for Facebook services in the EU, was subject to a decision by the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) regarding the processing of personal data of EU users transferred to the US. The processing involved the use of the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and supplementary measures for data transfers following the Schrems II judgment. The Irish DPC issued a draft decision in 2020 finding that Meta's transfer mechanisms did not ensure adequate protection. This draft was sent to concerned supervisory authorities (CSAs) under the GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism. Several CSAs, including those from France, Germany, Italy, and others, provided relevant objections, arguing that the draft decision did not fully address systemic issues in Meta's data transfers and processing practices across the EU. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) was tasked with issuing a binding decision to resolve the dispute. The process highlighted the application of GDPR Article 65 dispute resolution procedures involving multiple national authorities.
process_and_outcome: In April 2023, the EDPB adopted Binding Decision 5/2023, requiring the Irish DPC to amend its draft decision. The final decision by the Irish DPC in December 2023 imposed a 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta and ordered the cessation of unlawful data transfers with suspension of future transfers until compliance. Meta implemented measures including ceasing certain transfers, notified the Irish DPC of compliance actions, and the DPC informed CSAs. Meta appealed the decision to the Irish courts.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Concerned Supervisory Authorities (e.g., CNIL France, BfDI Germany)

example_name: TikTok Ireland GDPR Decision 2/2023 Multi-Country Enforcement
example_background: TikTok Ireland Limited, responsible for processing personal data of users on the TikTok platform across the EU, faced scrutiny over its age-appropriate design code and default privacy settings. The Irish DPC initiated an own-volition inquiry in 2020 into child data processing. The draft decision found issues with high privacy settings not being default and processing of children's data without adequate verification.
