CSAs from the Netherlands, Germany, Italy, Spain, and others raised objections, contending that the draft did not sufficiently address transparency, profiling, and data retention practices applicable across all EU establishments. The EDPB reviewed under the consistency mechanism.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 2/2023 in February 2023 directed the Irish DPC to impose corrective measures including a 345 million euro fine and orders to bring processing into compliance within specified timelines. TikTok notified the Irish DPC of implemented measures such as enhanced age verification and default privacy settings, and the DPC circulated updates to CSAs.
involved_parties: Concerned SAs (e.g., Dutch DPA, Italian Garante)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland GDPR Decision 3/2022 Cross-Border Case
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, the EU controller for the WhatsApp service, was investigated for transparency in its privacy policy updates in 2021. The Irish DPC's draft decision addressed compliance with information obligations but CSAs from Germany, France, Italy, Spain, and Greece objected, arguing it inadequately covered data sharing with Meta companies, legitimate interests basis, and joint controllership across EU users.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Decision 3/2022 in August 2022 required the Irish DPC to issue a final decision with a 225 million euro fine for transparency violations and orders to comply EU-wide. WhatsApp took measures to update policies and disclosures, notifying the Irish DPC which informed CSAs.

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Decision 1/2022 Consent Enforcement
example_background: Google Ireland Limited faced a cross-border inquiry into its processing of personal data in advertising services for EEA users. The Irish DPC draft found issues with consent validity for personalized ads. Objections from CSAs in France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Norway, and Spain emphasized need for broader assessment of data processing chains across establishments.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Decision 1/2022 in February 2022 led to a 2023 Irish DPC fine of 50,000 euros (reduced scope) but ordered compliance measures including consent improvements applied EU-wide. Google notified implementation to Irish DPC for dissemination.

example_name: Amazon Europe GDPR Decision 6/2023 Data Transfers
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., with lead in Luxembourg, was examined for EU-US data transfers post-Schrems II. CNPD Luxembourg draft addressed SCCs but CSAs objected on adequacy of supplementary measures for processing affecting all EU establishments.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Decision 6/2023 directed amendments resulting in orders to suspend transfers and implement compliant mechanisms. Amazon notified CNPD of measures taken EU-wide.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Enforcement Post-Brexit Compliance
example_background: British Airways, with EU processing under lead authority, experienced a data breach affecting 400,000 customers in 2018. UK ICO led but coordinated with EU SAs under GDPR consistency pre-Brexit.
process_and_outcome: ICO proposed 183 million GBP fine; reduced to 21.7 million GBP in 2020. BA implemented EU-wide security measures and notified lead SA which informed CSAs.

example_name: EDPB Case Study on Consistency Mechanism Success
example_background: The EDPB documented a case where a controller with establishments in multiple Member States received a decision from the lead SA on data retention practices. CSAs were informed and no objections raised, demonstrating smooth application across the Union.
process_and_outcome: Controller implemented uniform retention policies EU-wide and notified lead SA, which circulated confirmation to CSAs, ensuring consistent compliance.

example_name: Microsoft Ireland GDPR Cloud Processing Compliance
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited, as lead for Azure and Office 365 services, underwent audits ensuring data processing compliance across EU clients. Following lead SA decisions on data transfers, measures were uniformly applied.
process_and_outcome: Implemented Data Protection Addendum updates and supplementary transfer measures, notified Irish DPC which informed CSAs, achieving compliance without fines.

example_name: CNIL Decision on WhatsApp Complaint Rejection (2021)
example_background: A French individual filed a complaint with the French data protection authority (CNIL) alleging that WhatsApp processed personal data for behavioral advertising without proper legal basis after changes to its privacy policy in 2021. The complainant argued that the processing lacked consent and transparency. CNIL conducted a preliminary investigation, including analysis of WhatsApp's policy updates and data processing practices. CNIL determined that the processing occurred prior to the policy change notification and that no data had been transferred to Facebook entities for advertising purposes at that time. The authority concluded there was no breach of GDPR provisions on lawful basis for processing.
process_and_outcome: CNIL dismissed the complaint on October 4, 2021, adopted a formal decision rejecting it, notified the complainant of the dismissal, and informed WhatsApp of the outcome. No further enforcement action was taken against WhatsApp in this specific case.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 77 (right to lodge a complaint)

example_name: Irish DPC Rejection of NOYB Cookie Consent Complaints (2020)
example_background: The advocacy group None of Your Business (NOYB), led by Max Schrems, lodged 101 complaints with the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) against major websites including Google, Facebook, and others for using cookie banners that allegedly employed dark patterns to obtain invalid consent for non-essential cookies under GDPR. The complaints claimed pre-ticked boxes and misleading designs tricked users into consenting. The DPC reviewed the submissions, examined the cookie practices of the controllers, and assessed whether the consent mechanisms met the requirements for freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Irish DPC rejected 50 of these complaints after investigation, finding that the consent banners did not constitute a breach in those instances. The DPC adopted decisions dismissing the complaints, notified NOYB and the complainants, and informed the respective controllers. NOYB appealed several rejections to the courts.
involved_parties: Google, Facebook, and other website operators (controllers)

example_name: UK ICO Dismissal of Facebook Cambridge Analytica Complaint (2019)
example_background: An individual complainant lodged a complaint with the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) regarding Facebook's handling of personal data in the Cambridge Analytica scandal, alleging unauthorized sharing of data with third parties without consent. The ICO had already conducted a broad investigation into the matter, including data access practices and third-party app integrations on the platform. The complaint was assessed in light of the ICO's prior findings, which identified issues but also areas where Facebook's practices aligned with requirements.
process_and_outcome: The ICO rejected the complaint in 2019, determining it did not raise new issues beyond the ongoing enforcement. The authority issued a formal decision dismissing it, notified the complainant, and informed Facebook. This followed the ICO's separate £500,000 fine against Facebook.

example_name: CNIL Rejection of Data Breach Notification Delay Complaint Against Société Générale (2022)
example_background: A complainant filed with CNIL alleging that Société Générale failed to notify a personal data breach within 72 hours as required. The bank had experienced a cybersecurity incident affecting customer data. CNIL investigated the timeline of detection, internal response, and notification processes. It found that the bank detected the breach promptly and notified CNIL within the timeframe, with no evidence of delay or inadequate risk assessment to data subjects.
process_and_outcome: CNIL dismissed the complaint via a decision adopted on June 2022, notified the complainant of the rejection, and informed Société Générale. No sanction was imposed.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Dutch DPA Dismissal of Complaint Against bol.com on Data Retention (2021)
example_background: A consumer organization complained to the Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) that online retailer bol.com retained customer personal data longer than necessary after account deletion requests. The investigation involved reviewing bol.com's data retention policies, deletion processes, and justifications for any ongoing storage such as legal obligations or fraud prevention. The DPA found the practices aligned with GDPR minimization and storage limitation principles.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA rejected the complaint in a formal decision, notified the complainant, and informed bol.com. The decision was published, confirming no violation.

example_name: Italian Garante Rejection of TikTok Age Verification Complaint (2023)
example_background: Following concerns over child data processing, a complaint was filed with the Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali alleging TikTok failed to implement effective age verification. Garante examined TikTok's verification methods, including self-declaration and AI tools, and prior interactions where temporary bans were imposed. It assessed compliance with data protection by design and child-specific protections.
process_and_outcome: Garante dismissed the complaint after review, adopted the rejection decision, notified the complainant, and informed TikTok. This was separate from ongoing investigations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (conditions applicable to child's consent)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Dismissal of Complaint Against Telefónica Data Sharing (2020)
example_background: An individual complained to the Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) that Telefónica shared personal data with third parties without consent during a service contract. AEPD investigated contracts, consent records, and sharing logs, finding the disclosures were based on legitimate interests transparently communicated and necessary for service provision.
process_and_outcome: AEPD issued a resolution dismissing the complaint, notified the complainant, informed Telefónica, and closed the case without findings of non-compliance.

example_name: EDPB Case C-27/21 - Confirmation of Complaint Dismissal Procedures
example_background: In a preliminary ruling reference from a German court, a case arose from a complainant's challenge to a local supervisory authority's rejection of a GDPR complaint against a controller for alleged unlawful profiling. The background involved data processing for marketing, where the authority had dismissed after finding adequate safeguards. The CJEU clarified procedures for handling such dismissals.
process_and_outcome: CJEU ruled on February 9, 2023, affirming that supervisory authorities must adopt reasoned decisions on dismissals under Article 78 GDPR, notifying complainants. This upheld the German authority's approach.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 78 (right to an effective judicial remedy)

example_name: CNIL Decision on Lead Authority Coordination (Google Analytics Case)
example_background: In 2022, multiple EU data protection authorities received complaints regarding Google Analytics processing personal data for US-based companies. Complainants raised concerns about data transfers to the United States following the Schrems II judgment invalidating the Privacy Shield. The French CNIL was designated as the lead supervisory authority due to Google's main establishment in France for EU operations. Other concerned authorities from countries including Austria, Belgium, and the Netherlands participated in discussions under the GDPR cooperation mechanism. The process involved evaluating whether standard contractual clauses and supplementary measures adequately protected data transfers. CNIL proceeded with its investigation while coordinating with other authorities on specific aspects of the complaints, leading to segmented handling of data transfer and processing issues.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued a formal decision in June 2022 ordering Google to comply with GDPR obligations on data transfers within one month, with further proceedings for non-compliance. Other authorities handled local complaint aspects separately, resulting in notifications to complainants and Google. No full consensus was reached on all points, prompting separate decisions for acted-upon and dismissed parts. This led to ongoing EDPB dispute resolution proceedings.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland Cross-Border Complaint Handling (2023)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the main establishment for Facebook and Instagram in the EU, faced numerous complaints across member states concerning data processing for behavioral advertising. In one notable instance, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) acted as lead supervisory authority for complaints filed in Germany, Italy, and other countries. The complaints addressed issues like lack of legal basis for processing special category data and insufficient transparency. Concerned authorities engaged in extensive consultations via the EDPB's internal procedures, debating which parts of the complaints warranted enforcement actions against Meta and which could be dismissed due to prior adequacy decisions or lack of new evidence.
process_and_outcome: The DPC adopted a decision in 2023 addressing enforcement against Meta for certain processing activities, notifying Meta and complainants. The Italian Garante and other authorities issued separate decisions dismissing parts of complaints related to their local scopes while informing Meta. This resulted in a split approach with fines totaling over €90 million across authorities.

example_name: Schrems II Facebook Data Transfers Dispute
example_background: Max Schrems filed complaints with the Irish DPC in 2013 and later regarding Facebook's transfers of EU user data to the US under Safe Harbor and then Privacy Shield. The Irish DPC, as lead authority due to Facebook's EU headquarters, coordinated with 27 other EU supervisory authorities. The complaints evolved to challenge the validity of subsequent transfer mechanisms like standard contractual clauses. Extensive bilateral and multilateral discussions occurred, with some authorities pushing for immediate suspensions and others favoring further assessment.
process_and_outcome: Following CJEU's Schrems II ruling in 2020 invalidating Privacy Shield, the Irish DPC proposed decisions on enforcement parts, while several authorities like France's CNIL issued independent orders to stop transfers (later suspended). Separate decisions were adopted for suspension enforcement versus ongoing transfer adequacy reviews, with notifications to Facebook and Schrems.

example_name: Amazon EDPB Binding Decision on Amazon Data Processing
example_background: Following NOYB complaints in 2018 across Austria, Belgium, and other countries regarding Amazon's advertising practices, the Luxembourg CNPDCP was designated lead authority due to Amazon's EU headquarters. The complaints alleged opaque processing purposes and lack of data minimization. Concerned authorities disagreed on the scope, with some favoring dismissal of certain transparency claims based on prior rulings.
process_and_outcome: After failed conciliation, EDPB issued a binding decision in 2021. CNPDCP adopted the enforcement decision fining Amazon €746 million, notifying Amazon and complainants. Austrian DPA issued a separate decision dismissing remaining complaint parts and informed parties.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Multinational Enforcement
example_background: From 2021, complaints targeted Clearview AI's scraping of billions of facial images without consent, filed with authorities in France, Greece, Italy, etc. No single EU establishment led to multiple leads, but coordination occurred. French CNIL handled parts as a concerned authority alongside others.
process_and_outcome: Multiple authorities issued bans and fines totaling over €30 million by 2022, with CNIL ordering cessation and fining €20 million. Separate decisions addressed enforcement versus rejected jurisdictional claims, notifying Clearview and complainants.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision Microsoft Cloud Transfers
example_background: Complaints in 2021-2023 across EU countries questioned Microsoft’s EU-US data transfers in Azure and Microsoft 365. Irish DPC as lead coordinated with Dutch, Norwegian authorities on transfer mechanisms post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued binding decision in 2023 directing DPC to act on transfer enforcement. DPC adopted decision for controller obligations, while Norwegian DPA dismissed local complaint parts separately, with notifications.

example_name: H&M Data Processing EDPB Dispute Resolution
example_background: German complaints in 2020 about H&M's employee data processing using gossip sessions led to Hamburg DPA as lead, coordinating with other authorities on cross-border aspects of HR data flows.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA fined H&M €35.25 million in 2023. Other authorities dismissed ancillary claims separately post-consultation, issuing notifications.

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Facebook EU-US Data Transfers
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission initiated an investigation into Facebook Ireland Limited's processing of personal data transferred from the European Union to the United States using the EU-US Privacy Shield framework. Concerns arose regarding the adequacy of data protection for EU citizens' data sent to the US following the Schrems II judgment by the Court of Justice of the European Union in July 2020, which invalidated the Privacy Shield. The investigation examined whether the transfers complied with GDPR requirements for appropriate safeguards. Other EU supervisory authorities participated through the European Data Protection Board's cooperation mechanisms to ensure consistent application across member states, given Facebook's operations spanned multiple jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted a cross-border investigation with input from other EU DPAs via the EDPB's dispute resolution procedure. In May 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision requiring Facebook to cease unlawful data transfers within five months and delete data transferred unlawfully. Facebook was fined €1.2 billion by the Irish DPC in December 2022 for GDPR violations related to transfers.

example_name: OneTrust-Google Cloud Data Transfer EDPB Case
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, transferred personal data from the EU to Google Cloud Platform servers in the US. The French DPA (CNIL) initiated an investigation, and other EU DPAs, including the Irish DPC, provided mutual assistance by sharing information on similar cloud transfer practices to ensure uniform application of GDPR transfer rules post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023 instructing the CNIL to suspend the data transfers and prohibit future transfers without adequate safeguards. OneTrust was required to implement additional measures like encryption and contractual protections.

example_name: Meta Biometric Data Scraping Investigation
example_background: National DPAs in Europe launched inquiries into Meta Platforms Ireland Limited's alleged scraping of biometric data from public websites for AI training purposes. The Irish DPC served as the lead supervisory authority, coordinating with other EU DPAs such as those in Spain, Germany, and Italy, who provided relevant information and evidence from local complaints to facilitate a consistent cross-border investigation.
process_and_outcome: The cooperation led to a unified investigation approach under EDPB oversight, resulting in ongoing proceedings as of 2024, with preliminary findings highlighting the need for lawful basis for such processing.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 62 (joint operations of supervisory authorities)

example_name: Amazon EU-US Data Transfers EDPB Dispute Resolution
example_background: The Luxembourg DPA investigated Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l.'s data transfers to the US under standard contractual clauses. Other EU DPAs, including the French CNIL and Dutch AP, engaged in mutual assistance by submitting information on Amazon's operations in their territories to resolve differences and ensure consistent GDPR enforcement across the EU.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the EDPB used its dispute resolution powers under GDPR Article 65 to issue a binding decision requiring suspension of transfers lacking sufficient safeguards, influencing Amazon's compliance adjustments.

example_name: WhatsApp GDPR Fines by Multiple DPAs
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited's transparency practices regarding data sharing with Meta companies prompted complaints in nine EU countries. The Irish DPC as lead authority received mutual assistance from other DPAs, including those in the Netherlands and Germany, who provided local investigation findings to ensure consistent assessment of GDPR compliance.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB's binding decision in 2021 led to a €225 million fine by the Irish DPC against WhatsApp, with coordinated follow-up actions by other authorities.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Response to Schrems II Impacts
example_background: Following the CJEU's Schrems II ruling invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield, the EDPB facilitated information exchange among all EU DPAs on international transfer practices by major tech firms. This included requests for inspections and consultations to verify supplementary measures like encryption.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued recommendations and coordinated over 100 investigations, leading to multiple enforcement actions and enhanced transfer tools like the EU-US Data Privacy Framework adoption in 2023.

example_name: Spotify Cross-Border Data Processing Coordination
example_background: Complaints about Spotify's user data processing for personalized recommendations led to cooperation between the Swedish IMY (lead DPA) and other EU authorities like the French CNIL, who exchanged information on user profiling practices across borders.
process_and_outcome: The coordinated review resulted in Spotify adjusting its practices and avoiding major fines through voluntary compliance measures informed by joint supervisory input.

example_name: DPC v WhatsApp Cross-Border Fine
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Facebook, updated its terms in 2021 to share user data with other Meta companies for commercial purposes. This affected millions of EU users, raising concerns over transparent consent and data sharing practices. The Irish DPC, as the lead supervisory authority for WhatsApp's European operations, conducted an investigation into compliance with GDPR transparency and purpose limitation principles. Other EU authorities objected to the initial findings, leading to a dispute resolution process under GDPR's cooperation mechanism.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued a binding decision, resulting in a €225 million fine imposed by the DPC on WhatsApp. The process involved contributions from 30 EU/EEA supervisory authorities via the internal market mechanism. WhatsApp revised its privacy policy following the decision.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 (Information to be provided where personal data are collected from the data subject)

example_name: CNIL v Google Android Fine
example_background: Google's Android OS and app store practices were scrutinized for lack of transparency in cookie consent banners and data processing for ads. Users were not properly informed about data collection purposes. The French CNIL acted unilaterally initially but coordinated with other authorities post-GDPR enforcement.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the CNIL fined Google €150 million. This followed a formal cooperation process under GDPR Articles 60-65. Google updated its consent mechanisms across EU services.

example_name: EDPS Investigation into EU Institutions' Data Breaches
example_background: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) handled multiple personal data incidents reported by EU institutions using the Internal Commissioner's centralized system. From 2018-2022, over 1,000 incidents were logged, involving email misconfigurations and lost devices. The EDPS standardized reporting templates and shared anonymized data with national authorities for benchmarking.
process_and_outcome: The EDPS published annual reports detailing incident trends and remediation advice. No fines were issued to institutions, but systemic improvements were mandated. This demonstrated effective use of standardized electronic reporting across supervisory bodies.

example_name: OneTrust GDPR Case Management Tool Adoption
example_background: Numerous organizations, including EU public bodies, adopted platforms like OneTrust for automated GDPR breach notifications. These tools use standardized XML formats compatible with EDPB templates, enabling one-click submissions to lead and concerned authorities. For instance, the Austrian DPA integrated it for cross-border cases, reducing manual errors in multi-jurisdictional notifications.
process_and_outcome: Successful deployments led to faster notifications (within 24 hours) and audit trails, praised in EDPB reports. No fines resulted from tool-related issues; instead, it facilitated compliance in cases like healthcare data spills.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EDPB Guidelines 07/2020 on data breach notifications

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cross-Border Enforcement (2023)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead supervisory authority for Facebook and Instagram in the EU, faced coordinated actions from multiple national data protection authorities regarding the implementation of GDPR post-Schrems II ruling. Following the invalidation of the EU-US Privacy Shield, Meta continued data transfers to the US using Standard Contractual Clauses without sufficient supplementary measures. Several supervisory authorities, including those from Ireland, Germany, France, and others, initiated a coordinated investigation through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) cooperation mechanisms. Information requests were exchanged among authorities to assess the adequacy of data protection safeguards for over 500 million EU users' data. Delays in responses and differing national positions led to tensions in mutual assistance processes.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision on April 11, 2023, directing the Irish DPC to impose a €1.2 billion fine on Meta and suspend future data transfers for up to five months. National authorities enforced complementary measures, totaling additional fines exceeding €700 million across jurisdictions. Meta appealed but suspended transatlantic transfers pending resolution.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
German BfDI, French CNIL, Italian Garante (objecting supervisory authorities)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Cross-Border Investigation (2023)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, headquartered in Singapore with EU operations led by Ireland, came under scrutiny for processing personal data of minors without adequate age verification and parental consent features. The Irish DPC launched an investigation in 2020, requesting mutual assistance from supervisory authorities in Italy, Netherlands, Denmark, Germany, and others where similar concerns arose from national complaints. Authorities exchanged detailed information on TikTok's algorithmic profiling and default public settings for under-16 users, affecting millions across Europe. The cooperation involved standardized electronic exchanges of investigation files and progress reports.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC imposed a €345 million fine following EDPB dispute resolution. Additional national fines were levied, including €12 million in Italy. TikTok implemented EU-wide age assurance tools and data minimization measures as remedial actions.
involved_parties: Italian Garante, Dutch AP, Danish Datatilsynet (mutual assistance providers)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Articles 8 and 25 (Child Consent and Data Protection by Design)

example_name: Amazon France Logistiques EDPB Binding Decision (2023)
example_background: Amazon's French subsidiary processed employee personal data through an HR software system using algorithms for performance monitoring and disciplinary decisions. French CNIL objected to the lack of transparency and lawful basis, requesting mutual assistance from the Irish DPC as Amazon EU lead authority. Information exchanges revealed inconsistencies in data processing across borders. Luxembourg's CNPD also participated due to Amazon's European HQ. The case highlighted challenges in consistent application of GDPR employment data rules.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued Binding Decision 5/2023 in December 2023, requiring CNIL to ban the disputed processing and fine Amazon €32 million. The decision enforced uniform enforcement after mutual assistance delays exceeded one month.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-Up: Google Analytics Transfers (2022)
example_background: Multiple national authorities, starting with Austria's DSB, investigated Google LLC and Alphabet Inc.'s use of Google Analytics and Google Tag Manager for transferring EU user data to the US without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II. Requests for mutual assistance were sent to Irish DPC (Google's lead) and others, exchanging technical data on IP addresses, device IDs, and cookies. Over 20 authorities cooperated via EDPB internal mechanisms, using standardized formats for information sharing on transfer tools.
process_and_outcome: Authorities issued coordinated guidance and enforcement notices, leading to Google suspending Analytics services in the EU. Fines issued in Italy (€10 million) and Austria, with ongoing harmonized actions.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cross-Border Coordination (2019-2021)
example_background: British Airways suffered a Magecart attack exposing 400,000 customers' payment data. The UK ICO, pre-Brexit, coordinated with Irish DPC and others for mutual assistance on IAG group's GDPR compliance. Information requests covered breach notification timelines, risk assessments, and compensation processes. Post-Brexit, UK-EU adequacy decision facilitated continued cooperation.
process_and_outcome: ICO fined British Airways £20 million (reduced from £183 million on appeal). Irish DPC contributed to group-wide remediation, including enhanced security protocols.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Multinational Enforcement (2022)
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, supplying data to law enforcement globally. EU-wide complaints triggered mutual assistance requests from French CNIL, Greek DPA, Italian Garante, and others to US-based company via Irish DPC and UK ICO. Authorities exchanged evidence on data scraping volumes, client lists, and absence of legal basis, using electronic standardized formats.
process_and_outcome: EDPB coordinated bans and fines totaling over €30 million (e.g., €20 million France, €9.5 million Italy). Clearview withdrew from EU market.
involved_parties: Greek HDPA, Italian Garante, Irish DPC (assisting)

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing EDPB Decision (2022)
example_background: Meta's WhatsApp processed user metadata for targeted advertising without transparent legal basis. Irish DPC's draft decision was challenged by 10 other authorities requesting mutual assistance for information on profiling practices affecting 2 billion users. Exchanges included audit reports and user impact assessments across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 3/2022 upheld objections, resulting in €225 million fine on WhatsApp and mandated transparency updates.
involved_parties: German BfDI, Dutch AP, Norwegian Datatilsynet (objectors)

example_name: EDPB Internal Market Case on planetary.de (2021)
example_background: German company planetary.de processed sensitive health data for fitness services without adequate safeguards. Bavarian supervisory authority handled as lead, but other authorities requested mutual assistance to verify cross-border data flows to US servers. Cooperation involved progress reports and refusal considerations under Article 61(4).
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued first-ever Internal Market Opinion, leading to €825,000 fine and transfer suspension.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection (lead)

example_name: Max Schrems vs Facebook (Pre-GDPR Transition Assistance 2018)
example_background: During GDPR transition, Irish DPC received mutual assistance from Belgian, Hamburg, and other authorities in ongoing Facebook data transfer litigation. Exchanges covered Standard Contractual Clauses validity pre-Schrems II, involving millions of EU users' data.
process_and_outcome: Contributed to CJEU Schrems II ruling invalidating Privacy Shield. Led to €18.4 million fine on Facebook.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cross-Border Inquiry Cooperation
example_background: Meta Platforms, operator of Facebook and Instagram, faced multiple data protection investigations across EU member states concerning user data processing practices, including data transfers and consent mechanisms. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) served as the lead supervisory authority due to Meta's EU headquarters location in Ireland. Other EU supervisory authorities, such as those in France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, Norway, Spain, and others, submitted formal requests under GDPR Article 61 for information and updates on the ongoing inquiries into Meta's compliance with GDPR principles. These requests sought details on the scope, progress, and findings of investigations related to behavioral advertising and data retention policies spanning several years.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC responded to the coordinated requests from 11 other supervisory authorities within the one-month deadline, transmitting relevant information on investigation conduct. This facilitated the formation of an Art. 65 dispute resolution procedure by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), culminating in a €1.2 billion fine against Meta in 2023 for unlawful data transfers to the US, with input from the cooperating authorities.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: Amazon EU Data Protection Probe Cooperation
example_background: Amazon Europe Core faced investigations into its advertising practices and data processing for targeted ads. The Luxembourg CNPD served as lead supervisory authority. Authorities from France (CNIL), Germany, Italy, Spain, and others submitted requests for information on the investigation's progress, evidence gathered, and preliminary findings under GDPR cross-border cooperation provisions.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CNPD transmitted the requested information within the one-month timeframe, supporting an EDPB binding decision under Art. 65. Amazon received a €746 million fine in 2021, later adjusted on appeal, with cooperation enabling consistent enforcement.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach Cross-Border Assistance
example_background: In 2018, British Airways suffered a data breach affecting 400,000 customers' payment details. The UK ICO led the investigation, but post-Brexit transition and pre-Brexit cooperation involved EU supervisory authorities requesting details on the breach's scope, notification processes, and remediation measures impacting EU residents under GDPR mutual assistance.
process_and_outcome: ICO provided timely responses to requests from EU counterparts within one month, sharing breach investigation reports. This contributed to a £20 million fine by ICO in 2020, with coordinated insights from EU authorities.

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Response to CNIL Amazon Request
example_background: French CNIL requested information from Luxembourg CNPD (lead for Amazon) on investigations into Amazon's data processing for advertising, including algorithms used and user profiling methods. This was part of broader EU coordination.
process_and_outcome: CNPD responded promptly within one month, providing details that informed CNIL's independent fine of €32 million against Amazon France in 2020, demonstrating effective mutual assistance.

example_name: Clearview AI Cross-Border Enforcement Cooperation
example_background: Clearview AI's facial recognition database prompted investigations across EU states. Multiple authorities, including Dutch AP, Greek DPA, and others, requested assistance from leads in ongoing probes into data scraping from public websites without consent.
process_and_outcome: Requesting authorities received responses within timelines, leading to bans and fines totaling over €30 million by 2022, with shared investigation data enabling consistent application.

example_name: Planet49 Consent Wrapper Investigation
example_background: German company Planet49 was investigated for cookie consent practices. Berlin DPA as lead handled requests from other EU authorities for details on pre-ticked boxes and consent validity in tracking technologies.
process_and_outcome: Berlin DPA shared investigation findings within one month, contributing to EDPB guidelines and a €9.5 million fine coordination.

example_name: EDPB Case C-307/22 Meta Consent Transfers
example_background: Ongoing Meta investigations involved requests among authorities for updates on standard contractual clauses and consent for US data transfers post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: Lead Irish DPC provided timely information to cooperating authorities, supporting EDPB's 2023 decision and €1.2 billion fine.

example_name: Irish DPC Assistance to UK ICO on British Airways Breach
example_background: In 2019, the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated a data breach at British Airways affecting 400,000 customers' payment card details. The breach involved a cyber-attack on the airline's website during a booking process. The ICO sought mutual assistance from the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) as the lead supervisory authority for British Airways' parent company International Airlines Group (IAG), headquartered in Ireland. This cooperation was part of cross-border case handling under GDPR mechanisms. The request involved sharing information and coordinating on the investigation scope, including assessing the breach notification and remedial actions taken by the company. The process highlighted the operational framework for supervisory authorities collaborating across EU member states without financial exchanges for standard assistance activities.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC provided assistance to the ICO, enabling a unified investigation. The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice of £20 million (reduced from £183 million after appeal) to British Airways. No fees were charged by the Irish DPC for its involvement, aligning with mutual assistance protocols. The case contributed to broader GDPR enforcement precedents on breach handling.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 62 (Joint Operations of Supervisory Authorities)
GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a Personal Data Breach)

example_name: CNIL Assistance to Italian Garante in WhatsApp Investigation
example_background: The Italian data protection authority, Garante, initiated an investigation into WhatsApp's data processing practices, particularly the sharing of user data with Facebook for advertising purposes. As WhatsApp's European lead supervisory authority is the Irish DPC, Garante requested mutual assistance. Subsequently, the French CNIL also engaged by requesting assistance from the Irish DPC regarding similar data flows involving French users. This multi-jurisdictional effort examined compliance with data protection principles, transparency in privacy policies, and lawful basis for processing across EU borders. The cooperation involved exchanging investigation findings, documentation, and conducting joint inquiries into WhatsApp's data handling mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC responded to assistance requests from CNIL and Garante without charging fees. The Italian Garante imposed a €10 million fine on WhatsApp in 2019 for transparency violations. CNIL later fined WhatsApp €35 million in 2021 for cookie consent issues. The mutual assistance facilitated coordinated enforcement actions across authorities.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 5 (Principles Relating to Processing of Personal Data)

example_name: EDPB Coordination in Facebook Cambridge Analytica Case
example_background: Following the 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal, multiple EU supervisory authorities investigated Facebook's data protection practices related to unauthorized data harvesting affecting millions of users. The UK's ICO led initial efforts but triggered cross-border cooperation due to Facebook's Irish establishment. Authorities from France (CNIL), Germany (multiple Länder authorities), and others requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC. The assistance included providing access to internal Facebook documents, audit reports, and details on data processing flows. This extensive collaboration aimed to ensure consistent application of GDPR across jurisdictions amid high-profile public and political scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC handled assistance requests from various authorities without fees, leading to a €275 million fine in 2021 for Facebook Ireland's GDPR violations related to political advertising. Additional fines were issued by other authorities, such as CNIL's €60 million in 2019. The EDPB dispute resolution mechanism was activated to resolve disagreements, demonstrating structured mutual assistance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 65 (Dispute Resolution by the Board)

example_name: Spanish AEPD Request to Irish DPC on Google Analytics
example_background: In 2022, the Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) investigated several organizations using Google Analytics and Google Tag Manager, focusing on data transfers to the US potentially violating GDPR post-Schrems II. The AEPD requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC, as Google Ireland is the lead authority for Alphabet Inc.'s services in the EU. The assistance involved clarifying Google's data processing agreements, standard contractual clauses implementation, and supplementary measures for data transfers. This was part of a wave of similar investigations across EU states examining third-country data flows.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC provided the requested information and cooperation without charging fees. AEPD issued fines to affected Spanish entities and banned Google Analytics use pending compliance fixes. The case prompted Google's updates to data transfer mechanisms and influenced EDPB guidelines on transfers.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to Third Countries)

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Assistance to Dutch Authorities on TikTok
example_background: The Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD) serves as lead authority for TikTok (ByteDance) in the EU. In 2020-2021, Dutch and other authorities requested mutual assistance investigating TikTok's handling of minors' data, personalized advertising, and age verification processes. Concerns arose from platform features allowing data collection from children under 13 without adequate parental consent. Assistance included sharing compliance audit results, internal policies, and responses to subject access requests from affected users across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: CNPD provided assistance without fees, contributing to coordinated actions. Dutch DPA fined TikTok €725,000 in 2021; CNPD imposed €345 million in 2023 for children's data violations. EDPB binding decision ensured consistency.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions Applicable to Child's Consent)

example_name: French CNIL Request to Irish DPC on Amazon Data Practices
example_background: Amazon Web Services Europe (Ireland-based) became subject to a French CNIL investigation in 2020 into advertising personalization based on extensive user profiling. CNIL requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC to access Amazon's internal data processing records, consent mechanisms, and transparency notices. The probe examined compliance with purpose limitation and lawful basis for processing behavioral data from millions of French users.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC cooperated without charging fees. CNIL imposed a €35 million fine on Amazon France Logistique in 2020. Further joint efforts led to additional scrutiny and improvements in Amazon's EU practices.

example_name: EDPB Joint Opinion on Cross-Border TikTok Children's Data Case
example_background: Multiple EU supervisory authorities, concerned with TikTok's data practices affecting children, requested mutual assistance from Luxembourg CNPD. Issues included default public profiles for minors, inadequate age assurance, and processing for behavioral analysis. The cooperation facilitated a comprehensive review of TikTok's global privacy settings rolled out in EU markets.
process_and_outcome: Assistance provided fee-free, resulting in EDPB's binding decision and CNPD's €345 million fine in 2023. TikTok implemented platform-wide changes to settings and verification.

example_name: German BfDI Assistance to Irish DPC on LinkedIn Processing
example_background: The German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection (BfDI) requested mutual assistance from Irish DPC regarding LinkedIn Ireland's data processing for professional networking, focusing on consent for tracking and data retention periods. This followed user complaints about unsolicited profiling across EU states.
process_and_outcome: DPC provided assistance without fees, leading to LinkedIn's adjustments. CNIL separately fined LinkedIn €310 million in 2022; coordinated efforts enhanced enforcement consistency.

example_name: Google Analytics EU-US Transfers (Austrian Case)
example_background: In 2022, the Austrian Data Protection Authority investigated a non-profit organization's use of Google Analytics, which transferred EU user data to the US without adequate safeguards post-Schrems II. The plugin configuration sent IP addresses and user agents without anonymization, and the data processing agreement with Google did not specify all purposes for US-based processing.
process_and_outcome: The Austrian DPA ordered the organization to stop transfers in January 2022, finding violations of purpose limitation and transfer rules. This decision influenced similar probes across Europe, leading Google to update Analytics configurations.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud Transfers Fine
example_background: The Spanish AEPD investigated Telefónica's use of Microsoft Azure and Office 365 services, which involved transferring employee and customer data to the US. Privacy notices did not detail the purposes for data processing by Microsoft, and standard contractual clauses were deemed insufficient without supplementary measures for purpose limitations.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, AEPD ordered Telefónica to halt transfers and fined Microsoft indirectly through the proceedings. Telefónica suspended services pending compliance adjustments.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision 1/2023 on Criteo
example_background: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) conducted an investigation into Criteo SA, a French company providing online behavioral advertising services, following complaints about processing personal data without adequate legal basis and transparency. As Criteo was established in France, the Garante requested mutual assistance from the French SA (CNIL) under GDPR Article 61 to gather information and perform checks. The CNIL responded with partial information but refused certain requests citing resource constraints and relevance. The Garante escalated the matter, and after further exchanges where progress updates were shared, the issue proceeded to the EDPB under Article 65 due to persistent disagreements on compliance with consent requirements and international transfers.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted Binding Decision 1/2023 in February 2023, finding Criteo in violation and instructing the CNIL to impose corrective measures. Throughout, the CNIL provided progress reports to the Garante on steps taken, including onsite inspections. The CNIL issued a €40 million fine to Criteo in 2023.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Case - EDPB Binding Decision 3/2023
example_background: In 2020, the Irish DPC opened an own-volition investigation into TikTok Technology Limited's processing of children's personal data in the EU. Nine other supervisory authorities were concerned authorities due to cross-border processing. Concurrently, the Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated TikTok Ireland following complaints and requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC under Article 61 regarding age verification and default privacy settings for minors. The DPC provided some information but the Dutch AP deemed responses insufficient, leading to a dispute. Extensive correspondence occurred with progress updates shared among authorities.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued Binding Decision 3/2023 in September 2023, requiring the DPC to supplement its draft decision with specific findings on children's data violations. The DPC had informed the Dutch AP of investigation progress and results from prior inquiries. The DPC imposed fines totaling €345 million on TikTok in September 2023.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, lead SA)
Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP, requesting SA)

example_name: Amazon France Logfiles Investigation - EDPB Decision 5/2021
example_background: The French CNIL investigated Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. for processing employee terminal session recordings (logfiles) without proper legal basis. As Amazon's lead SA is the Luxembourg CNPD, the CNIL requested mutual assistance under Article 61 to carry out data protection impact assessments and audits. The CNPD responded but refused parts of the request, prompting the CNIL to seek further reasons and updates. This led to a cooperation procedure under Article 63 and eventual dispute resolution.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB's Binding Decision 5/2021 ordered the CNPD to take corrective action, including fines. The CNPD had provided progress updates and reasons for partial refusal based on scope. CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in 2021.

example_name: Meta Biometric Data Transfers - Norwegian DSA Request to Irish DPC
example_background: The Norwegian Data Protection Authority (Datatilsynet) requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC in 2023 regarding Meta's processing of biometric data for facial recognition in photo tagging, as part of a cross-border case with Ireland as lead. The DPC was asked to investigate transfers and compliance, with ongoing updates requested on progress amid Schrems II implications.
process_and_outcome: The DPC informed the Norwegian DSA of investigation status and integrated findings into its broader Meta inquiries. This contributed to EDPB disputes, with progress shared transparently.

example_name: EDPB Decision on Facebook Ireland - NYOB Complaints
example_background: A coalition led by None of Your Business (NOYB) filed complaints in 13 EU countries against Facebook Ireland for behavioral advertising based on separate consent for tracking. Each SA requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC as lead authority, seeking information on consent mechanisms and updates on the ongoing investigation.
process_and_outcome: The DPC provided periodic progress reports to requesting SAs, culminating in draft decisions challenged via Article 65. EDPB Binding Decision 13/2023 in December 2023 ordered fines up to 4% turnover.

example_name: German SAs Request to French CNIL on Google Analytics
example_background: Hamburg DPA investigated Google Ireland for data transfers via Google Analytics and requested assistance from French CNIL (Google France) and others. Multiple SAs coordinated requests for transfer details, with progress on adequacy decisions shared.
process_and_outcome: CNIL provided updates and reasons; bans issued in Austria, etc. Demonstrated information sharing under Article 62.

example_name: Dutch AP Assistance Request to UK ICO on Meta Transfers
example_background: Post-Brexit, the Dutch AP requested mutual assistance from UK ICO regarding Meta's UK-EU data flows, analogous to intra-EU cooperation, seeking progress on transfer compliance.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO shared investigation results, aligning with adequacy decision framework.

example_name: Meta GDPR Transfer Dispute
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland processed EU users' data and transferred it to the US under Privacy Shield, which was invalidated by Schrems II. Following complaints by NOYB, the Irish DPC investigated as lead authority. Nine other EU SAs (Austria, Belgium, etc.) objected to the draft decision allowing continued transfers, citing inadequate safeguards. Requests for detailed transfer impact assessments were sent to Irish DPC, but responses took over a year, exceeding the one-month cooperation deadline.
process_and_outcome: Contributing SAs escalated to EDPB under Article 65. In 2023, EDPB issued a binding decision requiring Meta to suspend transfers or implement equivalent protections, resulting in a €1.2 billion fine by Irish DPC. Demonstrated use of urgency presumption due to cooperation delays.

example_name: Google AdTech Consent Dispute
example_background: Google's advertising technology processed EU users' data across borders without valid consent under TCF framework. Belgian DPA initiated investigation and sought assistance from Irish DPC (Google's lead SA) for user data access. Irish DPC did not provide requested information within one month, prompting Belgium to consider territorial action.
process_and_outcome: Belgian DPA fined Google €15 million in 2022 for consent issues. Dispute escalated to EDPB, which issued binding decision reinforcing consent requirements. Highlighted cooperation delays leading to independent enforcement.

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing
example_background: Meta's WhatsApp shared user metadata with Facebook for advertising, affecting EU users. Irish DPC investigated as lead, but Dutch DPA and others complained of delays in providing investigation details despite Article 61 requests exceeding one month.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined €225 million in 2021, later increased by EDPB binding decision after dispute. Contributing SAs' push invoked urgency mechanisms due to prolonged cooperation.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Facebook Ireland
example_background: NOYB complaints against Facebook behavioral advertising led to Irish DPC investigation. Multiple SAs requested status updates and evidence; delays prompted urgency presumption invocation.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision 2023 ordered €390 million fine for consent violations, following cooperation deadlock.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Action on Meta Platforms
example_background: Spanish AEPD investigated Meta's data processing for ads, requesting info from Irish DPC which delayed response beyond one month amid Schrems II issues.
process_and_outcome: AEPD imposed €30 million provisional fine in 2022, citing Article 55 competence and presumed urgency.

example_name: Dutch DPA Provisional Measures on Facebook
example_background: Prior to GDPR, but analogous, Dutch DPA took independent action on Facebook data sharing due to lack of timely cooperation from Irish predecessor authority.
process_and_outcome: €525,000 fine upheld, setting precedent for GDPR Article 55 provisional measures.

example_name: Irish DPC Assistance to Italian Garante in WhatsApp Case
example_background: The Italian data protection authority, Garante per la protezione dei dati personali, initiated an investigation into WhatsApp's data processing practices, particularly regarding data sharing with Meta platforms. As part of the cross-border GDPR enforcement, the Italian authority requested assistance from other EU supervisory authorities, including the Irish Data Protection Commission, where WhatsApp's lead supervisory authority is located. The request involved sharing detailed information on user data flows, consent mechanisms, and compliance assessments conducted by WhatsApp Ireland Limited. This cooperation occurred through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) framework, utilizing standardized electronic formats for transmitting case files, evidence, and preliminary findings to facilitate a coordinated investigation across multiple member states.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC provided the requested information electronically in a standardized format, enabling the Garante to incorporate it into their decision-making process. The EDPB coordinated the one-stop-shop mechanism, leading to a unified enforcement approach. WhatsApp was fined €225 million by the Italian Garante in September 2023 for transparency violations.
involved_parties: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (requesting authority)

example_name: CNIL Cooperation with Spanish AEPD on Google Analytics
example_background: The Spanish Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) launched an investigation into the use of Google Analytics by websites, focusing on data transfers to the US and compliance with GDPR adequacy decisions post-Schrems II. The AEPD requested mutual assistance from the French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL), which had prior experience with similar cases involving US-based analytics tools. The request included technical specifications on data processing agreements, standard contractual clauses implementation, and supplementary measures adopted by controllers using Google services. Information was exchanged electronically via the Internal Market Information System (IMI) using predefined GDPR templates.
process_and_outcome: CNIL supplied comprehensive data and analysis reports in the standardized format within the required timeframe. This assisted the AEPD in issuing fines against entities such as Vodafone Spain (€8 million) and others for unlawful data transfers. The cooperation was highlighted as a model in EDPB reports.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (requesting)
Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (assisting)

example_name: Hamburg DPA Assistance to Dutch AP in Cookie Consent Case
example_background: The Dutch Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP) examined cookie consent practices on various websites, identifying issues with granular consent for non-essential trackers. They requested assistance from the Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information, which had conducted similar audits on German websites using the same tracking technologies. The exchange involved detailed audit logs, anonymized consent rate statistics, and legal interpretations of 'freely given consent' under GDPR, all transmitted through secure electronic channels in a uniform XML-based format developed by the EDPB.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA delivered the requested datasets promptly, contributing to the AP's enforcement decision fining a major Dutch media company €150,000. The case underscored effective inter-authority collaboration documented in annual cooperation reports.

example_name: EDPB Dispute Resolution in Meta Platforms Case
example_background: National supervisory authorities across Europe, including those in Germany, Ireland, and France, identified discrepancies in handling Meta Platforms Ireland's behavioral advertising practices. When the lead Irish DPC hesitated on certain enforcement aspects, concerned authorities invoked the GDPR dispute mechanism, requesting standardized information on draft decisions, evidence compilations, and impact assessments. This involved extensive electronic data exchanges via the EDPB's secure platform to resolve inconsistencies in applying GDPR rules uniformly.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision in December 2022, mandating the Irish DPC to fine Meta €390 million for unlawful processing. The process relied on electronically shared standardized files, demonstrating the mutual assistance system's efficacy.

example_name: Polish UODO Request to Lithuanian DPA on TikTok
example_background: The Polish Urząd Ochrony Danych Osobowych (UODO) investigated TikTok's child data processing practices following parental complaints about age verification and profiling. They sought assistance from the Lithuanian State Data Protection Inspectorate, TikTok's EU lead authority, requesting internal policies, algorithmic audit reports, and user data metrics in a standardized electronic format to assess cross-border compliance.
process_and_outcome: Lithuania provided the information via electronic means, leading to coordinated fines totaling over €345 million against TikTok by multiple authorities, including Ireland (€310 million) and the Netherlands (€17 million).

example_name: Annual EDPB Mutual Assistance Statistics 2022
example_background: The European Data Protection Board publishes annual statistics on inter-authority cooperation under GDPR, detailing thousands of mutual assistance requests processed electronically. In 2022, over 4,000 requests were handled using standardized formats via the IMI system, covering topics from data breach notifications to joint controller assessments across sectors like health, finance, and e-commerce. This systemic practice ensures consistent application of data protection rules EU-wide.
process_and_outcome: 98% of requests were fulfilled electronically within deadlines, facilitating over 1,500 enforcement actions. The report highlights the standardized format's role in efficiency.

example_name: Dutch AP Assistance to Belgian GBA on Health App Data
example_background: The Belgian Gegevensbeschermingsautoriteit (GBA) probed a health tracking app's cross-border data processing for EU users, requesting the Dutch AP for insights from prior investigations into similar mobile health applications. The exchange included anonymized datasets on data minimization practices, encryption standards, and DPIA excerpts, shared via GDPR-compliant electronic templates.
process_and_outcome: Prompt provision of information supported the GBA's administrative fine of €50,000 against the app developer, with lessons incorporated into joint guidance.

example_name: Finnish DPA Cooperation with Swedish IMY on HR Software
example_background: The Swedish Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten (IMY) examined an HR software provider's data processing for Nordic clients, seeking assistance from the Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman on equivalent Finnish implementations. Requests covered processor agreements, data retention logs, and breach incident reports, exchanged electronically in standardized formats through bilateral channels.
process_and_outcome: Finland's response enabled IMY to issue a €1.2 million fine in 2023 for insufficient security measures, promoting harmonized enforcement.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines 2/2020 Implementation Example - Spanish AEPD and Portuguese CNPD
example_background: Following EDPB Guidelines on mutual assistance, the Portuguese Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados (CNPD) requested the Spanish AEPD for information on a cross-border e-commerce platform's order processing data flows. The case involved sharing standardized electronic dossiers with transaction samples, privacy policy analyses, and vendor assessment forms.
process_and_outcome: AEPD complied with the electronic standardized format, aiding CNPD's €200,000 sanction and contributing to EDPB case studies on effective cooperation.
involved_parties: Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados Portugal (requesting)
Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (assisting)

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Meta Platforms Ireland (C-252/21)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the European entity responsible for Facebook services in the EU, engaged a U.S.-based cloud provider for data processing. The data included personal information of EU users such as names, email addresses, IP addresses, and behavioral data collected through tracking technologies across websites. Following complaints from privacy advocate Max Schrems via NOYB, the Austrian DPA forwarded a request to the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) seeking assistance in an investigation concerning data transfers to the United States after the invalidation of the Privacy Shield framework by the CJEU in Schrems II. The request specifically addressed compliance with GDPR Chapter V requirements for international data transfers, including the use of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and supplementary measures. The Austrian DPA sought cooperation under Article 61 GDPR to examine whether transfers complied with EU law, given concerns over U.S. surveillance laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and EO 12.333 that provided insufficient protections compared to EU standards. The case highlighted complexities in cross-border data flows between EU member states and third countries, involving millions of EU users whose data was routinely transferred for advertising and analytics purposes.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC initially planned to close the investigation but, following input from other concerned supervisory authorities including the Austrian DPA, referred the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under Article 65 GDPR for a binding decision due to a disagreement on whether to pursue the investigation. The EDPB issued a binding decision on April 6, 2023, requiring the Irish DPC to proceed with enforcement, finding that Meta's transfers violated GDPR requirements. Subsequently, the Irish DPC imposed a fine of €1.2 billion on Meta Platforms Ireland on December 14, 2023, for unlawful data transfers, along with orders to bring processing operations into compliance and cease future transfers without an adequate legal basis. Meta announced plans to appeal the decision.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Austrian Data Protection Authority (requesting supervisory authority)
European Data Protection Board (dispute resolution body)

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision 2/2023 on Germany v Spain (TikTok Transfers)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform, processed personal data of millions of EU users including minors, involving behavioral data, location data, and device information. Following a complaint in Spain regarding data transfers to China, the Spanish AEPD initiated an investigation and requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC as the lead supervisory authority under GDPR Article 56, seeking examination of TikTok's transfer mechanisms such as SCCs and reliance on adequacy decisions. The Irish DPC proposed closing the case without enforcement, prompting objections from the Spanish AEPD. The matter escalated to the EDPB, revealing tensions in cross-border cooperation where the lead authority's competence and willingness to act were questioned, particularly concerning transfers to China under Chinese national intelligence laws that required data localization and access by authorities.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued Binding Decision 2/2023 on May 2, 2023, siding with the Spanish AEPD and directing the Irish DPC to resume the investigation into TikTok's data transfers, impose corrective measures if necessary, and report back within specified timelines. The decision emphasized the obligation of the requested authority to comply unless lacking competence or facing legal infringement. The Irish DPC subsequently advanced enforcement actions against TikTok.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD, requesting authority)

example_name: French CNIL v Irish DPC on Google Analytics Transfers (2022)
example_background: Google Ireland Ltd used Google Analytics and Google Tag Manager on websites of French entities, transferring IP addresses and user IDs of EU users to the U.S. for analytics purposes. The French CNIL conducted an investigation into third-country transfers post-Schrems II and requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC under Article 61 GDPR to assess compliance with transfer requirements. The Irish DPC did not adequately respond or take action, leading to the CNIL proceeding independently with enforcement against French websites using the tools. This case involved pseudonymized data processing for performance measurement and highlighted challenges in cooperation where the lead authority deferred action.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL issued formal notices to Google Ireland on June 21, 2022, and later fined Google entities €150 million in other proceedings. The lack of cooperation from the Irish DPC was raised in EDPB discussions, contributing to broader scrutiny. The CNIL independently ordered cessation of transfers without adequate safeguards.

example_name: Hamburg DPA Assistance to Lower Saxony on H&M Data Processing (2020)
example_background: H&M operated a global data processing operation where employee data from a German store in Nuthetal was transferred to India for analysis. Around 400 current and former employees' personal data, including sensitive information on health, family situations, and personal crises gathered through 'welcome-back talks,' was processed by over 50 Indian employees. The Commissioner for Data Protection of Lower Saxony received a complaint and, lacking competence over H&M's lead establishment in Hamburg, requested mutual assistance from the Hamburg DPA under Article 61 GDPR to investigate the processing and transfers.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA accepted the request, conducted the investigation, imposed a fine of €35.25 million on October 1, 2020, for violations including inadequate legal basis for processing sensitive data and insufficient transfer safeguards, and ordered compliance measures. The cooperation proceeded without refusal.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection (lead supervisory authority)
Commissioner for Data Protection of Lower Saxony (requesting authority)

example_name: Dutch DPA Request to Irish DPC on WhatsApp (2021)
example_background: WhatsApp, operated by WhatsApp Ireland Ltd, shared user personal data such as phone numbers, device information, and usage data with Meta companies for advertising purposes. The Dutch DPA received complaints from users and requested mutual assistance from the Irish DPC under Article 61 GDPR to investigate the legal basis for metadata sharing post-2021 privacy policy updates, involving millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC as lead authority took primary responsibility, conducting its own investigation and issuing a €225 million fine to WhatsApp on September 2, 2021, for transparency violations, with cooperation acknowledged between the authorities. No refusal occurred.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision 1/2022 on Max Schrems v Facebook Transfers
example_background: Following Schrems II, NOYB filed complaints in multiple EU countries regarding Facebook's data transfers to the U.S. The Belgian DPA requested assistance from the Irish DPC to investigate public communication data transfers of EU users for analytics and ads.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC sought to close the case, but the EDPB's Binding Decision 1/2022 on February 16, 2022, overruled, mandating continuation of enforcement under Article 61 obligations.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Request to Irish DPC on Meta Transfers (2021)
example_background: Post-Schrems II, complaints targeted Meta's U.S. transfers of Spanish users' data via SCCs. AEPD requested Irish DPC assistance for investigation.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC's closure proposal led to EDPB involvement; cooperation issues noted but enforcement proceeded in lead authority.

example_name: Italian Garante Assistance from Irish DPC on Instagram (2022)
example_background: Instagram processed children's data and transferred to U.S.; Italian Garante requested Irish DPC help under Article 61.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC investigated, issued temporary restrictions and €405 million fine in 2022 to parent companies for child data handling, with cooperation.

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Cooperation with Irish DPC on Amazon Transfers (2020)
example_background: Amazon transferred EU customer data to U.S. servers; Luxembourg CNPD requested mutual assistance from Irish DPC on transfer safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC handled as lead, ongoing investigation with cooperation; no refusal reported.

example_name: Amazon EDPB Amazon France Logistics Fine
example_background: In 2024, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) addressed a case involving Amazon's data processing practices for employees at its French logistics sites. The French SA initially fined Amazon for issues in data processing for performance monitoring, algorithmic management, and lack of transparency in HR data handling.
process_and_outcome: Following a dispute, the EDPB adopted a binding decision in June 2024, leading to a €32 million fine upheld against Amazon. The case involved coordinated input from multiple EU supervisory authorities on cross-border processing.

example_name: Meta GDPR Schrems II Transfers Fine
example_background: In 2023, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's use of the EU-US Privacy Shield for data transfers to the US following the Schrems II court ruling that invalidated it. The authority found Meta continued transfers without adequate safeguards post-ruling, exposing EU user data to US surveillance laws.
process_and_outcome: The DPC proposed fines totaling over €1 billion, with the EDPB issuing a binding decision. In December 2023, Meta was fined €1.2 billion for unlawful transfers and ordered to suspend future transfers, prompting adoption of new Standard Contractual Clauses.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: OneTrust GDPR Compliance Platform Adoption
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, assisted numerous organizations in implementing GDPR-compliant data mapping, consent management, and automated reporting tools. Companies using the platform standardized data flows and reporting formats for internal audits and regulatory interactions, facilitating smoother cooperation with authorities.
process_and_outcome: Adoption led to successful GDPR audits and avoidance of penalties for clients like Unilever and Equifax. The platform supported DSAR fulfillment and breach notifications within required timelines, earning recognition in industry reports for enabling compliance.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Joint Investigation (2024)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited processed personal data of minors across the EU via its short-video platform, with features allowing under-16 users to share content publicly and engage with algorithmic recommendations. Concerns arose in multiple Member States including Ireland, Netherlands, Denmark, and Norway about inadequate age verification, default public settings for children's accounts, and processing of special category data without appropriate safeguards. The operations affected a significant number of child data subjects in several countries, necessitating joint investigative efforts among supervisory authorities to address systemic issues in child data protection.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC, as lead authority, led a coordinated cross-border investigation involving eight EU supervisory authorities. In September 2024, TikTok was fined €345 million collectively for failures in protecting children's data. The authorities issued a joint statement emphasizing the coordinated approach, with corrective measures imposed including enhanced age assurance tools and privacy settings changes. TikTok implemented updates to comply.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Age verification for information society services)

example_name: Amazon EU Data Transfers Joint Case (2021)
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. engaged in personal data transfers from the EU to the US for cloud services and advertising, affecting users across multiple Member States. Following the Schrems II judgment invalidating the Privacy Shield, authorities in Luxembourg, France, and others scrutinized the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) used by Amazon, identifying insufficient supplementary measures against US surveillance risks. The cross-border nature involved establishments in several states and impacted data subjects EU-wide, prompting joint operations.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD, as lead authority, collaborated with French CNIL and others in a joint investigation. In 2021, Amazon received a €746 million fine from Luxembourg CNPD for GDPR violations including unlawful data processing for ads and non-compliant transfers. The decision followed input from concerned authorities, with Amazon appealing but implementing additional safeguards.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: WhatsApp Multinational Fining (2021)
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, handling data for over 2 billion users including EU residents, shared user data with Meta group companies for advertising without transparent legal bases. This affected data subjects in multiple Member States, leading supervisory authorities in Ireland, Germany, Italy, and Spain to question compliance with transparency obligations under GDPR. The pan-EU scope required joint operations to harmonize enforcement.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC led the cross-border procedure with input from nine other authorities via the EDPB. In September 2021, WhatsApp was fined €225 million by the DPC, one of the largest GDPR penalties at the time, for opaque privacy policies. Concurrently, Italy's Garante imposed an additional €10 million fine. WhatsApp updated its policies.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Enforcement on Cookie Banners (2023)
example_background: Multiple websites operated by various controllers used cookie banners that did not properly distinguish between necessary and non-necessary cookies, pre-ticking consent boxes for analytics trackers, affecting users across EU Member States. Supervisory authorities noted widespread similar practices requiring joint guidance and enforcement to ensure uniform application.
