process_and_outcome: In October 2023, the EDPB published coordinated enforcement guidance following joint operations and workshops among 23 EU DPAs. Several authorities launched parallel investigations, resulting in fines like €2 million against RTB House by the French CNIL and others. Authorities committed to ongoing joint monitoring.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Joint Probe (2022)
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites worldwide, building a database accessed by law enforcement, impacting EU data subjects across Member States without consent or legal basis. Complaints in France, Greece, Italy, and others led to recognition of cross-border effects.
process_and_outcome: Multiple DPAs, including French CNIL and Italian Garante, conducted joint investigations. CNIL fined Clearview €20 million in 2022, upheld by courts, while Italy fined €20 million separately. The EDPB facilitated coordination, with ongoing actions in other states. Clearview suspended EU operations.

example_name: EDPB One-Stop-Shop Joint Investigation Framework (Meta 2022)
example_background: Meta's processing of behavioral data across Facebook and Instagram services involved establishments in multiple Member States and affected EU users broadly. The EDPB tested the one-stop-shop mechanism with joint operations to handle complex cross-border cases efficiently.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the EDPB adopted a binding decision in the 'Facebook Ireland' case after joint input from 11 concerned authorities, leading to a €405 million fine by the Irish DPC for child data violations. This demonstrated successful coordination under joint operations provisions.

example_name: Google Android GDPR Joint Enforcement (2019)
example_background: Google LLC and Google Ireland processed Android user data for personalized ads without valid consent, pre-ticking boxes and not allowing granular choices, affecting users EU-wide through multiple national establishments.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL led with input from other DPAs in a joint procedure, fining Google €50 million in 2019, upheld by courts. This spurred coordinated actions across EU, with Google revising consent mechanisms.

example_name: EDPB COVID-19 Contact Tracing Joint Operations (2020)
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic, EU Member States developed contact tracing apps processing location and proximity data across borders for travelers. Supervisory authorities coordinated to ensure interoperability and GDPR compliance amid urgent public health needs.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB facilitated joint operations and issued coordinated guidance in April 2020, leading to compliant deployments without major enforcement actions. Authorities from 27 Member States participated in ongoing harmonization efforts.

example_name: French CNIL Enforcement Against Google (with EDPB Involvement)
example_background: In 2019, the French data protection authority CNIL issued a fine of 50 million euros against Google LLC for violations related to processing personal data for personalized advertising purposes without a valid legal basis. The case gained cross-border dimensions as it affected users in multiple EU member states. Under GDPR mechanisms, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) was involved to ensure consistent application of the regulation. CNIL acted as the lead supervisory authority, with input from other concerned authorities. Staff and experts from various national data protection authorities coordinated through the EDPB's dispute resolution procedure to handle the cross-border processing activities conducted by Google across the EU. This coordination highlighted operational collaboration among supervisory authorities operating beyond their national territories in a structured framework.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's decision was upheld after EDPB binding decision in April 2020, confirming the fine and approach. Google continued operations with adjustments to consent mechanisms.

example_name: Irish DPC Cross-Border Investigation into WhatsApp
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an own-volition inquiry in 2020 into WhatsApp Ireland Limited's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising following a 2018 complaint. As WhatsApp operates services across the EU with Ireland as its main establishment, the investigation involved input from 30 other EU/EEA supervisory authorities. The process required coordination among authorities, including exchanges of information and draft decision reviews. This setup involved supervisory authorities from multiple member states providing expertise and opinions, effectively operating in a collaborative framework to address the cross-border nature of the data processing.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, EDPB issued a binding decision instructing DPC to impose a fine of 225 million euros on WhatsApp, finalized in September 2021.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Facebook (Ireland Lead)
example_background: Facebook Ireland Limited faced a cross-border investigation by the Irish DPC starting in 2018 over data processing practices, including sharing data with third parties like device manufacturers. Concerned supervisory authorities from multiple member states, including France, challenged the DPC's initial draft decision, leading to EDPB involvement. This required operational collaboration where staff from various national authorities analyzed compliance across jurisdictions, sharing findings and expertise in joint case handling procedures.
process_and_outcome: EDPB's binding decision in November 2022 imposed an 1.2 billion euro fine on Meta Platforms Ireland for unlawful data transfers to the US, with additional orders for compliance measures.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Case (Irish DPC with EDPB)
example_background: In 2020, the Irish DPC opened an investigation into TikTok Technology Limited regarding processing of children's personal data on its platform, which serves users across the EU. As a cross-border processing case, it involved consultations with other EU supervisory authorities. The coordination process included joint analyses and draft decision circulations, demonstrating how authorities from different member states contribute to handling operations in other territories through the consistency mechanism.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2023 led to a 345 million euro fine against TikTok for violations related to children's data.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Articles 8 and 25 (Children's data and DPIA)

example_name: Amazon EU Sarl Cross-Border Advertising Investigation
example_background: Luxembourg's CNPD initiated a cross-border investigation in 2021 into Amazon's processing of personal data for targeted advertising, affecting users EU-wide. As Luxembourg is Amazon's main EU establishment, the case involved the lead authority coordinating with 12 concerned supervisory authorities from other member states, including exchanges of operational information and joint assessments.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing as of 2023, with EDPB involvement in dispute resolution; preliminary findings noted transparency issues.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Case (Cross-Border Coordination)
example_background: Clearview AI faced complaints and investigations from multiple EU supervisory authorities starting in 2021 for scraping facial images without consent. The Dutch, French, Greek, Italian, and Portuguese authorities issued orders, leading to EDPB coordination. This involved joint operations and enforcement across member states, with authorities assuming shared responsibilities for data processing activities spanning territories.
process_and_outcome: EDPB urged consistent enforcement; individual fines issued, e.g., 30 million euros by Dutch AP in 2022.
involved_parties: Dutch DPA, French CNIL, other national authorities

example_name: EDPB Dispute Resolution on Meta Platforms Advertising
example_background: Following Luxembourg CNPD's 2021 decision fining Meta 28 million euros for personalized ads, concerned authorities from 11 member states objected, triggering EDPB's mutual assistance and dispute resolution. This process involved detailed operational cooperation, with staff from various authorities reviewing evidence and compliance across borders.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2022 upheld and expanded the fine to 390 million euros.

example_name: Microsoft Bing Privacy Cross-Border Case
example_background: Irish DPC investigated Microsoft Ireland Operations Ltd in 2020 over Bing search engine data processing for ads, involving 29 other EU authorities due to EU-wide effects. Coordination included joint controller assessments and information sharing.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2022 resulted in a 20 million euro fine for transparency failures.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cross-Border Fine (Ireland and Luxembourg)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the lead controller for Facebook services in the EU, engaged in processing operations that affected data subjects across multiple Member States. The company's data processing practices, including the use of cookies and tracking technologies for behavioral advertising, were examined due to their widespread impact on users in various EU countries. The processing involved collecting personal data from users throughout the EU and transferring it to servers in the United States, raising questions about the applicability of EU data protection rules to non-EU establishments. This situation triggered involvement from multiple national supervisory authorities as the processing substantially affected users in more than one Member State.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted the main investigation as the lead authority under the one-stop-shop mechanism. Supervisory authorities from Luxembourg, France, and other Member States participated in joint case operations and issued objections to draft decisions. Following an inquiry by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB), the DPC imposed a fine of 1.2 billion euros on Meta in 2023 for violations related to international data transfers. Meta appealed the decision.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Cross-Border Case
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with establishments in Ireland and operations affecting users across the EU, processed personal data of minors through its short-video platform. The processing activities, including age verification and default privacy settings, impacted a significant number of data subjects in multiple Member States. Concerns arose over the platform's handling of children's data without adequate safeguards, leading to coordinated scrutiny from various national authorities due to the pan-European nature of the user base.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC led the investigation under Article 56, inviting participation from supervisory authorities in the Netherlands, Denmark, and others through joint operations. In 2023, the DPC fined TikTok 345 million euros for violations concerning children's data processing. The EDPB coordinated the cross-border aspects via the dispute resolution mechanism.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 61 (mutual assistance and joint operations)

example_name: WhatsApp Cross-Border Processing Investigation
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, responsible for processing data of WhatsApp users across the EU, shared user data with Meta group companies for advertising purposes. This processing affected millions of data subjects in multiple Member States, prompting complaints and investigations into transparency and legal basis for processing. The company's Irish establishment served as the main EU base, but the effects were felt Europe-wide.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC initiated the cross-border investigation and involved authorities from Germany, Italy, Spain, and others in joint operations. In 2021, the DPC fined WhatsApp 225 million euros. The process involved EDPB coordination after objections from concerned authorities.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection

example_name: Google AdSense Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: Google Ireland Limited operated the AdSense platform, processing personal data of publishers and users across multiple EU Member States for targeted advertising. The processing involved tracking and profiling activities that substantially affected data subjects in several countries, leading to coordinated regulatory oversight.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL acted as the lead authority after Google's choice, with participation from supervisory authorities in 29 other Member States through joint operations. In 2022, Google was fined 150 million euros by the CNIL, upheld after EDPB involvement and objections from other authorities.
involved_parties: French Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL, lead)

example_name: Amazon EU Data Processing Joint Case
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., based in Luxembourg, processed personal data for its e-commerce services affecting customers and sellers across the EU. Advertising personalization practices impacted users in multiple Member States, triggering cross-border cooperation among supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg CNPD led the investigation, inviting participation from authorities in France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and others. In 2021, Amazon received a 746 million euro fine from the Luxembourg CNPD, with ongoing EDPB coordination for consistency.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on Meta Schrems II Transfers
example_background: Following the Schrems II court ruling, Meta's data transfers from EU establishments to the US were scrutinized due to their impact on data subjects across multiple Member States. Processing operations involved systematic transfers affecting users Europe-wide.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC led, with supervisory authorities from 11 Member States participating in joint operations and raising objections. In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision under Article 65, leading to a 1.2 billion euro fine by the DPC.
involved_parties: Supervisory authorities from Austria, Belgium, etc.

example_name: Clearview AI Cross-Border Facial Recognition Case
example_background: Clearview AI, Inc., a US-based company with processing affecting EU data subjects through facial recognition technology, scraped images from public websites across Europe. The processing substantially affected data subjects in multiple Member States, leading to joint actions.
process_and_outcome: Multiple supervisory authorities, including from France, Greece, Italy, and others, conducted joint operations. Fines were issued individually, totaling over 30 million euros, with coordination via the EDPB for consistent application.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Meta 'Pay or Consent' Model Investigation
example_background: Meta introduced a 'pay or consent' model for EU users, processing behavioral data for advertising across Member States from its Irish establishment. This affected a significant number of data subjects Europe-wide.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC initiated a cross-border investigation, inviting 26 other EU supervisory authorities to participate in joint operations. The EDPB handled objections, demonstrating active cooperation.

example_name: OneTrust Vendor Portal Cross-Border Cooperation Success
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider with EU establishments, facilitated data processing for clients across Member States. When a potential issue arose with vendor data handling, it involved multiple authorities proactively.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC coordinated with authorities from Germany and the Netherlands in joint operations, resolving the matter without fines through remediation. This demonstrated effective use of cross-border mechanisms.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Joint Action
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, collected personal data from millions of users under 16 in the EU without proper parental consent mechanisms. The platform's age verification and default privacy settings were found inadequate. Complaints originated from multiple member states, leading to a coordinated effort. The Irish DPC acted as the lead authority due to TikTok's EU establishment, with supervisory authorities from the Netherlands, Denmark, Norway, Germany, France, Spain, Italy, and others involved in discussions and evidence gathering.
process_and_outcome: Under the GDPR cooperation mechanism, the EDPB issued a binding decision in 2023, resulting in a €345 million fine imposed by the Irish DPC. TikTok was required to implement enhanced age assurance tools, delete data of underage users processed without consent, and align practices EU-wide. The process involved joint inspections and shared findings.
involved_parties: Data protection authorities of 8+ member states (participants)

example_name: Google Analytics Cross-Border Data Flows
example_background: Google's analytics tools were used by websites across Europe, transferring IP addresses and user IDs to US servers without adequate safeguards. Complaints filed in Austria led to involvement of multiple DPAs. The Italian Garante, Austrian DSB, and others coordinated, with the French CNIL taking enforcement action after consultations. The case highlighted issues in standard contractual clauses post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the French CNIL declared Google Analytics in breach and ordered cessation of transfers, supported by EDPB opinion. Joint efforts resulted in similar declarations across member states, prompting Google to update tools with IP anonymization. No direct fines but widespread compliance actions.

example_name: WhatsApp Metadata Processing Multinational Case
example_background: WhatsApp shared user metadata with Meta companies for advertising purposes without transparent legal basis. Users across EU countries filed complaints. The Irish DPC was lead authority, facing objections from 10+ other DPAs including Netherlands, Norway, Greece, Spain, Belgium, and Germany, who argued for stronger measures.
process_and_outcome: EDPB triggered Article 65 procedure in 2021, binding the Irish DPC to fine WhatsApp €225 million in 2021 (upheld after appeal). Further proceedings in 2023 added transparency obligations. Joint operations involved shared evidence and draft decision reviews.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Cookie Consent Enforcement
example_background: Multiple DPAs jointly addressed cookie consent practices where websites set non-essential cookies before user consent. Authorities from France, Italy, Netherlands, and others inspected major platforms, sharing methodologies and findings.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, EDPB issued coordinated guidance and enforcement. Fines issued, e.g., €20 million to Google by CNIL. Resulted in widespread website updates across EU.
involved_parties: French CNIL, Italian Garante, Dutch AP

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Joint Investigation
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites worldwide, selling access to law enforcement. EU-wide complaints led to a coordinated probe into GDPR compliance for processing biometric data.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, multiple DPAs including France (€20m provisional fine), Italy (€20m), Greece (€20m), and others banned processing and fined collectively over €90 million total. EDPB facilitated joint statements.
involved_parties: French CNIL, Italian Garante, Greek DPA (enforcers)

example_name: Twitter (X) GDPR Multinational Fine Coordination
example_background: Twitter faced issues with data access requests, deletion rights, and ban evasion processing. Irish DPC as lead coordinated with Luxembourg, Netherlands, and others on cross-border aspects affecting EU users.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2023 led to €350 million fine by Irish DPC for violations from 2018-2021. Required improvements in data subject rights handling.

example_name: EDPB Joint Investigation into WhatsApp Data Transfers
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, operating WhatsApp, faced scrutiny over its data processing practices and international data transfers from the European Economic Area to the United States. Multiple national supervisory authorities across EU member states initiated coordinated actions due to concerns regarding the legal basis for data transfers following the invalidation of the EU-US Privacy Shield framework. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) served as the lead authority, while supervisory authorities from France (CNIL), Netherlands (AP), and others submitted structured requests and engaged in dispute resolution under GDPR cooperation mechanisms. This process highlighted the need for cross-border collaboration, involving information exchanges, draft decision reviews, and authorizations for joint investigative efforts to ensure consistent application of GDPR rules. The case involved analysis of standard contractual clauses, supplementary measures, and compliance with Schrems II requirements, spanning several years of regulatory dialogue.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in 2023 binding on the Irish DPC, resulting in a €1.2 billion fine imposed on Meta by the Irish DPC for violations related to data transfers. Meta appealed the decision, and remedial measures were required, including suspension of certain data transfers pending compliance. The joint operation led to enhanced enforcement coordination.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)

example_name: OneTrust Vendor Portal GDPR Cross-Border Audit
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, collaborated with multiple EU supervisory authorities in a joint audit of its vendor management platform to verify compliance with GDPR data protection requirements across member states. The audit involved supervisory authorities from Germany, Spain, and Italy, who seconding staff under authorization from host authorities to examine data processing activities, data flows, and security measures implemented by OneTrust for its clients. This operation was conducted to assess adherence to cross-border processing rules, including data minimization, lawful basis for processing, and rights fulfillment. Staff from the seconding authorities participated under the guidance of the host authority's personnel, applying host member state procedures while sharing expertise from their jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The joint audit concluded with confirmation of compliance, issuance of non-binding recommendations for process enhancements, and publication of a case study by the EDPB showcasing effective cooperation. No fines were imposed, and OneTrust received recognition for facilitating the operation.
involved_parties: German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection (host authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 62 (joint operations of supervisory authorities)

example_name: Meta Biometric Data Scraping Investigation (2023)
example_background: Clearview AI collected biometric facial recognition data from publicly available sources across the internet, including EU social media platforms like those operated by Meta, affecting millions of EU data subjects. Following complaints from privacy advocates such as NOYB, supervisory authorities in Austria, France, Greece, Italy, and others launched coordinated investigations into Clearview AI's practices. The French SA (CNIL) took a lead role, authorizing seconding staff from Italian and Greek authorities to assist in data access and interviews under French law. This joint operation involved on-site inspections and forensic analysis of databases, conducted with host authority oversight to ensure procedural compliance.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €20 million fine on Clearview AI in 2022, upheld after appeals. Other authorities issued bans and fines totaling over €30 million. The company was ordered to cease processing EU data, demonstrating effective use of cross-border powers.

example_name: EDPB Coordinated Enforcement on TikTok Children's Data
example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, processed personal data of minors across EU member states, prompting a coordinated enforcement action by 17 supervisory authorities led by the Irish DPC. Authorities from Netherlands, Denmark, Germany, and others participated in joint operations, where seconding staff exercised powers to conduct audits and request documentation on age verification, default privacy settings, and profiling practices. The operations followed host authority procedures, with seconding members present during key interviews and data extractions to address concerns over child data protection.
process_and_outcome: In 2023 and 2024, the Irish DPC imposed fines totaling €530 million on TikTok for violations, with additional child-specific penalties. Remediation orders were issued, and the platform adjusted settings EU-wide following the joint findings.

example_name: Cross-Border Investigation into Planet49 Consent Banners
example_background: Webtech (Planet49) provided cookie consent management tools used by websites across the EU, which were found to pre-check consent boxes for tracking cookies without clear withdrawal options. Following a complaint by NOYB, the Hessian Commissioner for Data Protection in Germany coordinated with authorities in Austria, Belgium, and others. Seconding staff from Austrian DPA assisted in the German-led inspection, exercising powers under German law with host supervision to review code implementations and user data flows on multiple websites.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision in 2020, leading to a €2.4 million fine by the Hessian authority and orders for remedy. The case set precedents for cookie consent practices EU-wide.
involved_parties: Hessian Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information (lead)

example_name: Schrems II Follow-Up Joint Operations on Data Transfers
example_background: Post-Schrems II CJEU ruling in 2020, supervisory authorities from Luxembourg, Ireland, and Portugal conducted joint operations on companies like Oracle and Amazon Web Services regarding data transfers to the US. Luxembourg's CNPD served as host, authorizing seconding staff from Irish DPC to participate in audits of cloud service data flows, encryption measures, and contractual safeguards. The operations involved joint access to servers and review of transfer impact assessments under host law.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, CNPD fined Oracle Luxembourg €200,000 for insufficient transfer safeguards. Similar findings led to remediation requirements for AWS, with ongoing coordinated monitoring.
involved_parties: Commission nationale pour la protection des données (CNPD, Luxembourg host)

example_name: Italian Core Doc Joint Investigation on Google Analytics
example_background: Google's Analytics and Ads services were investigated by 11 EU supervisory authorities following complaints about data transfers to the US without adequate safeguards. The Italian Garante initiated proceedings, with seconding staff from Spanish AEPD and others authorized to assist in reviewing IP anonymization, consent mechanisms, and server logs under Italian oversight. This operation addressed configurations used by thousands of websites.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Garante banned further use and issued orders, influencing coordinated pauses by other authorities. Google updated tools in response, with ongoing EDPB discussions.
involved_parties: Spanish Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 62 (investigative powers in joint operations)

example_name: EDPS-SSA Joint Case on EU Institutions Data Processing
example_background: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) collaborated with national supervisory authorities in a joint operation concerning data processing by EU institutions involving member state data. Seconding staff from Spanish AEPD assisted EDPS in reviewing processing logs and access controls, operating under EDPS guidance and applicable EU law.
process_and_outcome: The operation resulted in recommendations for improved data governance, adopted in 2022, with no penalties but enhanced compliance frameworks.

example_name: Booking.com Data Breach Cross-Border Investigation
example_background: Booking.com experienced a data breach affecting EU users, prompting the Dutch Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (AP) to lead a joint investigation with Germany's BfDI. Seconding German staff participated in Dutch-led on-site inspections of incident response records and security configurations, under Dutch law and AP supervision.
process_and_outcome: Findings led to a €475,000 fine in 2020 by AP, with shared learnings published for sector-wide improvement.

example_name: Schrems II Case and Data Transfers
example_background: Max Schrems, an Austrian privacy activist, filed complaints against Facebook Ireland for transferring personal data of European users to the United States, alleging that the Safe Harbor framework did not provide adequate protection against government surveillance. The case highlighted discrepancies in data protection standards between the EU and US. Following the invalidation of Safe Harbor in 2015, Facebook continued using successor mechanisms like Privacy Shield, but Schrems challenged these as well. The litigation revealed ongoing issues with cross-border data flows and the need for uniform application of data protection rules across EU member states. The Court of Justice of the EU examined whether national supervisory authorities could suspend such transfers independently, emphasizing the tension between national enforcement and EU-wide consistency.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU invalidated Privacy Shield in July 2020, ruling that it did not ensure an adequate level of protection. The Irish Data Protection Commission was directed to suspend data transfers, leading to widespread adjustments by companies relying on US cloud services. Facebook implemented Standard Contractual Clauses with additional safeguards.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: OneTrust VendorPortal EDPB Endorsement
example_background: OneTrust, a privacy management software provider, developed VendorPortal, a platform designed to assist organizations in managing data processing agreements and ensuring compliance with GDPR requirements for data processors. The tool was submitted to the European Data Protection Board for assessment under the consistency mechanism to confirm its alignment with uniform EU standards. This initiative addressed the need for standardized tools to handle controller-processor relationships across different member states, promoting consistent application of GDPR obligations throughout the Union.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB reviewed and endorsed the tool in 2021, recognizing it as a valid means for concluding data processing agreements compliant with GDPR Article 28. This approval facilitated its adoption by numerous organizations seeking to standardize their compliance processes.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Dispute
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission initiated an investigation into TikTok's processing of children's personal data, focusing on age verification and default privacy settings. Parallel investigations occurred in other EU countries, including the Netherlands and France, raising concerns about divergent approaches to enforcement. TikTok, headquartered in Ireland for EU operations, faced scrutiny over whether national authorities could act unilaterally or if a coordinated consistency mechanism was required to ensure uniform application of children's data protection rules across the EU.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million for violations related to children's data. The decision followed coordination with other authorities under the consistency mechanism, with the EDPB resolving disputes on the fine amount.

example_name: Amazon Ads Processing Investigation
example_background: Amazon Europe Core was investigated by the Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD) for its use of advertisers' data to train advertising algorithms. Other EU authorities, including France's CNIL, expressed concerns that the processing lacked a proper legal basis and raised issues of consistent application across borders, particularly regarding automated decision-making and targeting.
process_and_outcome: In July 2024, the EDPB adopted a binding decision mandating a ban on such processing and requiring Amazon to bring practices into compliance, following a dispute under the consistency mechanism.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Video Devices
example_background: National authorities submitted draft decisions on GDPR compliance for video surveillance devices to the EDPB for an opinion, addressing issues like data retention periods and public access to footage. This process ensured that interpretations of processing principles were aligned across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: In May 2020, the EDPB issued Opinion 12/2020, providing uniform guidance adopted by authorities, promoting consistent application without divergent national rulings.

example_name: Meta GDPR-WRITO Dispute
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated Meta's use of the GDPR-WRITO legal basis for behavioral advertising, relying on 'contract necessity.' Other concerned authorities from 11 member states objected, arguing it did not meet uniform standards for legitimate interests in processing behavioral data.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the EDPB issued a binding decision rejecting GDPR-WRITO, requiring Meta to cease processing based on that ground EU-wide, enforcing consistency.

example_name: Planet49 Opinion on Consent
example_background: The German supervisory authority addressed pre-ticked consent boxes for cookies on the Planet49 website, prompting a consistency opinion from the EDPB on whether such mechanisms constituted valid freely given consent under GDPR, amid varying national practices.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the EDPB issued Opinion 5/2020, clarifying that pre-ticked boxes do not meet consent requirements, standardizing enforcement across the EU.

example_name: OneTrust Schrems II Data Transfers Case
example_background: OneTrust, a US-based data privacy software provider, processed personal data of EU residents through its platform that facilitated international data transfers. Following the CJEU's Schrems II judgment in July 2020, which invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield, OneTrust continued to enable data transfers to the US using standard contractual clauses without conducting transfer impact assessments or implementing supplementary measures to address risks from US surveillance laws. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) identified this during a cross-border processing investigation initiated in 2021, focusing on the platform's role in joint operations across multiple EU member states where data controllers and processors relied on OneTrust's tools for compliance with GDPR Chapter V transfer rules. The case highlighted tensions between the lead authority (Ireland) and other concerned supervisory authorities from countries like France and Germany, who objected to the adequacy of OneTrust's transfer mechanisms amid ongoing joint controller and processor arrangements.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC conducted a formal inquiry and issued a decision in December 2022, ordering OneTrust to suspend data transfers to the US until compliance was achieved. Other supervisory authorities challenged this under GDPR Article 65, leading to referral to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). The EDPB issued a binding decision in April 2023, requiring supplementary measures and impact assessments. OneTrust implemented changes, including encryption and pseudonymization, and resumed operations without further penalties.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
French CNIL and German supervisory authorities (concerned authorities)

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp Cross-Border Transfers
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead processor for WhatsApp services in the EU, managed personal data from users across 27 member states, involving joint processing operations with group entities in the US for storage and analytics. Post-Schrems II, WhatsApp's reliance on standard contractual clauses for transfers to the US was scrutinized due to lack of supplementary safeguards against access by US intelligence agencies. The Irish DPC launched an investigation in 2020 into these transfers as part of broader GDPR compliance checks on Meta's ecosystem, which included data flows integral to WhatsApp's cloud-based services. Other EU supervisory authorities, including those from Germany, Italy, and Spain, raised concerns about the consistency of the lead authority's approach, noting potential risks to data subjects' rights in multinational joint operations.
process_and_outcome: After own-volition inquiry, the Irish DPC proposed no breach in 2022, but 11 other supervisory authorities objected under Article 64, triggering EDPB involvement. In April 2023, the EDPB issued Binding Decision 2/2023, finding violations and fining Meta €1.2 billion. Meta was ordered to cease transfers and implement measures; the company suspended US transfers and appealed aspects of the decision.

example_name: TikTok Children’s Data Transfers Investigation
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with EU operations led from Ireland, processed personal data of minors across Europe, transferring data to China and Singapore for algorithmic processing and content moderation in joint operations with global affiliates. Following complaints about child data handling, the Irish DPC initiated a cross-border investigation in 2020, examining transfer mechanisms under Chapter V. Other authorities, particularly the Dutch and French, expressed concerns over the one-month consultation period, arguing for provisional measures due to risks to children's data in a high-volume joint processing setup.
process_and_outcome: Objections from five authorities led to EDPB Binding Decision 1/2023 in February 2023, harmonizing findings on transfers. TikTok faced separate fines (e.g., €345 million from Ireland in 2023 for child data) and was directed to audit and supplement transfer tools. No direct provisional measures were imposed, but the process presumed urgency under Article 66.

example_name: Amazon EU Data Transfers Dispute
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) Ireland, as part of Amazon's EU data processing operations, handled customer data from multiple member states, transferring it to the US for core cloud services in joint controller-processor arrangements. A 2021 Irish DPC investigation into AWS's transfer practices post-Schrems II revealed reliance on SCCs without adequate supplementary measures. Authorities in Luxembourg, Germany, and France objected to the Irish DPC's draft decision within the one-month period, citing the need for consistent application across joint operations serving multinational clients.
process_and_outcome: Referred to EDPB under Article 65, resulting in Binding Decision 5/2023 in July 2023. AWS was found non-compliant and ordered to suspend transfers; Amazon implemented encryption and other safeguards. Luxembourg later fined Amazon separately €746 million in 2021 for other GDPR issues.

example_name: French CNIL Google Analytics Ban
example_background: The French CNIL examined similar Google Analytics and Tag Manager implementations by French websites, involving joint EU-US data flows for advertising and analytics processing.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, after one-month consultations yielded no agreement, CNIL ordered websites to halt transfers using formal notice, invoking provisional powers and Article 66 presumption. This prompted Google's EU tool updates.

example_name: Meta Facebook Transfers (French Provisional Action)
example_background: Amid ongoing Irish DPC investigations into Meta's EU-US transfers for Facebook, the CNIL considered complaints about advertising data flows in joint operations across France and other states.
process_and_outcome: In August 2022, CNIL issued formal notices to stop transfers after stalled consultations, applying provisional measures under Article 55 with Article 66 urgency presumption, contributing to EDPB-wide consistency efforts.

example_name: H&M Data Transfers Dispute
example_background: H&M's German subsidiary processed EU employee data, transferring it to global entities including Sweden and the US for HR management in joint operations. The Hamburg DPA investigated post-Schrems II compliance.
process_and_outcome: Objections from other authorities led to EDPB involvement; Hamburg imposed a €35.25 million fine in 2023 for related issues, with transfer suspensions ordered provisionally.

example_name: DPC Meta Platforms WhatsApp Fine
example_background: WhatsApp shared user data with Meta companies for advertising purposes without fully informing users or obtaining proper consent. The 2021 policy update integrated data across services, processing EU user data under terms that did not meet transparency requirements under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission fined WhatsApp €225 million in September 2021 after investigation. WhatsApp revised its privacy policy and data-sharing disclosures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 14 (information where data not obtained from data subject)

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Cross-Border Fine
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement and private clients. EU residents' biometric data was included, with no transparency or rights mechanisms for data subjects from 2018.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA led a coordinated EDPB action, fining Clearview €30.5 million in September 2022. Additional fines from other EU authorities followed, totaling over €50 million; Clearview ceased operations in the EU.

example_name: Irish Data Protection Commission v. Facebook (Schrems II Enforcement)
example_background: In 2020, the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield framework in the Schrems II case, determining that data transfers from the EU to the US did not provide adequate protection due to US surveillance laws. Following this, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated enforcement proceedings against Facebook Ireland Limited, which acted as the lead authority for Facebook's EU operations. Facebook had continued transferring personal data of EU users to the US for processing. The DPC found that these transfers occurred without sufficient safeguards after the invalidation of Privacy Shield. The case highlighted complexities in cross-border data processing within the EU single market, where supervisory authorities from different Member States needed to coordinate. Facebook's reliance on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) was scrutinized, as supplementary measures were deemed necessary but not implemented adequately. This involved millions of EU users' data being handled across borders, with potential exposure to third-country laws.
process_and_outcome: The DPC issued a decision in 2023 suspending Facebook's EU-US data transfers for up to five months and imposing a fine of €1.2 billion on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited. Meta appealed the decision and paid the fine under protest. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted a binding decision directing the Irish DPC after other EU supervisory authorities raised objections during the one-stop-shop mechanism process. Meta subsequently developed new transfer tools compliant with Schrems II requirements.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 46 (transfers subject to appropriate safeguards)

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Fine by Dutch and Irish Authorities
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with its EU lead in Ireland, processed personal data of children under 16 across multiple EU Member States without proper age verification or parental consent mechanisms. Reports emerged in 2020-2021 about lax default privacy settings allowing children to share content publicly and receive messages from strangers. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) initiated an investigation focusing on Dutch users, while the Irish DPC handled the lead supervisory role under the one-stop-shop mechanism. Coordination between authorities was required as the processing affected users in multiple territories. TikTok's centralized data processing model routed data through Ireland but impacted users in the Netherlands and beyond, raising issues of consistent enforcement.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the Dutch DPA imposed a €345 million fine on TikTok for mishandling children's data, following input from the Irish DPC and other authorities via EDPB dispute resolution. The Irish DPC continued separate investigations into broader GDPR issues. TikTok adjusted its app settings to default private accounts for minors and enhanced age assurance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (conditions applicable to child's consent)

example_name: Amazon EDPB Dispute on Advertising Data Processing
example_background: Amazon Services Europe S.à r.l., established in Luxembourg, processed EU customers' personal data for targeted advertising using data from outside users' purchases, such as browsing history and third-party sources. This occurred across all EU Member States, with Luxembourg as the lead supervisory authority (SA). In 2021, the French SA (CNIL) prohibited the processing nationally and referred the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) due to its cross-border nature. Other SAs, including those from Germany and Spain, joined the dispute, highlighting inconsistencies in enforcement. The processing involved vast datasets centralised in Luxembourg but affecting users in multiple territories.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the EDPB issued a binding decision under Article 65 GDPR, finding Amazon's processing unlawful for lacking valid legal basis and transparency. The Luxembourg SA was directed to impose corrective measures, resulting in Amazon suspending the advertising program in Europe and facing ongoing national proceedings. No final fine specified yet, but compliance adjustments were mandated.

example_name: Meta Biometric Data Processing EDPB Case
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited planned to process biometric data from photos and videos of EU users for facial recognition technology across its platforms. The Irish DPC, as lead SA, conducted a cross-border investigation starting in 2018, involving input from other EU SAs due to the pan-EU impact. Concerns arose over lack of explicit consent and necessity for such processing, with data handled centrally in Ireland but affecting users in territories like Germany and Spain.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the EDPB issued a binding decision prohibiting the processing and ordering Meta to suspend it within six months. The Irish DPC imposed a €210 million fine in 2023 for separate violations, with biometric issues resolved through cessation. Meta discontinued the feature in the EU.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on WhatsApp Processing (Ireland-Lead)
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, subsidiary of Meta, combined user data from its service with Facebook for advertising purposes across the EU. The Luxembourg SA initiated investigation in 2019, but Irish DPC was lead under one-stop-shop. Processing affected users EU-wide, with concerns from Dutch, German, and other SAs over legal basis and transparency.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued binding decision in 2021 directing Irish DPC to fine WhatsApp €225 million in 2021 for transparency violations. Additional proceedings on data combination led to further scrutiny. WhatsApp updated privacy policies.

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers Coordinated Actions
example_background: Google Ireland Ltd provided analytics services to websites across EU Member States, involving data transfers to the US via cookies tracking user behavior. Following Schrems II, multiple SAs (Austria, France, Italy, etc.) investigated from 2022, finding insufficient safeguards despite SCCs. Irish DPC as lead coordinated with concerned authorities.
process_and_outcome: National bans issued (e.g., French CNIL ordered suspension), with EDPB discussions ongoing. Google offered updated SCCs and addendums, leading some to lift bans after verification. No unified fine, but heightened compliance required.

example_name: Microsoft Cloud EU Data Residency Success
example_background: Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited provides Azure cloud services to EU customers, ensuring personal data of EU data subjects remains within EU territories or approved jurisdictions. Through its EU Data Boundary, data processing is localized, with supervisory oversight shared among relevant SAs.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft complied with GDPR transfer rules by implementing EU-based data centers and adequacy-compliant mechanisms. Audits by Irish DPC and others confirmed adherence, avoiding enforcement actions and earning trust in public sector contracts.

example_name: EDPB One-Stop-Shop Mechanism Success Case
example_background: The EDPB's cooperation framework under GDPR Chapter VII facilitated consistent handling of a cross-border case involving a multinational retailer processing loyalty program data EU-wide, with lead SA in the Netherlands coordinating with others.
process_and_outcome: Through mutual assistance and consistency mechanism, authorities reached aligned decisions without disputes, enabling the retailer to implement uniform compliance measures across states.

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Standard Contractual Clauses for Facebook Transfers
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission conducted an investigation into Facebook Ireland Limited's data transfers to the United States following the Schrems II judgment. The inquiry focused on whether the standard contractual clauses used for these transfers provided adequate safeguards under the GDPR. The process involved communication of a draft decision to the European Data Protection Board due to the cross-border nature and the need for Board opinion on measures concerning standard data protection clauses as per Article 64(1). The draft decision was shared with the EDPB, which then issued an opinion after considering inputs from other supervisory authorities. The background included extensive examination of supplementary measures implemented by Facebook to address risks identified in the CJEU ruling, including encryption and pseudonymization techniques. Multiple rounds of submissions and hearings occurred over several years, highlighting the complexity of international data transfers post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued a binding decision on 21 May 2020 urging the Irish DPC to suspend data transfers unless adequate safeguards were in place. The Irish DPC finalized its decision on 14 July 2020, prohibiting the transfers and requiring Facebook to cease processing EU-US data transfers within five months unless compliant measures were implemented. Facebook appealed aspects of the decision, leading to ongoing legal proceedings.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 46(2)(d) - Standard data protection clauses

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Meta Platforms Ireland TikTok Transfers
example_background: The Dutch Data Protection Authority submitted a draft decision to the EDPB regarding TikTok's data transfers from the Netherlands to China. The case involved scrutiny of standard contractual clauses and supplementary measures for transfers outside the EU. The Dutch SA communicated the draft decision to the EDPB as required when intending to adopt measures on standard data protection clauses. The EDPB process included a two-week objection period for members, followed by adoption of an opinion within eight weeks. The background encompassed analysis of TikTok's data processing operations, risk assessments, and the adequacy of technical and organizational measures to protect user data, particularly of minors, in light of Chinese intelligence laws.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted Binding Decision 2/2023 on 19 December 2023, finding TikTok's measures insufficient and requiring suspension of data transfers. The Dutch SA was instructed to finalize a decision prohibiting transfers unless compliant supplementary measures were implemented within six weeks.

example_name: French SA CNIL Draft on Google Analytics Transfers
example_background: The French data protection authority (CNIL) investigated Google LLC's use of Google Analytics for transferring personal data from France to the US. The inquiry determined that the standard contractual clauses did not ensure an essentially equivalent level of protection due to US surveillance laws. CNIL prepared a draft decision intending to prohibit such processing and communicated it to the EDPB for opinion under Article 64(1) since it concerned standard data protection clauses. The process involved detailed assessment of Google's transfer mechanisms, including the Analytics Consent Mode and IP anonymization claims, over multiple formal notice stages since 2020.
process_and_outcome: CNIL adopted the prohibition decision on 3 June 2022 after consulting the EDPB. One member state authority objected, triggering further EDPB review, but CNIL proceeded with the formal prohibition measure against Google.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on BCR Approval for Total Specific Site
example_background: The Spanish AEPD submitted a draft decision to approve Binding Corporate Rules submitted by Total Specific Site for intra-group international data transfers. The AEPD communicated the draft to the EDPB as required under Article 64(1) for measures concerning BCRs under Article 47. The Board reviewed the BCR content, including data retention periods, security measures, and rights of data subjects across group entities in multiple countries.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a positive opinion on 26 April 2019, confirming compliance of the BCRs with GDPR requirements. The AEPD subsequently approved the BCRs.

example_name: Luxembourg CNPD Decision on Amazon EU Transfers Referred to EDPB
example_background: The Luxembourg CNPD investigated Amazon Europe Core's data transfers to the US using AWS services. The draft decision on standard contractual clauses was communicated to the EDPB due to cross-border implications. The investigation covered advertising data processing and transfer impact assessments, with input from other concerned authorities.
process_and_outcome: Ten supervisory authorities objected to the draft, triggering EDPB binding decision procedure under consistency mechanism. Ongoing as of 2024.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on DPIA List by Italian Garante
example_background: The Italian Garante notified the EDPB of its draft list of processing operations subject to mandatory Data Protection Impact Assessments under Article 35(4). The communication followed the standardized format, including grounds for inclusion of specific high-risk processing activities like biometric data processing and large-scale profiling.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued Opinion 10/2020 on 17 November 2020, providing recommendations on the list's scope and criteria. The Italian Garante took utmost account and finalized the list.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on BCRs for Roche
example_background: The Belgian DPA submitted draft approval of Binding Corporate Rules for Roche Diagnostics International Ltd to the EDPB. The process involved review of BCRs covering data transfers within the Roche group for research and healthcare purposes across multiple Member States.
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted Binding Decision 1/2021 on 11 June 2021 under Article 65 after objections, confirming the BCRs met GDPR requirements with minor amendments.

example_name: Danish SA Draft on Clearview AI and EDPB Referral
example_background: The Danish Datatilsynet prepared a draft decision on standard contractual clauses used by Clearview AI for data transfers related to facial recognition processing. Due to the matter's general application across Member States, it was referred to EDPB for opinion.
process_and_outcome: The case contributed to coordinated EDPB actions against Clearview AI, resulting in bans across multiple EU countries.

example_name: British Airways Data Breach EDPB Involvement
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 400,000 customers, where payment card details and personal information were accessed by unauthorized actors through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The incident involved skimming of credit card data during the booking process over several weeks. The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) initiated an investigation, and due to the cross-border nature involving EU customers, the case was referred to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the GDPR consistency mechanism. The EDPB coordinated supervisory authorities to ensure uniform application of GDPR principles across member states, examining issues like notification timelines and controller responsibilities.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a monetary penalty notice proposing a fine of £183.39 million, later reduced to £20 million following appeal. The EDPB issued binding decisions on cross-border processing aspects. British Airways implemented enhanced security measures and data protection officer reviews as part of remediation efforts.

example_name: Marriott International Data Breach EDPB Coordination
example_background: Marriott International disclosed in 2018 a breach of its Starwood guest reservation database, impacting up to 383 million guest records worldwide, including passport numbers, payment information, and travel details from systems acquired in 2016. The breach occurred due to access credentials compromised years earlier. Affecting customers across multiple EU countries, the case triggered GDPR's one-stop-shop and consistency mechanisms. The EDPB facilitated cooperation between Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC), as lead authority, and other EU supervisory authorities to address divergent views on penalty calculations and compliance obligations.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €20.6 million fine in 2020, with input from the EDPB. Marriott enhanced encryption, monitoring, and segmentation protocols. Further investigations continued into adequacy of prior notifications.

example_name: Google Analytics and Consent EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: In 2020, the French SA (CNIL) ordered Google to stop processing personal data via Google Analytics due to insufficient consent banners under GDPR. Other EU authorities challenged this for potential inconsistency. The case highlighted varying national interpretations of cookie consent and data transfers to the US. The EDPB activated the dispute resolution procedure under the consistency mechanism, involving coordination among 27 supervisory authorities and the lead authority in Ireland to develop uniform guidance on consent requirements for analytics tools.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the EDPB issued binding Decision 3/2022, requiring cessation of data transfers without adequate safeguards. Google adjusted its services, and national authorities aligned enforcement.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to Third Countries)

example_name: Meta Platforms Facial Recognition EDPB Referral
example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) operated facial recognition technology across the EU without fully compliant legal bases, affecting millions of users. After Ireland's DPC issued a draft decision, eight other supervisory authorities objected due to concerns over uniform application, particularly on Article 9 processing of biometric data. The EDPB stepped in via the consistency mechanism to resolve disputes and ensure consistent enforcement across the Union.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued Binding Decision 5/2023, mandating Meta to suspend biometric data processing and align practices. A €1.2 billion fine was imposed separately for transfers, with ongoing remediation.

example_name: TikTok Children's Data Processing Dispute
example_background: TikTok faced scrutiny for age verification and default privacy settings that exposed children's data to public profiles and algorithmic targeting. Ireland's DPC investigated as lead authority, but other SAs raised concerns about inconsistent application for non-EU headquartered operations. The EDPB coordinated to address child rights protections uniformly under GDPR.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 1/2023 in 2023 required TikTok to implement age assurance measures. DPC fined €345 million; platform adjusted settings globally.

example_name: Amazon France LogFiles CNIL-EDPB Dispute
example_background: CNIL fined Amazon France €35 million in 2020 for placing advertising cookies without consent. Other SAs objected, arguing the decision lacked assessment of data processing necessity. The EDPB used the consistency mechanism to review and harmonize interpretations of legitimate interest vs. consent for tracking technologies across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Binding Decision 6/2021 upheld elements but required broader evaluation. Amazon updated cookie practices Union-wide.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Schrems II Standard Contractual Clauses EDPB Guidance
example_background: Following the CJEU Schrems II ruling invalidating Privacy Shield, uncertainties arose on EU-US data transfers. Supervisory authorities issued divergent guidance on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). The EDPB convened under the consistency mechanism to produce uniform recommendations, involving cooperation with the European Commission.
process_and_outcome: EDPB Recommendations 01/2020 adopted by all SAs, providing a six-step transfer impact assessment framework. Thousands of organizations revised transfer tools accordingly.

example_name: EDPB COVID-19 Contact Tracing Apps Coordination
example_background: During the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, EU member states developed national contact tracing apps with varying data protection implementations. To prevent fragmentation, the EDPB and European Commission established a coordination group under the consistency mechanism to align on privacy-by-design principles, data minimization, and cross-border interoperability.
process_and_outcome: Issued coordinated Guidelines 07/2020 and facilitated API standards. Apps like Germany's Corona-Warn-App launched with harmonized protections, enabling EU-wide functionality.

example_name: French CNIL Decision on Standard Contractual Clauses for WhatsApp
example_background: In 2021, the French data protection authority, CNIL, conducted an in-depth analysis of WhatsApp's data processing practices, particularly focusing on the transfers of personal data from European Economic Area users to servers located in the United States. WhatsApp, owned by Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, relied on standard contractual clauses (SCCs) approved under the previous EU-US Safe Harbor framework, which had been invalidated by the Schrems I judgment in 2015. Following the Schrems II judgment in July 2020, which further invalidated the Privacy Shield and emphasized the need for supplementary measures to ensure an equivalent level of protection for data transfers, CNIL examined whether WhatsApp's SCCs, supplemented by additional measures, adequately protected user data against access by US intelligence authorities under laws such as FISA 702 and Executive Order 12333. CNIL communicated its draft decision to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) pursuant to Article 64 GDPR, highlighting concerns over the lack of effective safeguards for personal data transferred outside the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL submitted the draft decision to the EDPB for an opinion under Article 64(2). The EDPB issued a binding decision on May 20, 2021, confirming the need for a formal decision on the validity of the SCCs. Subsequently, CNIL issued a formal decision on July 6, 2021, ordering WhatsApp to comply with GDPR requirements for international data transfers within two months, failing which a €20 million fine or 4% of global turnover would apply. WhatsApp adjusted its practices and faced ongoing scrutiny.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 46(2)(d) - Standard data protection clauses
GDPR Article 64 - EDPB opinions on draft decisions

example_name: Irish DPC Investigation into Meta's EU-US Data Transfers
example_background: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated proceedings in 2020 against Meta Platforms Ireland Limited concerning the lawfulness of personal data transfers from the EU to the US by Facebook services. Following complaints under the Schrems II framework, the DPC assessed Meta's use of SCCs and supplementary measures. Detailed inquiries revealed potential issues with government access to data in the US. In line with GDPR cooperation mechanisms, the DPC shared its draft decision with other concerned supervisory authorities and communicated it to the EDPB when aiming to determine the adequacy of the standard data protection clauses employed.
process_and_outcome: The DPC submitted the draft decision to the EDPB under Article 65 for a dispute resolution procedure. On May 12, 2023, the EDPB adopted a binding decision directing the DPC to impose corrective measures, including a ban on data transfers to the US after a six-week suspension period, and a fine of €1.2 billion on Meta. Meta paid the fine and implemented changes pending new adequacy mechanisms.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 64 and 65 - EDPB involvement in draft decisions

example_name: EDPB Opinion on TikTok Binding Corporate Rules
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, part of ByteDance, sought approval for its Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) to facilitate intra-group international transfers of personal data across its global operations, including from the EU to entities in China and the US. The application process involved detailed submissions on governance, risk assessments, and safeguards. The competent supervisory authority, the Irish DPC as lead authority, reviewed the BCR draft and, pursuant to GDPR requirements, communicated it to the EDPB for an opinion before final approval.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB provided a positive opinion on the BCRs after review. The Irish DPC approved TikTok's BCRs on October 31, 2023, allowing compliant intra-group data transfers. This enabled TikTok to streamline its data flows while maintaining GDPR equivalence.

example_name: CNIL Draft List of DPIA Processing Operations
example_background: Under GDPR Article 35(4), EU member state supervisory authorities are required to publish lists of processing operations likely to require a data protection impact assessment (DPIA). The French CNIL periodically updates its list, which includes activities such as large-scale profiling, systematic monitoring, and processing of sensitive data in sectors like health and finance. When aiming to adopt or amend this list, CNIL communicates the draft to the EDPB for an opinion to ensure consistency across the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL submitted draft updates to the EDPB multiple times, including in 2018 and 2023. The EDPB issued opinions endorsing the lists with minor harmonization suggestions. CNIL published the updated list on February 2023, effective for controllers in France.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 35(4) - Lists of DPIA-required processing

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on BCRs for Siemens
example_background: Siemens AG applied for approval of its Binding Corporate Rules for controllers and processors to manage personal data transfers within its multinational group, covering Europe, Asia, and the Americas. The lead supervisory authority, the German BfDI, conducted a thorough review of the BCR documentation, including accountability measures, data protection principles, and audit mechanisms. Prior to approval, the draft was sent to the EDPB for opinion due to cross-border implications.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB issued Opinion 4/2018 in April 2018, approving the BCRs after clarifications. BfDI granted final approval in June 2018, enabling Siemens to use BCRs for compliant group-wide transfers.

example_name: Dutch DPA and EDPB on Max Schrems Complaint Against Google
example_background: Max Schrems filed complaints against Google LLC regarding EU-US data transfers for advertising purposes, questioning the sufficiency of standard contractual clauses in light of US surveillance laws. The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated as a concerned authority and prepared a draft decision on the adequacy of Google's SCCs, communicating it to the EDPB as part of coordinated actions post-Schrems II.
process_and_outcome: The matter was referred to the EDPB under Article 65. While the primary outcome involved ongoing proceedings, it contributed to broader EDPB guidance on supplementary measures for SCCs in May 2020.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on BCRs for Uber
example_background: Uber B.V. submitted Binding Corporate Rules for approval to handle personal data transfers across its international operations, including driver and rider data. The Irish DPC, as lead authority, reviewed the submission for compliance with GDPR standards on data protection principles, rights of data subjects, and transfer safeguards, then forwarded the draft to the EDPB.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued a favorable opinion. Irish DPC approved Uber's BCRs in February 2021, facilitating legal data flows within the Uber group.

example_name: Italian Garante Review of Standard Clauses for Yahoo
example_background: The Italian Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali examined Yahoo's data transfer practices to the US using standard contractual clauses. Amid post-Schrems II concerns, the authority assessed supplementary measures and prepared a draft decision, which was communicated to the EDPB for opinion on the determination of applicable standard clauses.
process_and_outcome: EDPB provided input via coordinated procedures. Garante issued orders in 2021 requiring Yahoo to cease certain transfers or implement additional safeguards, aligning with EU-wide standards.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines Development on DPIA Lists
example_background: Following GDPR implementation, several supervisory authorities submitted draft lists of high-risk processing operations requiring DPIAs. The EDPB coordinated to ensure harmonization, issuing guidelines and opinions on submissions from authorities like the UK's ICO and Germany's BfDI when adopting or amending lists under Article 35(4).
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted Guidelines 4/2019 on Article 35, influencing national lists. Multiple authorities published harmonized lists post-opinion.

example_name: OneStopPay GDPR Cross-Border Processing Case
example_background: OneStopPay, a Dutch payment service provider, processed personal data of over 1.5 million EU citizens across multiple member states without appointing a lead supervisory authority or establishing consistent mechanisms for cooperation between national data protection authorities. The company collected payment data, including names, addresses, bank account details, and IP addresses, through its platform used by merchants in various EU countries. This setup involved data transfers and processing activities that triggered the need for cross-border coordination under GDPR mechanisms, but the firm operated without proper designation of a main establishment or lead authority, leading to fragmented oversight by multiple national supervisory bodies. The case highlighted challenges in identifying the primary processing location for digital service providers operating decentralized operations across borders.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) coordinated with other EU supervisory authorities through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) procedures. Following an investigation initiated in 2019, OneStopPay was fined €750,000 in 2021 for failing to comply with cross-border processing obligations. The decision emphasized the need for standardized communication and cooperation among authorities.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 65 (Dispute resolution by the Board)

example_name: H&M Data Breach Notification Delay Across EU
example_background: H&M, the global fashion retailer, experienced a data breach in April 2019 affecting up to 45 million customers' personal data, including names, email addresses, and purchase histories stored in its German subsidiary's systems. The breach occurred due to unauthorized access to a customer database. As H&M operates across multiple EU countries with centralized data processing in Germany, the incident required notification to the lead supervisory authority and communication to concerned authorities in other member states where data subjects resided. However, notifications were delayed and not uniformly formatted, complicating cross-border assessment and response coordination among supervisory authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA) as lead authority investigated, fining H&M €35.25 million in October 2020. The process involved input from other EU authorities via the EDPB consistency mechanism, with the fine reflecting inadequate timely and standardized communication of breach details across borders.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 33 (Notification of breach to supervisory authority)
GDPR Chapter VII Section 2 (Cooperation between authorities)

example_name: Meta Platforms LinkedIn Data Scraping Case
example_background: In 2023, Meta Platforms faced issues with unauthorized scraping of public LinkedIn profiles by its data broker Bright Data, affecting users across the EU. Processing involved transfers and analysis crossing multiple jurisdictions, necessitating cooperation between the Irish DPC (lead for Meta) and other authorities like the CNIL in France. The case involved sharing summaries of facts, draft enforcement measures, and views on data access rights, but delays in standardized communication prolonged resolution.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted a binding decision in December 2023 mandating uniform application, resulting in coordinated fines totaling over €100 million across authorities. This followed structured exchanges highlighting communication gaps.
involved_parties: French CNIL and other DPAs (concerned authorities)

example_name: EDPB Meta Geoblocking Dispute Resolution
example_background: Meta's practices involved differential treatment of EU users based on location, with processing in Ireland affecting users across the EU. The Hamburg DPA challenged the Irish DPC's draft decision not to act, requiring the EDPB to facilitate communication of summaries, grounds, and views from concerned authorities.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision under Article 65, instructing the Irish DPC to proceed with a fine of up to €210 million, showcasing successful standardized electronic exchanges.

example_name: Dutch DPA Meta Child Data Transfers Case
example_background: Meta transferred children's behavioral data from the Netherlands to the US without adequate safeguards, involving cross-border elements with the Irish DPC as lead. The Dutch DPA urged action, necessitating formal communication of draft measures and views through EDPB channels.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB's 2024 binding decision fined Meta €136 million, exemplifying prompt standardized communication between authorities.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on WhatsApp Processing
example_background: In 2021, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an investigation into WhatsApp Ireland's processing of personal data for behavioral advertising following complaints. The case involved cross-border data processing affecting EU users. Other concerned supervisory authorities participated in the process. The lead authority drafted a decision, which was circulated among the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) members for objections.
process_and_outcome: Several authorities objected within the timeframe, triggering the EDPB's involvement under the GDPR dispute resolution mechanism. The EDPB adopted an opinion within the eight-week period, binding on the Irish DPC, resulting in a €225 million fine against WhatsApp in September 2021 for transparency violations.

example_name: TikTok EDPB Binding Decision
example_background: The Irish DPC investigated TikTok Ireland for children's data processing practices, including age verification and default privacy settings. Complaints from NOYB highlighted issues with under-13 users' data handling. The draft decision process involved objections from other EU supervisory authorities on aspects like world-wide privacy settings and geolocation.
process_and_outcome: Objections led to EDPB involvement. The Board issued a binding decision in 2023 after adopting its opinion within the extended timeframe due to complexity, resulting in an €345 million fine by the Irish DPC against TikTok for GDPR infringements related to children's data.

example_name: Meta Platforms EDPB Opinion 3/2022
example_background: Following the Schrems II judgment, the Irish DPC investigated Meta's EU-US data transfers. The draft decision addressed Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) implementation post-ruling. Multiple authorities objected, focusing on supplementary measures and risk assessments for government access.
process_and_outcome: The EDPB adopted Opinion 3/2022 within the eight-week period (extended), mandating the Irish DPC to amend its draft. This led to a €1.2 billion fine against Meta in 2023 for unlawful data transfers, with orders to suspend transfers and implement compliant mechanisms.

example_name: Clearview AI EDPB Opinion
example_background: Multiple EU DPAs investigated Clearview AI for scraping billions of facial images from the internet without consent to build a biometric database sold to law enforcement. The Dutch DPA's draft decision was objected to by others, leading to EDPB coordination.
