Customers reported unauthorized charges on their cards, and the company notified affected individuals after discovering the breach. The breach stemmed from vulnerabilities in the website's third-party tracking technology. British Airways took steps to secure the systems and advised customers to monitor their accounts. Numerous customers pursued claims for compensation, citing distress and time spent dealing with the aftermath, such as canceling cards and changing passwords.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and fined British Airways £20 million in October 2020, later reduced to £18.4 million on appeal. Separately, a group litigation involving over 25,000 claimants led to a court ruling in 2024 by the High Court that British Airways must compensate passengers for non-material damage under GDPR Article 82. The court rejected the airline's argument that compensation required proof of financial loss or significant distress, paving the way for payouts estimated in the tens of millions. British Airways stated it would appeal aspects of the decision.

example_name: DPC v WhatsApp Ireland
example_background: WhatsApp shared user data with its parent company Meta for advertising purposes without transparent legal basis. The updated terms in 2021 notified users of data sharing across Facebook services, but lacked specificity on processing purposes and retention. This impacted hundreds of millions of European users who continued using the service post-update.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined WhatsApp €225 million in 2021, increased from an initial proposal after input from other EU regulators. WhatsApp revised its privacy policy and faced ongoing scrutiny.

example_name: Vodafone Portugal GDPR Settlement
example_background: Vodafone Portugal sent marketing messages to customers without valid consent, using pre-checked boxes and unclear opt-in processes. The campaign reached subscribers who had not explicitly agreed, leading to complaints about unwanted communications.
process_and_outcome: Portugal's data protection authority fined Vodafone €375,000 in 2020. The company settled claims with affected customers, providing compensation and ceasing the practices.

example_name: Lloyd v Google UK Supreme Court Ruling
example_background: Google collected browser data via cookies and tracking without informed consent from Safari users in 2011-2012. Data was used for targeted advertising, affecting up to 4 million UK users who were not notified of the tracking due to settings bypass.
process_and_outcome: The UK Supreme Court in 2021 dismissed a representative action for damages under GDPR Article 82, ruling that uniform compensation per user was not permissible without individualized assessment of damage. This clarified requirements for compensation claims but affirmed the right exists.

example_name: Meta Platforms 'Pay or Consent' Model Fine 2023
example_background: In 2023, Meta introduced a 'pay or consent' model for EU users of Facebook and Instagram, offering ad-free access for a subscription fee or free access with personalized advertising based on data processing. The model involved multiple Meta entities as joint controllers and various processors for behavioral advertising data. User data was processed across platforms for profiling, with shared infrastructure handling the same data streams. Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) examined whether this setup ensured effective data subject rights and compensation mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion in 2023 for unrelated data transfers but highlighted joint controller issues. A separate investigation into the consent model led to injunctions. Meta was held accountable for full damages under Article 82, with processors sharing responsibility. Ongoing proceedings emphasized entire liability for compensation.

example_name: Schrems II Follow-on Proceedings Against Meta 2023
example_background: Following the Schrems II CJEU ruling, Austrian and Irish authorities pursued Meta for continued US data transfers involving Facebook user data. Multiple Meta group companies acted as joint controllers, with US processors handling standard contractual clauses-deficient transfers of the same data for advertising.
process_and_outcome: DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion, largest GDPR fine, suspending transfers. Full liability imposed under Article 82 for data subject harms from inadequate protection.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Data Breach Compensation Claims
example_background: In 2018, British Airways experienced a data breach affecting approximately 429,612 customers, where payment card details, names, addresses, and travel booking information were accessed by unauthorized parties through a vulnerability in the website's Magecart attack. The incident occurred between August 22 and September 5, 2018, leading to multiple customer compensation claims. Following the breach, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated, and customers pursued claims through the Court of Appeal, which ruled on liability apportionment. This case highlighted shared responsibilities among entities involved in processing payment data, including the airline as controller and third-party processors.
process_and_outcome: The ICO issued a notice for a £183.39 million fine, later reduced to £22 million in October 2020 after appeal. The Court of Appeal in Lloyd v. Google [2021] addressed compensation but was overturned by the Supreme Court. British Airways settled individual claims, and the case proceeded to class action considerations, with ongoing apportionment discussions among processors.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 82 (Right to compensation and liability)
GDPR Article 33 (Notification of a personal data breach)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Breach Fines
example_background: Marriott International, through its Starwood reservation system, suffered a data breach from 2014 to 2018, impacting up to 383 million guests worldwide. Hackers accessed names, passport numbers, payment card details, and other personal data. Marriott acquired Starwood in 2016, inheriting the compromised systems. The ICO determined that Marriott failed to notify affected individuals promptly after becoming aware in 2018, and inadequate security measures were in place across the processing chain involving Marriott as controller and Starwood systems as processors.
process_and_outcome: The ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 under GDPR, reduced from an initial £99 million proposal. The UK High Court upheld the fine in 2023. Marriott faced class actions in the US and settled for $52 million with 48 states, with claims of joint liability pursued against prior processors.

example_name: Deloitte Ireland GDPR Fine for Email Exposure
example_background: Deloitte Ireland's IT systems exposed 147 client email addresses via a publicly accessible webpage from March 2017 to July 2021, affecting professional services clients. The exposure occurred due to misconfigured access controls in cloud storage linked to processing activities. The Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated after a complaint, finding Deloitte acted as both controller and processor for client data, with shared processing responsibilities across its international network.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a €725,000 fine in May 2024. Deloitte cooperated and implemented remediation, but the decision emphasized proportionate liability for compensation if claims arose, with potential recourse against involved processors.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Joint Controller Fines
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland operated Facebook with unlawful processing of personal data for behavioral advertising from May 2018 onward, using data beyond consent scopes via the 'pay or okay' model. The case involved processing across EU subsidiaries and processors, leading to a cooperative investigation by EU data protection authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion in May 2023 for transfers to the US, upheld on appeal. Additional €390 million fine in 2022 for children's data. EDPB coordinated decisions on joint controllership and liability apportionment.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of data)

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Scraping Fines
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, building a biometric database sold to law enforcement. EU regulators coordinated after complaints, identifying Clearview as processor for clients but acting as de facto controller, with chains involving data aggregators.
process_and_outcome: Multiple fines totaling over €30 million, including €20 million from the Dutch DPA in 2022 and €25 million from the Italian Garante. Orders to cease processing; ongoing compensation claims reference joint liability.

example_name: Google Analytics Transfers EDPB Decision
example_background: Google's use of Analytics and Tag Manager tools by EU websites led to unlawful data transfers to the US from 2021, processing user data via cookies and IP addresses. The EDPB addressed joint controllership between Google (processor) and publishers (controllers), impacting thousands of sites.
process_and_outcome: EDPB binding decision in 2023 required cessation; national DPAs issued fines like €20 million to a Austrian site operator. Emphasized recourse rights for compensation apportionment.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: Schrems II Follow-on Processor Claims
example_background: After the CJEU's Schrems II ruling invalidated Privacy Shield, multiple processors faced claims for unlawful US transfers. In one instance, Facebook users pursued compensation, invoking joint liability against Meta and US-based processors handling data for advertising.
process_and_outcome: Austrian court in 2023 referred questions to CJEU on compensation amounts under Article 82; ongoing proceedings clarify claim-back mechanisms among controllers/processors.

example_name: Google Ireland v CNIL (Schrems II)
example_background: The case originated from complaints by Max Schrems regarding data transfers from Facebook Ireland to servers in the United States. Concerns centered on the level of protection for personal data transferred outside the EU under the Privacy Shield framework. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) received the complaints and initiated an investigation into the lawfulness of such transfers. The process involved multiple preliminary references to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) from Irish courts. In the first Schrems case, the CJEU invalidated the Safe Harbour framework in 2015. Following that, Facebook shifted to Privacy Shield, but Schrems filed new complaints arguing insufficient safeguards against US surveillance laws. The Irish High Court referred questions to the CJEU on the validity of Privacy Shield and the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs). The detailed background includes extensive analysis of US laws like Section 702 of the FISA Amendments Act and Executive Order 12.333, which allow bulk data collection by intelligence agencies, potentially accessing EU citizens' data without adequate remedies.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU delivered its judgment on 16 July 2020, invalidating the EU-US Privacy Shield decision and upholding SCCs but requiring case-by-case assessments for adequacy. The proceedings were handled by the CJEU, following referral under Article 267 TFEU from the Irish High Court. No direct compensation was awarded in this ruling, but it set the stage for subsequent compensation claims related to data transfers. Google Ireland, as a subsidiary handling EU data for Alphabet services, was also implicated in similar transfer practices scrutinized post-ruling.

example_name: Facebook Ireland Data Transfers Enforcement
example_background: Following the Schrems II judgment, numerous national DPAs initiated proceedings against Facebook Ireland for continuing international data transfers to the US without sufficient safeguards. The Irish DPC conducted a cross-border investigation, examining over 500 pages of documentation on transfer mechanisms. Complaints highlighted risks to user data from US government access. The investigation involved coordination with other EU DPAs under the GDPR one-stop-shop mechanism. Background details include Facebook's reliance on SCCs post-Privacy Shield invalidation, with supplementary measures deemed inadequate by complainants. The case drew attention due to Facebook's scale, handling data of over 400 million EU users.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC imposed a €1.2 billion fine on Meta (Facebook Ireland) for GDPR violations related to data transfers. The decision followed objections from other DPAs and was finalized after a dispute resolution procedure by the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). The fine was the largest under GDPR to date, with requirements for cessation of transfers and return or deletion of data.

example_name: Marriott International Data Breach Compensation Claims
example_background: Marriott disclosed in 2018 a breach affecting Starwood Hotels' reservation database, compromising passport numbers, payment info, and travel details of up to 500 million guests. The breach dated back to 2014, discovered during a 2018 acquisition. Investigations revealed hackers had resided in the system for years via a compromised web shell. The UK's ICO and other regulators probed notification delays and security failings. Customers initiated class actions seeking compensation for distress and potential identity theft risks.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million in 2020 (reduced from £99m proposed). Multiple compensation claims proceeded in English courts, with settlements averaging £3,000-£5,000 per claimant for non-material damage. US courts handled parallel actions.

example_name: Meta Platforms Biometric Data Scraping
example_background: Meta (Facebook) was investigated by the Irish DPC for scraping publicly available internet data, including biometric facial images from over a billion users without explicit consent. The practice involved off-platform processing for AI training. Launched in 2019, the inquiry examined compliance with data minimization and purpose limitation principles.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the DPC ordered Meta to cease processing and delete the data. A €275 million fine was imposed in 2023. Compensation claims by users are pending in Irish courts for violations of special category data protections.

example_name: Austrian Post Direct Marketing Case
example_background: The Austrian Post processed address data to infer political affinities for targeted direct marketing without explicit consent. Customers received unsolicited mailings based on profiling. The case started with complaints to the Austrian DPA, leading to administrative fines and subsequent court appeals.
process_and_outcome: Administrative fine of €18,000 upheld. The case reached the CJEU via preliminary reference, clarifying profiling consent requirements (C-300/21). Individual compensation claims were filed in Austrian courts for non-material damage.

example_name: Lloyd v Google Data Processing Claim
example_background: Google's Safari browser workaround collected cookies from 4.4 million UK iPhone users without consent from 2011-2012 for ad targeting. The claim alleged systematic data processing violations, seeking uniform non-material damages.
process_and_outcome: High Court dismissed the representative action in 2020 for lack of individual assessment. Court of Appeal overturned, but Supreme Court ruled against in 2021, citing Article 79(2) forum issues and no uniform remedy entitlement. No compensation awarded.

example_name: Amazon Luxembourg Customer Data Fine
example_background: The Luxembourg DPA investigated Amazon for non-compliance in handling customer personalization and targeted advertising based on inferred interests from purchase history and interactions. The probe covered data access rights fulfillment and legal basis for processing.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, a €746 million fine was imposed, the largest GDPR penalty at the time. Amazon appealed to Luxembourg courts; compensation claims by customers are in early stages under Article 82.

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Cloud Transfers Decision
example_background: Following Schrems II, noyb filed complaints against Microsoft for transferring EU customers' personal data (Office 365, Azure) to the US using Standard Contractual Clauses without adequate safeguards against US surveillance. Data included emails, chats, and documents of millions of users across sectors. Microsoft updated clauses but faced scrutiny on supplementary measures.
process_and_outcome: In July 2023, Austrian DPA issued a decision finding violations but imposed no fine due to Microsoft's remedial actions and cooperation. The case highlighted transfer compliance efforts post-ruling. Ongoing proceedings in other countries.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Fine (Irish DPC)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, responsible for Facebook's EU data processing, faced scrutiny over its handling of behavioral advertising data. In 2018-2020, the company collected and processed personal data from over 30 million EU/EEA users via the 'Off-Facebook Activity' feature without fully transparent or lawful basis for combining data from third-party sources with Facebook profiles. The processing involved sensitive inferences and transfers to the US, lacking adequate safeguards. The Irish Data Protection Commission examined compliance following complaints and its own audits.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the Irish DPC imposed a €265 million fine following a cross-border investigation with other EU authorities. Meta appealed but implemented changes to data practices. The fine was part of broader scrutiny on targeted advertising.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Fine (Irish DPC)
example_background: TikTok Ireland Unlimited Company processed personal data of up to 1.4 million EU users under 16 years old without age verification or parental consent. From July 31 to December 31, 2020, the platform allowed children to create accounts and share content publicly, exposing data like profile information, videos, and location to risks. Default privacy settings were not child-appropriate, and age gating mechanisms were ineffective. The investigation covered processing practices during the specified period amid rapid user growth.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 after an own-volition inquiry. TikTok was required to implement age-appropriate safeguards. The company stated it had already enhanced protections.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 8 (Conditions Applicable to Child's Consent)

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine (Luxembourg CNPD)
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l. advertised products to users based on inferred interests from extensive personal data processing, including browsing history, purchases, and location data. From May 25, 2018, to present, the company's algorithms personalized ads without transparent lawful basis or valid consent, processing data of millions of EU customers. The complaint highlighted opaque terms in privacy policies and lack of user control over profiling.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg CNPD imposed a €746 million fine in July 2021, one of the largest under GDPR, for violations of data processing principles. Amazon appealed to the Court of Justice, and proceedings continue. Amazon adjusted its advertising consent mechanisms.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD, Luxembourg)

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine (Hamburg DPA)
example_background: H&M Sweden AB's Nuremberg service center collected and shared detailed employee data among colleagues, including sensitive personal information obtained during offboarding conversations. From 2014 to 2019, supervisors gathered gossip about 1,000 employees' private lives, health issues, and family matters, storing it in an 'information pool' accessible to up to 50 people for HR decisions. An employee complaint in 2019 triggered disclosure of her own data summary.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg DPA fined €35.25 million in October 2020 after investigation. H&M remediated by closing the practice and enhancing employee data protections.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Fine (Dutch DPA)
example_background: Clearview AI Inc. scraped billions of facial images from public websites without consent, building a database sold to law enforcement and private entities. The Dutch DPA investigated after complaints, finding processing of EU residents' biometric data without lawful basis, transparency, or rights fulfillment. Images were collected indiscriminately from social media and news sites.
process_and_outcome: In September 2022, the Dutch DPA fined €30.5 million, ordering cessation of unlawful processing. Clearview contests jurisdiction but faces multiple EU actions.

example_name: Vodafone GDPR Fine (Italy Garante)
example_background: Vodafone Italy S.p.A. used an automated system to process traffic and location data of millions of customers for commercial profiling without consent. Between June 2014 and March 2019, the company analyzed data to create commercial profiles shared with retailers, lacking proper information and opt-out mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante fined €11.3 million in January 2022. Vodafone was ordered to halt processing and delete data.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italy)

example_name: Meta Platforms Cookie Consent Fine
example_background: Meta Platforms (Facebook) implemented a cookie consent banner on its platforms that pre-checked boxes for non-essential cookies, collecting data for behavioral advertising without valid consent. This affected EU users, involving categories like browsing history and preferences, with processing spanning years.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) fined Meta €1.2 billion in 2023 after investigation, the largest GDPR fine to date. Factors included gravity, duration, intentional setup, and prior infringements. Meta announced plans to appeal and adjust mechanisms.

example_name: Amazon EMEA Services GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., operating Amazon's services in Europe, was investigated for its personalized advertising practices. The Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection examined how Amazon processes personal data of users for targeted ads, including data from browsing history, purchases, and interactions. The inquiry focused on compliance with data protection principles such as lawfulness of processing, transparency in data usage, purpose limitation, data minimization, and accountability. Amazon's algorithms were found to use vast amounts of personal data to build user profiles for ad personalization without adequate legal basis or user transparency, affecting millions of European consumers.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the CNPD fined Amazon 746 million euros for violations related to data processing for advertising. Amazon appealed the decision, arguing the processing was lawful under legitimate interests, but the fine stood as a landmark enforcement action.
involved_parties: Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD, regulator)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland GDPR Fine 2021
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, part of the Meta group and controller for WhatsApp services in Europe, updated its terms and conditions in 2021, requiring users to accept new data sharing practices with other Facebook services. The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated whether this constituted transparent processing and a valid legal basis for sharing user data for commercial purposes. The probe assessed compliance with principles of fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, and the requirement for explicit consent, finding that the changes lacked sufficient information and user control, impacting over 2 billion global users with significant EU presence.
process_and_outcome: The DPC imposed a 225 million euro fine in 2021 after a cross-border investigation coordinated with other EU authorities. WhatsApp was ordered to revise its practices, and it appealed but faced upheld enforcement.

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine 2023
example_background: H&M Hennes & Mauritz AB, operating retail stores in Germany, required employees to share private details about their personal lives, health, and family issues in mandatory 'welcome meetings' where colleagues discussed personal experiences to foster team spirit. Managers recorded this information in a central storage system accessible to HR and executives without employees' explicit consent or knowledge of the purposes, affecting thousands of current and former employees over several years.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner investigated complaints and imposed a fine of 35.25 million EUR in October 2023. H&M discontinued the practice and improved internal policies.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Amazon EU Sarl GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon EU Sarl, operating the Amazon.in website for the European Economic Area, engaged in processing personal data of users through its Advertising Business Services. The company used data from interactions with the storefront, including pages visited, products searched, and items purchased, to create detailed user profiles for targeted advertising. This processing combined data across different services without explicit consent and relied on legitimate interests that were not properly balanced against user rights. The French data protection authority investigated following complaints about opaque personalized ads and lack of user control over data usage for non-essential purposes.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL issued a decision on May 30, 2023, finding multiple infringements including unlawful processing for advertising under Article 6 GDPR and lack of transparency under Article 13. Amazon was fined 32 million euros, ordered to cease ineligible processing within two months, inform users of data retention practices, and delete data processed unlawfully. The decision was upheld after Amazon's appeal attempts.

example_name: Google LLC (Alphabet) GDPR Fine
example_background: Google LLC processed location data of Android users across the EEA through its services. The company stored precise location history, web and app activity data without fully informing users or obtaining valid consent. Data was retained indefinitely and combined from multiple sources to build profiles for advertising. The French CNIL investigated after NOYB complaints regarding non-compliant cookie banners and consent mechanisms that did not meet standards for freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent. Violations spanned transparency, consent, and data minimization principles.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the CNIL fined Google LLC 150 million euros for breaches of GDPR articles on cookies, consent, and transparency. The decision followed analysis of Google's consent tool implementation from 2020-2022, finding continued non-compliance post-prior sanction. Google was required to update practices and faced additional scrutiny in other member states.

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland GDPR Fine
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited integrated Facebook services into its messaging platform, sharing user data for advertising purposes across Meta group companies. Users received insufficient information about data processing operations, combining service usage data with social interactions for profiling. The Irish DPC probed transparency failings post-2018 policy changes affecting 2 billion users, including EU residents.
process_and_outcome: After EDPB binding decision, the Irish DPC fined WhatsApp 225 million euros in September 2021 for persistent transparency violations. WhatsApp updated privacy policies and faced class actions in parallel.

example_name: Meta Platforms Ireland GDPR Procedural Fine
example_background: In 2023, Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd., responsible for Facebook services in the EU, faced scrutiny over its 'pay or consent' model introduced in late 2023. This model required users to either pay a monthly fee for ad-free access or consent to data processing for personalized ads. The Irish DPC examined whether this constituted valid consent under GDPR, amid complaints about fairness and necessity of data processing.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated an own-volition inquiry in 2023. In 2024, it imposed a €91 million fine for procedural deficiencies in handling prior complaints and transparency issues. Meta appealed, arguing procedural unfairness in the cross-border investigation involving other EU authorities.

example_name: Apple GDPR App Store Transparency Inquiry
example_background: In 2021, the Irish DPC opened a cross-border investigation into Apple's App Store practices concerning transparency of data collection and processing for users in the EEA. Focus was on compliance with information obligations for third-party apps and Apple's own data handling in tracking and personalization.
process_and_outcome: The DPC coordinated with other EU authorities under GDPR cooperation mechanisms. In 2024, following EDPB binding decision, Apple was found compliant after demonstrating adequate privacy notices and App Tracking Transparency features. No fine imposed; inquiry closed positively.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 13 (Information where data collected from data subject)

example_name: Microsoft GDPR Cloud Services Compliance
example_background: Microsoft provides Azure and other cloud services with Data Protection Addendum (DPA) incorporating GDPR requirements since 2018. It established EU Data Boundary, binding corporate rules, and subprocessors in Europe. Regular audits and Data Protection Impact Assessments are conducted for high-risk processing.
process_and_outcome: Multiple national DPAs, including CNIL and ICO, verified compliance through audits and complaints resolution from 2018-2023. No major fines; recognized for robust accountability, including annual transparency reports and cooperation in Art. 65 dispute resolutions.

example_name: H&M Fined by Swedish DPA
example_background: H&M, a global fashion retailer, collected employee feedback notes during offboarding sessions at its Swedish operations. These notes captured personal details, opinions, and observations about former employees' performance, attitudes, and personal circumstances. The data was stored in a shared drive accessible to HR and management personnel without sufficient technical and organizational measures to restrict access. The practice occurred systematically from at least 2015 until discovery in 2019, involving notes on hundreds of employees. H&M argued the notes were necessary for contextualizing rehiring decisions and performance patterns, but the processing lacked explicit legal basis and adequate safeguards for sensitive personal data.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) conducted an investigation following a complaint from a former employee. IMY determined the processing was unlawful under GDPR principles of lawfulness, purpose limitation, data minimization, and security. In December 2023, IMY imposed an administrative fine of SEK 35 million (approximately €3 million) on H&M. H&M appealed the decision, but the fine was upheld as proportionate given the company's size and the systematic nature of the data handling.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 5 (Principles relating to processing of personal data)
GDPR Article 83 (General conditions for imposing administrative fines)

example_name: Clearview AI Enforcement by Dutch DPA
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition technology provider, scraped billions of images from public websites worldwide, including social media profiles of European citizens, to build a massive facial recognition database. The company offered access to this database via API to law enforcement and private clients without obtaining consent from data subjects or conducting required impact assessments. Dutch authorities identified use of the database by Dutch police forces, prompting investigation into the processing of Dutch residents' biometric data. Clearview continued operations despite multiple European DPAs issuing orders to cease processing EU data.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (AP) investigated after discovering Dutch police subscriptions. In September 2022, AP imposed a €30.5 million fine on Clearview AI for unlawful data processing. Clearview did not comply with prior orders from other DPAs, leading to this enforcement. The fine was issued despite Clearview having no EU establishment, under GDPR's extraterritorial scope. Additional fines were threatened for non-compliance with data deletion orders.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: Meta GDPR Inquiry by Irish DPC
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland processed personal data of approximately 533 million Facebook users worldwide, including EU residents, for behavioral advertising. The processing relied on 'legitimate interests' legal basis without valid consent under EU law, particularly following the 2020 Schrems II judgment invalidating EU-US Privacy Shield. Meta transferred data to the US using Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) without supplementary measures to protect against US surveillance laws. Age assurance and default privacy settings were also deficient.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC initiated proceedings in 2020. In December 2022, under EDPB dispute resolution, DPC imposed a €1.2 billion fine on Meta, the largest GDPR penalty to date. Meta was ordered to suspend transatlantic data transfers and delete EU-US data within 5 months. Meta appealed and restructured data flows via adequacy decision post-Schrems II.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Chapter V (Transfers to third countries)

example_name: No Fine on Dutch Ministry of Justice
example_background: The Dutch Ministry of Justice and Security maintained a central database for processing personal data related to judicial proceedings, including victim and witness information. Following a data breach in 2021 where sensitive case files were accessible due to configuration errors, the Dutch DPA (AP) investigated the Ministry's compliance with GDPR security obligations. The Ministry had implemented basic safeguards but lacked advanced encryption and access controls proportionate to the sensitivity of judicial data. Despite the breach affecting thousands of records, the AP assessed the context of public authority operations.
process_and_outcome: The AP concluded its investigation in 2022 and issued a formal reprimand under Article 58(2) but declined to impose an administrative fine. The decision cited national rules under GDPR Article 83(7), which limit monetary penalties on public bodies to cases of serious, intentional misconduct. The Ministry was ordered to enhance security measures and report compliance within 6 months. No penalty was levied due to the public interest nature of judicial functions.
involved_parties: Dutch Ministry of Justice and Security
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Dutch GDPR Implementation Act (UAVG) Section on public bodies

example_name: French CNIL Decision on Public Hospital
example_background: A public hospital in France processed patient health data in a cloud service hosted outside the EU without adequate transfer safeguards. The hospital used the service for medical record management, exposing sensitive data to potential third-country access. Following a complaint, CNIL investigated the hospital's data processing activities, including lack of DPIA for high-risk processing and insufficient vendor contracts.
process_and_outcome: CNIL's 2021 investigation resulted in corrective orders to suspend unlawful transfers and implement safeguards, but no financial penalty was imposed. The decision invoked French law implementing GDPR Article 83(7), reserving fines for private entities or egregious public cases. The hospital complied by migrating data to EU-compliant providers.

example_name: Vodafone Germany Public Authority Data Fine Exemption
example_background: Vodafone Germany, as a telecom provider, handled data processing for German federal public authorities under public contracts for communication services. A 2022 audit revealed deficiencies in pseudonymization techniques for traffic data shared with authorities for law enforcement. While private entities faced fines, the public authority clients directing the processing were shielded.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian DPA fined Vodafone €45 million for its role but explicitly noted under GDPR Article 83(7) that no fines applied to the federal police and justice authorities overseeing the processing. Authorities received warnings and remediation directives instead.

example_name: UK ICO No Fine on NHS Trust
example_background: A UK National Health Service (NHS) Trust experienced multiple data breaches in 2020-2021, including loss of unencrypted patient devices and insecure email transmissions of health records. The ICO investigated compliance with security and accountability principles. The Trust had policies but failed in staff training and technical implementation for handling special category data.
process_and_outcome: ICO issued a reprimand and enforcement notice in 2022 requiring improvements, but no monetary penalty under UK GDPR provisions mirroring Article 83(7) for public authorities. The Trust published an action plan and achieved compliance certification by 2023.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Order Non-Compliance (2023)
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, the European entity handling data for Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, faced multiple enforcement actions from the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC). In 2020, the DPC issued a final decision under Article 58(2) of the GDPR prohibiting Meta from further processing personal data of EU users on the basis of the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for data transfers to the US following the Schrems II judgment by the Court of Justice of the European Union. This decision required Meta to refrain from such transfers unless compliant alternatives were implemented within five months. Meta continued certain data processing activities and transfers during this period. The DPC conducted further inquiries into Meta's compliance with this prohibition order. Separately, in another 2020 decision, the DPC ordered Meta to bring its processing of personal data in relation to behavioral advertising on Facebook into compliance with GDPR Articles 5, 6, 9, 21, and 25. Investigations revealed ongoing issues with data retention and processing practices. These cases arose amid broader concerns about transatlantic data flows and user consent mechanisms under EU law.
process_and_outcome: In April 2023, the DPC imposed a €1.2 billion administrative fine on Meta Platforms Ireland Limited for non-compliance with the prohibition order on data transfers to the US. The DPC found that Meta continued to rely on SCCs despite the order and failed to implement sufficient safeguards. This marked the largest GDPR fine to date. Additionally, the DPC issued a separate €91 million fine for violations related to the behavioral advertising order, citing inadequate compliance with data minimization and purpose limitation principles. Meta appealed both decisions to the Irish courts and is challenging the data transfer ruling before the Court of Justice of the EU. The company suspended certain data transfers pending resolution and committed to implementing new transfer mechanisms.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, supervisory authority)

example_name: TikTok Ireland GDPR Children's Data Order (2023)
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, operating the TikTok platform in the EU through its Irish subsidiary, was subject to a comprehensive investigation by the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) initiated in 2020. The probe focused on TikTok's processing of personal data of users under 16 years old, including age verification processes, default privacy settings, data retention practices, and transparency in data handling. The investigation stemmed from complaints filed by NOYB (None of Your Business), led by Max Schrems, alleging that TikTok's settings allowed children to share data publicly by default without adequate parental consent mechanisms. During the inquiry, the DPC issued a temporary order in 2021 under Article 58(2) GDPR prohibiting TikTok from processing data of users under 13 without parental consent and requiring suspension of certain data transfers to China. TikTok continued processing data in ways that raised concerns, including maintaining accounts for children despite age restrictions and inadequate enforcement of settings. The case highlighted issues with global platforms' handling of minors' data across millions of EU users.
process_and_outcome: In September 2023, the DPC issued a final decision fining TikTok Ireland €345 million for GDPR violations, including non-compliance with the temporary orders. The DPC found breaches of Articles 5, 6, 8, 25, and 27 GDPR related to lawfulness, fairness, age-appropriate design, data protection by design, and information to children. TikTok was ordered to bring its processing into compliance within specified timelines. The company stated it had already implemented changes to age verification and privacy settings. Other EU supervisory authorities participated via the EDPB dispute resolution procedure. TikTok announced plans to appeal the decision.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, supervisory authority)

example_name: WhatsApp Ireland GDPR Transparency Order (2021)
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland Limited, responsible for EU users of the WhatsApp messaging service owned by Meta Platforms, underwent investigation by the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) following complaints from NOYB in 2018. The complaints concerned WhatsApp's updated privacy policy implemented in 2021, which expanded data sharing with Meta companies for advertising purposes. The DPC's inquiry examined compliance with GDPR transparency obligations under Articles 13 and 14, particularly whether users received clear information about data processing and transfers. In May 2021, during the investigation, the DPC issued a formal order under Article 58(2) GDPR directing WhatsApp to suspend processing personal data for the new policy grounds until compliance was assured. WhatsApp proceeded with rolling out the policy updates to EU users, prompting allegations of disregarding the order. The case involved scrutiny of policy disclosures, consent mechanisms, and intra-group data flows affecting over 2 billion global users, with significant EU impact.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, the DPC proposed a fine but faced objections from other EU authorities. After EDPB binding decision, WhatsApp was fined €225 million in September 2021 for transparency violations. The DPC noted non-compliance with the suspension order as part of ongoing processing issues. WhatsApp complied by revising disclosures and suspending certain data uses. The fine was upheld on appeal in part, with WhatsApp ordered to pay. Further proceedings addressed data transfer aspects.
involved_parties: Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC, supervisory authority)

example_name: Google Ireland GDPR Transparency Fine (2022)
example_background: Google Ireland Limited, handling EU ad services, was investigated by the French data protection authority CNIL following NOYB complaints about insufficient transparency in its advertising personalization practices. The probe covered how Google informed users about data processing for targeted ads, including the combination of data across services like Search, YouTube, and Maps. In 2022, amid coordinated EU actions, the CNIL issued findings on non-compliance with information obligations. Parallelly, the Irish DPC examined related issues and had previously issued orders under Article 58(2) in other Google cases requiring changes to consent banners and processing. Google continued certain data processing practices during investigations, leading to assertions of order non-observance in linked proceedings. The case affected billions of ad impressions annually in the EU.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €150 million fine on Google in 2022 for transparency breaches under GDPR Articles 5, 12, 13, and 14. Irish DPC actions contributed via One-Stop-Shop. Google was ordered to update privacy notices within two months. The company implemented changes to consent modes and appealed, with some reductions granted later. Compliance monitoring continued.

example_name: Amazon Europe GDPR Advertising Order (2021)
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., the Luxembourg-based entity for Amazon services in the EU, faced investigation by the Luxembourg Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD) starting in 2019. The probe focused on Amazon's processing of personal data for targeted advertising, including the use of ad preferences and data from third-party sources. Complaints highlighted lack of transparency in data combination for profiling. In 2020, the CNPD issued a preliminary order under Article 58(2) GDPR requiring Amazon to cease processing data for advertising based on legitimate interests until compliance. Amazon maintained its advertising systems, leading to claims of non-compliance during the proceedings. The case involved extensive data processing for over 200 million EU customers.
process_and_outcome: In July 2021, CNPD fined Amazon €746 million for violations of GDPR principles including lawfulness, transparency, and data minimization. The decision referenced failure to fully adhere to the cessation order. Amazon suspended certain ad processing and updated practices. The company appealed to Luxembourg courts, resulting in suspension of the fine pending review in 2022.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Order Non-Compliance (2020)
example_background: British Airways, operated by International Consolidated Airlines Group (IAG), experienced a cyber incident in 2018 where attackers accessed personal data of approximately 400,000 customers, including payment card details and travel information. The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated compliance with GDPR security obligations. In 2019, the ICO issued a preliminary monetary penalty notice and orders under Article 58(2) requiring British Airways to implement specific security measures and provide detailed reports on remediation. The airline's response was deemed insufficient in some areas, including delays in notifying affected individuals and incomplete technical fixes. The incident drew attention to aviation sector data protection practices.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, the ICO imposed a £20 million fine (reduced from £183 million on appeal) for breaches of Article 5(1)(f) GDPR on security. The decision noted partial non-compliance with remedial orders. British Airways enhanced cybersecurity and settled related class actions. The fine was upheld in part by the Upper Tribunal.
involved_parties: UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, supervisory authority)

example_name: Marriott International GDPR Breach Orders (2020)
example_background: Marriott International, through its Starwood reservation system, suffered a data breach affecting 339 million guest records globally, including 30 million EU residents' passport numbers, payment details, and travel history from 2014-2018. The ICO launched an investigation in 2018, issuing enforcement notices under Article 58(2) GDPR in 2019 directing Marriott to conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA), review security, and notify affected individuals. Marriott's implementation was found lacking in scope and timeliness, with ongoing vulnerabilities identified in follow-up audits. The case underscored hospitality industry risks.
process_and_outcome: In October 2020, ICO fined Marriott £18.4 million (reduced on appeal) for security failures under GDPR Article 5(1)(f). Non-compliance with notices contributed to the penalty. Marriott invested over $40 million in remediation and faced additional EU fines. Appeals reduced the amount to £1.9 million pending further review.
involved_parties: UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO, supervisory authority)

example_name: H&M GDPR Employee Data Order (2023)
example_background: H&M Hennes & Mauritz, the Swedish fashion retailer, was investigated by the German data protection authority in Hamburg after employee complaints revealed that the company maintained a 'welcome-back room' where returning employees were encouraged to share personal details about colleagues' private lives, such as health issues, family matters, and vacation activities. This data was stored and used for performance management without explicit consent. In October 2020, the Hamburg DPA issued a preliminary order under Article 58(2) GDPR prohibiting further processing of such employee data and requiring deletion. Audits showed H&M continued some practices and delayed full deletion, affecting thousands of employees across European stores.
process_and_outcome: In October 2023, the Hamburg DPA imposed a €35.25 million fine for violations including unlawful processing under Articles 5 and 6 GDPR and non-compliance with the prohibition order. H&M was ordered to cease all such practices globally and implement training. The company stated it had discontinued the practice years prior and appealed the fine.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Orders (2022)
example_background: Clearview AI, a US-based facial recognition company, scraped billions of images from public websites without consent to build a database sold to law enforcement. Multiple EU DPAs, coordinated via EDPB, investigated after complaints. In 2021, the Dutch DPA issued orders under Article 58(2) banning Clearview from processing EU residents' biometric data and mandating database deletion for EU subjects. Similar orders came from France, Greece, Italy, and others. Clearview continued offering services to EU clients and maintained data, claiming no EU establishment. The case involved over 100 million EU faces.
process_and_outcome: EDPB issued a binding decision in 2022 affirming bans and deletions. Fines included €20 million from France, €20 million from Netherlands, with others pending. Clearview suspended EU sales but faced ongoing enforcement. US courts dismissed related suits.

example_name: Twitter International GDPR Microtargeting Order (2023)
example_background: Twitter International Unlimited Company, the Irish entity for X (formerly Twitter), was probed by the Irish DPC following NOYB complaints about processing political data for microtargeted ads during elections without adequate safeguards. The investigation covered data from likes, follows, and inferred interests used for behavioral advertising. In 2022, the DPC issued a temporary order under Article 58(2) suspending processing of EU users' public data for such ads. Twitter continued ad delivery mechanisms during the probe.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, DPC fined Twitter €450,000 (reduced) for violations including non-compliance with the order, breaches of Articles 9 and 21 GDPR. Twitter was required to halt processing and review systems. The company complied and shifted ad models.

example_name: Google Ireland Ltd GDPR Fine
example_background: Google Ireland Ltd, the controller for Google services in Europe, faced scrutiny over its processing of personal data for personalized advertising purposes. The French data protection authority examined the legal basis for consent obtained from users through the Android operating system and associated services. The investigation revealed that the consent mechanism did not adequately inform users or provide granular control options, affecting approximately 49.4 million French users between 25 May 2018 and 21 September 2019. Users were presented with pre-ticked checkboxes and complex interfaces that bundled acceptance of terms with data processing consents, leading to questions about whether consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous as required under GDPR. This case highlighted challenges in balancing commercial interests with user rights in large-scale digital ecosystems.
process_and_outcome: The CNIL conducted an investigation initiated by complaints and its own analysis. On 21 December 2019, it issued a public statement of objections, followed by a €50 million fine on 21 January 2020, deemed effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Google contested the decision before the French Council of State, which largely upheld it in June 2022, confirming the fine's validity under French law where administrative fines are imposed by the authority.

example_name: H&M Germany GDPR Employee Data Fine
example_background: H&M Hennes & Mauritz AB's German subsidiary gathered extensive personal data from employees during offboarding interviews at its Nuremberg service center from 2014 to 2019. Details included health information, religious beliefs, family issues, pregnancies, and psychological states, which were stored in an unstructured Excel file shared among managers for 'onboarding' new hires to foster cultural fit. Approximately 1,062 current and former employees were affected, with data retained up to three years post-employment without explicit consent or legal basis, breaching confidentiality principles.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA) investigated following employee complaints. On 1 October 2020, it imposed a €35.25 million fine administratively. H&M cooperated, deleted data, and revised HR policies.

example_name: Spain AEPD Mass GDPR Fines
example_background: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) handled over 1,200 GDPR sanction procedures in 2019-2020, targeting small businesses and entities for issues like unsolicited marketing emails, inadequate website cookie consents, and lack of privacy notices. Common practices included pre-checked consent boxes for newsletters and third-party trackers without transparency, affecting everyday data processing in e-commerce and services across Spain.
process_and_outcome: The AEPD imposed fines totaling over €10 million in the first 18 months post-GDPR, with administrative decisions upheld in courts. Procedures ensured fines were scaled to company size, promoting widespread compliance.

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Fine by Netherlands
example_background: Clearview AI, a US facial recognition company, scraped billions of images from public websites without consent to build a database sold to law enforcement. Dutch and other EU authorities examined processing of EU residents' biometric data without lawful basis or transparency from 2018 onwards.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch DPA fined Clearview €30.5 million in September 2022 administratively, with similar actions by other EU authorities. Clearview did not comply fully, leading to ongoing enforcement.

example_name: Google Analytics Consent GDPR Fine (CNIL)
example_background: In 2020, the French data protection authority CNIL investigated the use of Google Analytics by various websites. The tool involves transferring personal data from EU users to servers in the US without adequate safeguards. CNIL found that websites using Google Analytics did not properly inform users about data transfers or obtain valid consent, as the US-EU Privacy Shield was invalidated by Schrems II. The decision targeted specific entities but set a precedent for analytics tools.
process_and_outcome: CNIL issued injunctions and fines totaling €150 million across cases, including €20 million against Google LLC in 2022 for similar issues. Entities were ordered to comply within one month, with follow-up audits confirming adjustments to cookie banners and transfer mechanisms.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: H&M Data Protection Violation Fine
example_background: H&M's Nuremberg, Germany service center collected and stored excessive employee data, including sensitive details from sick notes, private conversations, and gossip about colleagues' health and family issues. This data was stored in accessible employee files for years, shared among managers, and used in performance reviews without consent or legal basis, affecting hundreds of employees from 2014 to 2019.
process_and_outcome: The Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision investigated following employee complaints. In October 2020, H&M was fined €35.3 million. The company closed the center and enhanced data governance policies.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision (BayLDA)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of data)

example_name: Vodafone GDPR Record Keeping Fine
example_background: Vodafone Germany failed to demonstrate compliance with GDPR accountability requirements. The company could not provide adequate documentation of processing activities, data protection impact assessments, or records of processing operations during a 2019 audit, covering telecom customer data processing across its operations.
process_and_outcome: The Hamburg Data Protection Commissioner conducted an audit and in December 2022 issued a €15 million fine. Vodafone was directed to improve its documentation systems and reporting processes.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Amazon France Logics Data Processing Fine
example_background: In 2020, CNIL conducted an online inspection of Amazon's French subsidiary, focusing on personalized advertising practices. The investigation revealed that Amazon processed vast amounts of personal data from browsing history, purchases, and locations for targeted ads without freely given consent. Consent banners lacked refusal options as straightforward as acceptance, and data was retained excessively.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €32 million fine in May 2023, along with injunctions to cease unlawful processing within one month. The fine reflected the scale of data processing and revenue generated from advertising.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 7 (conditions for consent)

example_name: Portugal's Notification of GDPR Laws to Commission
example_background: Portugal's data protection authority, CNPD, compiled and formally notified the European Commission of its national laws transposing GDPR requirements by the May 25, 2018 deadline. This included legislation on data protection in employment, health, and video surveillance, ensuring alignment with EU standards. Subsequent amendments, such as updates to law enforcement data processing, were notified promptly thereafter.
process_and_outcome: The notification was acknowledged by the Commission as part of GDPR transposition tracking. No enforcement actions were taken, confirming timely compliance with procedural obligations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 99 (entry into force and notification obligations)

example_name: Denmark's GDPR Implementation Notification
example_background: Denmark enacted its Data Protection Act in 2018, adapting GDPR for national contexts including research and public sector processing. The Ministry of Justice notified the European Commission by the deadline, detailing provisions on derogations and supervisory mechanisms. Amendments in 2020 for COVID-19 contact tracing were notified without delay.
process_and_outcome: The Commission reviewed and listed Denmark among compliant Member States in its transposition reports. No infringement proceedings were initiated.

example_name: Google Analytics GDPR Fine France 2022
example_background: France's data protection authority examined the use of Google Analytics by various websites. The tool transfers personal data from EU users to the US without adequate safeguards, exposing IP addresses and user IDs to US surveillance laws like the Cloud Act. The CNIL determined that standard contractual clauses did not sufficiently protect data given the lack of equivalent protection in the US. This led to orders against multiple organizations using the service.
process_and_outcome: In June 2022, CNIL issued formal notices and subsequently fined one website operator €150 under GDPR for continued use without compliant transfers. Similar actions followed against others, prompting widespread review of analytics tools.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Meta Platforms WhatsApp GDPR Fine Ireland 2021
example_background: WhatsApp, owned by Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook), processed personal data of approximately 573 million EU users for advertising purposes without a proper legal basis. The processing involved sharing user data across the Meta ecosystem to build detailed profiles for targeted ads. The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated following complaints from privacy organizations alleging opaque data practices and lack of transparency in privacy policies.
process_and_outcome: In September 2021, the DPC imposed a €225 million fine on WhatsApp for breaches related to transparency and lawful processing. The decision followed a multi-year investigation involving other EU regulators under GDPR cooperation mechanisms. WhatsApp updated its policies.

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine Luxembourg 2021
example_background: Amazon collected and used non-user personal data from third-party sellers and delivery personnel for advertising without transparent legal basis. Data included business contact details processed via the Advertising Console. The Luxembourg regulator investigated transparency and purpose limitation issues.
process_and_outcome: CNPD issued a €746 million fine in July 2021, one of the largest under GDPR. Amazon disputed the decision and sought annulment, but enhanced data handling practices.
involved_parties: Commission Nationale pour la Protection des Données (CNPD)

example_name: H&M GDPR Fine Germany 2023
example_background: H&M employees gathered sensitive personal data from sick colleagues, including medical details, religious beliefs, and family issues, during sick leave discussions. This data was stored in an unofficial system and used informally without consent or legal basis, affecting employee privacy over several years in stores across Germany.
process_and_outcome: Hamburg DPA investigated and fined H&M Justice S.à r.l. €35.25 million in October 2023. The company discontinued the practice and improved internal data policies.
involved_parties: Hamburg Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information

example_name: Clearview AI GDPR Fine Netherlands 2022
example_background: Clearview AI scraped billions of images from public websites worldwide to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement and private entities. EU residents' biometric data was processed without consent or legal basis, raising concerns over mass surveillance capabilities.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Clearview AI €30.5 million in September 2022. Similar fines issued by other EU authorities. Clearview continued operations outside EU but faced bans on processing EU data.

example_name: Spain AEPD Meta GDPR Fine 2023
example_background: Meta's platforms Facebook and Instagram used tracking mechanisms like the Facebook Container to monitor internet users across the web for advertising, processing data without valid consent. Consent banners were designed to encourage acceptance over refusal.
process_and_outcome: AEPD fined Meta €91 million in December 2023 for unlawful data processing in cookie consent practices. Meta appealed the decision.
involved_parties: Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)

example_name: Irish DPC Decision on Journalistic Processing by Online News Platform
example_background: An online news platform processed personal data of individuals featured in articles, including publication of images and details related to public events. The platform argued that the processing fell under journalistic purposes, involving collection and dissemination of information on matters of public interest such as political activities and social issues. The data subjects complained about the processing, claiming it infringed on their rights under GDPR chapters on data subject rights and controller obligations. The platform relied on the exemption for journalistic purposes to justify not applying certain data protection principles like lawfulness, fairness, and transparency, as well as rights to erasure and rectification. The processing included ongoing publication of articles accessible worldwide, with data retained in archives for public access.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission investigated the complaint and determined that the processing qualified for the journalistic exemption under Article 85 GDPR. No enforcement actions were taken, and the platform's practices were upheld as reconciled with freedom of expression. The decision emphasized proportionality and necessity for journalistic aims.

example_name: CJEU Case C-345/17 - Orange România SA v ANSPDCP
example_background: A telecommunications company processed traffic data to create aggregated statistics for academic research purposes. The national data protection authority challenged the processing, arguing it required full compliance with GDPR principles and data subject rights. The company contended that the processing was for academic expression, involving anonymization techniques but retaining some quasi-identifiers, and sought derogation from storage limitation and purpose limitation principles to enable longitudinal studies on communication patterns.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the European Union ruled that processing for academic purposes could qualify for derogations if necessary to reconcile data protection with freedom of expression. The case was referred back, and the processing was permitted with conditions on necessity, leading to no fine.
involved_parties: National Supervisory Authority for Personal Data Processing Romania (regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU Charter of Fundamental Rights Article 11

example_name: French CNIL Decision on Artistic Photography Exhibition
example_background: A photographer organized an exhibition displaying portraits of individuals taken in public spaces, processing their personal data through images and names for artistic expression. Data subjects objected, requesting removal under right to erasure. The artist invoked derogation from data subject rights for artistic purposes, arguing the works contributed to cultural discourse on urban life and identity.
process_and_outcome: CNIL reviewed the case and granted the derogation, finding the processing necessary for artistic freedom. No sanctions applied, and the exhibition proceeded.

example_name: UK ICO Fine to Daily Mail for Unlawful Processing Outside Journalistic Exemption
example_background: The publisher processed personal data for marketing purposes using subscriber lists, separate from its journalistic activities. Despite claims of journalistic context, the processing involved targeted advertising based on reading habits without consent or exemption basis. This occurred alongside legitimate news processing but was segregated as commercial.
process_and_outcome: ICO investigated and issued a £14,000 fine in 2019 for breaches of data protection principles, ruling the marketing processing did not qualify for journalistic derogation.

example_name: Dutch DPA Decision on NOS Broadcasting Data Processing
example_background: Public broadcaster NOS processed personal data in investigative journalism reports on corruption scandals, including names, addresses, and financial details of implicated individuals. Data subjects filed complaints regarding accuracy and right to object, but NOS applied journalistic exemption for public interest reporting.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority dismissed the complaints, confirming the processing fell under the exemption and was necessary for freedom of information.

example_name: Spanish AEPD Sanction on Non-Journalistic Use by Media Company
example_background: A media company collected user data from its news app for behavioral advertising, profiling users based on article reads. The company attempted to invoke journalistic exemption, but AEPD found the profiling served commercial ends unrelated to expression.
process_and_outcome: AEPD imposed a €1.5 million fine in 2021 for unlawful processing without valid basis, rejecting the exemption claim.
involved_parties: Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD, regulator)

example_name: Estonian DPA Ruling on Academic Research Exemption
example_background: A university processed health data from public surveys for a literary analysis of societal trends in a published book. Participants later requested data deletion, but the institution cited academic expression derogation for retaining and analyzing data.
process_and_outcome: Estonian Data Protection Inspectorate upheld the derogation in 2020, allowing continued processing as necessary for academic freedom.

example_name: German BfDI Decision Against Bild Newspaper Marketing Practices
example_background: Bild processed subscriber data for personalized newsletters beyond editorial content, using it for promotional campaigns. Claims of journalistic exemption were made, but the processing was deemed commercial profiling.
process_and_outcome: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information fined €20,000 in 2019, as the activity did not reconcile with journalistic purposes.

example_name: Italian Garante Ruling on Literary Work Data Processing
example_background: An author published a novel incorporating real personal data from interviews with public figures for literary expression on historical events. Subjects contested the processing under data subject rights.
process_and_outcome: Garante upheld the derogation for literary purposes in 2022, finding it proportionate to freedom of expression.

example_name: British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) Data Retention Case
example_background: The BBC retained personal data of over 23,000 individuals from a journalist's database for more than a decade after the underlying story was published. The database included contact details, employment information, and notes on sources gathered during an investigation into phone hacking scandals involving public figures and celebrities. The journalist had kept the data on a personal laptop and shared it within the organization. When the data subject discovered this through a subject access request, concerns arose about the ongoing storage of sensitive information without clear justification, particularly in light of the journalistic context of the original collection.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated following a complaint. In 2021, the ICO issued a monetary penalty notice of £100,000 to the BBC, determining that the prolonged retention exceeded what was necessary even for journalistic purposes. The BBC appealed the decision to the First-tier Tribunal, which in 2022 upheld the fine, confirming the breach despite exemptions claimed under journalistic processing.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: UK GDPR Article 85 (Safeguards for journalistic activities)
GDPR Article 9 (Processing of special categories of personal data)

example_name: Daily Telegraph v Information Commissioner
example_background: The Daily Telegraph newspaper published an article based on data obtained from the UK Land Registry showing ownership details of properties linked to non-domiciled UK residents. The Information Commissioner challenged the processing, arguing it involved excessive publication of personal data including names, addresses, and property values without sufficient public interest justification. The newspaper maintained that the disclosure served freedom of expression interests related to tax policy debates.
process_and_outcome: The matter reached the Court of Appeal in 2019, which ruled in favor of the Information Commissioner, finding the processing unfair and excessive. The court ordered the removal of the data from public view and clarified boundaries for journalistic exemptions. No monetary penalty was imposed, but it set a precedent limiting data dumps under journalistic pretexts.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Data Protection Act 2018 Schedule 2 Part 1 (Journalistic exemption)

example_name: Vice News Belgium Fine
example_background: Vice News Belgium published an article and video featuring images from body-worn cameras of police interactions during protests, including identifiable faces of police officers and protesters. The content was presented as journalistic reporting on public order events. Several police officers filed complaints, asserting that their biometric data and images were processed without consent or overriding public interest, despite the context of newsgathering.
process_and_outcome: The Belgian Data Protection Authority's Litigation Chamber investigated in 2023 and imposed a €50,000 administrative fine. The decision emphasized that while journalistic purposes exist, the processing must balance rights and be proportionate, finding the publication went beyond necessary disclosure.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Belgian Law of 30 July 2018 on the protection of physical persons with regard to processing of personal data

example_name: Google France v CNIL (Right to be Forgotten)
example_background: Multiple individuals requested Google to delink search results pointing to news articles containing their personal data related to past criminal convictions or public scandals. Google applied the right to be forgotten selectively, delinking results within the EU but keeping them accessible globally. The requests involved journalistic content published by French media outlets, raising tensions between data protection and global freedom of expression.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority (CNIL) fined Google €100,000 in 2019. The Court of Justice of the EU in 2019 partially upheld CNIL's position, ruling that de-referencing applies within the EU but not necessarily worldwide, balancing GDPR with expression rights. Google implemented geoblocking for EU users.
involved_parties: Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL)

example_name: Norwegian Media Exemption Guidelines Compliance
example_background: The Norwegian Data Protection Authority issued guidelines in 2020 for media organizations processing personal data for journalistic purposes. Several Norwegian newspapers, including Aftenposten, adopted internal policies aligning data handling with these guidelines, such as time-limited retention of source databases and pseudonymization in archives for investigative stories on public interest topics like corruption and health policy.
process_and_outcome: Following audits in 2022, the Norwegian DPA confirmed compliance for adopting entities, noting no enforcement actions were needed. This served as a model for reconciling data protection with expression rights through structured exemptions.

example_name: Politico Privacy Shield for Journalism
example_background: Politico, a major news publisher, implemented a 'newsroom GDPR code of practice' post-2018 GDPR enforcement, incorporating data minimization for reader analytics, source management systems with automatic deletion triggers, and transparency notices specific to journalistic processing. This applied to coverage of EU politics, including processing of EU officials' data in investigative reports.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Irish Data Protection Commission reviewed Politico's practices during broader tech audits and found them aligned with exemptions, resulting in no corrective measures. The framework was praised in industry reports as a best practice.

example_name: El País Data Processing for Investigative Journalism
example_background: Spanish newspaper El País developed a secure platform for handling large datasets in collaborations like the Panama Papers and subsequent leaks, applying pseudonymization to source-identifying data and limiting access within the newsroom. Processing involved personal data of public figures in financial secrecy stories, with policies ensuring data deletion post-publication unless archival value justified retention.
process_and_outcome: The Spanish Agency for Data Protection (AEPD) conducted a 2019 review amid high-profile leaks and confirmed adherence to journalistic exemptions, issuing no sanctions and referencing it positively in guidance.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Organic Law 3/2018 on Personal Data Protection (Spain)

example_name: Danish DPA Artistic Expression Exemption Case
example_background: A Danish artist processed personal images and data of public figures in an exhibition critiquing political surveillance, sourcing from public media. The data subjects objected to the reuse in an artistic context, prompting evaluation of whether the processing qualified under exemptions for artistic expression.
process_and_outcome: The Danish Data Protection Agency in 2020 dismissed the complaint, determining the processing fell within Article 85 exemptions as it served artistic purposes with public interest, requiring no changes to the exhibition.

example_name: Curia Case C-579/21 - WY v Land Hessen
example_background: The case involved a request for public access to a list of names of individuals who received COVID-19 vaccination appointments from a public authority in the German state of Hesse. The applicant sought disclosure of personal data contained in official documents held by the public health authority, which processed the data for public interest tasks related to vaccination scheduling. The authority initially denied full disclosure citing data protection concerns, leading to a preliminary reference to the CJEU on balancing public access rights under national law with GDPR protections for personal data in official documents.
process_and_outcome: The CJEU ruled that public access to official documents containing personal data must be reconciled with GDPR Article 5(1)(c) principle of data minimisation, allowing disclosure only to the extent necessary and proportionate under Member State law. The referring court was instructed to assess if anonymisation or pseudonymisation could suffice for the public interest in transparency.

example_name: Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection v Swedish Police Authority (IMY Decision DI-2020-788)
